### [1] [SD-GRPO: Verifiable Segment Decomposition for Long-Form Vision-Language Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09871)
*Hyunwoong Kim,Seongeun Lee,Hannah Yun,Junhyun Park,Jonggwon Park*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SD-GRPO introduces per-segment, z-normalized rewards for long-form vision-language generation, yielding a vector of segment-wise advantages instead of a single scalar and substantially improving performance across controlled and real-world tasks. Segmentation-aware credit assignment outperforms holistic GRPO, though entangled segments require blending holistic and per-segment signals for best results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Coarse-grained, single-scalar credit assignment in GRPO underfits long-form VL outputs whose segments are semantically meaningful. By leveraging the natural segmentation of outputs, SD-GRPO aims to provide finer-grained credit signals to improve long-form generation in multimodal settings.

Method: Introduce Segment-Decomposed GRPO (SD-GRPO) that computes per-segment rewards and z-normalizes them across the rollout group to produce a vector of per-segment advantages. Evaluate on three settings with increasing semantic entanglement: (1) DOCCI-based controlled multi-panel dense-captioning (semantically independent segments); (2) MultiChartQA-controlled long-form VQA (cross-segment credit misattribution grows with length); (3) MMSci real-world figure captioning (shared context across subfigures). Demonstrate integration with Dr. GRPO to show compatibility with existing GRPO frameworks with minimal overhead.

Result: SD-GRPO outperforms GRPO on the DOCCI task, with larger gains at higher segment counts. On MultiChartQA, both theory and experiments show rollout-level rewards suffer from cross-segment misattribution that scales with output length. On MMSci, blending holistic and per-segment rewards yields further improvements, indicating per-segment normalization helps but is not sufficient when segments are semantically entangled. SD-GRPO integrates smoothly into Dr. GRPO, confirming broad applicability with minimal implementation cost.

Conclusion: Per-segment normalization via SD-GRPO improves credit assignment for long-form VL generation and is compatible with existing GRPO frameworks. However, when segments are semantically entangled, a hybrid approach that blends holistic and per-segment rewards yields the best performance. SD-GRPO offers a general enhancement for long-form multimodal generation tasks.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and its variants, originally developed for Large Language Models (LLMs), have recently been applied to Multimodal LLMs and produced strong results. However, their coarse-grained holistic credit assignment from a single scalar advantage underfits vision-language (VL) tasks, where outputs are often long-form responses grounded in semantically rich images. To address this limitation, we exploit a structured signal that single-scalar formulations discard: the natural segmentation of long-form VL outputs. Concretely, we propose Segment-Decomposed GRPO (SD-GRPO), which z-normalizes verifiable per-segment rewards across the rollout group, yielding a vector of per-segment advantages in place of a single scalar. We evaluate SD-GRPO across three settings spanning controlled and real-world long-form VL generation, organized by increasing semantic entanglement across segments. On a controlled multi-panel dense-captioning task constructed from DOCCI, where segments are semantically independent, SD-GRPO consistently outperforms the GRPO baseline, with larger gains at higher segment counts. Extending to a controlled multi-chart long-form VQA task constructed from MultiChartQA, we show both theoretically and empirically that rollout-level rewards suffer from cross-segment credit misattribution that scales with output length. On a real-world scientific figure captioning task on the MMSci dataset, where subfigure captions share context across the figure, blending holistic and per-segment rewards further improves on both, suggesting per-segment normalization alone is insufficient when segments are semantically entangled. Finally, by integrating SD-GRPO into Dr. GRPO, we confirm that it can be applied to any GRPO framework with minimal implementation overhead to enhance long-form VL generation.

</details>


### [2] [WHU-Infra3D: A Full-stack Multi-modal Dataset and Benchmark for 3D Roadside Infrastructure Inventory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09882)
*Chong Liu,Luxuan Fu,Xuyu Feng,Zhen Dong,Bisheng Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WHU-Infra3D is a large-scale, multi-modal roadside infrastructure dataset combining panoramic imagery and LiDAR with 2D-3D instance association and cross-frame tracking, enabling five core tasks and revealing cross-city domain gaps and vulnerabilities in long-tailed defect statuses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap between coarse visual perception datasets and the need for strict 2D-3D alignment, attribute/status diagnostics, and lifecycle management for urban infrastructure maintenance in digital twin cities.

Method: Dataset construction and annotation: panoramic imagery + LiDAR across 53.8 km in three cities, with rigorous 2D-3D instance association and cross-frame tracking. Outputs include over 175k multi-view 2D bounding boxes, thousands of 3D infrastructure instances, and over 181k attribute/status annotations. Establish baselines for five tasks: 2D detection, 2D cross-view matching, 3D geo-identification, 3D point cloud segmentation, and attribute recognition.

Result: Cross-city domain gaps and weaknesses of current models on long-tailed defective statuses were observed. WHU-Infra3D provides a challenging, multi-modal benchmark with rich attribute/status data to drive scalable AI-driven infrastructure inventory and lifecycle management.

Conclusion: WHU-Infra3D is a crucial testbed for advancing urban infrastructure inventory and maintenance in digital twin cities, with the dataset openly available for research at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: The paradigm of digital twin cities is shifting from coarse visual mapping toward more precise and actionable digitization of urban assets. However, existing datasets predominantly focus on coarse visual perception, lacking the strict multi-modal alignment and attribute and status diagnosis required for automated infrastructure maintenance. To bridge this gap, we introduce WHU-Infra3D, a large-scale, multi-modal benchmark dataset dedicated to roadside infrastructure inventory. Covering 53.8 km across three cities, WHU-Infra3D uniquely integrates panoramic imagery and LiDAR point clouds with rigorous 2D-3D instance association and cross-frame tracking. Comprising over 175k multi-view 2D bounding boxes alongside thousands of 3D infrastructure instances, the dataset provides over 181k detailed attribute and status annotations (e.g., rust, occlusion) to empower operational health assessment. We establish comprehensive baselines across five core tasks: 2D detection, 2D cross-view matching, 3D geo-identification, 3D point cloud segmentation, and attribute recognition. Extensive evaluations expose significant cross-city domain gaps and inherent vulnerabilities of current models on long-tailed defective statuses, establishing WHU-Infra3D as an essential testbed for advancing scalable, AI-driven urban infrastructure inventory and lifecycle management. The WHU-Infra3D dataset is available at https://github.com/WHU-USI3DV/WHU-Infra3D.

</details>


### [3] [ABot-Earth 0.5: Generative 3D Earth Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09967)
*Ming Qian,Tianjian Ouyang,Mingchao Sun,Zijian Wang,Jincheng Xiong,Jiarong Han,Yongchang Zhang,Jiawei Zhang,Xu Wang,Yu Liu,Luyang Tang,Fei Yu,Zengye Ge,Mengmeng Du,Yuan Liu,Nianfei Fan,Song Wang,Yingliang Peng,Chunxue Jia,Yang Liu,Shiying Zeng,Haozhe Shi,Junnan Lai,Hongyu Pan,Zheng Wu,Ning Guo,Mu Xu,Hang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A generative 3D framework (ABot-Earth 0.5) that uses 3D Gaussian Splatting to synthesize large-scale 3D environments from satellite imagery, trained on real urban reconstructions; achieves under 10 minutes per km^2 inference, supports hierarchical LOD for web visualization, and aims to close the sim-to-real gap for Embodied AI (e.g., UAV navigation); claims ultra-low-cost, scalable large-scale 3D reconstruction and digital Earth visualization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for scalable, high-fidelity 3D world models derived from readily available satellite data to enable large-area digital earth visualization and Embodied AI applications. Existing pipelines are costly, slow, or domain-limited; a generative, geometry-texture consistent approach could reduce cost and enable real-time visualization and sim-to-real transfer.

Method: A generative model built directly on 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) representation. Trained on diverse urban reconstructions to learn realistic geometry and textures. Inference conditions on satellite imagery. Includes hierarchical Level-of-Detail (LOD) to enable real-time web visualization. Designed for fast, large-scale synthesis per area unit.

Result: Produces novel 3D scenes with realistic geometry and textures. Inference rate reported under 10 minutes per square kilometer. Demonstrates high realism and supports interactive visualization on web map engines. Aims to mitigate the sim-to-real gap for Embodied AI tasks such as UAV navigation.

Conclusion: ABot-Earth 0.5 presents a cost-efficient, scalable pathway to large-scale 3D reconstruction and digital Earth visualization, with potential benefits for Embodied AI applications and automated navigation, by leveraging 3DGS and satellite-conditioned generative modeling.

Abstract: We present ABot-Earth 0.5, a generative 3D framework designed to synthesize vast, seamless 3D environments from ubiquitous, geospatially referenced satellite imagery. To achieve this, we propose a novel generative model formulated directly with the 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) representation. The model is trained on a diverse corpus of existing real-world urban reconstructions, learning to generate realistic geometry and textures. At inference, it synthesizes novel 3D scenes conditioned solely on satellite imagery at a scalable rate of under 10 minutes per square kilometer, while demonstrating exceptional realism. The framework is designed for accessibility, with integrated hierarchical level-of-detail (LOD) structures that permit real-time, interactive visualization on web-based map engines. This high-fidelity simulation sandbox effectively mitigates the sim-to-real domain gap, enabling critical downstream Embodied AI applications like closed-loop UAV navigation. By providing an ultra-low-cost and high-efficiency solution, ABot-Earth 0.5 significantly lowers the technical and financial barriers to large-scale 3D reconstruction and empowers the future of global digital earth visualization.

</details>


### [4] [Generalized-CVO: Fast and Correspondence-Free Local Point Cloud Registration with Second Order Riemannian Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10019)
*Ray Zhang,Marcus Greiff,Thomas Lew,John Subosits*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A fast, correspondence-free local point cloud registration method using RKHS embeddings with anisotropic kernels, optimized with second-order on-manifold techniques to speed up registration and improve normal-aligned accuracy, outperforming first-order methods and ICP in tracking tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve robustness and speed of correspondence-free RKHS-based registration, particularly along surface normals, while maintaining accuracy in indoor/outdoor LiDAR/RGB-D tracking and under moderate misalignment.

Method: Represent point clouds as continuous functions via point-wise anisotropic kernels encoding local geometry, biasing alignment along normals and relaxing tangential directions. Solve the registration with a second-order on-manifold optimization using approximate Riemannian Hessians, yielding up to 10x speedups over prior first-order solvers.

Result: Empirically demonstrates improved frame-to-frame LiDAR and RGB-D tracking accuracy on diverse datasets. In driving-domain LiDAR tracking, achieves >55% reductions in both translational and rotational drift in feature-sparse environments. On object registration benchmarks, shows increased robustness compared with ICP-based methods and further gains when refining a global initialization under moderate misalignment.

Conclusion: The method delivers faster, more robust, and accurate registration by combining a continuous RKHS-based representation with second-order on-manifold optimization, with clear empirical gains in challenging tracking scenarios and robustness over ICP.

Abstract: We propose a fast and correspondence-free local point cloud registration method that leverages geometric surface structure and reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) embeddings. The method represents point clouds as continuous functions with point-wise anisotropic kernels that encode local geometry. This formulation improves alignment along surface normals while relaxing alignment along tangential directions. To solve the resulting registration problem, we propose a second-order on-manifold optimization scheme with approximate Riemannian Hessians, achieving a speedup of up to 10x over the first-order solvers used in prior correspondence-free RKHS-based methods. We demonstrate improved frame-to-frame LiDAR and RGB-D tracking accuracy across diverse indoor and outdoor datasets. On a LiDAR tracking registration task in the driving domain, we achieve a reduction of $>55\%$ in both translational and rotational drift in challenging feature-sparse environments. On object registration benchmarks, we show improved robustness over ICP-based methods and further gains when refining global initialization, particularly under moderate misalignment.

</details>


### [5] [SpineReport: Automated 3D Quantification and Reporting of Lumbar Spine Degeneration on MRI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10021)
*Nathan Molinier,Adrian A. Marth,Reto Sutter,Christoph Germann,Jacob A. Connolly,Mathieu Guay-Paquet,Nathan D. Schilaty,Kenneth A. Weber,Julien Cohen-Adad*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SpineReport is an open-source, fully automated framework for 3D lumbar spine MRI morphometrics that comprehensively analyzes the spine's anatomy and generates subject-specific reports, showing strong association with central canal stenosis (AUC up to 0.95) and good discrimination for lateral recess stenosis (AUC ~0.73), but limited for foraminal stenosis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current practice relies on 2D assessments that lack reproducibility and robustness; a robust, interpretable, 3D morphometric framework is needed for reliable quantification of lumbar spine degeneration and longitudinal analysis.

Method: Uses robust anatomical segmentations to extract 3D morphological and signal-based metrics for the spinal canal, spinal cord, vertebrae, intervertebral discs, and foramina from MRI. Generates cohort-comparative reports to improve interpretability. Clinically evaluated against radiologist grades for central canal, lateral recess, and foraminal stenosis.

Result: Central canal stenosis: high performance with T2-weighted CSF signal (AUC = 0.95). Canal AP diameter and area ratios strongly correlated with stenosis (AUC > 0.80). Lateral recess stenosis: moderate associations with lateral CSF signal (AUC = 0.73). Foraminal stenosis: no significant associations observed.

Conclusion: SpineReport provides a validated, open-access tool for comprehensive 3D lumbar spine morphometry with strong diagnostic potential for central canal stenosis and robust 3D metrics, contributing to improved interpretability and objective assessment in spine MRI.

Abstract: Lumbar spine conditions are a leading cause of disability worldwide, yet reliable quantification of degeneration from MRI remains challenging. In clinical practice, analysis is predominantly performed in two dimensions (2D), as manual three-dimensional (3D) assessment is time-consuming. However, 2D measurements suffer from limited reproducibility, particularly when anatomical structures are not aligned with the imaging plane. Existing automated approaches are often restricted to 2D, rely on discrete grading, or lack robustness and interpretability. We introduce SpineReport, an open-source, fully automated framework for comprehensive 3D morphometric analysis of lumbar spine MRI. Leveraging robust anatomical segmentations, the method extracts quantitative metrics from key structures, including the spinal canal, spinal cord, vertebrae, intervertebral discs, and foramina. These include both morphological and signal-based features, enabling cross-subject and longitudinal assessment. SpineReport further generates subject-specific reports that allow comparison with cohort distributions, improving interpretability and objective characterization of spinal morphology. Clinical relevance was evaluated against radiologist-reported severity grades for central canal, lateral recess, and foraminal stenosis. Metrics showed strong associations with central canal stenosis severity, with T2-weighted CSF signal providing the highest performance (AUC = 0.95). Canal AP diameter and area ratios also demonstrated strong correlations and high discriminative ability (AUC > 0.80). For lateral recess stenosis, associations were moderate, with lateral CSF signal being the most informative (AUC = 0.73). No significant associations were observed for foraminal stenosis despite robust region-of-interest extraction. SpineReport is released as an open-access tool: https://ivadomed.github.io/SpineReport/

</details>


### [6] [A Controlled Audit of Pretraining Contamination in Public Medical Vision-Language Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10066)
*Bruce Changlong Xu,Lan Wu,Alexander Ryu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Public medical-VLM benchmarks show measurable image-side overlap and mixed text signals; cohort-relative membership detectors are unreliable for small medical-VLM cohorts, suggesting overlap or leakage rather than robust memorization indicators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether open medical VLM benchmarks contain memorized data by auditing cross-model and leakage signals across multiple detectors and datasets.

Method: Apply four detector families—image-side near-neighbour overlap against PMC-OA-beta; canonical-order exchangeability; cohort-relative Min-K%++ tail enrichment; cross-model top-K overlap—to SLAKE-En, PathVQA, VQA-RAD, and OmniMedVQA mirror. Include manual adjudication, examine Qwen2.5-VL, and compare across several VLMs (e.g., BLIP-2) with external baselines.

Result: Image-side overlap detected on SLAKE-En (19.8% via SigLIP-B-16; 4.2% via SigLIP-SO400M); out-of-domain controls yield 0/2000; manual adjudication shows matches to different patients rather than pixel-duplicates, indicating source/distributional overlap rather than per-image memorization. Text-side canonical-order exchangeability signals persist for SLAKE-En (Qwen2.5-VL) and across five models on OmniMedVQA mirror, while BLIP-2 remains clean. Min-K%++ tail enrichment and top-K overlap collapse under external baselines; BLIP-2 reproduces signals without confirmed medical-VQA exposure.

Conclusion: Cohort-relative detectors alone are unreliable as membership-inference signals for small medical-VLM cohorts; signals may reflect distributional overlap or leakage rather than true memorization. More robust evaluation methods are needed.

Abstract: Medical vision-language models (VLMs) are evaluated on public benchmarks whose images and question-answer pairs have been freely downloadable for years, yet reported accuracy assumes these examples were absent from pretraining. We audit open VLMs on SLAKE-En, PathVQA, VQA-RAD, and an auxiliary public OmniMedVQA mirror using four detector families: image-side near-neighbour overlap against PMC-OA-beta, canonical-order exchangeability, cohort-relative Min-K%++ tail enrichment, and cross-model top-K overlap. We find measurable image-side source overlap on SLAKE-En: 19.8% of images are flagged under SigLIP-B-16 and 4.2% under SigLIP-SO400M, while out-of-domain controls produce 0/2000 flags. Manual adjudication shows same-modality, same-projection matches to different patients rather than verified pixel-level duplicates, so we interpret this as source or distributional overlap rather than confirmed per-image memorization. On the text side, Qwen2.5-VL on SLAKE-En shows a canonical-order exchangeability signal that survives ordering ablation and external non-medical baselines. On the OmniMedVQA mirror, exchangeability fires for five medical and general VLMs while BLIP-2 remains clean. In contrast, cohort-relative Min-K%++ tail enrichment and cross-model top-K overlap collapse under an external pre-domain baseline: BLIP-2 reproduces the apparent positive signals despite lacking plausible medical-VQA exposure. We conclude that these cohort-relative detectors are unreliable as standalone membership-inference signals on small medical-VLM cohorts.

</details>


### [7] [Interpretable Temporal Facial-Region Motion Analysis for In-the-Wild Parkinson's Disease Video Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10088)
*Riyadh Almushrafy*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Normalized facial-region motion descriptors (velocity-based) with a Random Forest achieve best PD-related video classification on YouTubePD, with balanced accuracy ~0.826 and AUROC ~0.855 on held-out data, stable across seeds and interpretable; region-level ablations and permutation importance support interpretability. Not intended for clinical severity scoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address reduced facial expressivity in Parkinson's disease (hypomimia) and evaluate whether lightweight, interpretable temporal motion features from facial-region keypoints can classify PD-related videos in-the-wild (YouTubePD).

Method: Extract geometric descriptors from 14 facial regions. Compare static geometry, normalized geometry, velocity-based descriptors, relative-velocity descriptors, and a GRU sequence baseline under a binary classification protocol. Include seed robustness analysis, region-level ablation, and permutation importance for interpretability. Use Random Forest with various feature sets, report held-out test performance.

Result: Best performance with normalized velocity descriptors and Random Forest: balanced accuracy 0.826 and AUROC 0.855 on the held-out test split; across 10 seeds: balanced accuracy 0.810±0.018 and AUROC 0.855±0.005. The representation appears stable and interpretable, suggesting normalized facial-region motion is lightweight for YouTubePD video classification.

Conclusion: Normalized facial-region motion provides a lightweight, interpretable representation for YouTubePD video classification in-the-wild; the work frames a benchmark-level analysis and does not claim clinical severity scoring or MDS-UPDRS facial-expression assessment.

Abstract: Reduced facial expressivity is a common motor manifestation of Parkinson's disease (PD), often described as hypomimia or facial bradykinesia. This paper examines whether temporal motion descriptors extracted from facial-region keypoints can support in-the-wild PD-related video classification on the YouTubePD benchmark. Each video is represented using geometric descriptors from 14 predefined facial regions. Static geometry, normalized geometry, velocity-based descriptors, relative-velocity descriptors, and a GRU sequence baseline are compared under the same binary classification protocol. To assess stability and interpretability, the study includes seed-robustness analysis, region-level ablation, and permutation importance. The best result is obtained with normalized velocity descriptors and a Random Forest classifier, reaching a balanced accuracy of 0.826 and an AUROC of 0.855 on the held-out test split. Across 10 random seeds, this representation remains stable, with balanced accuracy of 0.810 +/- 0.018 and AUROC of 0.855 +/- 0.005. Overall, the results suggest that normalized facial-region motion is a lightweight and interpretable representation for YouTubePD video classification. The study is framed as a benchmark-level analysis and does not claim clinical severity assessment or MDS-UPDRS facial-expression scoring.

</details>


### [8] [Maximum Matching Accuracy: An Instance Segmentation Evaluation Metric Utilizing Globally Optimal Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10107)
*Kaden Stillwagon,Alexandra D. VandeLoo,Craig R. Forest*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MMA is a threshold-free, globally optimal maximum matching accuracy metric for instance segmentation in biological imaging; it avoids hard IoU thresholds, per-object normalization, and greedy matching, aiming for more robust, interpretable rankings than AP@50, PQ, SEG, and AJI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation metrics for biological image instance segmentation have fundamental mathematical weaknesses: hard IoU thresholds that create discontinuities and low sensitivity, per-object normalization that biases scores with object size, and greedy or one-to-many matching that yield suboptimal, order-dependent correspondences. Collectively, these lead to unstable and unintuitive rankings in failure modes like split/merged cells and boundary imprecision.

Method: Propose Maximum Matching Accuracy (MMA), a threshold-free continuous metric that computes a globally optimal one-to-one matching between predicted and ground-truth objects and aggregates total overlap using per-pixel normalization.

Result: MMA produces scores that are more stable, more sensitive, and more interpretable than existing metrics (AP@50, PQ, SEG, AJI) across experiments involving synthetic failure cases, progressive corruption tests, and model ranking comparisons.

Conclusion: MMA offers a principled foundation for fair instance segmentation benchmarking in biological cell imaging by addressing hard thresholds, size bias, and non-optimal correspondences through threshold-free, globally optimal matching and per-pixel overlap aggregation.

Abstract: Reliable evaluation of instance segmentation models requires metrics that accurately and consistently reflect segmentation quality. However, the metrics most widely used in biological imaging carry fundamental mathematical weaknesses: hard Intersection-over-Union (IoU) thresholds that produce discontinuous, low sensitivity scoring; per-object normalization that distorts scores under object size variation; and greedy or one-to-many matching procedures that yield non-optimal, order-dependent correspondences. Together, these properties produce unintuitive and unreliable model rankings under common failure modes such as split cells, merged cells, and cell boundary imprecision. We propose Maximum Matching Accuracy (MMA), a threshold-free continuous metric that finds a globally optimal one-to-one matching between predicted and ground truth objects and aggregates total overlap using per-pixel normalization. We evaluate MMA against AP@50, PQ, SEG, and AJI across three experiments: synthetic failure cases, progressive corruption tests, and a model ranking comparison. MMA produces scores that are more stable, more sensitive, and more interpretable than existing alternatives, providing a principled foundation for fair instance segmentation benchmarking in biological cell imaging.

</details>


### [9] [Improving PET/CT-Based Whole-Body Lesion Segmentation Using Prediction Uncertainty-Augmented Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10115)
*Bashirul Azam Biswas,Biratal Raj Wagle,Zhihan Yang,Marc A. Seltzer,Matthew E. Maeder,James B. Yu,Indrani Bhattacharya*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Uncertainty-aware, Bayesian ensembling-based framework for whole-body PET/CT lesion segmentation that improves robustness over nnU-Net, with voxel-wise epistemic/aleatoric uncertainty, uncertainty-augmented training, and case-adaptive routing; evaluated on AutoPET-III and Deep-PSMA; shows improved lesion recovery but more false positives, and uncertainty maps correlate with misclassifications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical lesion delineation in PET/CT is challenged by subtle features, confounders, and reader variability. A reliable system requires uncertainty quantification, reduced training stochasticity, and strong performance across multi-tracer, pan-cancer data.

Method: Baseline nnU-Net; incorporate Bayesian ensembling to reduce training stochasticity; perform voxel-wise decomposition into epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty; train with epistemic-uncertainty augmentation; evaluate with two public datasets (AutoPET-III 1611 scans, Deep-PSMA 200 scans); introduce case-adaptive routing to select between base and augmented models.

Result: Bayesian ensembling improves robustness and Dice on unseen AutoPET-III test set compared with deterministic nnU-Net; uncertainty maps highlight model disagreement and correlate with misclassifications, especially false positives; uncertainty-augmented training improves lesion recovery but increases FPVol (precision-recall trade-off); case-adaptive routing further improves Dice by choosing between base and augmented models.

Conclusion: This is the first study to systematically investigate uncertainty quantification in multi-tracer, pan-cancer PET/CT segmentation and to combine Bayesian ensembling with uncertainty-aware modeling for this task; demonstrates potential of uncertainty quantification to enhance clinical reliability, while highlighting trade-offs that warrant further calibration and optimization.

Abstract: Accurate lesion segmentation from whole-body Positron Emission Tomography (PET)/Computed Tomography (CT) scans is essential for cancer staging and treatment planning. PET provides functional metabolic information with different radiotracers, while CT offers anatomical localization. Lesion delineation from PET/CT imaging is clinically challenging due to subtle imaging features, confounders, and inter-reader variability. Existing deep learning approaches suffer from training-related stochasticity, inconsistent predictions, missed lesions in high tumor-burden cases, and lack uncertainty quantification, limiting their clinical reliability. Using nnU-Net as a baseline, we propose an uncertainty-aware framework for whole-body PET/CT lesion segmentation that integrates (1) Bayesian ensembling to reduce training stochasticity, (2) voxel-wise uncertainty quantification with epistemic and aleatoric decomposition, and (3) epistemic uncertainty-augmented training to improve lesion detection. Two public datasets, AutoPET-III (1,611 scans) and Deep-PSMA (200 scans), comprising FDG and PSMA studies across multiple cancer types, are used for training and evaluation. Bayesian ensembling improves robustness and performance over deterministic nnU-Net models on the unseen AutoPET-III test set. Uncertainty maps highlight regions of model disagreement and correlate with misclassifications, particularly false positives. Uncertainty-augmented training improves lesion recovery at the cost of increased FPVol, reflecting a precision-recall trade-off. A case-adaptive routing strategy further improves Dice by selecting between the base and augmented models. To our knowledge, this is the first study to systematically investigate uncertainty quantification in multi-tracer, pan-cancer PET/CT segmentation and to combine Bayesian ensembling with uncertainty-aware modeling for this task.

</details>


### [10] [BiWM: Advancing Open-Source Interactive Video World Models with Bidirectional Autoregression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10135)
*Shaohao Rui,Xiaofeng Mao,Zhanyu Zhang,Peijia Lin,Yansong Zhu,Yibo Zhang,Haibin Wan,Weijie Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BiWM is a full-stack bidirectional autoregressive video world model framework that streamlines training (two stages) and enables real-time camera control, long-horizon rollouts, and open-source access.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of both causal pipelines (multi-stage training, error accumulation) and existing bidirectional models (lack of open-source frameworks and full-stack support), aiming for interactive, high-fidelity video world modeling with efficient training.

Method: From pretrained video backbone, inject camera control via fine-tuning; apply few-step Distribution Matching Distillation to convert backbone into action/camera-controllable world model; two training stages; implement history compression (FramePack/PackForcing); optional NVFP4 4-bit training/inference; add GAN and forward-KL objectives to counter mode-seeking degradation; supports multiple backbones (Wan2.1-1.3B, Wan2.2-5B, HunyuanVideo-1.5-8B, LTX-2.3-22B); open-source.

Result: Converges in a few hundred steps on 8xH200 GPUs; enables real-world camera control and long rollouts with improved fidelity; supports broad backbone compatibility; provides resource-constrained research feasibility.

Conclusion: BiWM offers a simplified, high-fidelity bidirectional autoregressive video world modeling framework with improved interactivity and open-source accessibility, reducing training steps from four to two and enabling broader experimentation.

Abstract: Transitioning bidirectional video diffusion models into an autoregressive paradigm improves the interactivity of video world models, but existing causal pipelines need many stages (control fine-tuning, autoregressive training, causal initialization, few-step distillation) and still trail bidirectional models in quality due to error accumulation. Recent world models such as Yume-1.5 and Matrix-Game-3.0 instead adopt a bidirectional autoregressive approach, gaining fidelity and stable long-horizon rollout from self-correcting error propagation, yet open-source frameworks (e.g., minWM) support only causal models. We present BiWM, the first full-stack framework for interactive video world models under the bidirectional autoregressive paradigm, jointly optimizing generation quality and inference speed. From a pretrained video backbone, BiWM injects camera control by fine-tuning, then runs a few-step Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) stage that turns the backbone into an action/camera-controllable world model: just two training stages instead of four in minWM, converging in a few hundred steps on 8xH200 GPUs. A single recipe spans Wan2.1-1.3B, Wan2.2-5B, HunyuanVideo-1.5-8B, and LTX-2.3-22B, and also supports secondary fine-tuning of existing bidirectional models. BiWM enables real-world camera control where minWM loses controllability, integrates pluggable history compression (FramePack-style and PackForcing-style) for long rollouts, and offers an optional NVFP4 4-bit training/inference pipeline. To counter DMD's mode-seeking degradation, we add GAN and mass-covering forward-KL objectives that preserve scene dynamics. We open-source BiWM for resource-constrained research and high-fidelity environment simulation.

</details>


### [11] [iSAGE: A Human-in-the-Loop Framework for Remote Sensing Semantic Segmentation via Sparse Point Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10136)
*Osmar Luiz Ferreira de Carvalho,Osmar Abilio de Carvalho Junior,Anesmar Olino de Albuquerque,Daniel Guerreiro e Silva*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: iSAGE uses expert clicks on model errors to guide sparse supervision for semantic segmentation in remote sensing, achieving dense-like performance with minimal labeling and no auxiliary machinery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce annotation cost and improve cross-sensor generalization by focusing on informative errors rather than model confidences; current HIL methods rely on post-hoc machinery tied to predictions.

Method: Interactive pipeline: experts click on confidently wrong pixels; an error-weighted loss amplifies gradients at click locations; annotation actions form the dataset; iterative updates; four reading mechanisms compared (oracle entropy, pseudo-labels, CRF propagation, uniform).

Result: On BsB Aerial: 74.79% mIoU with 0.040% pixels, 97.2% of dense supervision; On ISPRS Vaihingen: 76.78% mIoU with 0.011% pixels, matching dense baseline (76.65%); iSAGE outperforms competing methods by 7.4–14.5 pp; only iterative HIL without auxiliary machinery among 31 methods.

Conclusion: Expert-error-focused, annotation-record-driven approach can achieve dense supervision with minimal labeling, without auxiliary machinery, and yields an extensible, auditable dataset with strong cross-dataset performance potential.

Abstract: Semantic segmentation in remote sensing requires costly pixel-level annotations, and nearly every problem demands a new dataset since models rarely transfer across sensors, platforms, or geographies. Existing human-in-the-loop frameworks expand sparse clicks into dense supervision via auxiliary machinery (pseudo-labels, propagation, CRFs, foundation-model prompts, auxiliary heads), all operating on the model's predictive distribution. A confidently wrong pixel is indistinguishable from a confidently correct one in that distribution by construction, so no rule reading it can separate the two; the distinguishing signal is external to the model. This paper hypothesizes that expert clicks targeting confident model errors, not arbitrary pixels, suffice to match dense supervision, with no expansion machinery. iSAGE (Iterative Sparse Annotation Guided by Expert) realizes this hypothesis on an integrated open-source platform, where an error-weighted loss amplifies the gradient at each click and the annotation record itself is the dataset, extensible, correctable, and auditable. Experiments use a minimum-effort regime: at most one labeled pixel per class per frame. On BsB Aerial, iSAGE recovers 97.2% of dense supervision (74.79% mIoU on 0.040% of pixels) with contrasting class dynamics: amorphous classes (permeable areas) saturate from the seed, while small classes (cars) require late-iteration effort. On ISPRS Vaihingen (external benchmark), iSAGE reaches 76.78% mIoU with 0.011% of pixels, matching the dense baseline (76.65%) and exceeding all published methods. Under the same pipeline, four output-reading mechanisms (oracle entropy across budgets 1--100x, pseudo-labels across thresholds 0.90--0.99, CRF-based propagation, uniform random) plateau 7.4 to 14.5 pp below iSAGE. Across 31 surveyed methods, iSAGE is the only iterative human-in-the-loop framework operating without auxiliary machinery.

</details>


### [12] [DB-3DME: From Dataset to Benchmark for Human-aligned Automatic 3D Mesh Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10142)
*Nanshan Jia,Zhenyu Zhao,Sui Huang,Jingshen Wang,Zeyu Zheng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DB-3DME is a dataset and benchmark for automatic 3D mesh evaluation, comprising 2,619 synthetic meshes with human ratings on Geometry and Prompt Adherence; it reveals that 3D visual encoding quality drives human-aligned evaluation in VLMs, leading to a fine-tuned 3D-aware VLM (Qwen-2.5-VL-7B) with frozen language model that outperforms existing models; the benchmark and dataset are released publicly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluation of 3D assets lags behind generation capabilities. Existing evaluation methods (human judgments, learned metrics, VLMs) are costly, scale poorly, or lack task alignment. A scalable, human-aligned 3D mesh evaluation benchmark is needed to drive progress.

Method: Construct DB-3DME with 2,619 synthetic 3D meshes paired with human ratings on Geometry and Prompt Adherence. Benchmark various state-of-the-art VLMs on this data to identify bottlenecks. Discover that visual encoding of 3D representations correlates with human-aligned evaluation. Fine-tune an open-weight VLM (Qwen-2.5-VL-7B) by adapting its visual encoder while freezing the LM, to align with human judgments.

Result: The fine-tuned VLM substantially outperforms existing pre-trained VLMs across multiple evaluation dimensions, establishing a new benchmark for automatic 3D mesh evaluation. The dataset and benchmark are released on GitHub and Hugging Face for community use.

Conclusion: Visual encoding of 3D representations is a key determinant of human-aligned evaluation performance in VLMs. The work provides a scalable, open benchmark and a strong 3D-aware evaluation model, enabling future research in 3D mesh quality assessment.

Abstract: Recent advances in 3D generation have led to substantial improvements in realism, controllability, and efficiency, yet the evaluation of 3D assets remains underexplored. Existing evaluation paradigms, including human evaluation, learned metrics, and vision-language models (VLMs) as judges, suffer from limitations in cost, scalability, resolution handling, or task-specific alignment. In this work, we focus on 3D mesh evaluation and introduce DB-3DME, the Dataset and Benchmark for 3D Mesh Evaluation. DB-3DME contains 2,619 synthetic 3D meshes paired with human ratings on Geometry and Prompt Adherence. Using this dataset, we systematically benchmark state-of-the-art VLMs and identify visual encoding of 3D representations as a key factor for human-aligned evaluation performance. Motivated by this finding, we fine-tune an open-weight VLM, Qwen-2.5-VL-7B, for 3D mesh evaluation by adapting the visual encoder while freezing the language model. The fine-tuned model substantially outperforms existing pre-trained VLMs across multiple evaluation dimensions, establishing a new benchmark for automatic 3D mesh evaluation. We publicly release the benchmark dataset on GitHub and Hugging Face to facilitate future research.

</details>


### [13] [Fusing Satellite Imagery and Planimetric Maps for Cross-View Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10166)
*Quang Long Ho Ngo,Zimin Xia,Alexandre Alahi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A fusion module combines satellite imagery and planimetric maps for cross-view localization, introducing cross-modal conditioning and patch-level fusion; it augments standard encoders and yields state-of-the-art improvements, including a 30.13% reduction in mean localization error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current cross-view methods rely on a single aerial modality (satellites or planimetrics) and miss their complementary strengths. Integrating both could improve robustness, especially in occluded or detailed-area regions.

Method: Introduce a fusion module with (i) cross-modal conditioning (conditioning each modality's encoding on the other) and (ii) patch-level fusion (controlling granularity of information exchange). Integrates with standard encoders and demonstrates benefits within state-of-the-art pipelines.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art results with a 30.13% reduction in mean localization error. Qualitatively, the fusion adaptively selects the more informative modality, improving overall accuracy.

Conclusion: Integrating satellite imagery with planimetric maps via the proposed cross-modal conditioning and patch-level fusion yields significant localization gains over single-modality baselines and can enhance existing state-of-the-art methods.

Abstract: Current cross-view localization methods predominantly rely on satellite imagery as the aerial modality. Although recent work explores planimetric maps (e.g., OpenStreetMap tiles), these approaches often lag in performance. Yet both modalities are widely available and possess complementary properties. Satellite images are closer to ground-level camera imagery, offering finer detail, whereas planimetric maps contain annotated objects (e.g., streetlamps) and remain informative in areas where the ground is occluded, such as by foliage. Despite this, only one prior work provides an end-to-end method to fuse the two modalities, and it does not demonstrate their potential within state-of-the-art methods. To combine the strengths of both modalities, we propose a new fusion module that augments standard encoders and demonstrates that integrating satellite imagery with planimetric maps improves state-of-the-art single-modality methods. The module comprises (i) cross-modal conditioning, which processes each modality's encoding with awareness of the other, and (ii) a patch-level fusion rule that controls the granularity of information exchange. We achieve state-of-the-art results, reducing the mean localization error by 30.13\%. Qualitatively, the fusion adaptively selects the more informative modality, improving overall accuracy.

</details>


### [14] [FlexPath: Learned Semantic Path Priors for Image-Based Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10167)
*Taehyoung Kim,Tim Schoenbrod,David Eckel,Henri Meeß*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FlexPath is a two-stage framework that decouples feasibility from preferences in neural path planning: Stage 1 learns a task-independent spatial prior from visual maps via imitation learning; Stage 2 uses differentiable Path Shape Objectives to adapt to task-specific criteria without relearning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current learning-based planners are constrained by the shortest-path objective implicit in supervision, limiting flexibility for alternative criteria.

Method: Stage 1 uses imitation learning to capture a task-independent spatial prior over feasible paths from visual map inputs. Stage 2 introduces differentiable Path Shape Objectives (PSOs) to steer the prior toward task-specific criteria at the objective level, without re-learning path structure. A single pretrained model can adapt to multiple objectives. The approach is compatible with classical planners at inference time and supports shortest-path, obstacle clearance, semantic-aware avoidance, and waypoint guidance, with zero-shot generalization across unseen domains.

Result: For shortest-path planning on TMP, FlexPath reduces search effort by 14.3% compared to TransPath and achieves lower-cost paths on average, with strong zero-shot generalization across three unseen domains. For obstacle clearance with minimum clearance distance 2, it achieves 96.8% full obstacle avoidance while maintaining low search cost. The framework extends to semantic-aware avoidance and waypoint guidance via objective-level adaptation and remains compatible with classical planners. Code and data are released.

Conclusion: FlexPath provides a flexible, generalizable, objective-level adaptation framework for neural path planning that separates feasibility from task-specific preferences, improving efficiency and cross-domain generalization while remaining compatible with existing planning pipelines.

Abstract: Recent learning-based path planners use neural networks to process visual map representations and approximate heuristics for classical search algorithms, yielding near-optimal paths with reduced search effort. However, these methods are tied to the shortest-path objective implicit in their supervision, which limits their flexibility to accommodate alternative criteria. We introduce FlexPath, a two-stage framework that decouples feasibility from preference. In Stage 1, we use imitation learning to acquire a task-independent spatial prior over feasible paths from visual map inputs. In Stage 2, differentiable Path Shape Objectives (PSOs) adapt this prior toward task-specific criteria without relearning path structure, requiring only efficient objective-level adaptation. A single pretrained model can be adapted to multiple objectives. For shortest-path planning, FlexPath reduces search effort on TMP by 14.3% compared to the state-of-the-art TransPath, while also finding lower-cost paths on average and demonstrating strong zero-shot generalization across three unseen domains. For obstacle clearance with minimum clearance distance 2, it achieves 96.8% full obstacle avoidance while maintaining low search cost. The framework further extends to semantic-aware avoidance and waypoint guidance via objective-level adaptation, and remains compatible with classical planners at inference time. Data and code are available at https://github.com/FraunhoferIVI/FlexPath.

</details>


### [15] [A Large Scale Open-Source Image and Video Dataset for Robust Wildfire Detection and Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10174)
*Emadeldeen Hamdan,Yingyi Luo,B. Ugur Toreyin,Erdem Koyuncu,Adam J. Watts,Ugur Gudukbay,Ahmet Enis Cetin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A large-scale, open-source wildfire dataset (GWFP) for early fire and smoke detection, with diverse imagery and negative samples, benchmarking CNNs and transformers, and introducing HTE-ResNet for robustness under domain shift; results show strong cross-dataset generalization; dataset/code to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable robust, cross-domain wildfire detection by providing a diverse, real-world dataset that includes flames, smoke, infrared imagery, Ember, and challenging negatives, addressing domain shift and real-world monitoring needs.

Method: Compile GWFP with geographically diverse wildfire scenes and negative samples, including RGB, NIR, and Ember conditions. Benchmark multiple convolutional and transformer-based architectures in in-domain and cross-dataset settings. Propose Hadamard-enhanced residual connections (HTE-ResNet) to capture frequency–spatial feature interactions and improve representation under domain shift.

Result: Experiments show strong cross-dataset generalization and practical utility for real-world wildfire monitoring; the dataset and code will be released upon acceptance.

Conclusion: GWFP is a valuable resource for early wildfire detection research, enabling robust cross-domain evaluation and transfer to real-world monitoring; HTE-ResNet contributes to robustness against domain shift.

Abstract: Wildfire detection and monitoring are critical for mitigating fire spread and reducing environmental and infrastructural damage. In this work, we introduce GWFP (Global Wildfire Prevention Dataset), a large-scale, open-source dataset of wildfire images and videos designed to support early fire and smoke detection research. GWFP contains geographically diverse wildfire scenes, including flames, smoke, Waterdog/Fog environmental conditions, Near Infrared (NIR) imagery, Ember, and challenging negative samples collected from real-world scenarios worldwide. To evaluate dataset robustness and cross-domain generalization, we benchmark multiple convolutional and transformer-based architectures across both in-domain and cross-dataset settings. Additionally, we explore lightweight frequency--spatial feature interaction using Hadamard-enhanced residual connections (HTE-ResNet) to analyze representation robustness under domain-shift conditions. Experimental results demonstrate strong cross-dataset generalization and practical utility for real-world wildfire monitoring applications. The dataset and source code will be publicly released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [16] [Making Time Editable in Video Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10183)
*Konstantin Kuklev,Viacheslav Vasilev,Alexander Kunitsyn,Andrei Ivaniuta,Denis Dimitrov*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Temporal-control module added to pretrained Diffusion Transformers enables explicit time editing for video generation, allowing control over motion speed and temporal structure without retraining the backbone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current DiT-based video generation offers limited temporal control; enabling editable temporal dynamics is crucial for aligning generated content with user intent and for practical applications.

Method: Augment the pretrained DiT with a lightweight temporal module that performs explicit time editing, leaving the backbone intact and enabling adjustable temporal dynamics without redesigning the model.

Result: Preserves the original generative prior while expanding the controllable dynamic range, enabling finer control over temporal progression without redesigning the model.

Conclusion: The approach provides resource-efficient, flexible temporal control for diffusion-based video generation by integrating a small temporal module with existing DiT models.

Abstract: Modern Diffusion Transformers for video generation provide limited control over the progression of time and the editing of temporal dynamics. We propose a temporal-control methodology that extends a pretrained DiT with explicit time editing, allowing control over motion speed and temporal structure without redesigning the backbone. Its core implementation augments the pretrained model with a lightweight temporal module, preserving the original generative prior while expanding its controllable dynamic range.

</details>


### [17] [Fisher-Guided Progressive Parameter Selection for Adaptive Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10196)
*Ghodsiyeh Rostami,Po-Han Chen,Mahdi S. Hosseini*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces FisherAdapTune, a PAC-Bayesian, Fisher-geometry-guided dynamic PEFT method that adaptively freezes parameter groups based on stability of curvature, improving segmentation performance and zero-shot transfer; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PEFT often selects trainable parameters via fixed architectural heuristics rather than task-aware criteria. A dynamic, task-aware criterion could better balance adaptation and generalization by monitoring Fisher geometry drift across training.

Method: From a PAC-Bayesian perspective, decompose the generalization bound into Fisher-weighted update costs. Propose freezing parameter groups whose curvature contributions have stabilized, and use a scale-invariant Jensen-Shannon distance between consecutive Fisher distributions to quantify drift, yielding an adaptive active parameter set.

Result: Applied to a downstream segmentation task, FisherAdapTune improves in-distribution performance and zero-shot transfer across multiple settings, validating Fisher structural drift as a signal for efficient, task-aware adaptation.

Conclusion: Fisher structural drift provides a useful signal for efficient, task-aware PEFT; the method enables dynamic parameter selection and improved adaptation efficiency; code is publicly released.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) aims to adapt pretrained models with a small trainable parameter subset, however, most existing methods choose this subset from fixed architectural heuristics rather than using dynamic, task-aware criteria. We introduce \textbf{FisherAdapTune}, a Fisher-guided Adaptive Fine-Tuning framework that progressively selects parameter groups by tracking the temporal drift of their Fisher geometry. Starting from a PAC-Bayesian view of fine-tuning, we decompose the generalization error bound into Fisher-weighted update costs and show that parameter groups whose curvature contribution has stabilized can be frozen to reduce the error bound without interrupting the remaining adaptation dynamics. FisherAdapTune formulates this criterion with a scale-invariant Jensen-Shannon distance between consecutive Fisher distributions, yielding an adaptive active parameter set. We evaluate our approach on a downstream segmentation task, and results show FisherAdapTune improves the in-distribution performance and zero-shot transfer in multiple settings, validating that Fisher structural drift is a useful signal for efficient, task-aware adaptation. We release our \href{https://github.com/AtlasAnalyticsLab/FisherAdapTune}{code} publicly to enable further application of our proposed approach.

</details>


### [18] [An Improved Generative Adversarial Network for Micro-Resistivity Imaging Logging Restoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10200)
*Ahmed Faizul Haque,S. M. Riaz Rahman Antu,Saif Ahmed,Asadullah Hil Galib,Souvik Pramanik,Mohammad Ashrafuzzaman Khan,Mohammad Abdul Qayum,Mohsin Sajjad*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An enhanced GAN-based restoration approach for partially missing micro-resistivity imaging logs, using an FCN generator with depthwise separable residual blocks, Inception and multi-scale feature modules, spatial attention, and dual discriminators to enforce content and semantic coherence; achieves average SSIM ~0.903 (≈+0.3 over baselines).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To restore partially missing micro-resistivity imaging logs and improve semantic coherence and texture details for reliable interpretation.

Method: A generator based on FCN augmented with depth-separable convolutional residual blocks; an Inception module for multi-scale perception with reduced parameters; a multi-scale feature extraction module combined with a spatial attention residual block and channel attention; a global discriminative network and a local discriminative network that compete with the generator to improve content and semantic structure coherence.

Result: Experimental results show an average SSIM of 0.903 across five test sets with varying missing-region sizes, representing an improvement of about 0.3 over comparable methods, indicating better semantic coherence and texture restoration.

Conclusion: The proposed GAN-based approach provides a new deep-learning method for restoring micro-resistivity imaging logs with improved semantic coherence and texture, supporting more reliable downstream interpretation.

Abstract: An improved GAN-based imaging logging image restoration method is presented in this paper for solving the problem of partially missing micro-resistivity imaging logging images. The method uses FCN as the generative network infrastructure and adds a depth-separable convolutional residual block to learn and retain more effective pixel and semantic information; an Inception module is added to increase the multi-scale perceptual field of the network and reduce the number of parameters in the network; and a multi-scale feature extraction module and a spatial attention residual block are added to combine the channel attention. The multi-scale module adds a multi-scale feature extraction module and a spatial attention residual block, which combine the channel attention mechanism and the residual block to achieve multi-scale feature extraction. The global discriminative network and the local discriminative network are designed to gradually improve the content and semantic structure coherence between the restored parts and the whole image by playing off each other and the generative network. According to the experimental results, the average structural similarity measure of the five sets of imaged logging images with different sizes of missing regions in the test set is 0.903, which is an improvement of about 0.3 compared with other similar methods. It is shown that the method in this study can be used for the restoration of micro-resistivity imaging log images with good improvement in semantic structural coherence and texture details, thus providing a new deep learning method to ensure the smooth advancement of the subsequent interpretation of micro-resistivity imaging log images.

</details>


### [19] [FoA-SR: Faithful or Aesthetic? Profile-Aware Preference Optimization for Real-World Image Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10275)
*Amjad Mahdi Alqarni,Peizhong Ju*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FoA-SR introduces a profile-driven SR framework that learns distinct restoration preferences by fine-tuning LoRA adapters for Faithful (fidelity/structure) and Aesthetic (perceptual quality) goals using a shared candidate pool and reward-based ranking, enabling explicit control over restoration style.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world SR often optimizes a single objective, but users may prefer different restoration profiles. The work argues for explicit, controllable trade-offs between fidelity to the reference and perceptual quality in SR.

Method: Start with Flux2SR-based SR adapter (Flux2SR) trained with LR latent conditioning, flow matching, and image-space reconstruction losses. Build a shared stochastic candidate pool for each input image. Rank candidates with profile-specific Faithful and Aesthetic rewards to create winner-loser pairs. Fine-tune separate LoRA adapters (keeping the base model frozen) using these pairs. Evaluate on RealSR and DIV2K. Analyze candidate pools and perform Hybrid-LoRA ablation to assess profile integration.

Result: Faithful adapter improves reference-consistent metrics; Aesthetic adapter enhances perceptual metrics without references. Candidate-pool analysis shows Faithful and Aesthetic rewards often pick different winners. Hybrid-LoRA (merging profiles) yields implicit compromise rather than explicit control.

Conclusion: FoA-SR enables explicit, profile-based control of SR outputs through separate adapters, preserving base model capabilities and allowing users to choose Faithful or Aesthetic restoration. Merging profiles into a single reward diminishes controllability.

Abstract: Real-world image super-resolution (SR) is often designed with a single restoration objective, despite the current capacity of generative models to produce multiple high-quality reconstructions for the same input. In this paper, we argue that the best restoration strategy is subject to the specific restoration profile: a Faithful restoration prioritizes reference consistency, structure preservation, and hallucination suppression, whereas an Aesthetic restoration prioritizes visually pleasing and natural-looking details. We propose FoA-SR, a novel preference optimization approach to real-world SR based on profiles. To achieve this goal, FoA-SR starts with our supervised FLUX.2-based SR adapter (Flux2SR) trained with LR latent conditioning, flow matching, and image-space reconstruction losses for paired LR-to-HR image super-resolution. Following the development of the shared supervised super-resolution adapter, FoA-SR generates a shared stochastic candidate pool for each input image and ranks the same candidates using profile-specific Faithful and Aesthetic rewards to mine winner-loser pairs. These pairs are used to fine-tune separate LoRA adapters while keeping the base model frozen. Experiments on RealSR and DIV2K show that FoA-SR can steer the same SR adapter towards distinct restoration objectives: a Faithful adapter improves reference-consistent metrics while an Aesthetic adapter boosts metrics that measure perceptual quality without reference. Our candidate-pool analysis shows that Faithful and Aesthetic rewards frequently select different winners, and a Hybrid-LoRA ablation shows that collapsing both profiles into one reward yields an implicit compromise rather than explicit profile control.

</details>


### [20] [Dissect and Prune: Enhancing Robustness in AI-Generated Image Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10309)
*Dahye Kim,Jaehyun Choi,Hyun Seok Seong,Seongho Kim,Donghun Lee,Sungwon Yi,Jang-Ho Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DEAR improves robustness of AI-generated image detectors by pruning features that align with inpainted or non-inpainted regions, mitigating prediction asymmetry and susceptibility to post-processing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing detectors achieve high performance largely due to prediction asymmetry biased toward the real class, making them brittle to compression, resizing, and unseen generators; they rely on spurious features that obscure genuine generative artifacts.

Method: DEAR (Dissect and Prune) uses inpainted images to identify interfering components. It measures the alignment between channel activations and inpaint masks, removing features at extremes (highly aligned with inpainted or non-inpainted regions) and retaining features that capture genuine generative artifacts.

Result: Experimental results show markedly improved robustness to unseen generators and post-processing, effectively mitigating the prediction asymmetry; code is available at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: DEAR provides a principled, practical approach to robust GAN-generated image detection by content-aware feature pruning using inpaint-based masks, enhancing generalization under common post-processing operations.

Abstract: While existing AI-generated image detectors report high performance, we identify that this is largely driven by a critical prediction asymmetry: a bias toward the real class that severely limits sensitivity to generated content, especially under standard post-processing operations such as compression and resizing. We hypothesize that this stems from the model's reliance on spurious features, distracting signals that obscure true generative artifacts. To address this, we propose DEAR (Dissect and Prune), which leverages inpainted images to identify and prune these interfering components. Specifically, we find that features strongly aligned to either inpainted or non-inpainted regions are less robust to post-processing. By measuring the alignment between channel activations and inpaint masks, DEAR removes features at both extremes, retaining only those that capture genuine generative artifacts. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach significantly enhances robustness against unseen generators and post-processing, effectively mitigating the prediction asymmetry. Our code is available at https://github.com/dahyedahye/dear.

</details>


### [21] [Content-Induced Spatial-Spectral Aggregation Network for Change Detection in Remote Sensing Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10328)
*Yunlong Liu,Zekai Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CSI-Net fuses global spatial details and spectral difference information via SR, SD, and CGI modules to improve change detection, suppressing spectral differences in unchanged areas and achieving strong results on multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance change detection by effectively leveraging spatial details and mitigating spurious spectral differences in unchanged regions.

Method: A three-module network: SR (spatial reasoning) uses cascaded graph convolution blocks for global spatial modeling; SD (spectral difference) extracts spectral features by computing means/variances to reduce impact of spectral differences in unchanged regions; CGI (content-guided integration) fuses spatial-spectral features with high-level content guidance for efficient interaction.

Result: CSI-Net outperforms state-of-the-art methods on LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, and CLCD datasets and demonstrates applicability across different scenarios.

Conclusion: CSI-Net provides effective spatial-spectral fusion for change detection, achieving robust suppression of spectral differences and improved changed feature learning across diverse datasets.

Abstract: The integration of spatial and spectral information is beneficial to the improvement of change detection performance. However, existing methods cannot efficiently suppress the influences of spatial and spectral differences in unchanged areas. To address these issues, in this paper we propose a content-guided spatial-spectral integration network (CSI-Net) for the fusion of global spatial details and spectral difference information. Specifically, the proposed CSI-Net is composed of a spatial reasoning (SR) module, a spectral difference (SD) module, and a content-guided integration (CGI) module. In the SR module, the spatial information is learned by cascaded graph convolution blocks for global modeling. The SD module is responsible for the extraction of spectral features, by calculating the means and variances of features to reduce the impact of spectral differences in unchanged regions. In addition, in order to integrate the spatial-spectral features efficiently, we design a CGI module to further take advantage of their complementary information. In this module, high-level content information is introduced as a guide for a proper interaction. Due to the efficient spatial-spectral fusion, the proposed CSI-Net can learn the changed features better while achieving a suppression of spectral differences. Experimental results on LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, and CLCD datasets demonstrate that the proposed CSI-Net produces better performance compared to state-of-the-art methods, and is applicable to different scenarios

</details>


### [22] [Building Change Detection in Earthquake: A Multi-Scale Interaction Network and A Change Detection Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10329)
*Yunlong Liu,Zekai Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MSI-Net for post-earthquake change detection with short-interval multi-temporal imagery, introducing the TUE-CD dataset; outperforms state-of-the-art methods on WHU-CD, CLCD, and TUE-CD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-disaster rapid damage assessment requires accurate change detection from closely spaced multi-temporal images. Short imaging intervals cause viewing angle differences (side-looking) and data scarcity for training; a dedicated dataset and robust bi-temporal feature interaction method are needed.

Method: MSI-Net comprises joint cross-attention (JCA) for channel and spatial interaction, multi-scale offset calibration (MOC) to align bi-temporal features at multiple scales, and feature integration (FeI) to fuse calibrated multi-scale features for changed area prediction.

Result: Empirical evaluation on WHU-CD, CLCD, and the newly introduced TUE-CD shows MSI-Net achieves superior performance compared with considered state-of-the-art CD methods.

Conclusion: MSI-Net effectively enables efficient bi-temporal feature interaction and mitigation of side-looking artifacts for post-earthquake CD, and the TUE-CD dataset provides a benchmark for short-interval change detection in disaster scenarios.

Abstract: As one of the most destructive natural disasters, earthquakes have struck many countries around the world in recent years, causing serious economic losses. Change detection (CD) can be applied to post-earthquake damage assessment as it can infer destroyed change regions from multi-temporal remote sensing images. Furthermore, the CD with short imaging interval will better satisfy the needs of the emergency rescues after earthquakes. However, the capability of current methods built on deep neural networks is limited because the dataset with short imaging interval is absent. To meet post-disaster immediate relief, we create a CD dataset, Turkey earthquake CD dataset (TUE-CD), for the evaluation of building damage in the short term after an earthquake. Because of the short acquisition interval of the post-event images, the imaging angle is different for different temporal images, which leads to some side-looking problems. To deal with these challenges, we present a multi-scale feature interaction network (MSI-Net) for efficient interaction between bi-temporal features, as well as mitigating the effect of side-looking problems. Specifically, the proposed MSI-Net consists of joint cross-attention (JCA) modules, multi-scale offset calibration (MOC) modules, and feature integration (FeI) modules. The JCA module unifies channel cross-attention and spatial joint attention for sufficient feature interaction. The MOC module further estimates the offsets to align the bi-temporal image with the multi-scale features. Finally, calibrated features and multi-scale features are fused by FeI modules for the prediction of changed areas. Experiments on the WHU-CD, CLCD, and the constructed TUE-CD dataset indicate that the proposed MSI-Net provides better results than considered state-of-the-art CD methods.

</details>


### [23] [Multi-Angular Reflectance Anisotropy Observed from UAV Multispectral Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10350)
*Zhenqiang Qin,Chenguang Dai,Min Wang,Xian Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Geometry-aware BRDF analysis for UAV multi-angular multispectral imagery; workflow extracts BRDF-influenced radiometric variability by jointly estimating reflectance and viewing geometry across bands.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: UAV imagery at low altitude has multi-angular observations causing geometry-driven radiometric variability; quantify BRDF effects for radiometric consistency.

Method: Use structure-from-motion to refine camera intrinsics/extrinsics; annotate homogeneous regions on orthomosaic; reproject onto raw sub-images from different viewpoints; jointly extract per-band reflectance and viewing-geometry parameters; visualize in VZA-RAA domain.

Result: Demonstrates clear reflectance anisotropy across ten bands; red-edge and NIR bands show 119–137% variability between max and min reflectance; BRDF-related variability is non-negligible.

Conclusion: The proposed geometry-aware workflow effectively quantifies observation-geometry effects in UAV BRDF analysis and is necessary for radiometric consistency in multi-angular UAV imagery.

Abstract: UAV multispectral imagery naturally contains multi-angular observations due to low flight altitude and wide field-of-view imaging, which may introduce geometry-driven radiometric variability. This study proposes a geometry-aware multi-angular observation extraction workflow to quantify observation-geometry effects from a BRDF perspective. Specifically, camera intrinsics and extrinsics are refined via structure-from-motion (SFM), and homogeneous regions annotated on an orthomosaic are reprojected onto multiple raw sub-images acquired from different viewpoints. This enables joint extraction of multi-band reflectance and observation geometry parameters for the same ground targets under varying viewing directions. The extracted observations are further analyzed using band-wise polar visualization in the (VZA, RAA) domain. Results on a grassland target show clear reflectance anisotropy across ten bands, with red-edge and nearinfrared bands exhibiting 119-137% variability between maximum and minimum reflectance, indicating non-negligible observation-geometry effects on radiometric consistency.

</details>


### [24] [Benchmarking stereo reconstruction for 3D printable Martian terrain models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10364)
*Josephine Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RAFT-Stereo improves standard stereo accuracy over SGBM on a benchmark (Middlebury) but shows domain gap when reconstructing Martian terrain from Curiosity imagery, with edge alignment and reprojection errors. Geometry completion methods trade local fidelity vs global coherence: alpha shapes yield accurate but fragmented meshes, Poisson yields coherent meshes with extra surfaces, diffusion-fill sits in-between but stereo quality sensitivity. Overall, standard methods can yield printable approximations, but domain-specific validation is essential for reliable Martian terrain reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable printable 3D reconstructions of Martian terrain by reconstructing depth from rover imagery, evaluating stereo depth methods and geometry completion strategies, and assessing domain transfer from Earth benchmarks to Mars data.

Method: Pipeline: estimate stereo depth with RAFT-Stereo from NASA Curiosity images, complete geometry, and export watertight OBJ meshes. Comparative evaluation on Earth benchmarks (Middlebury) against SGBM. Investigate geometry completion strategies (alpha shapes, Poisson reconstruction, diffusion-fill) and assess on Curiosity imagery.

Result: Middlebury: RAFT-Stereo reduces disparity MAE from 3.22 px (SGBM) to 0.73 px and increases valid prediction coverage from 76.3% to 100.0%. Curiosity imagery: RAFT yields denser disparities but weaker edge alignment and higher photometric reprojection error, indicating benchmark accuracy does not directly transfer to Martian terrain. Geometry completion shows a tradeoff: alpha shapes preserve accuracy but fragment; Poisson reconstruction produces more coherent meshes but introduces unsupported surfaces; diffusion-fill baseline is intermediate but sensitive to stereo quality. Overall, standard stereo and completion methods can produce printable approximations of Martian terrain, yet reliable reconstruction requires stronger domain-specific validation.

Conclusion: Standard stereo and geometry completion methods can yield printable approximations of Martian terrain, but their performance does not fully transfer from Earth benchmarks to Mars. Domain-specific validation and possibly method adaptations are essential for reliable, printable Mars terrain reconstructions.

Abstract: Reconstructing printable 3D models from Mars rover imagery is challenging because Martian terrain is low-texture, irregular, and partially observed. We evaluate a pipeline that estimates stereo depth from NASA Curiosity images, completes geometry, and exports watertight OBJ meshes. On Middlebury, RAFT-Stereo outperforms semi-global block matching (SGBM), reducing disparity MAE from 3.22px to 0.73px and increasing valid prediction coverage from 76.3% to 100.0%. On Curiosity imagery, however, RAFT's denser disparities show weaker edge alignment and higher photometric reprojection error, suggesting that benchmark accuracy does not directly transfer to Martian terrain reconstruction. Geometry completion demonstrates a tradeoff between local fidelity and global connectivity. We find that alpha shapes preserve accurate but fragmented structure, Poisson reconstruction produces more coherent meshes but adds unsupported surfaces, and a deterministic diffusion-fill baseline is intermediate but sensitive to stereo quality. Overall, standard stereo and completion methods can produce printable approximations of Martian terrain, but reliable reconstruction requires stronger domain-specific validation.

</details>


### [25] [ClinReadNet: A clinical reading-inspired network for low-dose abdominal CT image quality assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10372)
*Xianye Xiao,Yulong Zou,Yujie Luo,Taihui Yu,Cun-Jing Zheng,Yuan-ming Geng,Shuihua Wang,Yudong Zhang,Jin Hong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ClinReadNet is a deep learning framework for no-reference image quality assessment of low-dose abdominal CT, aligning with radiologists' reading logic via (i) Sobel Ordinal Quality Network (SOQN) for edge/local detail and global quality distribution, (ii) shifted window multi-scale temperature multi-head self-attention ((S)W-MTMSA) to mimic global-to-local focus on regions of interest, and (iii) hierarchical ranked probability score (HRPS) loss combining coarse and fine grading with distance-aware ranking. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on LDCTIQAG2023 (PLCC 0.9507, SROCC 0.9554, KROCC 0.8629; Score 2.7690).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge automated IQA with radiologists’ subjective reading by modeling clinical reading behavior and attention patterns in CT imaging, enabling reliable No-reference IQA at low dose.

Method: ClinReadNet comprises: (1) Sobel Ordinal Quality Network (SOQN) for emphasizing edge details and overall quality distribution; (2) (shifted) window multi-scale temperature multi-head self-attention ((S)W-MTMSA) to emulate scanning from global to local focus and lock in ROIs via multi-sharpness attention; (3) hierarchical ranked probability score (HRPS) loss combining coarse and fine classification with distance-aware label ranking.

Result: On LDCTIQAG2023, PLCC=0.9507, SROCC=0.9554, KROCC=0.8629; Score=2.7690, surpassing prior methods and achieving SOTA.
