Abstract: Although pretrained text-to-image (T2I) generation models can produce high-quality images, they often fail to faithfully reflect the semantic intent of complex prompts due to stochastic noise and inherent model limitations. This issue frequently manifests as the model overlooking specific objects or failing to correctly bind attributes to their corresponding entities, a challenge referred to as semantic alignment. Unlike existing approaches that rely on computationally expensive fine-tuning or labor-intensive layout priors, we propose STEDiff, a training-free method designed to enhance semantic representations directly within the text-embedding space. Specifically, we introduce a method that primarily leverages the [EOT] token to strengthen the relevant semantics of sub-sentences and then replaces the corresponding tokens in the original prompt. Furthermore, a novel semantic enhancement loss is incorporated to enforce spatial constraints, ensuring that the semantics of each entity are precisely mapped to their respective image regions. Extensive quantitative and qualitative evaluations on the T2I-CompBench demonstrate that our method notably improves semantic consistency and generation integrity in complex scenarios.

</details>


### [52] [Envision4D: Envisioning Visual Futures via Feed-forward 4D Gaussian Splatting for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10656)
*Qi Song,Yifei He,Chi Zhang,Zheng Fu,Xuhe Zhao,Mengmeng Yang,Kun Jiang,Rui Huang,Diange Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Envision4D is a self-supervised, pose-free framework for future extrapolation in dynamic scenes, using iterative denoising for future pose prediction, in-layer temporal attention, conditioned motion lifting, and progressive training to achieve state-of-the-art future view synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome ghosting and limitations of interpolation-based methods when extrapolating to the future under large displacements, by removing reliance on hand-crafted priors and enabling robust, unsupervised learning.

Method: Future Pose Prediction via iterative denoising to estimate future camera parameters; In-layer Temporal Attention to capture non-linear dynamics; Conditioned Motion Lifting to transform uncertain extrapolation into robust relational mappings; Progressive Training Strategy to stabilize unsupervised motion learning against error accumulation.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, with significant improvements over existing methods in future view synthesis.

Conclusion: Proposes a robust, self-supervised framework for pose-free future extrapolation in dynamic scenes, addressing ghosting artifacts and instability while achieving strong performance on future view synthesis.

Abstract: Forecasting the future evolution of dynamic scenes is crucial in autonomous driving. However, existing feed-forward paradigms are primarily designed for interpolation. When extended to future extrapolation, they suffer from ghosting artifacts under large displacements and are constrained by simplified motion assumptions or strict future priors. To overcome these challenges, we propose Envision4D, a fully self-supervised feed-forward framework for pose-free future extrapolation. Specifically, we introduce a Future Pose Prediction module that infers future camera parameters via an iterative denoising process. Furthermore, to capture non-linear dynamics, we propose In-layer Temporal Attention and employ Conditioned Motion Lifting, which transforms the highly uncertain extrapolation process into robust relational mappings. Finally, a Progressive Training Strategy is utilized to stabilize unsupervised motion learning against error accumulation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Envision4D achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming existing methods in future view synthesis.

</details>


### [53] [Analyzing Training-Free Corruption Detection for Object Detection Datasets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10666)
*Christian Sieberichs,Simon Geerkens,Thomas Waschulzik,Viswanathan Ramesh,Alexander Braun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Adapts a training-free feature-space anomaly detection method to object detection; semantic mislabels are effectively exposed, while localization/positional errors are harder to detect; evaluated on VOC2012 and KITTI using various embeddings and synthetic/real-world corruptions; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Annotation errors degrade object detection performance and are costly to identify; training-free, interpretable methods in feature space offer a fast analysis route, but their behavior on object detection (with semantic and spatial information) is not well understood.

Method: Adapt an existing feature-space detection approach to object detection, and evaluate across multiple pretrained embedding models. Introduce synthetic noise types (symmetric, asymmetric, and positional) and test on real-world annotation errors using VOC2012 and KITTI. Report performance and insights; provide code and data via a public repository.

Result: The adapted method reliably exposes semantic mislabels in object detection annotations across embeddings; positional/spatial errors are challenging to detect. Performance varies with embedding model and dataset; demonstrates the viability of feature-space approaches for semantic error detection in detection datasets, but limitations persist for localization errors.

Conclusion: Feature-space annotation error detection is effective for semantic errors in object detection but insufficient for robustly detecting positional errors; future work should integrate spatial cues or hybrid methods. The work contributes empirical guidance and a publicly available codebase for bounding-box corruption detection.

Abstract: Annotation errors are widespread in computer vision datasets and can significantly degrade the performance of systems trained on them, particularly in complex tasks such as object detection. Several approaches exist to identify annotation errors, including training-free feature-space methods which provide a fast and interpretable way to analyze annotations. However, the behavior on object detection annotations, which include semantic and spatial information, remains largely unexplored.
  In this work we analyze the applicability of feature-space-based approaches for detecting annotation errors in object detection datasets. By adapting an existing feature-space method, we show that such approaches reliably expose semantic mislabel, while positional errors remain difficult to detect. We evaluate this behavior across multiple pretrained embedding models, synthetic noise types (symmetric, asymmetric, and positional), and real-world annotation errors using VOC2012 and KITTI.
  All code and real-world corruptions are publicly available at the following repository: https://github.com/ ChristianSieberichs/BoundingBox\_corruption\_detection

</details>


### [54] [FadeMem: Distance-Aware Memory Consolidation for Autoregressive Video Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10671)
*Yu Lu,Junjie Yang,Piotr Koniusz,YuXin Song,Yi Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FadeMem proposes a distance-aware KV memory consolidation for autoregressive video generation, creating a temporal hierarchy within a fixed cache to keep dense near and sparse far memory. This yields improved long-horizon consistency without architectural changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing bounded-cache strategies trade off memory cost against retentive power, often fixing roles for parts of history or failing to reflect temporal decay of information. A memory allocator that favors short-term detail while anchoring long-range structure can improve subject consistency and scene coherence in long videos.

Method: Historical KV blocks are organized into a temporal hierarchy under a fixed cache budget. New history is inserted as fine-grained entries; older neighboring entries are progressively merged following a power-law temporal allocation schedule, producing dense-near and sparse-far memory. This consolidation is distance-aware and does not require architectural changes.

Result: Experiments show improved subject consistency, background stability, and temporal coherence compared with existing bounded-cache strategies.

Conclusion: FadeMem enables long-horizon video generation by adaptively consolidating memory to preserve short-term dynamics and long-term identity under fixed memory budgets, improving coherence without changing model architecture.

Abstract: Autoregressive video generators synthesize long videos by generating successive temporal segments, but their historical KV cache grows with video length. Existing bounded-cache methods reduce this cost with local windows, sink tokens, or compressed memory states, yet they usually assign fixed roles to different parts of the history. We propose FadeMem, a distance-aware KV memory consolidation mechanism that organizes historical KV blocks into a temporal hierarchy under a fixed cache budget. This design is motivated by frequency-dependent temporal decay: fine details decorrelate quickly, while coarse scene structure and identity remain useful over longer horizons. During generation, new history is inserted as fine-grained entries, while older adjacent entries are progressively merged under a power-law temporal allocation schedule, yielding a dense-near, sparse-far memory within one cache. Without architectural changes, FadeMem preserves recent context for short-term dynamics and compact long-range anchors for identity and scene coherence. Experiments show improved subject consistency, background stability, and temporal coherence over existing bounded-cache strategies.

</details>


### [55] [Don't waste SAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10696)
*Nermeen Abou Baker,Uwe Handmann*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Fine-tuning the Segment Anything Model (SAM) on three real-world waste segmentation datasets significantly improves performance; a SAM-ViT-H variant outperforms Zerowaste on TACO and closely approaches TrashCan 1.0, indicating that fine-tuning SAM as a foundational model enhances generalization for downstream waste segmentation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess SAM's generalization to waste segmentation and quantify gains from fine-tuning on challenging real-scene datasets with occlusions, deformable objects, transparency, and background confusion.

Method: Fine-tune SAM variants (notably SAM-ViT-H) on three waste datasets; compare zero-shot and fine-tuned performance against state-of-the-art methods (Zerowaste, TrashCan 1.0) using IoU as a primary metric; analyze dataset difficulties.

Result: Fine-tuned SAM-ViT-H achieves substantial gains, outperforming Zerowaste on TACO by a large IoU margin and coming within 1.44 IoU of TrashCan 1.0, indicating strong generalization after fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Fine-tuning SAM as a foundational model yields better generalization for downstream waste segmentation tasks; SAM should be leveraged rather than discarded in this domain.

Abstract: Meta AI has recently released the Segment Anything Model (SAM), which demonstrates exceptional zero-shot image segmentation performance across various tasks with remarkable accuracy. Despite its inability to provide accurate segmentation across multiple research fields, SAM still serves as a valuable starting point for supporting the segmentation pipeline process, particularly for tasks that require extensive and senior skills annotations. This study aims to evaluate the generalization of SAM and fine-tuning SAM models using three waste segmentation datasets. Although they are captured from real scenes as SAM was pretrained on, these datasets present several challenges, including occlusions, deformable objects, transparency, and objects easily confused with backgrounds. In our findings, the fine-tuned SAM-ViT-H model outperforms the state-ofthe-art Zerowaste, and TACO datasets with a significant increase of +30 in IoU, and it closely approaches performance levels of TrashCan 1.0, with only a -1.44 difference. After evaluating these popular waste datasets, it became evident that fine-tuning SAM as a foundational model is a crucial step for providing better generalization for downstream waste segmentation tasks. Therefore, SAM should not be disregarded or wasted.

</details>


### [56] [Using the YOLOv12 Model for Verifying the Correct Color Sequence of Wires in Network Cables (Patch Cords) on the Production Line](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10699)
*Amin Doroodchi,Danial Soleimany*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An attention-enhanced single-stage detector (YOLOv1 variant) applied to microscopic images to verify wire-pair color sequencing in patch cords; achieves ~98% wire-detection precision and ~95% mean accuracy with ~99% classification precision and ~98% recall on 2,500 images; claims real-time, autonomous production-line verification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the tedium and errors of manual visual inspection for wire color sequencing in network cables, reducing defects, costs, and human error in manufacturing.

Method: Use the twelfth version of YOLO1 with attention mechanisms in a single-stage architecture. Train on 2,500 microscopic images of network connectors (70% train, 15% val, 15% test) to identify wire positions and verify color order.

Result: High performance: ~98% precision in wire detection; overall mean accuracy ≈95%; classification precision ≈99%; recall ≈98%. Demonstrates potential for real-time, autonomous verification on production lines.

Conclusion: The proposed system can reliably verify wire color sequencing on production lines without human intervention, reducing human error and increasing manufacturing efficiency.

Abstract: In the production process of network cables, ensuring the correct color sequence of wire pairs inside the standard connector plays a critical role in the final performance of the cable, as any misplacement or color-ordering error can lead to defective products and impose significant costs. Traditional inspection methods based on visual examination through digital microscopes are typically time-consuming, tedious, and prone to human error. In this study, an intelligent system based on the twelfth version of the YOLO1 object detection model was developed to identify the position and verify the correct color sequence of wires in patch cords. The dataset used consisted of 2,500 images captured from microscopic views of network connectors, which were divided into 70% for training, 15% for validation, and 15% for testing. The proposed model, leveraging a single-stage architecture and attention mechanisms during learning, achieved highly accurate wire detection with approximately 98% precision. Additionally, the overall mean accuracy, classification precision, and recall were around 95%, 99%, and 98%, respectively. The results demonstrate that this system can reliably and in real time verify the correctness of wire color sequencing on the production line without the need for human intervention, thereby reducing human error and enhancing efficiency in the manufacturing process.

</details>


### [57] [Vector Map as Language: Toward Unified Remote Sensing Vector Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10701)
*Yinglong Yan,Yunkai Yang,Haoyi Wang,Wei Fu,Linshan Wu,Honghu Pan,Shaobo Xia,Shanghang Zhang,Hao Chen,Leyuan Fang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VecLang: a unified text-based representation for multiclass vector mapping in remote sensing that treats vector data as a GeoJSON-like language, enabling cross-category modeling and open-vocabulary generalization; uses progressive vision-language mapping and RL-based optimization; introduces VecMap-Bench with 54k images and 800k instances and shows strong generalization; code and data released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing vector mapping methods treat objects as polygons or graphs, which cannot uniformly capture heterogeneous, multiclass structures and topology. A flexible, unified representation is needed to model geometry, semantics, and topology across categories.

Method: Encode geospatial elements into a GeoJSON-like vector language (VecLang). Use a progressive vision-language mapping framework to first localize vectorization units and then generate structured map elements. Employ Hierarchical Vector Language Optimization with reinforcement learning to improve syntax validity, content fidelity, and map executability. Develop VecMap-Bench with large-scale multiclass datasets to train and evaluate cross-domain/generalization performance.

Result: VecLang enables single-class and multiclass vector mapping within a shared textual format and demonstrates strong cross-dataset and open-vocabulary generalization. The provided VecMap-Bench contains 54k images and 800k instances, supporting standard and generalization evaluations.

Conclusion: A unified textual framework for vector mapping is viable and generalizes across datasets and vocabularies, addressing limitations of polygon- and graph-based representations. The accompanying dataset and code enable broader adoption and benchmarking.

Abstract: Remote sensing vector mapping aims to generate structured maps of geospatial entities, such as buildings, roads, and water bodies, from remote sensing imagery. In practice, vector maps usually contain multiple category layers and heterogeneous entity structures, requiring a unified model for diverse mapping needs. However, existing methods typically represent vector objects as polygons or graphs, making them suitable only for specific categories: polygons poorly capture topological relations, while graphs often blur instance boundaries. We observe that language, as a natural medium for human communication, offers a flexible and expressive representation that can accommodate heterogeneous map elements, including geometry, semantics, and topolog. Motivated by this insight, we propose Vector Map as Language (VecLang), a unified paradigm that reformulates multiclass vector mapping as structured text generation. VecLang encodes the common elements of different geospatial entities into a GeoJSON-like vector language, enabling cross-category modeling within a shared textual format. To generate this language reliably, we design a progressive vision-language mapping framework that first localizes vectorization units and then generates structured map elements. We further introduce Hierarchical Vector Language Optimization, which uses reinforcement learning to improve syntax validity, content fidelity, and map executability. We also build VecMap-Bench with 54K images and 800K instances, supporting training and evaluation across standard and generalization settings. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VecLang handles both single-class and multiclass vector mapping while achieving strong cross-dataset and open-vocabulary generalization. The model and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/yyyyll0ss/VecLang.

</details>


### [58] [Patient-Level Diagnosis of Acute Myeloid Leukemia via Deep Learning Analysis of Bone Marrow Smear](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10735)
*Yuqi Ma,Tianyi Wang,Weihua Meng,Hongru Chen,Fajin Tao,Qunxian Lu,Lin An,Xiaodong Mo,Gen Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A cell-to-patient deep learning pipeline for AML diagnosis from bone marrow smears using CBLC as target, leveraging YOLO for cell segmentation and EfficientNet-B0 classification; achieves high external F1 across six centers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual, single-cell interpretation is labor-intensive and patient-level diagnosis requires aggregating many observations; need a scalable, cross-center diagnostic aid.

Method: Fixed YOLO-based segmentation; CBLC concept comprising N/N1/M/M1/R/R1/J/J1; contour IoU matching to expert annotations; standardized cell crops; EfficientNet-B0 classifier trained in two-stage GT-to-YOLO and YOLO-to-YOLO with class-imbalance correction, center-border regularization, and morphology-assisted supervision; aggregation of cell-level predictions into patient-level CBLC ratios; ensemble weighting.

Result: Stable internal validation; good external generalization with ensemble weighted F1-scores of 0.9124 (Center6), 0.9076 (Center4), 0.8696 (Center5); 258 patients across six anonymized centers; external validation cohort of 89 patients.

Conclusion: CBLC-based cell-to-patient DL pipeline enables AML-oriented diagnostic support with robust cross-center performance and reduced manual workload; potential for deployment in clinical hematology labs.

Abstract: Bone marrow smear review remains important for acute myeloid leukemia (AML) assessment, but manual single-cell interpretation is labor-intensive and patient-level diagnosis requires aggregation of many cellular observations. We present a cell-to-patient deep learning pipeline for AML-assisted diagnosis from bone marrow smear images. The study included 258 patients from six anonymized centers, including a main cohort of 169 patients from Centers 1-3 and an external validation cohort of 89 patients from Centers 4-6. A 16-category cell annotation vocabulary was used to describe the global cellular composition, including granulocytic, monocytic, erythroid, lymphoid, eosinophilic, and other cells. Rather than identifying strict AML blasts or leukemic blasts, the model targets an expert-defined composite category termed Composite Blast-like Cells (CBLC), comprising N, N1, M, M1, R, R1, J, and J1 according to the project-wide morphological standard. A fixed YOLO-based segmentation module detected cells, predicted contours were matched to expert polygon annotations by contour IoU, and standardized single-cell crops were generated. An EfficientNet-B0 classifier was trained through a two-stage GT-to-YOLO and YOLO-to-YOLO strategy with class-imbalance correction, center-border regularization, and morphology-assisted supervision. Cell-level predictions were aggregated into patient-level CBLC ratios for AML-oriented diagnostic support. The pipeline achieved stable internal validation and maintained external generalization, with ensemble weighted F1-scores of 0.9076, 0.8696, and 0.9124 on Centers 4, 5, and 6, respectively.

</details>


### [59] [DD-INR: Dynamics-Driven Implicit Neural Representation for Accelerated Whole-Brain Functional MRI Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10756)
*Qiaoxin Li,Caini Pan,Pierre-Antoine Comby,Chaithya Giliyar,Philippe Ciuciu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DD-INR introduces a dynamics-driven implicit neural representation for accelerated fMRI, separating static background from dynamic activation signals to improve reconstruction under high undersampling and enhance detection of activation patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accelerated fMRI suffers from high k-space undersampling where small task-evoked BOLD signals are hard to recover with conventional anatomical reconstructions; there is a need for models that preserve temporal fidelity and activation-relevant dynamics.

Method: Split the data into static background and a dynamic component; model dynamics with an implicit neural representation (INR) using a spatiotemporal prior tailored for fMRI; leverage incoherent time-varying sampling; compare against traditional reconstruction methods in simulation and in-vivo; public source code provided.

Result: DD-INR outperforms traditional methods in image quality and retrieval of activation patterns; demonstrates potential to increase sensitivity and robustness of fMRI under practical scan times; validated in both simulations and in-vivo data.

Conclusion: DD-INR provides a promising, compact framework for accelerated fMRI reconstruction focusing on activation-relevant changes, with potential to improve detection power in fMRI studies; code available at the referenced GitHub repository.

Abstract: Accelerated acquisition of fMRI enables enhanced detection of neurovascular (BOLD) activity in the brain, but image reconstruction becomes challenging with high k-space undersampling: Task-evoked BOLD signals are small in magnitude, which traditional anatomical MRI reconstruction methods fail to recover, as they favor spatial accuracy over temporal fidelity. We present DD-INR, a Dynamics-Driven Implicit Neural Representation framework tailored for accelerated fMRI that benefits from incoherent time-varying sampling and a tailored spatiotemporal prior, outperforming traditional methods, demonstrated in simulation and in-vivo acquisition, both in terms of image quality and retrieval of activation patterns. DD-INR achieves this by splitting the fMRI data into a static background and a temporally varying dynamic component, representing only the dynamics with a dedicated INR, thereby focusing the model's capacity on activation-relevant changes while remaining compact. In general, DD-INR provides a promising framework for accelerated fMRI reconstruction, with the potential to improve the sensitivity and robustness of fMRI studies within practical scan time limits. The source code is available at https://github.com/JoosenLi/DD-INR.

</details>


### [60] [ZODS-RS -- Zero-training Oriented Detection & Segmentation for Remote Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10769)
*Zuan Gu,Tianhan Gao,Langxu Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free, unified detection+segmentation for aerial imagery (ZODS-RS) leveraging DINOv3 features and SAM-like proposals; delivers horizontal boxes and instance masks via closed-form modules PP, R-SEM, and UAM; shows cross-domain generalization and small-object gains across FAIR1M, xView, and a UAV dataset, with simple deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for models that generalize across platforms/viewpoints without task-specific training, particularly for oriented geometry, scale/rotation variation, and crowded scenes in remote sensing, while unifying detection and segmentation.

Method: Pipeline combines: PP (prototype purification via Tyler covariance), R-SEM (rotation-scale equivariant matching with separable kernels and global Hungarian assignment), UAM (uncertainty-aware pixelwise merging with adaptive priors and optional negative prototypes). A CWLA fuse step aggregates multiple DINOv3 layers; uses DINOv3 dense features and SAM-like proposals to output horizontal boxes and instance masks.

Result: Quantitatively, on FAIR1M (HBB): mAP0.50:0.95 = 13.06; AP_S = 2.93 (class-averaged over ships/airplanes). On xView (HBB): mAP = 16.69. On a UAV dataset: mask mIoU = 31.10; small-object AP improves by 30.70 over Grounded-SAM. Claims consistent gains for small/crowded targets and cross-domain shifts with simple deployment.

Conclusion: ZODS-RS offers a unified, no-training solution for horizontal-box detection plus instance segmentation in aerial imagery, with explicit closed-form formulations tied to DINOv3 and demonstrated cross-domain robustness and improvements on small targets, while maintaining deployment simplicity.

Abstract: Remote-sensing and UAV applications need models that generalize across platforms and viewpoints without task-specific training. Yet training-free pipelines often falter on oriented geometry, scale/rotation variation, and crowded ports or airfields, and rarely unify detection and segmentation. We introduce ZODS-RS, a training-free, closed-form pipeline that outputs horizontal boxes (HBB) and instance masks. Built on DINOv3 dense features and SAM-style proposals, ZODS-RS chains: PP (prototype purification via Tyler covariance), R-SEM (rotation-scale equivariant matching with separable kernels and global Hungarian assignment), and UAM (uncertainty-aware pixelwise merging with adaptive priors and optional negative prototypes). A lightweight CWLA fuses multiple DINOv3 layers. On FAIR1M (HBB) we obtain $\mathrm{mAP}_{0.50:0.95}=\mathbf{13.06}$ and $\mathrm{AP}_S=\mathbf{2.93}$ \emph{(class-averaged over ship/airplane)}; on xView (HBB) we report $\mathrm{mAP}=\mathbf{16.69}$. On our UAV dataset, ZODS-RS achieves mask $\mathrm{mIoU}=\mathbf{31.10}$ and improves small-object AP by $\mathbf{+30.70}$ over Grounded-SAM on a single 5090. This work offers a unified, \emph{no-training} solution for horizontal-box detection plus instance segmentation in aerial imagery; provides explicit closed-form formulations for PP/R-SEM/UAM tightly coupled with DINOv3; and demonstrates \emph{consistent} gains on small and crowded targets and under cross-domain shifts while keeping deployment simple.

</details>


### [61] [SAFE-Pruner: Semantic Attention-Guided Future-Aware Token Pruning for Efficient Vision-Language-Action Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29662)
*Shilin Ma,Chubin Zhang,Changyuan Wang,Yuji Wang,Yue Wu,Zixuan Wang,Jingqi Tian,Zheng Zhu,Yansong Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAFE-Pruner is a plug-and-play token pruning framework for real-time VLA models that forecasts deep-layer token saliency using future-layer attention cues and adaptive subtask division, achieving up to 1.89x speedup with minimal accuracy loss and outperforming state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accelerate vision-language-action model inference for robotic control without sacrificing critical visual information; existing pruning relies on shallow-layer cues, risking loss of important tokens.

Method: Introduce semantic attention consistency and a forward-looking forecasting strategy to predict token saliency in deep layers; integrate an adaptive subtask division to detect abrupt attention shifts; plug-and-play pruning framework.

Result: Demonstrates up to 1.89x speedup with degradation in success rate <1.7%; outperforms state-of-the-art by up to 1.9%; validated in simulation and real-world settings.

Conclusion: Future-attention-guided pruning can provide stable and reliable acceleration for VLA models in robotics, balancing speed and accuracy.

Abstract: Real-time inference of vision-language-action (VLA) models is essential for robotic control. While visual token pruning has shown strong potential for accelerating inference, most existing methods mainly base pruning decisions on shallow-layer cues and risk discarding visual information required by deep layers. To address this issue, we propose SAFE-Pruner, a plug-and-play pruning framework that incorporates attention cues of future layers into pruning decisions. Specifically, we identify semantic attention consistency, the tendency that VLA models concentrate their attention probability mass on the same semantic entity across execution steps. Based on this observation, we design a forward-looking strategy to forecast the token saliency in deep layers, which prevents the premature removal of critical tokens and leads to more stable acceleration. We further introduce an adaptive subtask division strategy to detect abrupt attention shifts, thereby improving forecasting accuracy and pruning reliability. Extensive experiments in simulation and real-world settings demonstrate that our method achieves up to 1.89x speedup with a minimal degradation in success rate of less than 1.7%, while outperforming state-of-the-art methods by up to 1.9%.

</details>


### [62] [Spatially Selective Self-Training for Unsupervised Building Change Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10775)
*Wafaa I. M. Hussin,Zhi Lu,Anas M. I. Mohammed,Xiang Zhou,Ratiba A. H. Abubaker,Zhenming Peng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SST-CD learns a task-specific, end-to-end detector for unsupervised building change detection by filtering noisy pseudo labels with a spatial reliability criterion, a lightweight feature adapter, and a prototype-based decoder, achieving state-of-the-art results on three benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing label-free approaches rely on generic temporal discrepancies that are often noisy and misaligned with building-level changes, leading to suboptimal detectors.

Method: Use temporal differences as candidate pseudo labels, estimate spatial reliability via a local consistency criterion to filter supervision, apply a lightweight feature adapter to recalibrate bi-temporal features, and employ a prototype-based decoder to generate compact change/no-change representations, enabling end-to-end detector learning under noisy supervision.

Result: On LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, and DSIFN-CD SST-CD achieves F1 scores of 83.08%, 91.69%, and 86.60% respectively, outperforming existing unsupervised and label-free baselines; code will be released.

Conclusion: SST-CD demonstrates that selective self-training with feature adaptation and prototype-based decoding can robustly learn building-change detectors from unlabeled data, mitigating the noise in pseudo supervision and improving detection accuracy.

Abstract: Unsupervised building change detection aims to learn building-change masks from unlabeled bi-temporal remote sensing images. Existing label-free methods often follow a discrepancy-to-mask paradigm, directly using temporal differences, frozen foundation-model responses, prompt-based outputs, or post-processing results as final change maps. Although these strategies provide annotation-free cues, they do not learn a task-specific building-change detector and remain vulnerable to the gap between generic temporal discrepancies and building-defined structural changes. In practice, such discrepancies are often noisy and task-irrelevant, as appearance shifts, registration errors, and non-building modifications can produce strong but misleading responses. To address this problem, we propose SST-CD, a spatially selective self-training framework that reformulates fully label-free building change detection as end-to-end detector learning under noisy pseudo supervision.
  SST-CD uses temporal discrepancies as candidate pseudo labels and trains the detector only on spatially reliable pixels, whose reliability is estimated by a local consistency criterion that filters inconsistent regions from supervision. To further stabilize noisy self-training, a lightweight feature adapter recalibrates bi-temporal features, while a prototype-based decoder produces compact change and no-change representations. Experiments on LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, and DSIFN-CD show that SST-CD achieves F1 scores of 83.08\%, 91.69\%, and 86.60\%, respectively, outperforming existing unsupervised and label-free baselines. Code will be made publicly available.

</details>


### [63] [From Patches to Patients: A study of the tile-to-slide performance transferability in Digital Pathology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10778)
*Sofiène Boutaj,Leo Fillioux,Maria Vakalopoulou,Stergios Christodoulidis,Pierre Marza*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Tile-level probing can efficiently proxy slide-level performance for selecting foundation models in WSI analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce the computational cost and steps involved in evaluating foundation models for whole-slide image (WSI) tasks by avoiding full slide-level pipelines for every candidate encoder.

Method: Benchmark 19 state-of-the-art foundation models on 42 slide-level and 16 tile-level tasks, compare tile probing metrics against slide-level outcomes using ABMIL and Mean Pooling aggregations, perform sensitivity analyses on transferability, cohort size, and number of tiles per slide, and assess agreement in best-performing models between tile and slide-level tasks.

Result: There is a high correlation between tile-level and slide-level performance; encoder representation quality is the primary determinant of WSI success. Transferability is stable across models and more affected by cohort sizes and tile counts than task difficulty. Tile benchmarks reliably shortlist strong candidate models for final slide-level evaluation.

Conclusion: Tile-level benchmarking provides an efficient and practical first step to narrow down candidate foundation models for WSI analysis, while slide-level evaluation remains essential for final clinical validation.

Abstract: Foundation Models (FMs) have recently redefined the state-of-the-art in histopathology by providing robust representations for whole-slide image (WSI) analysis. However, selecting the optimal foundation model (FM) for a specific clinical cohort currently requires multiple preprocessing steps, followed by computationally expensive feature extraction and the training of a Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) aggregator for every model. In this work, we investigate whether efficient tile-level linear probing can serve as a reliable proxy for slide-level performance, reducing the need to run full slide-level pipelines for every candidate encoder. We benchmark 19 state-of-the-art FMs on 42 slide-level and 16 tile-level tasks, comparing tile probing metrics against slide-level outcomes using ABMIL and Mean Pooling aggregations. We observe a high correlation between tile and slide performance across varying task difficulties, indicating that encoder representation quality is the primary determinant of WSI success. Sensitivity analyses show that transferability is stable across models and is more influenced by cohort sizes and numbers of tiles per slide than by average task difficulty. We also measure the agreement in best performing models between tile and slide-level tasks, showing tile benchmarks reliably shortlist strong candidates. Overall, our study indicates that tile-level benchmarking provides an efficient and practical first step for narrowing down candidate models, while slide-level evaluation remains essential for final validation on clinical tasks.

</details>


### [64] [A Multimodal RGB and Events Dataset for Hand Detection in First-Person View](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10790)
*Bharghav Kota,Yulia Sandamirskaya*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Synthetic, multi-modal event+RGB dataset for egocentric hand detection generated from RGB Egohands via v2e; uses YOLOv8 ground truth and evaluates a multi-modal detector achieving results on par with state-of-the-art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the scarcity of training data for event-based hand detection and leverage advantages of event cameras (HDR, high temporal resolution) for moving robotic systems.

Method: Create synthetic event-based data by transforming RGB Egohands with v2e; vary lighting and scale; obtain ground truth detections by fine-tuning YOLOv8 on RGB and projecting onto events; evaluate multi-modal detectors using both event and RGB data.

Result: Demonstrates performance comparable to state-of-the-art multi-modal methods on the synthetic dataset.

Conclusion: The synthetic egocentric event-RGB dataset enables training and evaluation for event-based hand detection, mitigating data bottlenecks and validating the utility of sensor fusion for improved robustness.

Abstract: Existing hand detection algorithms work on images and the detection rate is restricted by the frame rate of the camera. In hand detection applications for moving robotic systems, conventional cameras cause motion blur, especially in darker lighting conditions. We can leverage the use of event-based cameras which possess a high dynamic range, high temporal resolution, and low power consumption. Recent work has shown that using a stereo setup of an event-based and a frame-based camera improves detection accuracy and the bandwidth-latency tradeoff. The main bottleneck in using event-based cameras in object detection and recognition tasks is a relatively low amount of training data. In this work, we propose a methodology and an exemplary synthetic event-based hand dataset from an egocentric, first-person view perspective. The data is synthesized from the existing RGB Egohands dataset with the v2e toolbox. Parameters of the v2e toolbox are varied to provide versions of the dataset with different lighting conditions and scales. Ground truth detections are generated with a fine-tuned YOLOv8 model which is applied to the RGB images in the Egohands dataset and interpolated on the high-temporal resolution events. We use the multi-modal dataset to perform hand detection with existing object detection algorithms which use a multi-modal setup of event and RGB cameras and demonstrate performance comparable to the state-of-the-art.

</details>


### [65] [SCAIL-2: Unifying Controlled Character Animation with End-to-end In-Context Conditioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10804)
*Wenhao Yan,Fengjia Guo,Zhuoyi Yang,Jie Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SCAIL-2 enables end-to-end character animation by bypassing intermediate representations, directly using driving videos. It introduces MotionPair-60K for end-to-end motion transfer, leverages in-context mask conditioning and mode-specific RoPE, and Bias-Aware DPO to reduce synthesis bias, achieving state-of-the-art results across diverse tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Over-reliance on intermediate representations (e.g., pose skeletons, masked backgrounds) in prior character-animation methods causes information loss and limits end-to-end learning. An end-to-end approach could leverage full visual information from driving videos.

Method: End-to-end framework that concatenates driving videos with the target sequence; unifies sub-tasks with decoupled conditions; builds MotionPair-60K dataset of heterogeneous tasks; uses in-context mask conditioning and mode-specific RoPE for soft guidance; introduces Bias-Aware DPO to mitigate synthetic-region discrepancies.

Result: Extensive experiments show SCAIL-2 substantially outperforms state-of-the-art methods across various character-animation tasks; a MotionPair-60K dataset is introduced and data/model weights are slated for release on the project page.

Conclusion: SCAIL-2 demonstrates that end-to-end character animation is feasible and superior when comprehensive visual information is preserved and synthetic biases are mitigated; the MotionPair-60K dataset enables broader, end-to-end motion-transfer research.

Abstract: Controlled character animation requires transferring motion from a driving sequence to a reference character. Prior works heavily rely on intermediate representations, including pose skeletons to represent motion or masked background to represent environment, which inevitably leads to information loss. To address this, we present SCAIL-2, an framework that bypasses those intermediates and achieves \textbf{end-to-end} character animation. By directly concatenating driving videos to the sequence, the model can obtain all the required visual information from the input video. To address lack of end-to-end data, we unify sub-tasks of character animation with decoupled conditions and then curate a pipeline to synthesize MotionPair-60K, an end-to-end motion transfer dataset containing heterogeneous tasks of character animation. To archive the unification, we utilize in-context mask conditioning and mode-specific RoPE as soft guidance beyond textual instructions and raw visual information. To address synthetic discrepancy in detailed regions, we propose Bias-Aware DPO to construct preference items to mitigate the errors. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method substantially outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches in various character animation tasks. A large subset of synthetic data as well as model weights will be released at our project page: https://teal024.github.io/SCAIL-2/.

</details>


### [66] [Deep learning for echo sounder data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10811)
*Ketil Malde*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Domain-specific deep learning for underwater acoustics is needed due to data intrinsic properties; progress limited by lack of standardized data formats and high-quality benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ML has transformed data processing broadly, but underwater acoustics pose unique challenges; applying image-based DL directly yields modest results, necessitating exploration of methods tailored to acoustic data and better data standards.

Method: Conceptual, position-paper style argument advocating for domain-specific DL research, data standardization, and publicly available benchmark datasets; no empirical methodology is presented.

Result: No empirical results reported; the paper argues for identifying gaps and proposing directions rather than presenting experiments.

Conclusion: Advancement in ML for underwater acoustics requires standardized data formats and accessible, high-quality datasets with clear performance goals; this will enable meaningful method development.

Abstract: There is no doubt that over the last decade, techniques from the field of machine learning have revolutionized how we process and interpret data, especially images and text. For underwater observations acoustics is a primary source of information, and naturally, deep learning methods have been applied to echograms and other acoustics data, but so far with rather modest results. Here, we argue that due to intrinsic properties of acoustic data, substantial advances will likely require research into deep learning methods beyond mere recycling of models and techniques from image processing. Currently, the potential for breakthroughs in method development is hindered by the lack of standard data formats and organization, and even more by the lack of readily available, high quality data sets with established performance goals. To advance the field, these shortcomings should be remedied

</details>


### [67] [Earth-OneVision: Extending Remote Sensing Multimodal Large Language Models to More Sensor Modalities and Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10819)
*Miaoxin Cai,Guanqun Wang,Wei Zhang,Guangyao Zhou,Yin Zhuang,Tong Zhang,Hao Wang,He Chen,Jun Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Earth-OneVision is a compact 2B RS-MLLM that unifies six remote-sensing sensor modalities and nine task categories in a single autoregressive model, addressing cross-modal fusion with dedicated mechanisms and large-scale QA data, achieving strong performance rivaling larger models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the fragmented landscape of RS multimodal models by unifying diverse sensors (optical, SAR, infrared, multispectral, temporal, video) and tasks within one framework, enabling comprehensive geoscientific reasoning with cross-sensor knowledge.

Method: Propose Earth-OneVision with three novel components: (1) Full-Granularity Vision-Language Alignment (FGVLA) aligning multi-level visual features to a multi-dimensional language space; (2) Spatial-Linguistic Isomorphic Serialization (SLIS) converting heterogeneous spatial outputs into autoregressive tokens; (3) Progressive Cross-Modality Adaptation (PCMA) breaking the domain gap into stages. Train on MMRS-OneVision (~34M QA pairs across six modalities and nine tasks) in an autoregressive RS-MLLM setting, using 2B parameters. Benchmark across 9 task categories.

Result: Achieves competitive or state-of-the-art results with far fewer parameters than many RS-MLLMs: 87.52% P@0.5 on OPT-RSVG; 80.68% on SARLANG-Bench; 75.74% recall on BigEarthNet-MS; 81.94% MCQ accuracy on EarthMind-Bench. Outperforms 7B models by over 7% on SAR tasks; matches or exceeds 4B–72B RS-MLLMs across tasks.

Conclusion: A carefully designed triad of alignment, serialization, and adaptation, combined with large-scale cross-modal QA data, enables a compact 2B RS-MLLM to rival much larger models across optical, SAR, multispectral, and temporal modalities and multiple task families, highlighting the potential for efficient, multi-sensor, cross-modality LLMs in remote sensing.

Abstract: RS-MLLMs enable natural-language understanding and spatial reasoning over earth observation imagery. However, existing models support only a narrow range of sensor types and tasks, yielding a fragmented view of the earth and leaving cross-modal geoscientific knowledge largely unexploited. This work presents Earth-OneVision, a 2B RS-MLLM that unifies six sensor modalities (i.e., optical, SAR, infrared, multispectral, temporal, and video) and cross-sensor fusion across 9 task categories within a single autoregressive framework. Three dedicated mechanisms address three bottlenecks. Full-Granularity Vision-Language Alignment (FGVLA) aligns multi-level visual features with the multi-dimensional language space. Spatial-Linguistic Isomorphic Serialization (SLIS) unifies heterogeneous spatial outputs as autoregressive tokens. Progressive Cross-Modality Adaptation (PCMA) decomposes the compound domain gap into sequential stages, tackling the viewpoint and imaging physics gaps in turn. To support joint training, MMRS-OneVision is constructed with ~34M QA pairs spanning all six sensor modalities and cross-sensor fusion across 9 task categories, substantially exceeding existing RS multimodal instruction datasets. With only 2B parameters, Earth-OneVision achieves competitive or state-of-the-art results across extensive benchmarks, consistently matching or outperforming 4B-72B RS-MLLMs. It achieves 87.52% P@0.5 on the OPT-RSVG testset for optical visual grounding and 80.68% on the SAR VQA benchmark SARLANG-Bench, exceeding 7B models by over 7%. It further achieves 75.74% recall on the BigEarthNet-MS testset for multispectral classification, and 81.94% MCQ accuracy on EarthMind-Bench for cross-modality reasoning.

</details>


### [68] [HarmoView: Harmonizing Multi-View Constraints for Identity-Consistent Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10839)
*Cong Wang,Zhentao Yu,Hongmei Wang,Weicong Liang,Zixiang Zhou,Zilin Yang,Jiarong Ou,Rui Chen,Yuan Zhou,Qinglin Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HarmoView introduces multi-view cue integration via four architectural refinements and a staged training curriculum to achieve identity-consistent video generation with large viewpoint changes; it outperforms baselines and matches state-of-the-art closed-source engines on a bespoke 52-identity, 100-case multi-view benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Preserve appearance identity under viewpoint changes; lack of effective multi-view frameworks and data; need to leverage multi-view references.

Method: 1) Multi-level Feature Injection injects frontal ViT features and text tokens via cross-attention; 2) Learnable proxy tokens unify reference layouts; 3) Jump-RoPE isolates identity features to reduce crosstalk; 4) Progressive View Curriculum with four-stage training and view dropout; 5) large-scale multi-view dataset; evaluation on 100 cases, 52 identities.

Result: Significant improvements over open-source baselines and parity with leading closed-source engines; state-of-the-art identity-consistent video generation on the benchmark.

Conclusion: Presents a robust framework for identity-consistent video generation under multi-view inputs, addressing data scarcity and achieving strong identity fidelity; potential applicability to broader video synthesis tasks.

Abstract: Current identity-consistent video generation methods struggle to preserve appearance fidelity under large viewpoint changes. While introducing multi-view reference input offers a natural solution, progress remains constrained by the lack of effective frameworks for multi-view inputs and the scarcity of multi-view data. We address these challenges by proposing HarmoView, a robust framework for identity-consistent video generation that effectively integrates multi-view cues through three architectural refinements complemented by a staged training curriculum. Specifically, we first introduce Multi-level Feature Injection to anchor identity fidelity; by injecting raw ViT features from frontal references alongside text tokens via cross-attention, MFI provides persistent low-level appearance anchors that complement the high-level identity features within DiT blocks, leading to enhanced identity preservation. Then, we employ learnable proxy tokens to unify heterogeneous reference layouts across single-/multi-view settings while simultaneously resolving the reference-view mismatch problem. Jump-RoPE is further developed for identity-wise feature isolation to reduce identity crosstalk. To activate these structural capabilities while preserving the original generative priors, we propose the Progressive View Curriculum. This four-stage training strategy employs view dropout to facilitate a stable transition from vanilla T2V generation to high-fidelity, identity-persistent spatial reasoning. Furthermore, we construct a large-scale multi-view dataset to address the issue of data scarcity. Extensive evaluation on our multi-view benchmark, comprising 100 manually-curated cases spanning 52 unique identities, demonstrates that HarmoView significantly outperforms open-source baselines and matches leading closed-source engines, achieving state-of-the-art performance in identity-consistent video generation.

</details>


### [69] [LIBERO-Occ: Evaluating and Improving Vision-Language-Action Models under Scene-Induced Occlusion via Viewpoint Imagination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10862)
*Taishan Li,Jiwen Zhang,Siyuan Wang,Xuanjing Huang,Zhongyu Wei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LIBERO-Occ extends LIBERO to address scene-induced occlusion in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models by introducing Viewpoint Imagination (VIM), which imagines a complementary view of occluded scenes to improve decision-making without extra cameras. The approach yields stronger robustness under occlusion, and code is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Occlusion leads to partial observability in real-world manipulation, breaking assumptions of fully visible task-relevant objects in VLA benchmarks. A robust VLA should perform under scene-induced occlusion.

Method: Extend LIBERO to LIBERO-Occ and propose Viewpoint Imagination (VIM). VIM generates an imagined secondary view from the occluded observation and conditions action prediction on both observed and imagined evidence.

Result: VIM improves robustness across task suites, occlusion types, and severity levels compared to baselines, without requiring additional cameras at deployment; improved perception completion under partial observability.

Conclusion: Viewpoint imagination is a promising mechanism for perception completion in partially observable manipulation; the proposed benchmark (LIBERO-Occ) and code support evaluation of occlusion-aware VLA methods.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models achieve strong performance on standard manipulation benchmarks, but most evaluations assume that task-relevant objects are fully visible. This assumption often fails in realistic settings, where occlusion makes manipulation partially observable. In this paper, we study \textit{scene-induced occlusion} as a fundamental challenge for VLA models and introduce \textbf{LIBERO-Occ}, an occlusion-oriented extension of LIBERO. Experiments show that state-of-the-art VLAs suffer substantial performance degradation under occlusion. To address this issue, we propose \textbf{Viewpoint Imagination (VIM)}, which generates a complementary view from an occluded primary observation and conditions action prediction on both observed and imagined evidence. VIM improves robustness across task suites, occlusion types, and severity levels without requiring additional cameras at deployment time, suggesting that viewpoint imagination is an promising mechanism for perception completion in partially observable manipulation. Our benchmark and corresponding code are available at: \href{https://github.com/litsh/Libero-Occ}{https://github.com/litsh/Libero-Occ}.

</details>


### [70] [Schmidt Decomposition-Based Methods for Efficient Quantum Image Encoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10874)
*Ana-Maria Pangeva,Yassine Ferhi,Alexander Geng,Andreas Weinmann,Desislava Ivanova,Ali Moghiseh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Low-rank, Schmidt-based state approximation reduces circuit resources in quantum image encodings (FRQI, QPIE, NEQR) while preserving most image information; FRQI benefits with large depth reduction (~97%) and minor MSE increase (~0.27).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the resource bottlenecks of NISQ-era quantum image encoding by exploiting entanglement-sparsity via low-rank approximations, aiming to lower circuit depth and gate counts without substantial loss of image fidelity.

Method: Apply Schmidt decomposition-based low-rank approximation to the quantum image encodings (FRQI, QPIE, NEQR), keeping only the most significant entanglement components. Compare original and low-rank versions on metrics: circuit depth, CNOT count, MSE, and reconstructed image quality.

Result: Demonstrates trade-offs between accuracy and resource efficiency. FRQI with low-rank achieves up to ~97% reduction in circuit depth with a near-perfect reconstruction (MSE ≈ 0.27); overall, low-rank approximations can markedly reduce resource demands while preserving substantial image information across encodings.

Conclusion: Low-rank state approximation is a promising approach to enable practical quantum image processing on NISQ devices by dramatically reducing circuit complexity with tolerable loss in fidelity.

Abstract: In quantum image processing, a fundamental step is encoding classical image data into quantum states. This can be achieved using methods such as Flexible Representation of Quantum Images (FRQI), Quantum Probability Image Encoding (QPIE), and Novel Enhanced Quantum Representation (NEQR). However, on real quantum hardware, these encodings can quickly lead to circuits with many gates, large circuit depth, and high qubit usage, which is a problem for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices. In this work, we investigate whether low-rank state approximation, formulated via Schmidt decomposition, can help reduce this complexity. The method keeps only the most significant parts of a quantum state's entanglement structure, making state preparation more efficient while preserving most of the image information. We compare the three encoding techniques in their original form and with low-rank approximation, evaluating metrics such as circuit depth, CNOT count, MSE, and visual quality of reconstructed images. The results reveal meaningful trade-offs between accuracy and resource efficiency, with the FRQI model achieving a 97 percent reduction in circuit depth while maintaining a near-perfect reconstruction (MSE of about 0.27). This demonstrates the potential of low-rank techniques for advancing practical quantum image processing on near-term hardware.

</details>


### [71] [Advancing Wood Identification in the Philippines: Utilizing the Xylorix Platform for Efficient AI Model Development and Deployment for Five Key Species](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10876)
*Rosalie C. Mendoza,Vivian C. Daracan,Arlene D. Romano,Ronniel D. Manalo,Xin Jie Tang,Yi Hong Wong,Yong Haur Tay*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Non-programmer-friendly AI wood-ID models (Xylorix) classify five Philippine hardwoods from macroscopic cross-sections with high accuracy; feasible for field deployment, though Rain Tree is data-limited.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enforceable wood identification challenges due to illegal logging in the Philippines and limited access to specialized equipment/expertise; evaluate if non-programmers can build effective macroscopic ID models using Xylorix.

Method: Binary classifiers trained on 10,663 cross-section images from 260 specimens; specimen-level mean scoring to reflect field conditions; evaluated with AUC and Average Precision (AP); five target species; analysis of errors and misclassifications.

Result: AUC ranged 0.969–1.000; AP ranged 0.589–1.000. Four of five species achieved AA (AUC and AP ≥0.90); Rain Tree showed AE (AUC ≥0.90, AP <0.60) due to small test set (3 specimens). All classifiers ranked targets above non-targets with near-perfect fidelity. Specimen-level errors: 9 false negatives for Ipil (artifacts) and 3 false positives for Rain Tree and 1 for Tindalo due to shared tribal-level traits.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that Xylorix enables non-programmer wood scientists to construct operationally reliable macroscopic ID models for field deployment at supply-chain checkpoints; generally strong performance with some species-specific data limitations (notably Rain Tree) that may be mitigated by increasing test/sample sizes.

Abstract: Illegal logging and timber trade continue to pose significant challenges in the Philippines, where accurate wood species identification is essential for enforcement but limited by the need for specialised equipment and expertise. This study aims to evaluate whether AI models for macroscopic wood identification can be developed and deployed by wood scientists without programming expertise using the Xylorix platform, focusing on five Philippine hardwood species: Mangium (Acacia mangium Willd.), Rain Tree [Samanea saman (Jacq.) Merr.], Banuyo (Wallaceodendron celebicum Koord.), Tindalo [Afzelia rhomboidea (Blanco) Vidal], and Ipil [Intsia bijuga (Colebr.) O. Kuntze]. Binary classifiers were trained on 10,663 verified cross-section images from 260 specimens and evaluated using specimen-level mean scoring to mirror operational field conditions. Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) values ranged from 0.969 (Ipil) to 1.000 (Mangium), and Average Precision (AP) values ranged from 0.589 (Samanea) to 1.000 (Mangium). Four of five species achieved AA grade (AUC and AP both \geq 0.90); Rain Tree received AE (AUC \geq 0.90, AP < 0.60) due to AP compression from its small positive test set (3 specimens). All five classifiers rank their target specimens above non-target specimens with near-perfect fidelity. Specimen-level error analysis revealed 9 false negatives from Ipil, primarily stemming from localized image artifacts and 3 false positives for Rain Tree and 1 false positive for Tindalo caused by shared tribal-level anatomical traits. These findings demonstrate that Xylorix non-programmers can leverage the Xylorix platform to construct operationally reliable wood identification models suitable for field deployment at supply chain checkpoints.

</details>


### [72] [Listen, Look, and Learn: Learning Without Forgetting through SAM-Audio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10887)
*Avi Gupta,Nilotpal Sinha,Vishnu Raj,Sambuddha Saha,Pratik Joshi,Koteswar Rao Jerripothula,Tammam Tillo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Integrates SAM-Audio priors into class-incremental learning for audio-visual tasks, using guided attention and dual-level distillation to reduce forgetting and achieve state-of-the-art results on AV-CIL benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap of audio-visual class-incremental learning (CIL) and the underutilization of static multimodal priors like SAM-Audio, which perform poorly under incremental settings. Leverage dense audio-visual representations to improve continual learning performance.

Method: Utilize SAM-Audio’s dense audio and visual features; introduce a guided attention mechanism where audio features contextually steer visual representations; apply dual-level distillation at feature and logit levels to mitigate catastrophic forgetting.

Result: Empirical evaluations on audio-visual CIL benchmarks show consistent improvement over state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed guided attention and distillation strategy.

Conclusion: Integrating SAM-Audio priors into CIL with guided cross-modal attention and multi-level distillation yields robust AV continual learning, suggesting a favorable direction for leveraging static multimodal priors in incremental learning.

Abstract: Class-Incremental Learning (CIL) aims to continuously learn new classes without forgetting previously acquired knowledge. While recent CIL advances have spurred significant interest across various modalities, the audio-visual setting remains underexplored. Furthermore, although foundational multimodal models like SAM-Audio encapsulate rich static priors, our empirical analysis reveals that these representations struggle in incremental settings. This work bridges this gap by integrating SAM-Audio's audio-visual priors into the CIL setting. Specifically, we leverage its dense audio and visual representations and employ a novel guided attention strategy where the audio features contextually guide the visual representations. To further mitigate catastrophic forgetting, we introduce dual-level distillation objectives at both the feature and logit levels. Extensive evaluations on audio-visual CIL benchmarks demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [73] [Improving Text-Instance Alignment Of Foreground Conditioned Out-Painting Via Customized Concept Embedding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10892)
*Yihao Zhao,Xuan Han,Bin He,Mingyu You*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Customized Concept Embedding Diffusion (CCE-Diffusion) introduces a plug-and-play CCE module to reduce artifacts in foreground-conditioned outpainting by tailoring concept embeddings with Instance-Aware Loss and a semantic-preserving prompt, bridging generic nouns and a specific instance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Artifacts in text-driven FCO arise from misalignment between instance and text-derived concept embeddings, causing the background to echo the foreground semantics and dampen object prominence.

Method: Propose CCE-Diffusion with a CCE-Module to customize concept embeddings; train with Instance-Aware Loss; use Semantic-Preserving Prompt Template to avoid distorting other prompt words; acts as a plug-in to various FCO methods.

Result: Qualitative and quantitative evaluations demonstrate a significant reduction of artifacts in outputs; CCE-Module can be integrated with different FCO methods to enhance performance.

Conclusion: CCE-Diffusion effectively mitigates artifacts in FCO outputs and provides a versatile, plug-and-play solution for improving text-driven background customization.

Abstract: To showcase products, merchants often incur substantial costs creating high-quality display images. Foreground Conditioned Outpainting (FCO) meets this demand, allowing users to create desired backgrounds for foreground instances at a low cost by adjusting the text prompt. However, existing text-driven FCO methods exhibit critical flaws in their outputs, most notably the presence of artifacts, which refer to regions in the synthesized background that share the same semantics as the foreground instance. Such artifacts diminish the object's prominence and degrade image quality. We attribute the issue to the misalignment between the given instance and text-derived concept embeddings. To address this, we propose the Customized Concept Embedding Diffusion (CCE-Diffusion) framework. Its core is a CCE-Module to customize concept embeddings, bridging the gap between generic noun semantics and a specific visual instance. An Instance-Aware Loss guides the module's optimization, while a Semantic-Preserving Prompt Template prevents customized embeddings from distorting other words in the prompt. Both qualitative and quantitative evaluations demonstrate that CCE-Diffusion significantly reduces artifacts in the outputs. As a plug-and-play component, the CCE-Module can integrate with various FCO methods, enhancing their performance.

</details>


### [74] [The 1st PortraitCraft Challenge: A CVPR 2026 Workshop Competition on Portrait Composition Understanding and Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10894)
*Zijie Lou,Youyun Tang,Xiaochao Qu,Haoxiang Li,Ting Liu,Luoqi Liu,Xun Zhu,Zheng Zhang,Xi Chen,Miao Li,Ji Wu,Dizhe Zhang,Xian Ge,Sujia Wang,Ruiyang Zhang,Jiaming Wang,Xianshun Wang,Lu Qi,Boao Kang,Wei Zhou,Jinghui Sun,Zhenyu Yan,Jiliang Zhao,Rui Yang,Yipo Huang,Boyuan Liu,Shanglin Li,Zifan Xie,Yichen Zhang,Anlan Wang,Wenfeng Lin,Mingyu Guo,Dong Li,Xinghao Wang,Yanting Li,Shanzhao Tong,Shuai He,Qiu Zhou,Yongqi Yang,Taoyang Mu,Dianqiao Lei,Anlong Ming,Huadong Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CVPR 2026's PortraitCraft Challenge introduces two tracks—portrait composition understanding and generation—backed by a ~50k real-portrait dataset with multi-level supervision. It provides a unified evaluation framework and aims to advance portrait aesthetics analysis and controllable image synthesis, offering a standardized platform and initial results/analysis of submitted solutions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap where prior work emphasizes global aesthetic scoring; promote structured, controllable portrait understanding and synthesis through a unified, reproducible evaluation framework and large-scale, annotated data.

Method: Two-track challenge (Track 1: structured composition understanding; Track 2: portrait image generation from structured composition descriptions with explicit constraints). Public release of a ~50k curated real-portrait dataset with multi-level supervision. Detailed challenge setup, evaluation protocols, dataset composition, and analysis of submitted solutions in the report.

Result: Final results and analysis are reported; the framework is standardized and reproducible; expected to spur progress in portrait aesthetics and controllable image generation; no quantitative metrics provided in abstract.

Conclusion: PortraitCraft provides a unified, two-track evaluation platform and a large-scale, annotated portrait dataset to advance portrait composition understanding and controllable generation, potentially accelerating progress in related AI areas.

Abstract: This paper presents an overview of the inaugural PortraitCraft Challenge, held as one of the official competitions at CVPR 2026. The challenge focuses on portrait composition understanding and generation, aiming to advance AI research in portrait aesthetics analysis and controllable image synthesis. Unlike existing datasets and tasks that primarily focus on global aesthetic scoring, PortraitCraft introduces a unified evaluation framework comprising two complementary tracks. Track 1 requires models to perform structured portrait composition understanding, and Track 2 requires models to generate portrait images from structured composition descriptions under explicit compositional constraints. To support the challenge, we constructed and publicly released a large-scale portrait composition dataset consisting of approximately 50,000 curated real portrait images, providing multi-level supervision. This report describes the challenge setup, evaluation protocols, dataset composition, and final results, along with an analysis of the technical characteristics of the submitted solutions. The PortraitCraft Challenge provides a standardized and reproducible platform for research on portrait composition understanding and generation, and is expected to foster further progress in the fields of portrait aesthetics and controllable image generation.

</details>


### [75] [Pose-ICL: 3D-Aware In-Context Learning for Pose-Controllable Subject Customization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10902)
*Xuan Han,Yihao Zhao,Mingyu You*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Pose-ICL offers tuning-free, 3D-aware ICL for customizable subjects, using Surface-Anchored Position Embedding to anchor tokens to volumetric surfaces; outperforms current methods on pose accuracy and identity consistency; compatible with DiT.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current 2D-native backbones struggle with accurate pose control for customized subjects; cross-pose identity consistency suffers, suggesting a need for explicit 3D understanding.

Method: Introduce Pose-ICL: tuning-free framework leveraging 3D-aware In-Context Learning with multiple paired image-pose references; Surface-Anchored Position Embedding (SAPE) anchors image tokens to volumetric surface coordinates; refinements ensure compatibility with DiT models.

Result: Extensive evaluations on 3D assets and real-world subjects show Pose-ICL significantly improves pose accuracy and identity consistency relative to baselines.

Conclusion: 3D-aware ICL with SAPE enables effective pose control for customized subjects without fine-tuning, highlighting the value of volumetric grounding for 2D backbones.

Abstract: Subject Customization is a foundational task in modern image generation. By providing a few reference images and a text prompt, users can generate images of a specific object in any desired scene. However, existing methods still struggle to achieve effective pose control for customized subjects. In practice, they often exhibit inaccurate poses or inconsistent cross-pose appearances. These limitations suggest that understanding objects in a volumetric manner remains a significant challenge for 2D-native backbones. To address this challenge, we propose Pose-ICL, a tuning-free framework that leverages 3D-aware In-Context Learning (ICL) to directly adapt to new subjects through multiple paired image-pose references. Its core mechanism,Surface-Anchored Position Embedding (SAPE), equips the model with explicit 3D awareness by anchoring image tokens to the surface coordinates of a volumetric bounding box. Dedicated refinements ensure its seamless compatibility with existing DiT models. Extensive evaluations on both 3D assets and real-world subjects demonstrate that Pose-ICL significantly outperforms current methods in both pose accuracy and identity consistency.

</details>


### [76] [Beyond Model Size: Probing the Gaps in Visual in-Context Learning by Training a Tiny Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10905)
*Sunil Khatri,Steven Landgraf,Markus Ulrich,Simon Reiß*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A tiny Visual In-Context Learning (VICL) model (1M params, 70k images) is evaluated against much larger VICL models across three adaptive settings, revealing that current VICL benchmarks inadequately measure adaptive capabilities and are sensitive to task encoding, pre-training choices, and metrics, underscoring the need for new evaluation paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether the advances claimed by VICL depend on large-scale models and data, or if smaller, constrained models can achieve comparable adaptive behavior; assess the sufficiency of existing benchmarks for evaluating adaptive capability in vision models.

Method: Train a 1M-parameter VICL model on 70k images and compare its adaptive performance against 7,000× larger VICL models across three settings: (a) small distribution shifts in image data, (b) unseen task encodings, and (c) a completely new task as envisioned by VICL. Analyze how task encoding, pre-training tasks, and evaluation metrics influence measured adaptability.

Result: The experiments highlight a gap in current VICL benchmarking: adaptive capabilities are not consistently captured by existing evaluation protocols. Differences in how tasks are encoded, which tasks were used during pre-training, and the chosen metrics significantly affect measured adaptability, indicating a need for more innovative and standardized evaluation of adaptive capabilities.

Conclusion: Scaling alone (in model size or data) may not fully capture VICL's adaptive abilities. The study calls for new evaluation paradigms that robustly quantify adaptive performance, including consistent task-encoding schemes, task selection for pre-training, and agreed-upon metrics to benchmark adaptation.

Abstract: Visual in-Context Learning (VICL) aims at making progress towards adaptive vision models, that can -- based on a few examples -- adapt to a new task at test-time. With the history of in-context learning in natural language processing research, where large, parameter-heavy models are in use, one pathway that current VICL methods take is model- and data-scaling as key ingredients. Yet, it is not clear, whether these ingredients are the key for in-context learning to take shape in vision models. To stress-test such large models, we challenge them with an extreme counterexample: we train a tiny visual in-context model with merely $1$ million parameters and a modest amount of $70,000$ images. We compare the results of this severely capacity capped tiny model to $7,000\times$ larger VICL models in different adaptive settings, (1) on image data with small distribution shifts, (2) on unseen task encodings and (3) on a completely new task, i.e., the setting VICL envisions. With the chasm of training resources between the tiny- and large models, our experiments showcase a lack in how adaptive capabilities are measured, with respect to how tasks are encoded, which tasks were used in pre-training and the choice of metrics. These gaps in current VICL benchmarking underscore a need for innovation in evaluation of adaptive capabilities.

</details>


### [77] [PENet+: A Lightweight Residual Transformer Framework for Efficient Image Steganalysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10939)
*Jincheol AN,Dongsu Kim,Haneol Jang,YoungJoon Yoo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PENet+ is a lightweight PENet-inspired image steganalysis model that preserves the self-attention structure while cutting compute and parameters via classifier-streamlining, an activation-aware HPF stem with a balanced SRM-Gabor top-K subset, and a MobileNetV2-style backbone; evaluated on ALASKA2 JPEG QF90 512x512 with substantial efficiency gains and minimal accuracy loss. Device-level latency/power remain for future work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable accurate steganalysis on resource-constrained devices by reducing model size and FLOPs without sacrificing detection performance.

Method: Preserve PENet's self-attention topology; introduce classifier-streamlining to reduce SPP-to-FC1 channel dimensionality; refine HPF stem with activation-aware aggregation and top-K SRM-Gabor filter subset; replace backbone with MobileNetV2 inverted residual blocks; adopt a balanced K=31 filters (16 Gabor + 15 SRM); justify PReLU to preserve negative activations.

Result: On ALASKA2 JPEG QF90 at 512×512 (5k training/validation/internal testing; 19k evaluation): PENet+ achieves up to 45.5% fewer parameters and ~97% fewer FLOPs than the re-evaluated PENet baseline, with negligible accuracy loss; device-level latency/power not yet measured.

Conclusion: PENet+ offers a practical, computationally efficient direction for resource-constrained steganalysis, with promising efficiency gains and maintained performance, pending hardware-level evaluations.

Abstract: Image steganalysis, the detection of hidden information embedded in digital images, is a core component of modern cybersecurity and digital forensics. Recent residual Transformer architectures, such as the Pixel-Difference-Convolution and Enhanced-Transformer-Network (PENet) [1], achieve strong detection accuracy, but their computational and memory demands hinder deployment in resource-constrained settings. We present PENet+, a lightweight steganalysis framework that preserves PENet's discriminative structure while substantially improving efficiency. Rather than redesigning or compressing the attention blocks, we retain PENet's self-attention topology for reproducibility and add a classifier-streamlining stage that progressively narrows the SPP-to-FC1 input channels (SPP: spatial pyramid pooling; FC1: first fully connected layer), yielding large reductions in parameters and FLOPs with negligible accuracy loss.
