### [102] [Blurry Window Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09862)
*Axel Laborieux,Christos Sourmpis,Juan Gabriel Kostelec,Qinghai Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BLA is a Dirichlet-kernel-based extension of Attention with bounded memory that reduces state and achieves linear complexity; it shows strong state-efficiency gains over SWA and competitive performance with linear attention on synthetic recall tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the quadratic complexity and expanding KV cache of Softmax attention in Transformers to enable long-context processing with a finite, efficient state.

Method: Introduce Blurry Window Attention (BLA) which stores a frequency window and reconstructs a blurry KV history via Dirichlet kernel interpolation; interpretable as a generalization of Sliding Window Attention (SWA) or a special case of Gated Slot Attention (GSA) with Dirichlet-decayed attention; includes theory and efficient implementation details.

Result: On MQAR synthetic task, BLA achieves about 8x state efficiency over SWA and is competitive with popular linear attention models; on RegBench synthetic task, BLA and SWA are the only linear models that improve as state size grows among those tested.

Conclusion: BLA offers a principled, efficient linear-attention alternative with bounded memory that preserves recall-relevant behavior and scales with state size; further benchmarking on diverse tasks and hardware considerations would clarify practical gains.

Abstract: The Softmax Attention operation in Transformer language models has a quadratic complexity in the sequence length and a growing state size in the form of KV cache, which becomes a bottleneck in long context scenarios. To overcome this limitation, alternative architectures with linear complexity and finite state size have been introduced, such as State-Space Models (SSMs), Linear Attention (LA), and Attention with Bounded-memory Control (ABC). Though linear models achieve similar language perplexity as Transformers, they are still behind in tasks which require retrieval or recall of specific information. In this work, we introduce Blurry Window Attention (BLA) a novel ABC method inspired by SSMs. BLA stores a frequency window from which a blurry KV history is reconstructed via interpolation using Dirichlet kernels. BLA can be understood as a generalization of Sliding Window Attention (SWA) depending on the Dirichlet kernels resolution or as a special case of the Gated Slot Attention (GSA), where the decay factor is implemented with Dirichlet kernels. We describe in details the theory and efficient implementation of BLA. On the Multi-Query Associate Recall (MQAR) synthetic task, we show that the state efficiency of BLA is 8$\times$ better than SWA and is competitive with popular linear attention models, and in the RegBench synthetic task, only BLA and SWA improve their performance as the state size grows among the linear models we tested.

</details>


### [103] [From Confident Closing to Silent Failure: Characterizing False Success in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09863)
*Laksh Advani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: False success by LLM agents is frequent and hard for judges to detect; lightweight detectors outperform judges for flagging false success across tau2-bench and AppWorld, suggesting detectors as primary triage signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify and characterize false-success failures in LLM agents and evaluate detection methods (LLM judges vs lightweight detectors) across diverse benchmarks and model families.

Method: Analyze 9,876 tau2-bench trajectories across 8 model families and 1,879 AppWorld trajectories across 4 model families. Evaluate multiple judge configurations (5 judges, 5 prompts, full task specs) and lightweight TF-IDF detectors. Compare AUROC and latency, and quantify flag-rate performance and false-success recovery under varying domains (single-control tau2-bench, dual-control telecom, AppWorld self-assessing coding trajectories).

Result: False success is prevalent: ~45-48% in single-control tau2-bench, ~3% in dual-control telecom, and 75.8% in AppWorld self-assessing coding trajectories. LLM judges perform poorly (AUROC ≤0.65 on tau2-bench; ≤0.54 on AppWorld). Judges rely on surface proxies rather than verified state changes. Lightweight TF-IDF detectors achieve AUROC 0.83 (tau2-bench) and 0.95 (AppWorld), recovering 4–8× more false successes than the best judge at the same flag rate with ~3,300× lower latency.

Conclusion: For production monitoring of LLM agents, use lightweight, domain-calibrated detectors as primary triage signals rather than relying on LLM judges for false-success detection; consider detectors as first line of monitoring with judges as secondary, governance-oriented checks.

Abstract: LLM agents can fail silently by asserting task completion when the environment state shows otherwise. We study this failure mode, false success, across two agent benchmarks: 9,876 tau2-bench trajectories from 8 model families and 1,879 AppWorld trajectories from 4 model families with text-independent ground truth. False success is common but varies by setting: 45--48% of failures in single-control tau2-bench domains, 3% in dual-control telecom, and 75.8% among AppWorld self-assessing coding-agent trajectories with explicit status claims. LLM judges fail reliably: no configuration across 5 judges, 5 prompt strategies, and full task specifications exceeds AUROC 0.65 on tau2-bench, and the same judges reach only 0.54 AUROC on AppWorld API-call traces. Judges rely on surface completion proxies -- confident closing language in tau2-bench and coarse action-sequence volume in AppWorld -- rather than verified state changes. Lightweight TF-IDF detectors achieve task-disjoint AUROC 0.83 on tau2-bench and 0.95 on AppWorld, recovering 4--8x more false successes than the best judge at the same flag rate with 3,300x lower latency. These results suggest that production monitoring should use lightweight, domain-calibrated detectors as triage signals rather than relying on LLM judges as the primary monitor for false success.

</details>


### [104] [Alignment Collapse Under KV Cache Quantization: Diagnosis and Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09864)
*Bruce Changlong Xu,Adarsh Kumarappan,Mu Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Low-bit KV-cache quantization can silently degrade safety alignment in LLMs; a training-free diagnostic PCR recovers alignment by classifying failure modes and guiding mitigation, with strong generalization and practical runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantization for KV cache reduces memory but can harm safety alignment; standard metrics (perplexity/accuracy) miss these safety degradations; need reliable, transferable mitigation.

Method: Empirical study across 11 instruction-tuned models (3.8B–72B) and 5 benchmarks (1,894 prompts); identify geometric root cause (safety features in a low-dimensional subspace). Propose Per-Channel Reduction (PCR) diagnostic to classify failure modes into three categories; calibration prompts (20 prompts) to predict mitigation direction; test on unseen models/prompts and production quantizers (KIVI), with a training-free protocol (~35 GPU-minutes).

Result: Low-bit quantization can destroy safety alignment (e.g., Mistral-7B loses 15.2% refusals at 1.03x perplexity); no universal safe bit-width; PCR correctly predicts mitigation direction across models and generalizes to new prompts/models and production quantizers; achieves up to 97.2% recovery (KIVI); practical recovery with minimal memory overhead.

Conclusion: PCR offers a training-free, broadly effective protocol to preserve safety alignment under KV-cache quantization, highlighting a geometric subspace vulnerability and enabling safer deployment in production vLLM with FP8 KV caches.

Abstract: Key-value (KV) cache quantization is widely used to reduce Large Language Model (LLM) inference memory, yet existing evaluations solely focus on measuring perplexity and accuracy without assessing the safety impact. In this study, we explore alignment preservation under KV cache quantization. Across eleven instruction-tuned models (3.8B-72B) and five benchmarks (1,894 prompts), we find that low-bit quantization can silently destroy safety alignment: Mistral-7B loses 15.2% of its refusals at only 1.03x perplexity, and no universal safe bit-width exists, with sharp model-specific phase transitions invisible to standard metrics. We identify that the root cause is geometric: safety features occupy a low-dimensional activation subspace 10^2-10^3x more vulnerable to quantization noise than the full representation space perplexity averages over. Inspired by this observation, we propose Per-Channel Reduction (PCR), a diagnostic that classifies each model into one of three mechanistic failure modes: outlier-crushes-safety, where safety lives in non-outlier channels collaterally damaged by outlier-driven scale factors; outlier-as-safety, where safety overlaps outlier channels and finer granularity cannot rescue it; and multi-layer dilution, where safety is distributed across many layers and per-layer fixes fail. PCR predicts the correct mitigation direction on all nine primary models and one held-out model from an independent family using 20 calibration prompts. PCR generalizes across unseen prompts, models, and production quantizers, including KIVI with up to 97.2% recovery, succeeding where attention-based allocation methods fail. The resulting training-free protocol, requiring approximately 35 GPU-minutes, recovers up to 97% of lost alignment at minimal memory overhead, addressing vulnerabilities confirmed in production vLLM serving with FP8 KV cache on NVIDIA GPUs.

</details>


### [105] [LLM-as-a-Discriminator: When Synthetic Tables Still Look Real](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09865)
*Manel Slokom,Malek Slokom,Thierno Kante*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM-based discrimination can audit privacy of synthetic tabular data; two settings (table only vs with distributional metadata); evaluated across three synthesis models and two datasets; shows promise but sensitivity to model choice and encoding; reproducible via code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Privacy risk in data sharing and difficulty of auditing synthetic data; humans struggle to distinguish real vs synthetic; propose LLM as practical audit signal.

Method: Ask an LLM to classify samples as REAL or SYNTHETIC; two settings C1 (table only) and C2 (table plus distributional metadata); compare LLaMA and Gemini; synthesis models CTGAN, TVAE, Gaussian Copula; datasets UCI Adult and ACS Census; 451 trials; baselines include C2ST and record linkage; human pilot with 2 annotators and 240 trials; code released for reproducibility.

Result: Divergent detection rates by model and dataset: on Adult, LLaMA DRS=0% while Gemini DRS=100% for CTGAN and TVAE; on Census, LLaMA tends to classify as SYNTHETIC; Gemini strong in C1 but drops for CTGAN/TVAE in C2; when compared to C2ST and record linkage baselines, LLM discrimination provides a practical privacy audit signal under careful handling of model choice, reporting, and encoding; human pilot shows partial alignment with model-based signals.

Conclusion: LLM-based discrimination can serve as a practical privacy audit signal for synthetic data, but effectiveness depends on model choice, data encoding, and reporting practices; should complement distributional tests and human evaluation; reproducible via released code.

Abstract: Privacy and data sharing are often in tension. Many organizations use synthetic data to reduce privacy risk and still share useful data. For tabular data, auditing privacy remains hard. In many cases, even humans cannot easily tell if a table is real or synthetic. In this paper, we propose a method based on LLM discrimination. We ask an LLM to classify each table sample as REAL or SYNTHETIC. We test two settings: C1 with table only, and C2 with table plus distributional metadata. We use LLaMA as an open model and Gemini as a reference model. In our experiments, we run three synthesis models, CTGAN, TVAE, and Gaussian Copula, on two public datasets, UCI Adult and ACS Census. We collect 451 valid trials. Our results show clear differences between models. On Adult, LLaMA reaches DRS=0% in reported cells, while Gemini reaches DRS=100% for CTGAN and TVAE. On Census, LLaMA predicts SYNTHETIC for most samples, while Gemini stays high in C1 but drops for CTGAN and TVAE in C2. We also compare with a classifier two-sample test (C2ST) and record linkage as distributional baselines, and with a human pilot of 2 annotators and 240 trials. Our results show that LLM discrimination is a practical privacy audit signal when model choice, per provider reporting, and data encoding are handled with care. For reproducibility, code and experiment scripts are available at https://github.com/SlokomManel/LLM-as-a-Discriminator.

</details>


### [106] [Two to Tango: Coupled Task-Reference Selection for Safe LLM Fine-tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09866)
*Xinrui Chen,Jianhao Zhang,Ou Wu,Di Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DualSelect jointly selects safety references and task samples for LLM fine-tuning, aligning safety constraints with evolving tasks to preserve safety while maintaining task utility, via a minimax, entropy-regularized framework with lazy reference refresh.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Downstream fine-tuning of safety-aligned LLMs often degrades learned safety because fixed safety references and one-sided filtering fail to capture task-dependent safety constraints. A dynamic, joint mechanism is needed to adapt safety references and training samples to evolving task updates.

Method: Propose DualSelect: a coupled framework that refreshes task-conditioned safety references before filtering task samples that are compatible with the induced reference direction. Framed as a minimax problem, it selects safety references with high preservation loss and task conflict, along with compatible task samples, using entropy-regularized scoring surrogates, lazy reference refresh, and gradient correction.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 1B–8B LLMs shows DualSelect preserves safety without sacrificing task utility. Using REDORCA judgments, Safety Avg improves by at least 5.10 points over the strongest baseline and remains highest across judges, with moderate computational overhead.

Conclusion: DualSelect offers a principled, dynamic, joint selection mechanism to align safety references with task updates, enabling robust safety preservation during fine-tuning and potential applicability to continual, retention-focused learning.

Abstract: Fine-tuning safety aligned large language models (LLMs) on downstream data improves adaptation but may erode learned safety behavior. Existing methods use fixed safety examples, global constraints, or one-sided task filtering. Our diagnostics show task updates expose different safety constraints, motivating joint selection of relevant references and compatible task samples. We propose DualSelect, a coupled framework for task and reference selection that refreshes task conditioned safety references before filtering whole task samples compatible with the induced reference direction. Under a minimax view, DualSelect selects safety references with high preservation loss and task conflict, together with compatible task samples, through entropy-regularized scoring surrogates, lazy reference refresh, and gradient correction. On 1B-8B LLMs, DualSelect preserves safety without losing task utility; using the REDORCA judge, it improves Safety Avg. over the strongest baseline by at least 5.10 points and remains highest in Safety Avg. across judges with moderate overhead. This view extends to retention focused continual learning.

</details>


### [107] [SPACE: Source-free Proxy Anchor Concept Erasure for MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09868)
*Zhijing Zhang,Jiaqi Ding,Qianshan Wei,Nan Zhou,Jiaqi Li,Yongliang Wu,Tongxin Zhu,Xiaolin Fang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPACE: a source-free unlearning framework for Multimodal LLMs that uses TPAS to select text-guided proxy anchors and DCSI to erase target concepts while preserving retained knowledge, with null-space updates and theoretical guarantees; experiments on six datasets show competitive performance to data-dependent methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Growing privacy risks and regulatory constraints limit access to target data; conventional unlearning methods rely on data that may be unavailable or restricted. This creates a need for source-free unlearning that preserves model utility while removing sensitive concepts.

Method: Two-stage approach: (1) Text-Guided Proxy Anchor Selection (TPAS) retrieves semantically aligned proxy anchors from the shared feature space. (2) Dual-Constraint Semantic Isolation (DCSI) optimizes these anchors to indirectly erase target concepts, constraining updates to the null space of retained knowledge to preserve structure.

Result: Theoretical guarantees show SPACE bounds perturbations on retained knowledge and maximizes feature spectral entropy. Empirical evaluation across six datasets demonstrates performance comparable to state-of-the-art data-dependent methods in source-free MU scenarios.

Conclusion: SPACE provides an effective, theoretically grounded solution for source-free unlearning in MLLMs, maintaining model performance while removing sensitive concepts; code release anticipated.

Abstract: As Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) face growing privacy risks and regulatory constraints, machine unlearning (MU) has emerged as a crucial solution for removing sensitive data while preserving model performance. However, existing MU methods typically rely on visual data of the target concepts, which is often unavailable due to strict data retention policies, thus creating a demand for source-free unlearning approaches that operate without access to the target data. In this work, we propose Source-free Proxy Anchor Concept Erasure (SPACE), the first source-free unlearning framework specialized for MLLMs. SPACE consists of two stages: (1) Text-Guided Proxy Anchor Selection (TPAS), which retrieves semantically aligned proxy anchors from the shared feature space. (2) Dual-Constraint Semantic Isolation (DCSI), which optimizes these anchors to indirectly erase target concepts. DCSI confines updates to the null space of retained knowledge, ensuring structural integrity. We theoretically prove that SPACE strictly bounds the perturbation on retained knowledge and maximizes feature spectral entropy, thereby maintaining the model's performance. Furthermore, extensive experiments across six datasets show that SPACE achieves performance comparable to that of state-of-the-art data-dependent methods, validating its effectiveness in source-free MU scenarios. The source code will be released.

</details>


### [108] [QSplitFL: Capability Aware Deep Q-Learning for Optimal Split Point Selection in Split Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09869)
*Nazmus Shakib Shadin,Xinyue Zhang,Jingyi Wang,Miao Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QSplitFL is a capability-aware DQN-based framework for optimal split point selection in Split Learning-based Federated Learning, using lightweight hardware-derived state and a decayed loss-drop reward with a committee DQN to adapt to heterogeneous devices. It achieves better convergence and higher accuracy across standard datasets/models, with code available publicly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optimal split points in Split Learning under hardware heterogeneity remain a challenge. Fixed splits can overload weaker devices and increase communication/server load, hindering convergence and stability in FL+SL systems.

Method: A lightweight state representation built from client hardware metrics (CPU utilization, memory, battery level, and network latency). The method employs a decayed loss-drop reward to prioritize early convergence and uses a committee-based DQN architecture with majority voting to mitigate reward hacking. Evaluations are conducted on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 using CNN, ResNet50, MobileNetV4, and ConvNeXt architectures.

Result: Experiments show better convergence and higher accuracy than existing methods, with effective adaptation to heterogeneous device resources across multiple datasets and model architectures.

Conclusion: QSplitFL provides an effective, resource-aware mechanism for split point selection in SFL, improving convergence stability and accuracy in heterogeneous environments; the work includes publicly available source code.

Abstract: Federated Learning (FL) combined with Split Learning (SL) is a privacy preserving paradigm that enables training deep neural networks (DNNs) on resource constrained devices while reducing overall training cost. However, determining the optimal split point, meaning the layer where the model is divided still remains a critical challenge, especially when clients have heterogeneous hardware capabilities. Fixed split points can overload weak devices and increase the communication and server load, which slows convergence and reduces stability. This paper introduces QSplitFL, a novel capability-aware Deep Q-Network (DQN) framework for optimal split point selection in Split learning based Federated Learning (SFL) environments. Unlike existing approaches that rely on high-dimensional model weight representations, QSplitFL employs a lightweight state representation derived directly from client hardware metrics, including CPU utilization, memory, battery level, and network latency. The proposed framework incorporates a decayed loss-drop reward function that prioritizes early convergence, and a committee-based DQN architecture with majority voting to mitigate reward hacking. Extensive experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 datasets using CNN, ResNet50, MobileNetV4, and ConvNeXt architectures demonstrate that our approach achieves better convergence and higher accuracy compared to existing methods, while effectively adapting to heterogeneous device resources. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/AIPO-Lab/QSplitFL.

</details>


### [109] [PatchSTG: Scalable Spatiotemporal Graph Transformers for Traffic Forecasting on Irregular Sensor Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09872)
*Jichao Li,Xuanming Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PatchSTG introduces a patch-based spatiotemporal graph Transformer for irregular sensor networks, achieving near-linear computational complexity with competitive multi-horizon traffic forecasting performance on real-world and large-scale datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traffic forecasting on real networks is hampered by irregular sensor distributions and high computational costs of modeling large-scale spatiotemporal dependencies. Graph-based and attention-based models struggle with non-uniform spatial structure and scalability.

Method: Construct a hierarchical spatial representation by partitioning sensors into balanced, locality-preserving patches using geographic information. Apply a dual attention encoder that alternates intra-patch attention (local interactions) and inter-patch attention (global dependencies), reducing complexity from quadratic to near-linear.

Result: Empirical evaluation on Rhode Island data and large-scale datasets shows stable, competitive forecasting across multiple horizons with substantially improved computational efficiency. Ablation studies confirm the gains from spatial partitioning and dual attention in modeling local and long-range dynamics.

Conclusion: Patch-based spatiotemporal modeling provides a scalable, effective framework for traffic forecasting under irregular spatial settings, addressing both accuracy and efficiency challenges.

Abstract: Traffic forecasting is a fundamental component of intelligent transportation systems, yet remains challenging in real-world settings due to irregular sensor distributions and the high computational cost of modeling large-scale spatiotemporal dependencies. In practical traffic networks, sensors are unevenly distributed across regions, leading to non-uniform spatial structures that limit the effectiveness and scalability of existing graph-based and attention-based models. To address these challenges, we propose PatchSTG, a patch-based spatiotemporal graph Transformer designed for efficient forecasting on irregular sensor networks. The key idea is to introduce a hierarchical spatial representation that partitions sensors into balanced, locality-preserving patches based on geographic information. On top of this structure, a dual attention encoder alternates between intra-patch attention for capturing local interactions and inter-patch attention for modeling global dependencies, reducing computational complexity from quadratic to near-linear scaling. We evaluate PatchSTG on real-world traffic data from Rhode Island and additional large-scale datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves stable and competitive forecasting performance across multiple horizons, while significantly improving computational efficiency. Ablation studies further validate the effectiveness of spatial partitioning and dual attention in capturing both local and long-range traffic dynamics. These results suggest that patch-based spatiotemporal modeling provides a scalable and effective framework for traffic forecasting under irregular spatial settings.

</details>


### [110] [Rotate2Think: Geometric Priming via Orthogonal Rotation to Improve Language Model Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09873)
*Aditya Sharma,Christopher J. Pal,Amal Zouaq*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Rotate2Think is a training-free, Procrustes-based method that learns a rotation between input and thinking embeddings, inserting a synthetic thinking vector during inference to prime reasoning; it yields accuracy gains in 30/32 model-benchmark pairs and even generalizes to multimodal reasoning (MATH-Vision).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The reasoning traces used by large models reveal a highly conic embedding structure for both input prompts and thinking traces. The non-collinear, distinct directions of input versus thinking embeddings suggest a geometric mapping can transform prompts into reasoning steps, potentially boosting inference-time reasoning without re-training.

Method: Estimate an orthogonal rotation between input and thinking embedding spaces via orthogonal Procrustes using a small set of solved examples. Inject the resulting synthetic thinking vector between thinking delimiters at inference time to seed the reasoning trace. The approach is training-free and model- and task-agnostic, applicable to math, science, code, and multimodal tasks.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows improvements in 30 of 32 model-benchmark configurations across math, science, and coding tasks, with zero-shot generalization to multimodal reasoning on MATH-Vision.

Conclusion: Reasoning transitions can be cast as a rotation in embedding space; Rotate2Think provides a lightweight, training-free mechanism to prime reasoning traces and improve performance across diverse tasks.

Abstract: Reasoning models achieve strong performance on challenging tasks by generating explicit intermediate reasoning traces before producing a final answer. Yet the internal structure of representation space when reasoning remains poorly understood: how do a model's hidden representations differ during thinking versus the embeddings of the input prompt, and can this structure be exploited to elicit stronger reasoning at inference time? We show that both input embeddings and thinking embeddings (mean-pooled last-layer hidden states over the prompt and reasoning trace, respectively) exhibit extremely high conicity, with all vectors clustering tightly around a single mean direction. Crucially, these mean input and thinking directions are non-collinear, with thinking embeddings occupying a geometrically distinct region of embedding space across many different models and benchmark tasks. This observation motivates casting the input-to-thinking transition as a rotation problem admitting a closed-form solution via orthogonal Procrustes analysis. We propose Rotate2Think, a training-free method that estimates this rotation from a small set of correctly solved examples and injects the resulting synthetic thinking vector between thinking delimiters at inference time, providing a geometric primer at the onset of the reasoning trace. Evaluated across multiple benchmarks and model families, Rotate2Think improves accuracy in 30 of 32 model-benchmark configurations across mathematics, science, and code tasks, and generalizes zero-shot to multimodal reasoning on MATH-Vision.

</details>


### [111] [Disjoint or Overlapping? Inference Windowing for Reconstruction-Based Time Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09874)
*Guillaume Coulaud,Reza Akbarinia,Florent Masseglia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reconstruction-based univariate time series anomaly detection benefits from overlapping inference; unified protocol reveals that inference stride substantially affects performance, with up to 28% relative gains and possible ranking changes; baselines remain competitive on TSB-AD and UCR when evaluated under standardized training/tuning/multi-seed settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address lack of comparability in reconstruction-based anomaly detection results due to varied evaluation practices and inference procedures; specifically, to examine the role of inference stride in offline univariate settings and establish a reproducible protocol.

Method: Systematic evaluation of multiple reconstruction-based models (PCA-based, DLinear, AutoEncoder, TimesNet, Transformer variants) on univariate offline data; compare disjoint vs overlapping inference windows; unified training/tuning/multi-seed protocol; evaluation on TSB-AD benchmark and full UCR archive with sliding-window localization criteria.

Result: Overlapping inference consistently improves performance across models; average relative gains up to 28%; can change method rankings; results robust to dataset/seed/hyperparameter variability; reconstruction-based baselines perform strongly on both benchmarks.

Conclusion: Inference choices (specifically window overlap) substantially influence reconstruction-based anomaly detection; a clear, reproducible protocol is essential; reconstruction-based baselines are competitive and practical for univariate TSD in both curated and large-scale benchmarks.

Abstract: Reconstruction-based methods are widely used for time series anomaly detection, where models are trained to reconstruct subsequences, and anomalies are identified through reconstruction errors. However, reported results are often hard to compare due to heterogeneous evaluation practices and underspecified inference procedures. In this paper, we revisit reconstruction-based anomaly detection in the univariate offline setting and study the role of the inference stride, which controls whether subsequences are processed as disjoint windows or with overlap. We propose a unified training, tuning, and multi-seed evaluation protocol on the curated TSB-AD benchmark, and study how overlapping inference affects anomaly detection performance for a range of reconstruction models, including PCA-based baselines, DLinear, an AutoEncoder, TimesNet, and Transformer variants. The results show that across all models, overlapping windows yield consistent improvements, with average relative gain up to +28%, and can alter method rankings. We further analyze variability across datasets, random seeds, and hyperparameter configurations. Finally, we complement the benchmark study with an evaluation on the full UCR archive using localization criteria aligned with sliding-window reconstruction. Overall, our results highlight that reconstruction-based anomaly detection performance depends not only on model architecture and training, but also on inference choices, motivating a clear and reproducible protocol. Our results show that reconstructionbased baselines achieve strong performance on both TSB-AD and UCR benchmarks, supporting them as competitive and practical approaches for univariate time series anomaly detection.

</details>


### [112] [Integrating Local and Global Entropy for Uncertainty Quantification in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09875)
*Johanne Medina,Tianyi Zhou,Keivin Isufaj,Aristides Gionis,Sanjay Chawla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Global-Local Uncertainty (GLU) fuses hidden-state geometry (global uncertainty) with token-level entropy (local uncertainty) into an unsupervised, single-pass score for LLM reliability, outperforming baselines across multiple models and benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs hallucinate with high confidence; existing uncertainty methods rely on token-level signals and ignore the geometric structure of hidden states. The authors propose that hidden-state geometry captures a distinct, global failure mode not detected by token-level signals.

Method: Compute hidden-state matrix geometry to derive a global uncertainty measure (geometric entropy) and compute token-level entropy as a local measure. Show near-orthogonality between the two signals. Fuse them with a multiplicative gate to produce GLU, an unsupervised, single-pass, length-normalized, architecture-agnostic score.

Result: GLU matches or outperforms all unsupervised baselines across three model families and six benchmarks, while requiring only a single forward pass and maintaining length normalization and architecture-agnostic properties.

Conclusion: Incorporating global hidden-state geometry with local token entropy yields a complementary, effective uncertainty estimator for LLMs. GLU provides a practical, efficient tool for reliability assessment without supervision and with broad applicability.

Abstract: Large language models hallucinate confidently, making uncertainty quantification (UQ) essential for reliable deployment. Existing methods rely predominantly on token-level signals, leaving the geometric structure of intermediate hidden states underused. In this paper, we take the geometric complexity of hidden-state matrices as a measure of the global uncertainty of LLMs, while treating token-level uncertainty estimation as a local metric. We show that hidden-state geometric entropy (global uncertainty) and token-level entropy (local uncertainty) are statistically near-orthogonal, capturing distinct failure regimes for reliability prediction. In particular, global geometry recovers the confident-but-wrong failure mode that local signals systematically miss. Building on this, we propose Global-Local Uncertainty (GLU), an unsupervised, single-pass score that fuses the two signals via a multiplicative gate. Across three model families and six benchmarks, GLU matches or outperforms all unsupervised baselines while requiring only a single forward pass and remaining length-normalized and architecture-agnostic.

</details>


### [113] [Calibrating Overconfidence Without Sacrificing Confidence: Probe-Conditioned Head Intervention for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09876)
*Ke Li,Chongzhe Zhang,Zifan Zeng,Feng Liu,Qunli Zhang,Zheng Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PCHI introduces a probe-conditioned, inference-time intervention to suppress unwarranted confidence in LLMs by re-scaling downstream attention-head outputs based on a frozen probe that detects likely wrong-but-confident responses. It significantly reduces miscalibrated confidence (ECE) with modest harm to correct answers, demonstrated on Qwen3-4B-Instruct/OpenMathInstruct (and gemma models).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate and mitigate verbalized overconfidence in LLMs without global calibration that harms warranted confidence; achieve selective suppression of unwarranted confidence during inference.

Method: Train-free, inference-time technique: use a frozen probe to detect likely incorrect-but-confident outputs; apply readout-token PCHI and upstream-token confidence-template interventions to conditionally rescale attention-head outputs during confidence generation; evaluate via calibration metrics (ECE) and readout labels (yes/no).

Result: On Qwen3-4B-Instruct solving OpenMathInstruct, 82.2% of wrong-yes readouts become no; joint upstream/downstream interventions reduce ECE from 21.9% to 9.2%, with 5.1% damage to originally correct-yes readouts. Gemma3-4B shows similar but weaker effects.

Conclusion: Verbalized overconfidence can be selectively reduced through internal, conditional intervention without fully sacrificing warranted confidence; potential for more robust calibration with limited performance trade-offs.

Abstract: Large language models often express high confidence in answers that are wrong. Standard calibration remedies typically act globally or at the score level, reducing unwarranted confidence but also risking erosion of warranted confidence on correct answers. We introduce Probe-Conditioned Head Intervention (PCHI), an inference-time method that uses a frozen probe to detect likely wrong-but-confident responses and conditionally rescales downstream attention-head outputs during confidence generation. On Qwen3-4B-Instruct solving OpenMathInstruct problems with a structured binary confidence field, readout-token PCHI converts 82.2% of originally wrong-yes confidence readouts to $\texttt{no}$, while a joint intervention across upstream confidence-template tokens reduces ECE from 21.9% to 9.2% and damages only 5.1% of originally correct-yes readouts. The readout-token effect also appears on Gemma3-4B, though upstream interventions are weaker and more mask-dependent. These results show that verbalized overconfidence can be selectively reduced through conditionally applied internal intervention, partially decoupling the suppression of unwarranted confidence from the loss of warranted confidence.

</details>


### [114] [Streaming Knowledge Compilation: Proactive Materiality-Scored Pinning for Time-Evolving LLM Wikis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09877)
*Juan M. Huerta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Streaming Knowledge Compilation minimizes cumulative regret in dynamic knowledge settings by using a materiality signal to pre-pin documents before queries arrive; achieves an O(√(T log K)) regret bound; validated in finance and Wikipedia and shows regret-based evaluation is robust for compiled knowledge systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM wiki systems assume static corpora; evolving information requires continuous selection of documents to cache. The work formalizes streaming knowledge compilation to maintain a compiled wiki under a fixed token budget with no knowledge of future queries, optimizing regret relative to an oracle.

Method: Define a per-entity materiality signal φ_t(k,n) in [0,1] predicting document importance and query relevance; use φ as a surrogate for proactive pinning, derive regret bound O(√(T log K)) depending on ε = E[|φ_t−φ̂_t|]; instantiate in finance (volatility signal from frozen Llama 3.1 8B classifier; AUROC 0.728 on 76k articles) and Wikipedia (AER: abnormal edit ratio); end-to-end QA on matched pairs to compare against oracle and to detect LLM-judge confounds.

Result: Regret bounds hold; finance cumulative regret converges to -20.0 (-0.12/step); Wikipedia to +16.0 (+0.13/step); observed that post-training content improves QA scores (No Wiki 3.80 vs Oracle 4.74) and mitigates LLM-as-judge confounds; the same algorithm generalizes across domains.

Conclusion: The O(√(T log K)) guarantee applies to any domain where knowledge gaps can be predicted from streaming signals; streaming knowledge compilation with a materiality signal is a principled approach to maintain proximal knowledge under a fixed budget and evolving information.

Abstract: LLM wiki systems compile knowledge into pre-filled KV caches for efficient inference, but assume a static corpus -- an assumption that fails whenever the underlying information landscape evolves. We formalize Streaming Knowledge Compilation: given a document stream, a fixed token budget, and future queries unknown at ingestion time, maintain a compiled wiki that minimizes cumulative regret against an offline oracle with perfect foresight. The enabling insight is a materiality signal $φ_t(k,n)\in[0,1]$ that scores document importance for entity $k$ at time $t$, acting as a query-relevance surrogate for proactive pinning before queries arrive; we prove an $O(\sqrt{T\log K})$ regret bound where $\varepsilon=\mathbb{E}[|φ_t-\hatφ_t|]$ is the only domain-specific quantity. We instantiate in two domains: finance, where $φ_t$ is abnormal stock volatility predicted by frozen Llama 3.1 8B classification head (AUROC = 0.728 on 76K articles, strict temporal split; $1.49\times$ higher realized forward volatility for predicted-material articles); and Wikipedia, where $φ_t$ is the Abnormal Edit Ratio (AER), a cross-sectionally normalized edit velocity -- showing the same algorithm generalizes beyond the finance domain. End-to-end QA evaluation on 173 matched pairs (finance) and 119 (Wikipedia) reveals a pervasive LLM-as-judge confound on post-training knowledge, establishing that regret analysis -- not absolute QA scores -- is the reliable evaluation metric for compiled knowledge systems. Finance cumulative regret converges to -20.0 (-0.12/step); Wikipedia to +16.0 (+0.13/step), with the positive sign confirming that Wikipedia edit content is genuinely post-training -- richer context consistently improves scores (No Wiki 3.80 vs. Oracle 4.74) -- and eliminates this confound. The $O(\sqrt{T\log K})$ guarantee applies to any domain where knowledge gaps can be predicted from streaming signals.

</details>


### [115] [FailureScope: Cross-Regime Behavioral Diagnosis of Language Model Weaknesses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09878)
*Nicholas Saban*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FailureScope is a behavioral-diagnosis framework that clusters evaluation probes by cross-model pass/fail patterns (LOMO) to produce portable failure taxonomies across single-turn, multi-turn, and adversarial tasks; it achieves high stability and predictive power and reveals meta-failures, releasing data and taxonomies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practitioners need to know specific capabilities lacking, not just aggregate accuracy; existing benchmarks are brittle and regime-specific; portable diagnostic primitives are needed for robust model assessment.

Method: Cluster evaluation probes by cross-model pass/fail patterns using leave-one-model-out (LOMO); taxonomy-conditioned sampling; evaluate on 2,664 single-turn tasks across 18 models; predict failures across models; apply to a 363-task multi-turn corpus and 630 adversarial traces; identify meta-failure where LLM-judge ASR diverges from real execution.

Result: Taxonomies are stable across regimes (cluster cohesion); Kendall's tau = 0.81 at 50 tasks (vs 0.34 random); cross-model failure prediction AUC = 0.88; interpretable clusters in single-turn, multi-turn, and adversarial settings; exposes a meta-failure gap of 73–100 percentage points between ASR and real execution; results generalize, supporting portability of the diagnostic primitive.

Conclusion: Behavioral clustering serves as a portable, regime-general diagnostic primitive for model evaluation; release includes pipeline, three annotated corpora, and cross-regime taxonomies; enables targeted diagnostics and benchmarking beyond aggregate accuracy.

Abstract: Standard benchmarks report aggregate accuracy, but practitioners need to know which specific capabilities a model lacks. We introduce FailureScope, a behavioral-diagnosis method that clusters evaluation probes by their cross-model pass/fail patterns (leave-one-model-out, LOMO), and show it yields stable, interpretable failure taxonomies across three regimes usually studied separately: single-turn benchmarks, multi-turn dialogue, and adversarial agent attacks. On 2,664 single-turn tasks across 18 models, taxonomy-conditioned sampling reaches Kendall's tau = 0.81 at 50 tasks (versus 0.34 for random selection), and cross-model failure prediction reaches AUC 0.88. The same primitive recovers interpretable clusters on a 363-task multi-turn corpus and on 630 adversarial agent traces, where it exposes a meta-failure mode: a 73-100 percentage-point gap between LLM-judge ASR and real execution. Cluster cohesion remains strong across all three regimes, which we take as evidence that behavioral clustering is a portable diagnosis primitive that generalizes beyond any single benchmark. We release the pipeline, three annotated corpora, and the cross-regime taxonomies.

</details>


### [116] [Operator Fusion for LLM Inference on the Tensix Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09879)
*Qingbo Wu,Ke Li,Wenzhu Wang,Jie Yu,Ruian Zhang,Lili Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Operator fusion and NoC-based data distribution improve on-device Transformer inference on Tensix; fused RMSNorm with matmul reduces DRAM traffic and latency while preserving numerical accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address on-device inference bottlenecks for Transformer models on Tenstorrent's Tensix: memory-bound and compute-bound stages, DRAM bandwidth, scheduling overhead, and need for efficient multi-core parallelism.

Method: Fuse RMSNorm with matrix multiplication in self-attention and FFN; execute fused ops back-to-back in on-chip SRAM to minimize intermediate DRAM traffic; use a NoC-based multicast to distribute inputs/weights across cores; evaluate on Wormhole platform with Qwen models.

Result: Latency reductions: up to 37.44% for attention, 15.89% for MLP, up to 7.91% per decoder layer; PCC > 98.75% indicates numerical consistency.

Conclusion: The combination of operator fusion and NoC-based data distribution yields significant end-to-end efficiency gains for on-device Transformer inference with preserved accuracy; approach improves data locality and parallelism, reducing DRAM bandwidth pressure.

Abstract: This study addresses on-device inference bottlenecks of Transformer models on Tenstorrent's Tensix architecture and proposes an operator fusion strategy that enhances data locality. RMSNorm is fused with matrix multiplication in self-attention and in the FFN, enabling back-to-back execution of memory-bound and compute-bound operators in on-chip SRAM to significantly reduce DRAM reads/writes of intermediate results and scheduling overhead. To support multi-core parallelism, a NoC-based multicast mechanism is leveraged in which row/column master nodes efficiently distribute inputs and weights across the core mesh, alleviating DRAM bandwidth contention. Experiments on the Wormhole platform with Qwen2.5-0.5B, Qwen3-0.6B, and Qwen3-4B show up to 37.44% latency reduction for attention and 15.89% for MLP, with up to 7.91% reduction per decoder layer, while Pearson Correlation Coefficient (PCC) remains above 98.75%, confirming significant end-to-end efficiency gains under numerical consistency.

</details>


### [117] [Hyperparameter Learning for Latent Factorization of Tensors for Representation Learning to Large-scale Dynamic Weighted Directed Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09880)
*Yaqian Zhan,Jialan He,Tianzhu Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Automates hyperparameter tuning for latent factorization of tensors (LFT) in dynamic weighted directed networks using Differential Evolution; improves MAE/RMSE vs manual tuning on four real datasets; reduces tuning effort.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hyperparameter selection in LFT is computationally expensive and often manual; automatic optimization of regularization parameters aims to enhance predictive performance and efficiency.

Method: Incorporates Differential Evolution into LFT training to optimize three regularization parameters (λ1, λ2, λ3); DE searches the hyperparameter space during model training, evaluating candidates via predictive error (MAE/RMSE) on validation data.

Result: DE-LFT achieves lower MAE and RMSE compared to manually tuned baselines across four real-world datasets; demonstrates reduced need for extensive parameter tuning.

Conclusion: An automated, DE-based hyperparameter optimization framework is effective for LFT in DWDNs, enabling adaptive hyperparameter search and improved predictive accuracy while saving tuning resources.

Abstract: Large-scale dynamic weighted directed networks (DWDNs) are widely used to model time-varying interactions among nodes. Latent factorization of tensors (LFT) extracts target knowledge from DWDNs via low-rank embedding. However, similar to many machine learning models, the performance of LFT heavily depends on the selection of hyperparameters. In practice, these parameters are often tuned manually or through grid search, which requires significant computational resources and human effort. Motivated by this challenge, this paper proposes an automated hyperparameter optimization framework based on Differential Evolution (DE) for LFT (DE-LFT). The proposed method integrates DE into the training process of the LFT model to automatically learn optimal regularization parameters $λ_1$, $λ_2$ and $λ_3$. As a result, the model can adaptively search the hyperparameter space and improve prediction accuracy. Experimental results on four real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves lower MAE and RMSE compared with manually tuned baselines while reducing the need for extensive parameter tuning.

</details>


### [118] [Toward Calibrated, Fair, and accurate Deepfake Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09881)
*Ryan Brown,Chris Russell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A label-free, plug-and-play fairness framework for deepfake detectors that uses a lightweight Face-Feature Tuning calibrator on frozen embeddings to reduce demographic performance gaps, with two variants (FF-Max and FF-Discover) to optimize worst-group accuracy without requiring identity attributes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deepfake detectors exhibit fairness gaps across demographic groups. Existing fairness methods often require demographic labels, retraining, or degrade accuracy. A demographic-label-free, low-overhead mitigation is desired.

Method: Introduce Face-Fairness (FF) with Face-Feature Tuning (FFT) that performs logit remapping conditioned on frozen face embeddings. FF-Max maximizes worst-group accuracy when demographics are known; FF-Discover does so with embedding-discovered groups. The method is plug-and-play, detector-agnostic, with negligible runtime, and does not access identity attributes.

Result: In-domain and cross-dataset experiments show FF reduces FPR/TPR gaps, improves minimum group accuracy, and preserves or improves overall accuracy across detectors and datasets.

Conclusion: FF offers a practical, label-free fairness solution for deepfake detection that is broadly applicable, minimally invasive, and compatible with existing detectors.

Abstract: Deepfake detectors show large performance gaps across demographic groups. Existing fairness approaches require demographic labels, retraining, or sacrifice accuracy. We introduce Face-Fairness (FF), a plug-and-play framework for bias mitigation. Our primary contribution, Face-Feature Tuning (FFT), is the first demographic label-free fairness method demonstrated for deepfake detection: a lightweight calibrator that performs a logit remapping conditioned on frozen face embeddings. We complement FFT with two variants: FF-Max, which maximizes worst-group accuracy when demographics are available, and FF-Discover, which does the same with embedding-discovered groups. Across in-domain and cross-dataset test settings, FF consistently reduces FPR/TPR gaps and improves minimum group accuracy while maintaining (often improving) overall accuracy. The approach is detector-agnostic, adds negligible runtime overhead, and requires no access to identity attributes.

</details>


### [119] [TD-Grokking: Learning from Zero-Reward Problems by Training-Time Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09883)
*Ningyuan Xi,Hao Xu,Hongsheng Xin,Ning Miao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Training-time decomposition (TD-Grokking) converts zero-reward RL tasks into solvable subproblems via hierarchical decomposition, yielding reliable gains over GRPO and baselines on math and medical tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLVR-based learning fails on highly challenging zero-reward problems where all sampled trajectories yield no signal; existing methods are constrained and do not equip the policy to solve the original intractable tasks.

Method: Recursively decompose intractable root problems into self-contained, verifiable subproblems to form hierarchical trees; solvable leaves provide non-zero rewards; training-time decomposition generates useful training signals from otherwise zero-reward examples.

Result: TD-Grokking outperforms vanilla GRPO and all baselines; demonstrates that training-time decomposition converts zero-reward examples into usable training signals, yielding consistent performance gains on mathematical and medical tasks.

Conclusion: Training-time decomposition effectively addresses zero-reward issues, enabling reliable performance improvements; code and datasets are publicly available.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have made remarkable progress in reasoning tasks, largely driven by post-training paradigms, especially reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). However, a critical bottleneck persists: RLVR fails on highly challenging zero-reward problems, where all sampled reasoning trajectories yield uniformly failed outcomes, providing no optimization signal to drive model improvement. Prior efforts to address this limitation, such as dense process supervision, partial reward assignment, or prefix-guided exploration, suffer from inherent task constraints or do not fully equip the policy model with the capabilities necessary to solve the original intractable problems. To address this, we propose TD-Grokking, a training-time decomposition framework for zero-reward problems. It recursively decomposes intractable root problems into self-contained, verifiable subproblems, forming hierarchical trees where solvable leaves provide non-zero rewards. Evaluations on mathematical and medical tasks show that TD-Grokking outperforms vanilla GRPO as well as all baseline approaches. Together with detailed analysis, these results confirm that training-time decomposition effectively converts zero-reward examples into usable training signals, enabling consistent performance gains. Our code and datasets are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TD-Grokking-6567/.

</details>


### [120] [TENP: Trapezoidal Expert Neuron Pruning For Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09885)
*Jiangyang He,Shaolin Zhu,Deyi Xiong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TENP introduces a structured pruning framework for MoE LLMs that uses a trapezoidal pruning pattern across layers and expert neuron pruning (ENP) within less-important experts. It identifies important experts with a sample-based criterion that combines output magnitude and directional change, then prunes neurons within the rest. This yields significant parameter reductions with minimal accuracy loss: 1-point drop on DeepSeek with 40% routing sparsity and ~64% active parameters, plus a 10% win on code generation versus full-parameter baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE LLMs scale via sparse activation but carry a large static parameter footprint in experts. Existing compression either eliminates entire experts (disrupting routing) or relies on unstructured pruning (limited practical gains). There is a need for structured, hardware-friendly pruning that preserves routing while reducing parameters.

Method: Sample-based identification of important experts based on both the magnitude of the expert output and its ability to alter the input direction. Retain these important experts; apply expert neuron pruning (ENP) to less important experts by pruning neurons with low projected contribution to the expert output. Reserve model parameters in a trapezoidal pattern from shallow to deep layers, enforcing a structured distribution of remaining parameters across the network.

Result: Empirical evaluation on Qwen and DeepSeek. With 40% routing expert sparsity and ~63.76% activated parameters, DeepSeek shows only a 1-point accuracy drop vs full-parameter; additionally, it outperforms the full-parameter model by 10% on code generation tasks.

Conclusion: TENP demonstrates that structured trapezoidal pruning combined with ENP can substantially reduce parameter footprint in Mixture-of-Experts LLMs while preserving or surpassing full-parameter accuracy on certain tasks, indicating practical viability for efficient deployment.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts large language models (LLMs) scale efficiently through sparse activation, yet their deployment is fundamentally constrained by the large static parameter footprint of experts. Existing compression approaches either remove entire experts, disrupting routing topology and harming performance, or rely on unstructured weight pruning with limited practical efficiency. To address the limitations, we propose TENP, a structured Trapezoidal ExpertNeuron Pruning framework. Using a few samples, we identify and retain important experts, while applying expert neuron pruning (ENP) to less important experts, reserving model parameters in a trapezoidal pattern from shallow to deep layers. When evaluating expert importance, we jointly consider both the magnitude of the expert output and its ability to change the direction of the input vector. For ENP, we measure each neuron's projected contribution to the expert output to identify and retain important neurons. We conduct extensive experiments on the Qwen and DeepSeek models. Under a routing expert sparsity of 40% and an average of 63.76% activated expert parameters, the DeepSeek model suffers only a 1-point drop in accuracy compared to the full-parameter model. Moreover, it outperforms the full-parameter model by 10% on code generation tasks.

</details>


### [121] [SHAPE: Coalition-Aware Expert Pruning for Sparse Mixture-of-Experts LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09886)
*Yuhao Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Presents SHAPE, a Shapley-based intra-layer cooperative pruning of Mixture-of-Experts to identify essential expert combinations, maintaining performance with reduced memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE models require keeping the full expert pool; existing pruning is inadequate due to top-k coalitions; need to account for interactions between experts and preserve topology under budget.

Method: Calibrate on a small set; trace routing decisions; compute Shapley-style attribution over observed top-k coalitions; select minimal subset to cover alpha of non-negative Shapley mass per layer; apply bisection to hit target keep rate.

Result: Empirical results across three backbones show SHAPE yields robustness gains vs global and layer-wise pruning; maintains accuracy under 20-40% pruning without extra training; reduces peak GPU memory.

Conclusion: SHAPE enables task-driven, interaction-aware MoE pruning by modeling intra-layer cooperation via cooperative game theory; achieves memory reductions with minimal accuracy loss; code released.

Abstract: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) large language models achieve strong quality with low per-token compute, yet their deployment is often limited by the memory wall: the full expert pool must remain resident to support token-dependent routing. Expert pruning is a direct remedy, but prior criteria typically score experts independently and overlook that MoE inference is inherently \emph{coalitional}, where outputs arise from routed top-$k$ expert combinations. We propose \textbf{SHAPE}, a task-driven pruning framework that explicitly models \emph{intra-layer} expert cooperation. SHAPE formulates routing traces on a small calibration set as an empirical cooperative game and assigns interaction-aware expert values via a Shapley-style attribution over observed top-$k$ coalitions, enabling the identification of experts that are essential for high-utility collaborations rather than merely frequent. To preserve MoE topology under a global pruning budget, SHAPE further introduces a \emph{quality-coverage} selection rule that retains, in each layer, the minimal expert subset covering an $α$ fraction of non-negative Shapley mass, while using bisection to match a target keep rate. Experiments on three modern MoE backbones (Qwen3-30B-A3B, GPT-OSS-20B, and DeepSeek-V2-Lite) across diverse benchmarks show that SHAPE consistently improves robustness over global and layer-wise pruning variants, maintaining competitive accuracy under 20\% and 40\% expert pruning without additional training and delivering clear reductions in peak GPU memory footprint. The open-source code is available at https://github.com/Alizen-1009/Shapley-Moe.

</details>


### [122] [SocraticPO: Policy Optimization via Interactive Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09887)
*Zirui Liu,Jie Ouyang,Qi Liu,Xianquan Wang,Jiayu Liu,Tingyue Pan,Qingchuan Li,Jing Sha,Zhenya Huang,Shijin Wang,Enhong Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SocraticPO augments RL with Socratic-style, text-based guidance during rollout, using teacher-provided corrective feedback with reward decay to keep the standard objective intact; it plugs into policy-gradient methods and improves reasoning benchmarks using black-box teachers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scalar rewards in RL for LLMs lack explanatory feedback, often leading to shortcut learning and brittle policies. A method that provides interpretable, corrective guidance during training can steer reasoning and reduce such failures.

Method: In rollout, the student first answers independently. If incorrect, a teacher diagnoses the attempt and provides concise corrective guidance in natural language, after which the student continues with expanded context. The reward for post-correction correct answers is decayed, preventing the guidance from creating a free reward path. The approach preserves the standard RL objective and can be plugged into existing policy-gradient backends; teachers are text-only, enabling use of powerful black-box models without access to logits.

Result: On SciKnowEval undergraduate-level scientific reasoning benchmarks, SocraticPO outperforms strong RL and self-distillation baselines. Ablations indicate both targeted, textual guidance and reward decay are necessary, with reward decay reducing reliance on assisted correction.

Conclusion: SocraticPO provides a lightweight, plug-in enhancement to RL for LLMs by injecting interpretable, step-by-step guidance during training while maintaining the original reward objective; it improves reasoning tasks and mitigates shortcut learning, leveraging black-box teachers.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models usually supervises reasoning with scalar outcome rewards, such as binary correctness. Such rewards provide an optimization direction but rarely explain how a model should revise its mistaken reasoning, which can encourage shortcut learning and brittle policies. We propose \textbf{SocraticPO} (Socratic Policy Optimization), a policy-optimization framework that augments RL rollouts with Socratic-style natural-language guidance. During rollout, the student first answers independently; if the answer is incorrect, a teacher diagnoses the attempt and provides concise corrective guidance, after which the student continues under the expanded context. Crucially, this guidance is paired with reward decay: correct answers obtained after teacher intervention only receive decayed rewards, preventing the policy from treating teacher help as a free path to reward. Since SocraticPO only modifies the rollout process while leaving the standard expected-reward objective intact, it can be plugged into existing policy-gradient backends such as Reinforce++. Moreover, because the teacher provides only text-level guidance, SocraticPO can leverage stronger black-box teacher models without requiring access to logits or distribution matching. On undergraduate-level scientific reasoning benchmarks from SciKnowEval, SocraticPO improves over strong RL and self-distillation baselines. Ablations show that both targeted guidance and reward decay are necessary, with reward decay mitigating reliance on assisted correction.

</details>


### [123] [SinkRec: Mitigating Semantic State Sink in Long Sequence Recommendation with Memory-Conditioned Gated Delta Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09888)
*Zhuang Zhuang,Zhipeng Wei,Ji Dai,Jie Chen,Fei Pan,Peng Jiang,Kun Gai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SinkRec tackles semantic state sink in linear-attention long-sequence models by externalizing recurring semantics into a learnable conditional memory with residual vector quantization, and by a TDGD module to purify updates. This decouples pattern storage from dynamic transitions, restoring linear-time efficiency while improving recommendation performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In linear attention, compressed recurrent state can be overwhelmed by repetitive, recurring semantics, biasing reads and hindering dynamic pattern modeling (semantic state sink).

Method: Proposes SinkRec, a hybrid memory-transition looped architecture that externalizes recurring local patterns into a learnable conditional memory via residual vector quantization, reinjects retrieved codes, and exposes memory key-value pairs to the attention block. Also introduces Temporal-Aware State-Relation Differential Gated DeltaNet (TDGD), which uses memory to purify recurrent writes/reads by suppressing memory-covered updates and removing memory-aligned readout responses, turning recurring semantics into memory-retrievable patterns.

Result: Empirical evaluation on public and industrial datasets shows SinkRec achieves effective long-sequence recommendations with improved efficiency, demonstrating practical benefits of decoupling pattern storage from dynamic transition modeling while maintaining linear-time complexity.

Conclusion: By converting recurring semantics into memory-retrievable patterns and focusing recurrence on dynamic transitions, SinkRec alleviates semantic state sink and preserves linear-time efficiency in long-sequence recommendation.

Abstract: Linear attention provides an efficient backbone for long-sequence recommendation by avoiding the quadratic cost of standard Transformers, but its compressed recurrent state can be dominated by repetitive behavior patterns. We identify this phenomenon as semantic state sink, where recurring semantics over-occupy the recurrent state and bias subsequent readouts. To mitigate semantic state sink, we propose SinkRec, a hybrid memory-transition looped architecture that decouples collaborative behavioral pattern storage from dynamic transition modeling. SinkRec externalizes recurring local patterns into a learnable conditional memory through residual vector quantization, reinjects the retrieved codes, and exposes memory key-value pairs to the attention block. It further introduces Temporal-Aware State-Relation Differential Gated DeltaNet (TDGD), which uses memory to purify recurrent writing and reading by suppressing memory-covered updates and removing memory-aligned readout responses. This design turns recurring semantics from state-competing signals into memory-retrievable patterns, allowing the recurrent state to focus on dynamic transitions and alleviating semantic state sink with linear-time efficiency. Experiments on public and industrial datasets demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of SinkRec.

</details>


### [124] [Optuna Constrained Tree-Structured Parzen Estimator Is a Joint Density Generalization of c-TPE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09889)
*Shuhei Watanabe,Kaichi Irie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Optuna's constrained TPE is equivalent to a joint constrained TPE (c-TPE) using a joint likelihood for ECI; independent c-TPE degrades with constraint duplication, while joint c-TPE remains invariant to duplication.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify the algorithmic properties of constrained hyperparameter optimization methods, particularly how different ECI formulations (joint vs independent) affect robustness to constraint duplication and overall performance.

Method: Theoretical analysis showing Optuna's constrained TPE corresponds to a joint ECI with a joint density; compare joint vs independent ECI formulations; prove invariance of joint c-TPE to constraint duplication; discuss practical tradeoffs and implications.

Result: Joint c-TPE is invariant to constraint duplication, whereas independent c-TPE loses performance as duplicated constraint factors accumulate; analysis highlights core tradeoffs between formulations.

Conclusion: Choose between joint and independent ECI formulations based on duplication robustness and modeling assumptions; provide directions for empirical evaluation and guidelines for practitioners.

Abstract: Constrained hyperparameter optimization (HPO) is common in practice, yet Optuna's widely used constrained TPE lacks algorithmic analysis. While c-TPE proposes an expected constrained improvement (ECI) approach assuming independence between the objective and constraints, Optuna uses a single joint density over both. We show that Optuna's constrained TPE is joint c-TPE -- the same ECI acquisition function using a joint likelihood. We demonstrate joint c-TPE is invariant to constraint duplication whereas independent c-TPE degrades as the product accumulates duplicated factors. We outline practical tradeoffs between the formulations and directions for future study.

</details>


### [125] [PreAct-Bench: Benchmarking Predictive Monitoring in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09890)
*Hainiu Xu,Italo Luis da Silva,Jiangnan Ye,Yuhao Wang,Wei Liu,Linyi Yang,Jonathan Richard Schwarz,Nicola Paoletti,Yulan He,Hanqi Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Predictive Monitoring for LLM safety: inferring whether a partial action trajectory will lead to unethical outcomes before execution. Introduces PreActBench (1,000 paired trajectories across five domains) and evaluates LLMs and safety tools using Prefix Foresight F1; humans perform well, models struggle, highlighting the need for future-oriented risk reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the retrospective nature of current safety evaluations by enabling proactive risk assessment on partial trajectories to prevent unethical actions before they occur.

Method: Introduce PreActBench with 1,000 paired ethical/unethical action trajectories across five domains. Evaluate multiple LLMs, safety guardrails, and latent probing methods across different trajectory fractions using a new Prefix Foresight F1 metric.

Result: Humans show promising performance in predictive monitoring, but even strong models struggle to reliably predict future unethical outcomes from partial trajectories, indicating the task's difficulty and the need for improved forward-looking risk reasoning.

Conclusion: Calls for development of proactive safety mechanisms, benchmarks, and methods to enable predictive monitoring, enabling earlier intervention before unethical actions manifest.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents capable of executing multi-step action trajectories toward a given objective. While existing safety research has focused on detecting unethical behavior from complete trajectories, this paradigm is fundamentally retrospective: it identifies harm only after it has already occurred. In this work, we study a critical yet overlooked safety task, which we term Predictive Monitoring: given only a partial action trajectory, can a model infer whether it will culminate in an unethical action before the overt action is executed? To support this task, we present PreActBench, a benchmark of 1,000 paired ethical and unethical action trajectories spanning five domains. We evaluate a range of LLMs, safety guardrail models, and latent probing methods across varying fractions of the action trajectory using our Prefix Foresight F1 metric. Results show that while humans achieve promising performance, predictive monitoring remains challenging even for strong models, highlighting the need for future-oriented risk reasoning in LLM safety.

</details>


### [126] [Representation Curriculum: Stagewise Training for Robust Ranking and Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09891)
*Ehsan Ebrahimzadeh,Sina Baharlouei,Abraham Bagherjeiran*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A training-time Representation Curriculum (RC) staggers feature usage to reduce reliance on exposure-confounded signals in ranking, improving cold-start generalization and robustness, with theoretical guarantees in a Gaussian ridge setting and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exposure-confounded signals (popularity, CTR/CVR aggregates, ID-based representations) power predictions under stationarity but create learning shortcuts that entrench incumbents and degrade cold-start generalization under distribution shift.

Method: Introduce Representation Curriculum (RC): a training-time intervention that temporally stages feature utilization—first foregrounds content-based merit signals, then gradually incorporates exposure-dependent belief signals while anchoring the content pathway near the merit representation. Formalization is task-agnostic with ranking-specific instantiations. In a Gaussian linear ridge setting, derive closed-form solutions and sufficiency conditions showing RC reduces population risk on cold-start distributions, with a Pareto trade-off against source performance.

Result: RC, both theoretically and empirically, shifts model reliance away from historical exposure signals toward content-based merit signals. The theory yields conditions under which cold-start risk is reduced with a quantified trade-off in head performance; experiments on public LTR and recommender benchmarks and randomized online experiments in a large-scale e-commerce search system show consistent gains on cold populations with controlled head performance impact.

Conclusion: Representation Curriculum offers a general, training-time mechanism to mitigate shortcut learning from exposure-dependent signals in ranking, improving cold-start robustness and distribution-shift resilience. It provides theoretical guarantees in a Gaussian ridge setting and practical validation across datasets and a live system.

Abstract: Ranking in digital marketplaces is a dynamic exposure-allocation mechanism: displayed items shape discovery trajectories and success events logged by the platform to update future allocation policies. Modern ranking systems rely heavily on exposure-confounded signals (e.g. popularity estimates, CTR/CVR aggregates, and ID-based representation), because they are highly predictive under stationary demand. Yet this predictive power can become a learning shortcut: early access to exposure-dependent belief signals steers optimization toward over-reliance on them and away from exposure-independent merit signals (e.g., content-based competitiveness and semantic affinity). Consequently, the learned policy tends to entrench incumbents and degrade cold-start generalization and robustness under distribution shift. We propose Representation Curriculum (RC), a training-time intervention that temporally stages feature utilization. RC foregrounds content-based merit signals initially, then introduces exposure-dependent belief signals while anchoring the content pathway near the learned merit representation, curbing shortcut reliance on historical signals and mitigating gradient starvation on content signals. We formalize RC independently of task and hypothesis class and provide ranking-specific instantiations. In a Gaussian linear ridge setting, we derive closed-form solutions and sufficient conditions under which RC strictly reduces population risk on a cold-start target distribution, with a quantified Pareto tradeoff against source performance. Experiments on public learning-to-rank and recommendation benchmarks, and randomized online experiments in a large-scale e-commerce search system, show that RC measurably shifts reliance from historical belief signals toward content-based merit signals and yields consistent gains on cold populations with a controlled trade-off in head performance.

</details>


### [127] [LMT: A Bayesian Framework for Causal Discovery from Textual Alarm Records in Manufacturing Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09892)
*Xiaofeng Xiao,Jianhong Chen,Qiuzhuang Sun,Naichen Shi,Xubo Yue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LMT combines LLM-extracted textual semantics with a Poisson-based temporal model in a Bayesian framework to discover causal graphs from engineering alarm-event data, performing well in small samples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Textual descriptions hint at causality but are insufficient and may mimic correlations; need to integrate textual signals with timestamps to infer causal structure robustly in engineering contexts.

Method: 1) Use LLMs to extract semantic causal signals from event descriptions and form a prior over event-type/cluster causal graphs; 2) Use a Poisson-process-based likelihood on event timestamps to update/refine the prior; 3) Bayesian inference yields a joint posterior causal graph that is interpretable and data-supported.

Result: Simulation studies demonstrate effectiveness across varying settings, with particular advantage in small-sample alarm-event scenarios.

Conclusion: Integrating textual semantics and temporal evidence yields robust, interpretable causal graphs; LMT is effective for engineering event data and accessible in data-sparse regimes.

Abstract: Textual event records, such as alarm logs, have become an increasingly common data source in engineering and manufacturing systems. Beyond identifying correlations or recurring patterns, engineers are often interested in understanding which types of events causally trigger or influence other events during system operation. Textual event descriptions may contain semantic clues about such causal relationships, and recent large language models (LLMs) provide a promising tool for extracting these signals. However, relying solely on LLM-encoded textual information is insufficient for accurate causal discovery, since semantic patterns do not directly reveal causal mechanisms and may confuse causation with correlation or frequent sequential patterns. To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{LMT}, a Bayesian causal discovery framework for engineering event data that jointly leverages textual descriptions and timestamps. Specifically, LMT first uses LLMs to extract semantic causal signals from event descriptions and constructs a prior distribution over causal graphs among event types or event clusters.
