It then incorporates temporal evidence through a Poisson-process-based likelihood, allowing the LLM-informed prior to be refined by timestamp-based statistical evidence. By integrating the textual and temporal information, LMT produces a causal graph that is both interpretable and data-supported. Simulation studies show that the proposed framework is effective across different settings and is especially advantageous in small-sample alarm-event scenarios.

</details>


### [128] [A Navigable Manifold of Hypothesized Consciousness-Spectrum States in Language Model Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09894)
*Sophie Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer embeddings form a structured, navigable manifold aligned with a consciousness-spectrum taxonomy, with convex high/low regions and a transition corridor; trajectories move from low to high regions, suggesting intrinsic navigability for model behavior guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether language-model representations encode a human-interpretable spectrum of conscious states and how this geometry could inform guiding, evaluating, and aligning models.

Method: Geometric analysis of transformer embedding spaces across sentences, clustering to identify regions along the spectrum, metrics for convexity-like stability in high- and low-level regions, identification of a transition corridor, and tracing dynamic trajectories (utility-guided and geometry-only greedy) from lower- to higher-level regions.

Result: Embeddings exhibit globally organized geometry aligned with the spectrum; sentences with similar states cluster locally; high- and low-level regions show convexity-like stability; an intermediate transition corridor exists; both utility-guided and geometry-only greedy trajectories traverse from lower- to higher-level regions through intermediate tiers, indicating intrinsic navigability in the representation space.

Conclusion: Embedding spaces encode structured and navigable geometry aligned with a consciousness-spectrum taxonomy, offering a representation-level perspective for analyzing and guiding model behavior and potentially informing alignment strategies.

Abstract: Across contemplative, philosophical, and psychological accounts, human consciousness is often described along a similar spectrum, ranging from reactive and self-focused patterns to more integrative and coherent ones. Understanding whether language models encode such a structured, human-interpretable consciousness spectrum in representation space is important for model guidance, evaluation and alignment. In this work, we study the geometric structure and dynamics of patterns along this spectrum in transformer embedding spaces. We show that embeddings exhibit a globally organized geometry aligned with this spectrum: sentences associated with similar states cluster into locally coherent regions, forming a structured manifold. In particular, higher-level and lower-level regions exhibit convexity-like stability, while intermediate regions form a transition corridor. Dynamically, both utility-guided and geometry-only greedy trajectories consistently traverse from lower- to higher-level regions, passing through intermediate tiers, indicating that navigability is an intrinsic property of the representation space, guided but not dictated by a global directional signal. These results suggest that embedding spaces encode structured and navigable geometry aligned with a hypothesized consciousness-spectrum taxonomy, broadly inspired by recurring structural descriptions of human consciousness across contemplative traditions, philosophy, and modern psychology, providing a representation-level perspective for analyzing and guiding model behavior.

</details>


### [129] [TRAPS: Therapeutic Response Analysis via Pathway-informed Stratification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09898)
*Sujoy Banik,Sayantan Chakraborty,Boishakhi Das Toma,Zainab Ghafoor,Ushashi Bhattacharjee,Koushik Howlader,Tirtho Roy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified benchmark for pathway-guided cancer therapy modeling shows architecture-specific strengths: PATH excels in targeted molecular therapy, BINN in survival prediction, while radiation therapy remains challenging; GraphPath achieves a top AUROC of 0.92 in prostate therapy under low prevalence, highlighting the value of lateral co-regulation structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable fair, cross-architecture evaluation of pathway-informed deep learning models across multiple clinical outcomes, and to treat pathway-structured DL as a combined therapy and survival prediction problem.

Method: Five TCGA cancer cohorts (n=2,622) encoded with Reactome pathway activity scores were used to evaluate three architectures (BINN, GraphPath, PATH). Models were trained jointly on three clinical outcomes under identical data and evaluation conditions, addressing multi-task learning with pathway-informed structures.

Result: No single architecture dominates all tasks. PATH is strongest for targeted molecular therapy prediction, BINN is most reliable for survival prediction, and radiation therapy prediction remains poor due to non-genomic clinical drivers. GraphPath achieves AUROC 0.92 for prostate targeted molecular therapy, indicating strong discriminative power when lateral co-regulation aligns with a driver-focused cohort (11% positive prevalence).

Conclusion: Pathway-structured DL models offer complementary strengths and can be jointly optimized for multi-outcome cancer therapy planning. Radiation decisions may require clinical variables beyond gene expression; unified benchmarks enable fair cross-architectural comparisons and reveal that co-regulation-informed architectures excel in specific cohorts.

Abstract: Cancer treatment planning requires decisions across multiple clinical dimensions at once. Clinicians must determine whether a patient should receive targeted molecular therapy, radiation therapy, and whether they are likely to survive beyond six months. Existing pathway-informed deep learning models have been developed and tested in isolation, making fair comparison across architectures impossible. We present the first unified benchmark for pathway-guided therapy response modeling, evaluating three biologically informed architectures, BINN, GraphPath, and PATH, across five cancer cohorts drawn from The Cancer Genome Atlas, representing 2,622 patients encoded using Reactome pathway activity scores. Each model is trained jointly on all three clinical outcomes under identical data and evaluation conditions, the first study to treat pathway-structured deep learning as a combined therapy and survival prediction problem. Our results show that no single architecture wins across all tasks: PATH performs best for targeted molecular therapy prediction overall, BINN is most reliable for survival prediction, and no model produces useful predictions for radiation therapy, as the key drivers of that decision are clinical variables not captured in gene expression data. Most strikingly, GraphPath achieves an AUROC of 0.92 on prostate targeted molecular therapy prediction, the highest score in the entire benchmark, demonstrating that lateral co-regulation structure produces exceptional discriminative power when matched to a cohort with a narrow targetable driver programme, even under conditions of extreme class imbalance at only 11\% positive prevalence.

</details>


### [130] [When Attribution Patching Lies: Diagnosis and a Second-Order Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09899)
*Luyang Zhang,Jialu Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Identifies non-linear down-stream network effects as the main source of unreliability in attribution patching, introduces Hessian-vector-product (HVP) correction and reliability metrics to enable scalable, higher-fidelity circuit identification, plus a Screen-Flag-Fix workflow that concentrates effort on unreliable components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mechanistic interpretability requires reliable causal attribution of model behavior. Attribution patching (gradient-based) is cheaper than activation patching but its reliability is unclear; erroneous attributions can misidentify the underlying circuits. The work aims to diagnose, quantify, and correct this unreliability at scale.

Method: Analyze sources of error in attribution patching, showing the dominant contribution from downstream non-linearities rather than local curvature. Propose three tools: (1) a reliability score for untrustworthy estimates, (2) error bounds for attribution mis-specification, (3) a Hessian-vector-product (HVP) correction that removes leading-order error with a single extra backward pass. Evaluate across five model families (124M–9B) with random-token and name-swap perturbations; compare multi-step HVP to Integrated Gradients and prior second-order baselines; demonstrate improved circuit recovery and a scalable workflow.

Result: HVP eliminates the leading-order error with one additional backward pass and, in multi-step form, matches or surpasses Integrated Gradients at lower compute. HVP is the only second-order correction feasible at large scale among baselines considered. The approach yields higher-fidelity circuit recovery on standard benchmarks and enables a Screen-Flag-Fix workflow that focuses compute on unreliable components.

Conclusion: Second-order correction via Hessian-vector products provides a scalable, reliable enhancement to attribution-based mechanistic interpretability, enabling more accurate circuit identification and a practical workflow that allocates resources where uncertainty is highest.

Abstract: A central goal of mechanistic interpretability is to identify which internal components causally drive a language model's behavior. Because these importance estimates serve as the evidence for identifying circuits, systematic errors can lead to the misidentification of the underlying mechanisms. While activation patching provides a gold-standard causal metric, its computational cost is prohibitive at scale. Practitioners instead rely on attribution patching, a gradient-based, first-order approximation whose reliability remains poorly understood. In this work, we characterize the source of this unreliability, demonstrating that the dominant error stems from the non-linearities in the downstream network rather than local curvature at the patched component. This insight yields three practical tools: (i) a reliability score to detect untrustworthy estimates, (ii) error bounds quantifying potential attribution mis-specifications, and (iii) a Hessian-vector-product (HVP) correction that eliminates the leading-order error with only one additional backward pass. In evaluations across five model families (124M-9B parameters) and both random-token and naturalistic (name-swap) perturbations, HVP is the only second-order correction feasible at larger scale, where standard baselines like Integrated Gradients become computationally prohibitive. In comparative experiments, a multi-step HVP variant matches or exceeds the accuracy of Integrated Gradients at significantly lower compute, outperforming prior second-order baselines. These improvements lead to higher-fidelity circuit recovery on standard benchmarks and support a Screen-Flag-Fix workflow that targets computational effort only toward the components flagged as unreliable.

</details>


### [131] [LongMoE: Longitudinal Multimodal Learning via Trajectory-Aware Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09907)
*Maxx Richard Rahman,Prakhar Kumar,Wolfgang Maass*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LongMoE is a unified longitudinal multimodal framework that addresses missing modalities and irregular visit dynamics via context-aware imputation, frequency-domain temporal tokenization, a trajectory-aware encoder, and context-conditioned sparse MoE routing; it achieves robustness to missing data and maintains performance in full-modality settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle two core challenges in multimodal clinical learning: (i) modality missingness at patient visits, and (ii) longitudinal dynamics where diagnostic signals depend on disease trajectory. Existing work treats these issues separately (static missing-modality snapshots or fully-present modalities with no temporal context).

Method: LongMoE architecture combines: (1) a context-aware imputation module to fill missing modalities, (2) an attentional tokenization module that captures irregular visit sequences via frequency-domain temporal patterns, (3) a trajectory-aware encoder for disease progression modeling, and (4) context-conditioned Sparse Mixture-of-Experts routing to select patient-specific experts.

Result: Empirical evaluation on ADNI, OASIS-3, and MIMIC-IV shows improved robustness when modalities are missing or weak, while preserving competitive performance in full-modality scenarios.

Conclusion: A unified longitudinal multimodal learning framework effectively handles both missing data and temporal dynamics, yielding robust performance and setting a strong foundation for longitudinal multimodal clinical learning.

Abstract: Multimodal clinical learning is increasingly important for integrating diverse patient data, including imaging, text, and personalised health records. However, it faces two fundamental challenges: i) modality missingness, where arbitrary subsets of modalities are unavailable at a given patient visit, ii) longitudinal dynamics, where the diagnostic significance of an observation depends on the patient's evolving disease trajectory over time. Existing methods address these challenges in isolation: missing-modality frameworks treat each visit as an independent static snapshot and discard temporal context, while longitudinal models often assume complete modality availability and degrade under systematic modality incompleteness. We propose LongMoE (Longitudinal Mixture-of-Experts), the unified framework to jointly address both challenges. LongMoE combines a context-aware imputation module with an attentional tokenization module that captures frequency-domain temporal patterns across irregular visit sequences, a trajectory-aware encoder for modeling disease progression, and context-conditioned Sparse MoE routing for patient-specific expert selection. Experiments on ADNI, OASIS-3, and MIMIC-IV show that LongMoE improves robustness under missing or weak contemporaneous modalities and remains competitive in full-modality settings, establishing a strong foundation for longitudinally-aware multimodal clinical learning.

</details>


### [132] [Mix, Don't Pick: Why Synthetic Corpus Composition Matters for Time Series Foundation Model Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09912)
*Aaryan Nagpal,Debdeep Sanyal,Murari Mandal,Dhruv Kumar,Saurabh Deshpande*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An equal-weight mixture of all synthetic time-series generators matches or beats the best single generator across architectures, and combining this mixture with real data yields the strongest pretraining corpora. Synthetic pretraining should be treated as a corpus composition problem, with model-family-specific validation rather than assuming transfer of optimal generators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Choosing a synthetic data generator for time-series foundation-model pretraining is costly and brittle: large gaps in forecasting error under identical budgets, and generator rankings that vary across architectures. A principled method for generator selection is missing.

Method: Evaluated 11 generator families on Chronos-T5-Mini and Moirai-Small trained from scratch. Compared individual generators vs an equal-weight mixture of all generators, and also mixtures with real data. Assessed across architectures to test stability of generator usefulness.

Result: The equal-weight mixture of generators matches or exceeds the best individual generator for both Chronos-T5-Mini and Moirai-Small. When this mixture is combined with real data, it yields the strongest pretraining corpora overall.

Conclusion: Synthetic pretraining is a corpus composition problem, not a generator selection problem. Composition choices should be validated per model family rather than assumed to transfer across architectures; an equal-weight mixture of generators, possibly with real data, provides robust benefits across model families.

Abstract: Choosing the wrong synthetic generator for time-series foundation model pretraining is costly: under identical training budgets, the best and worst generators produce up to a $2\times$ gap in forecasting error, yet the field has no principled way to make this choice. The problem is compounded by the fact that generator rankings are not stable across architectures: across 11 generator families evaluated on Chronos-T5-Mini and Moirai-Small trained from scratch, we find that which generators are useful depends on the model architecture. Rather than solving the generator selection problem, we sidestep it: a simple equal-weight mixture of all generators matches or beats the best individual generator for both architectures, and composing this mixture with real data yields the strongest pretraining corpora overall. Synthetic pretraining is therefore a corpus composition problem, not a generator selection problem, and composition choices should be validated per model family rather than assumed to transfer.

</details>


### [133] [IntentKV: Cross-Turn Intent-Aware KV Cache Pruning for Agent Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09916)
*Junjie Li,Jiong Lou,Jie Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IntentKV introduces learned KV pruning to reduce memory and read bandwidth for long-horizon LLM agents, maintaining accuracy with a frozen base model and a session-level QueryMemory. It uses memory-attention to score history tokens and a residual cross-attention head, with slot-map eviction to preserve compatibility with existing caches. It achieves near-full-cache performance at modest KV budgets, with substantial reductions in peak tokens and raw KV reads on large models and long queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon LLM agents experience explosive growth in key-value memory and read bandwidth as trajectories unfold. The KV cache becomes the dominant bottleneck, limiting scalability and latency when handling long histories.

Method: Freeze the base LLM. Introduce IntentKV with: (1) a session-level QueryMemory capturing cross-turn intent, (2) a memory-attention rule to score live history tokens, (3) a zero-initialized residual head that cross-attends over current-query K-Vs, and (4) an eviction scheme based on slot-map redirection where discarded positions map to a sentinel dead slot, preserving surviving K/V rows, RoPE phases, and slot identities. This preserves compatibility with existing prefix caches while pruning KV usage.

Result: On 8k KV budgets, mean peak request tokens drop by 23.9% (Qwen3-8B) and 30.7% (Qwen2.5-14B). For the 100 longest BCP queries on Qwen2.5-14B, worst-case peak request tokens drop from 92.3k to 20.5k (77.8% reduction) and worst-case raw KV reads from 411M to 31M (92.6% reduction), with near-parity to the no-pruning full-cache baseline.

Conclusion: Learned KV pruning via IntentKV yields substantial KV-bottleneck reductions with minimal accuracy loss, enabling near-baseline performance under constrained KV budgets for long-horizon LLM agents. The approach shows strong efficiency gains on large models and long queries, though details on generalization, latency, and broader applicability warrant further study.

Abstract: Multi-turn LLM agents fan short queries into long trajectories of tool calls, search results, and intermediate reasoning. Both KV memory and KV read bandwidth grow by orders of magnitude across a single trajectory, making the key-value (KV) cache, not parameter compute, the dominant serving bottleneck for long-horizon agents. We introduce IntentKV, learned KV pruning that keeps the base LLM frozen. IntentKV maintains a session-level QueryMemory of cross-turn intent, scores live history tokens with a memory-attention rule, and adds a zero-initialized residual head with cross-attention over current-query K-vectors. To stay composable with prefix caches, eviction is a slot-map redirection: dropped positions route to a sentinel dead slot while surviving K/V rows, RoPE phases, and slot identities stay in place. IntentKV matches the no-pruning full-cache baseline with almost no accuracy drop under tight KV budgets: at an 8k KV budget, mean peak request tokens drop 23.9% on Qwen3-8B and 30.7% on Qwen2.5-14B. On the 100 longest BCP queries that all methods complete on Qwen2.5-14B, IntentKV-8k further cuts worst-case peak request tokens from 92.3k to 20.5k, a 77.8% reduction, and worst-case raw KV reads from 411M to 31M, a 92.6% reduction.

</details>


### [134] [SPDM: Geometry-Modulated State Space Modeling with Manifold Constraints for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09917)
*Xingsheng Chen,Siu-Ming Yiu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduce SPDM, a geometry-aware state-space model for multivariate time-series forecasting that treats cross-variable covariance as a continuous trajectory on the SPD manifold. It leverages Riemannian geometry, tangent-space projection, and a geometric gating mechanism to regularize and guide the SSM, preserving linear-time complexity. Achieves state-of-the-art results on eleven real-world datasets; geometry-constrained dynamics drive the gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing state-space models (SSMs) process time series by scanning tokenized temporal or spatial sequences, often ignoring the evolving geometric structure of cross-variable correlations. Modeling the cross-variable covariance trajectory on the SPD manifold provides principled geometric regularization to stabilize and improve forecasting.

Method: Propose SPDM architecture with two mechanisms: (1) a manifold trajectory path that projects evolving covariance matrices on the SPD manifold to a Euclidean tangent space, and (2) a geometric gating scheme that modulates SSM parameters based on geometric signals from the trajectory. The approach preserves linear-time complexity (like Mamba) while embedding geometric constraints.

Result: Extensive experiments on eleven real-world benchmark datasets show state-of-the-art forecasting performance. Ablation studies indicate that geometrically constrained state-space dynamics are the dominant factor behind the performance gains.

Conclusion: Geometric constraints on state-space dynamics yield improved forecasting accuracy and stability without sacrificing efficiency. Embedding manifold geometry into SSMs provides a principled and effective approach for multivariate time-series forecasting.

Abstract: Multivariate time series forecasting requires capturing the continuously evolving correlation structure among interacting variables. Existing state-space models process time series by scanning tokenized temporal or spatial sequences, discarding the evolutionary geometric structure. We address this limitation by introducing manifold constraints into state-space modeling: treating the cross-variable correlation structure as a continuous trajectory on the symmetric positive definite manifold, whose Riemannian geometric features, tangent space linearity, and Frechet mean centrality act as a principled geometric regularizer that guides and stabilizes the selective scanning dynamics of SSMs. We propose SPDM, a geometry-aware SSM architecture that realizes this principle through two cooperating mechanisms: a manifold trajectory path that projects dynamically evolving covariance matrices from the SPD manifold to a Euclidean tangent space, and a geometric gating scheme that directly modulates SSM's internal selective parameters based on geometric signals derived from the manifold trajectory. The parameterization preserves the linear-time complexity of the Mamba parallel scan while embedding rich structural constraints, making the architecture preserve prediction accuracy and computational efficiency simultaneously. Extensive experiments on eleven real-world benchmark datasets establish state-of-the-art forecasting performance, and further studies confirm that geometrically constrained state-space dynamics are the dominant architectural factor behind its performance gains.

</details>


### [135] [Co-GLANCE: Uncertainty-Aware Active Perception for Heterogeneous Robot Teaming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09919)
*Michal P. Podolinsky,Neel P. Bhatt,Pranay Samineni,Rohan Siva,Christian Ellis,Ufuk Topcu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A real-time onboard perception and decision-making system (Co-GLANCE) that distills vision-language model reasoning for occlusion segmentation and robot allocation, using conformal prediction with selective abstention for calibrated uncertainty, enabling active perception without cloud inference. It outperforms cloud baselines (25% better occlusion segmentation, 36% better robot allocation) and achieves 350x latency reduction, with a dataset released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Perceptual uncertainty from occlusions and varying scene structure hinders heterogeneous robot teams in unstructured outdoors. Cloud-based vision-language models offer strong priors but are computationally heavy and lack calibrated uncertainty, complicating real-time onboard decision-making and robot allocation.

Method: Onboard end-to-end model that distills semantic reasoning from a vision-language model into occlusion segmentation and robot allocation. Employs conformal prediction with selective abstention to provide statistically valid coverage and calibrated uncertainty. Triggers active perception by dispatching the most informative robot to acquire better viewpoints, eliminating cloud-based inference.

Result: Co-GLANCE achieves 25% higher occlusion segmentation accuracy and 36% higher robot allocation accuracy compared to cloud-based baselines, while reducing per-frame inference latency by 350x. Validated in real-world scenarios; releases an air-ground dataset and corresponding code/videos.

Conclusion: Co-GLANCE enables real-time, uncertainty-aware onboard perception and decision-making for heterogeneous robot teams, reducing reliance on cloud inference and providing calibrated uncertainty to drive active perception. The accompanying dataset (air-ground) facilitates future research.

Abstract: Perceptual uncertainty is a central challenge for heterogeneous robot teams operating in unstructured outdoor environments, where no single viewpoint affords reliable scene understanding. Perceptual uncertainty, arising from sources such as occlusions, manifests differently across robot viewpoints depending on scene structure. Detecting and resolving sources of perceptual uncertainty requires both scene-based contextual reasoning and capability-aware robot allocation. While vision-language models provide strong semantic priors for both, they are computationally prohibitive for onboard inference and lack calibrated uncertainty quantification. We introduce Co-GLANCE, a real-time onboard perception and decision-making system for uncertainty resolution in heterogeneous robot teams. Co-GLANCE distills the semantic reasoning capabilities of a vision-language model into an end-to-end model for occlusion segmentation and robot allocation, eliminating the need for cloud-based inference. To quantify perceptual uncertainty, Co-GLANCE combines conformal prediction with selective abstention to provide statistically valid coverage guarantees for segmentation, robot allocation, and detection outputs. These calibrated uncertainty estimates directly trigger active perception, dispatching the most appropriate robot to acquire informative viewpoints and resolve uncertainty. Across real-world scenarios, Co-GLANCE outperforms cloud-based vision-language model baselines in occlusion segmentation and robot allocation accuracy by 25% and 36%, respectively, while reducing per-frame inference latency 350x. We also release an air-ground dataset for future research. Code, videos, and dataset available at https://co-glance.github.io/ .

</details>


### [136] [Conformal Prediction for Neural Operators: Distribution-Free Uncertainty Quantification in Physics Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09923)
*Michael Chin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First application of split conformal prediction to neural operator surrogates for PDEs, yielding distribution-free, adaptive-width prediction intervals with finite-sample coverage; empirical 89.1% coverage at alpha=0.1; spatially adaptive intervals; uncertainty decomposition (epistemic vs. aleatoric).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety-critical engineering applications require reliable, formal uncertainty guarantees for neural operator-based surrogates; existing UQ methods (MC Dropout, Deep Ensembles) provide relative estimates without coverage guarantees.

Method: Apply split conformal prediction to neural operator models for PDE surrogates; introduce a normalized conformal scheme that uses MC Dropout to produce adaptive-width intervals; conduct experiments with a large model (33.7M parameters), 800 training samples, 5 ensemble members; provide open-source platform with REST API and 3D visualization.

Result: Empirical coverage of 89.1% at target alpha=0.1; intervals are spatially adaptive reflecting underlying physical uncertainty; uncertainty decomposition attributes 68% of total uncertainty to epistemic and 32% to aleatoric; platform released.

Conclusion: Conformal UQ for neural operators yields finite-sample, distribution-free coverage with adaptive intervals and actionable guidance for data collection; broad applicability to safety-critical PDE simulations.

Abstract: Neural operators such as the Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) have emerged as powerful surrogates for solving partial differential equations (PDEs), achieving speedups of several orders of magnitude over traditional numerical solvers. However, deploying these models in safety-critical engineering applications -- such as thermal management of electronic components and battery systems -- requires not only accurate point predictions but also rigorous uncertainty guarantees. Existing uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods for neural operators, including Monte Carlo Dropout and Deep Ensembles, provide only relative uncertainty estimates without formal coverage guarantees. In this work, we propose the first application of split conformal prediction to neural operator-based physics simulation, providing distribution-free prediction intervals with finite-sample coverage guarantees. We further introduce a normalized conformal prediction scheme that leverages MC Dropout uncertainty to produce adaptive-width intervals, yielding tighter intervals in regions of low uncertainty and wider intervals where the model is less certain. Full-scale experiments (33.7M parameters, 800 training samples, 5 ensemble members, NVIDIA V100) on steady-state heat conduction benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves 89.1% empirical coverage at the target level of alpha=0.1, while producing spatially adaptive prediction intervals that reflect the underlying physical uncertainty structure. We also provide an uncertainty decomposition framework that separates epistemic uncertainty (68% of total) from aleatoric uncertainty (32% of total), offering actionable guidance for data collection and model improvement. Our method is implemented in an open-source platform with REST API endpoints and interactive 3D visualization.

</details>


### [137] [Sigma-Branch: Hierarchical Single-Path Network Reconstruction for Dynamic Inference with Reduced Active Parameters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09924)
*Kohga Tanaka,Hiroaki Nishi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sigma-Branch (SigmaB) restructures pretrained dense nets into a hierarchical binary-tree network to reduce per-inference active parameters by ~58-60% without large accuracy loss, by routing inputs through a single root-to-leaf path while keeping the full parameter set in memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memory bottleneck from off-chip weight transfer on memory-constrained edge accelerators; dense networks cannot stay fully on-chip; compression often incurs permanent capacity loss. A method is needed to reduce per-inference memory traffic without sacrificing capacity.

Method: Construct a hierarchical binary tree with a shared backbone, hierarchical routers, and specialized leaves. Distribute pretrained weights across the tree via activation-based spherical k-means clustering to jointly initialize router weights and per-branch channel allocations; apply soft-routing fine-tuning to align each leaf with its routed input subset. During inference, execute a single root-to-leaf path, reducing active-parameter footprint while storing the complete dense parameter set in memory.

Result: SigmaB-Net reduces per-inference active parameters by 58-60% across CIFAR-100/ResNet-50, ImageNet-1K/ResNet-50, and ModelNet40/PointNet++, while achieving Top-1 accuracy within 1.72 percentage points of the dense baseline. At comparable ImageNet-1K Top-1, active-parameter reduction beats static structured pruning (FPGM, HRank) by 14-23 percentage points. Cross-modal evaluation supports decoupling per-inference memory traffic from total parameters.

Conclusion: SigmaB demonstrates a framework-level decoupling of per-inference memory traffic from total parameter count, with near-dense accuracy and superior active-parameter reduction compared to certain pruning baselines, applicable across 2D vision and 3D point-cloud backbones.

Abstract: Deploying deep neural networks on memory-constrained edge accelerators is bottlenecked by per-inference off-chip weight transfer rather than computation: the dense network cannot be retained on-chip, and every parameter must be loaded for every input. Existing model compression reduces this transfer only at the cost of permanent capacity loss. We propose Sigma-Branch (SigmaB), a framework that restructures a pretrained dense network into a hierarchical binary tree composed of a shared backbone, hierarchical routers, and specialized leaves. Pretrained weights are distributed across the tree via activation-based spherical k-means clustering, which jointly initializes router weights and per-branch channel allocations; soft-routing fine-tuning then aligns each leaf with its routed input subset. At inference, the resulting network executes only a single root-to-leaf path, reducing the active-parameter footprint while storing the complete dense parameter set in memory. Across CIFAR-100 / ResNet-50, ImageNet-1K / ResNet-50, and ModelNet40 / PointNet++, SigmaB-Net reduces per-inference active parameters by 58-60% while remaining within 1.72 percentage points (pp) of the dense baseline Top-1. At comparable ImageNet-1K Top-1, the active-parameter reduction exceeds static structured pruning (FPGM, HRank) by 14-23 pp. The cross-modal evaluation, spanning 2D vision and 3D point-cloud backbones, substantiates a framework-level claim that decouples per-inference memory traffic from the total parameter count.

</details>


### [138] [Sample Where You Struggle: Sharpening Base Model Reasoning via Entropy-Guided Power Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09926)
*Hong Guo,Nianhui Guo,Christoph Meinel,Haojin Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EGPS is a training-free, verifier-free MCMC sampler for sequence-level power distributions. It uses token-level entropy from the forward pass to generate entropy-guided proposals, localizes moves to high-entropy neighborhoods, and applies Multiple-Try Metropolis at decision points, achieving speedups and competitive accuracy on math and reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Metropolis–Hastings sampling from p^α is expensive and slow to mix due to a structural mismatch: sampling uniformly along the prefix wastes effort on near-degenerate conditionals and under-mixes where modes diverge. A targeted, entropy-aware sampler could reduce computation and improve mixing.

Method: Derive proposals from token-level entropy in the forward pass without training or verification. Skip deterministic blocks; constrain MCMC moves to high-entropy neighborhoods; use Multiple-Try Metropolis at decision points. Scale sampling cost to entropy mass rather than sequence length.

Result: On Qwen2.5-Math-7B, EGPS attains best or tied-best accuracy across three benchmarks: MATH500 75.8%, HumanEval 62.2%, GPQA 42.4%, with up to 12.6× wall-clock speedup over MH baseline.

Conclusion: Entropy-guided sampling provides a training- and verifier-free pathway to accelerate sequence-level sampling for reasoning tasks, aligning proposals with entropy structure to improve efficiency without sacrificing (and sometimes improving) accuracy. Further validation across models/datasets would strengthen generality.

Abstract: Sampling from the sequence-level power distribution $p^α$ elicits RL-level reasoning from base language models without any parameter updates, but the standard Metropolis--Hastings (MH), a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler, is both expensive and slow-mixing. We trace both to a structural mismatch: $p^α$ mainly departs from $p$ at a sparse, spatially clustered set of high-entropy decision points, yet MH proposes resampling positions uniformly along the prefix -- wasting compute on near-degenerate conditionals while under-mixing precisely where modes diverge. We propose Entropy-Guided Power Sampling (EGPS), a training-free and verifier-free sampler that re-derives its proposal from token-level entropy already in the forward pass. EGPS skips deterministic blocks, localizes each MCMC move to a high-entropy neighborhood, and applies Multiple-Try Metropolis at decision points -- making sampling cost scale with \emph{entropy mass rather than sequence length}. On Qwen2.5-Math-7B, EGPS reaches best or tied-best accuracy on all three benchmarks (MATH500 $75.8\%$, HumanEval $62.2\%$, GPQA $42.4\%$) at up to a $12.6\times$ wall-clock speedup over the MH baseline.

</details>


### [139] [Trainable Smooth-Rotation Transforms with Learned Channel Scales for LLM Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09927)
*Patrik Czakó,Gábor Kertész,Sándor Szénási*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Quantile-robust scaling for SmoothRot-style activation transforms with constrained gradient-based scale learning yields consistent PTQ gains for LLMs, outperforming max-based statistics and preserving the equivalent-transform framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Activation outliers in PTQ dominate quantization error; max-based statistics and over-migration in scaling-based transforms contribute to degradation. A robust, lightweight scaling strategy could improve activation quantization without abandoning equivalent-transform properties.

Method: Introduce a quantile-based activation statistic (replacing max-based statistics) for SmoothRot-style transforms. Augment with constrained gradient-based optimization of channel scales. Perform per-layer policy search on selected layers, including joint optimization of scale and quantile. Replay the best selected-layer policy across all decoder-block down-projection layers to obtain full-model gains, all while preserving the equivalent-transform formulation.

Result: Quantile-only policy search improves selected-layer error by 11.1% over the SmoothRot baseline. Joint (alpha, q) search improves by 12%. Training reaches 18.5% (unclear metric from abstract). Replaying the best selected-layer policy reduces full-layer mean error from 97.51 to 78.08 (19.9% relative). Gains are observed on LLaMA-3.2-1B with W4A4 quantization, across down-projection layers.

Conclusion: Robust migration control via quantile-based statistics and lightweight scale learning yields consistent PTQ improvements over max-based fixed policies while maintaining the equivalent-transform framework.

Abstract: Post-training quantization (PTQ) is one of the most practical ways to reduce the serving cost of Large Language Models (LLMs), but activation quantization remains difficult because outlier-dominated channels lead to large quantization errors. This paper investigates whether part of this degradation is caused by over-migration in scaling-based equivalent transformations. We introduce a quantile-robust scaling policy for SmoothRot-style transforms by replacing max-based activation statistics with high quantiles, and we complement it with constrained gradient-based optimization of channel scales. On LLaMA-3.2-1B under W4A4 quantization, quantile-only policy search improves selected-layer error by 11.1% over the SmoothRot baseline, joint (alpha, q) search improves it by 12%, and training reaches 18.5%. Replaying the best selected-layer policy on all decoder-block down-projection layers reduces the corresponding full-layer mean error from 97.51 to 78.08 (19.9%). The results show that robust migration control and lightweight scale learning provide consistent gains over max-based fixed policies while preserving the equivalent-transform framework.

</details>


### [140] [Forward-Only Convolutional Neural Networks with Learnable Channel-Class Assignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09928)
*Mohammadnavid Ghader,Saeed Reza Kheradpisheh,Bahar Farahani,Mahmood Fazlali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Learnable channel-class assignment and loss-aware layer contributions boost forward-only FF learning in residual CNNs, achieving state-of-the-art among FF methods on CIFAR-10/100 and Tiny-ImageNet and narrowing the gap to backprop.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of Forward-Forward (FF) learning in CNNs that rely on static channel-class partitions, which hamper performance on complex tasks; enable adaptive, data-driven specialization of channels and robust credit assignment through intermediate layers.

Method: Introduce a learnable channel-class assignment mechanism with entropy and orthogonality regularization to promote diverse, data-driven channel specialization. Propose a loss-aware layer contribution strategy that adaptively weights intermediate-layer predictions based on validation performance. Integrate these components into residual CNNs to enable effective forward-only inference.

Result: Consistent performance gains over existing FF-based methods on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet; establishes new state-of-the-art among FF-based models and significantly narrows the gap to backpropagation.

Conclusion: Adaptive channel specialization and loss-aware layer contribution weighting substantially enhance the representational capacity and effectiveness of forward-only learning in deep CNNs.

Abstract: The Forward-Forward (FF) algorithm offers a biologically inspired alternative to backpropagation by replacing gradient-based credit assignment with local, forward-only objectives. While recent extensions have adapted FF to convolutional neural networks (CNNs), existing formulations rely on static channel-class partitions and struggle to perform effectively in complex tasks. In this work, we introduce a learnable channel-class assignment mechanism that enables adaptive, data-driven specialization of convolutional channels, supported by entropy and orthogonality regularization to promote learning performance. We further propose a loss-aware layer contribution strategy that adaptively weights intermediate-layer predictions based on their validation performance, enhancing the effectiveness of forward-only inference. Integrated into residual CNNs, the proposed method achieves consistently superior performance across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet compared to existing similar forward-only methods. Notably, it establishes new state-of-the-art performance among FF-based models, substantially narrowing the gap with backpropagation. These findings demonstrate that introducing learnable channel specialization and layer contribution weighting significantly enhances the representational capacity of forward-only learning in deep CNNs.

</details>


### [141] [Between Amnesia and Chaos: A Memory Stability Expressivity Trilemma for Trainable Dissipative Oscillator Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09929)
*Caleb Munigety*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A damping-governed trilemma in end-to-end training of networks of nonlinear oscillators: memory horizon, gradient stability, and dynamical expressivity cannot all be maximized; stable training exists in a damping-dependent band that narrows with horizon and ends at a critical point.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Revisit the assumption that physical reservoirs must be frozen; assess end-to-end learnability of substrate parameters (mass, damping, stiffness) in oscillator networks and the gradient dynamics that govern training.

Method: End-to-end learning of oscillator network parameters using a symplectic integrator; perform damping sweeps on a 20-oscillator network; analyze backward gradient decay, forward sensitivities via Lyapunov exponents; compare learned vs frozen substrate on delayed recall across horizons.

Result: Identified a trilemma: increasing damping lowers Lyapunov exponent and reduces memory horizon, while lower damping raises gradient stability issues; training is feasible only within a stability band that contracts with horizon; learned substrates outperform frozen ones at short horizons but lose advantage near horizon ~11; empirical crossover exceeds analytic prediction by ~5x.

Conclusion: Provided a verified account of when training a physical substrate beats freezing it; training efficiency is constrained by a damping-induced stability band, with edge-of-chaos behavior guiding the regime of best performance.

Abstract: Physical reservoir computing harnesses nonlinear mechanical dynamics but, by convention, freezes the substrate and trains only a linear readout, presuming the substrate is not usefully trainable. We revisit that premise for networks of nonlinear oscillators whose mass, damping, and stiffness are learned end-to-end through a symplectic integrator. Our central result is a trilemma: memory horizon, gradient stability, and dynamical expressivity cannot be simultaneously maximized, because all three are governed by the damping. The backward gradient decays at a rate set by the damping, capping how far back credit can propagate, while forward sensitivities grow exponentially in the largest Lyapunov exponent, so usable gradients require damping above a stability floor. Since the Lyapunov exponent falls as damping rises while the memory ceiling falls as the horizon grows, stable training is confined to a band that contracts with horizon and closes at a critical point. We test every step on a twenty-oscillator network. A damping sweep finds the largest Lyapunov exponent monotone and crossing zero at a well-defined stability floor, confirming the theorem's key assumption. A compute-matched comparison of learned versus frozen substrate on delayed recall across nine horizons shows the learned substrate dominating at short horizons and the advantage closing and reversing near a horizon of eleven steps, the predicted signature of band closure; trained models settle near the stability floor, seeking the edge of chaos unprompted. The analytic ceiling overestimates the empirical crossover roughly fivefold, a gap between detectable and learnable gradient that we report rather than tune away. The contribution is a confirmed account of when training a physical substrate beats freezing it.

</details>


### [142] [When RL Fails after SFT: Rejuvenating Model Plasticity for Robust SFT-to-RL Handoff](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09932)
*Runze Liu,Jiashun Liu,Xu Wan,Yuqian Fu,Ling Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Excessive SFT harms RL by reducing model plasticity; introducing Rejuvenation—base-anchored model fusion with targeted neuron resets—restores plasticity and improves RL performance and OOD generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain and mitigate the observed decline in RL efficacy after heavy supervised fine-tuning, by dissecting parameters, outputs, and optimization dynamics, and proposing a remedy.

Method: Multi-perspective analysis of parameter changes, output distributions, and RL optimization; introduce Rejuvenation consisting of base-anchored model fusion to limit drift and targeted neuron reset to reduce rigidity.

Result: Findings show over-trained SFT causes over-confident token distributions and sharp parameter landscapes, hindering RL; Rejuvenation improves RL performance on such models and enhances generalization to out-of-distribution tasks across math reasoning and agentic domains.

Conclusion: A lightweight rejuvenation strategy can successfully restore plasticity while preserving SFT priors, enabling a more robust SFT-to-RL handoff.

Abstract: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) followed by Reinforcement Learning (RL) has become a standard pipeline for Large Language Model (LLM) post-training. SFT is expected to provide a useful behavioral prior for RL to further enhance model capabilities. However, checkpoints with excessive SFT often show limited improvement during RL. We attribute this failure to the loss of model plasticity: the reduced ability of an SFT-initialized policy to be effectively reshaped by subsequent RL. To better understand this phenomenon, we conduct detailed analysis from multiple perspectives, including parameter changes, output spaces, and RL optimization dynamics. Our results show that models from excessive SFT tend to produce over-confident token distributions and exhibit sharp parameter landscapes, which make them harder to optimize in the RL stage. To enable a more robust SFT-to-RL handoff, we propose \texttt{Rejuvenation}, a simple yet effective method that restores plasticity while preserving useful SFT-acquired priors. Rejuvenation leverages base-anchored model fusion to reduce excessive SFT-induced drift with targeted neuron reset to mitigate model rigidity. Experimental results on both math reasoning tasks and agentic tasks demonstrate that our approach consistently improves RL performance on over-trained SFT models, while also enhancing generalization to out-of-distribution tasks.

</details>


### [143] [nCMD: Benign-Anchored Feature Selection for Imbalanced Network Intrusion Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09934)
*Abu Fuad Ahmad,Istiaque Ahmed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: nCMD offers a benign-anchored, classwise mean deviation feature score for NIDS feature selection, providing a lightweight and interpretable filter that aligns with operational semantics and improves macro-F1 under challenging budgets and imbalance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the asymmetry in intrusion detection where attacks diverge from dominant benign traffic; standard symmetric global filters may miss discriminative deviations. Seek a lightweight, interpretable ranking aligned with NIDS semantics.

Method: Compute feature-wise deviations of attack-class distributions from the benign class mean (classwise mean deviation). Rank features by this benign-anchored score. Evaluate across four datasets (CICIDS2017, CICDDoS2019, NSL-KDD, UNSW-NB15), multiple feature budgets, and three classifiers, comparing against classical filter baselines.

Result: nCMD matches or surpasses classical filter baselines in macro-averaged F1-score across datasets, achieving the best results on 3 of 4 datasets and for all classifiers. Strongest gains observed with tight feature budgets and severe class imbalance; no additional computational cost.

Conclusion: Benign-anchored ranking is a scalable, interpretable preprocessing approach for resource-constrained NIDS, aligning feature selection with operational semantics and maintaining efficiency while improving performance under imbalance.

Abstract: Feature selection is critical for network intrusion detection systems (NIDS) operating under high-dimensional, highly imbalanced traffic, as found in operational and defense networks. Traditional filter methods rank features using global statistics computed symmetrically across classes and thus fail to capture the asymmetry of intrusion detection, where attacks are best characterized as deviations from dominant benign traffic.
  We propose benign-anchored Classwise Mean Deviation (nCMD), a lightweight and interpretable method that scores feature relevance based on the deviation of attack-class distributions from the benign-class mean, rather than a globally biased reference. This approach aligns feature selection with the operational semantics of NIDS at no additional computational cost.
  Across four benchmark datasets (CICIDS2017, CICDDoS2019, NSL-KDD, and UNSW-NB15), multiple feature budgets, and three downstream classifiers, nCMD matches or exceeds classical filter baselines in macro-averaged F1-score. It achieves the best result on three of the four datasets and under every classifier, with the strongest improvements observed under tight feature budgets and severe class imbalance. These results support benign-anchored ranking as a scalable and interpretable preprocessing component for resource-constrained NIDS.

</details>


### [144] [One Lens, Many Worlds : A Capability-Typed Interface for World-Model Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09936)
*Bhavith Chandra Challagundla,Sanskar Pandey,Param Thakkar,Rishikesh Mallagundla,Yugandhar Reddy Gogireddy,Wenhao Lu,Hindol Roy Choudhury,Shravani Challagundla,Mohamed Deraz Nasr,Spursh Deshpande*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes WorldModelLens, a capability-typed, open-source interpretability substrate that unifies analysis across diverse world-model architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpretability tooling is fragmented across latent-recurrent, token-based, and joint-embedding world models, largely due to transformers-centric hooks. A common, typed interface could unify analyses (probing, patching, surprise) across architectures.

Method: Introduce WorldModelLens: a capability-typed adapter with four required methods (encode, transition, initial_state, sample) and optional heads (decode, reward, continue, actor, critic) via a capability descriptor. Provide a single hook/cache layer exposing time-indexed activations, imagination rollouts, and intervention replay. Open-source implementation.

Result: A unified interpretability substrate enabling analyses to be written once and applied to RL and SSL world models alike; reduces cross-architecture re-implementation and tooling fragmentation.

Conclusion: The core insight is that world models share a small typed interface; building tooling around this interface makes RL and self-supervised world models first-class citizens and promotes cross-model interpretability.

Abstract: World models are now built on substantially different computational substrates. Latent recurrent state-space models such as PlaNet and the Dreamer family compress observations into recurrent states; token-based models such as IRIS quantize observations into a learned codebook and predict autoregressively with a transformer; and joint-embedding predictive architectures such as I-JEPA predict in a learned latent space with no pixel decoder. The interpretability methods applied to these models, including probing, activation patching, sparse autoencoders, and surprise analysis, share a common set of primitives, yet they are re-implemented from scratch for each architecture because existing hook-and-cache tooling assumes a transformer language model with no notion of actions, environment steps, or imagined rollouts. We argue that this fragmentation reflects the tooling rather than the models, and that the shared structure of world models is captured by a small typed interface. We present WorldModelLens, an open-source interpretability substrate organized around a capability-typed adapter: every model implements four required methods (encode, transition, initial state, sample) and declares a set of optional heads (decode, reward, continue, actor, critic) through an explicit capability descriptor, so that reinforcement-learning and self-supervised world models are first-class without either imitating the other. A single hook and cache layer exposes time-indexed activations, imagination rollouts, and intervention replay over this interface, allowing each analysis to be written once.

</details>


### [145] [RKSC: Reasoning-Aware KV Cache Sharing and Confident Early Exit for Multi-Step LLM Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09937)
*Anirudh Sekar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Training-free RKSC framework for reasoning-aware KV cache sharing and early exits to accelerate multi-branch LLM reasoning; achieves ~3x speedups with minimal accuracy loss without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce redundant computation and memory in multi-branch LLM inference by sharing key-value caches across semantically similar branches, applying early-exit strategies, and controlling KV cache growth.

Method: ASKS computes a shared prefix KV cache and broadcasts it to semantically similar branches via hidden-state cosine similarity, generalizing token-exact prefix caching (vLLM/SGLang). CGEE provides two exits: (1) skip the verification forward pass when generation confidence is decisive across branches; (2) terminate verification at an intermediate layer when per-layer entropy stabilises, using lightweight hooks on the transformer backbone. RSBCM uses attention-weighted depth-priority eviction to prevent unbounded KV cache growth. Evaluation spans five model families (7B–10B), four benchmarks, and 1,000 problems.

Result: Mean speedup of 3.008x over the No-KV baseline (peak 3.990x); 1.66x mean improvement over vLLM-equivalent prefix caching; CGEE-induced error rate 0.37% (6/1,616 verify calls). No fine-tuning or architecture changes required. Code available at the provided GitHub URL.

Conclusion: Demonstrates training-free, scalable acceleration for multi-branch reasoning pipelines with minimal accuracy impact; effective KV caching strategy and exit controls; applicable across 7B–10B LLMs; potential limitations include reliance on semantic similarity for branch广播 and cache eviction behavior needing tuning across deployments.

Abstract: We introduce RKSC (Reasoning-Aware KV Cache Sharing), a training-free inference framework that eliminates two structural redundancies in multi-branch LLM reasoning pipelines. ASKS (Attention-Similarity KV Sharing) computes the prefix KV cache once and broadcasts it to all semantically similar branches via hidden-state cosine similarity, strictly generalising the token-exact prefix caching used by vLLM and SGLang. CGEE (Confidence-Gated Early Exit) applies two complementary exit mechanisms: (1) it skips the verification forward pass entirely when generation confidence is decisive across branches, and (2) it terminates the verification pass at an intermediate layer when per-layer entropy stabilises, using lightweight hooks on the transformer backbone. RSBCM (Reasoning-Selective Block Cache Manager) prevents unbounded cache growth via attention-weighted depth-priority eviction. Across five model families (7B-10B), four benchmarks, and 1,000 evaluated problems, RKSC achieves a mean speedup of 3.008x over the No-KV baseline (peak 3.990x), a 1.66x mean improvement over vLLM-equivalent prefix caching, with a CGEE-induced error rate of only 0.37% (6 errors out of 1,616 verify calls). No fine-tuning or architecture changes are required. Code is available at https://github.com/AnirudhSekar/RKSC.

</details>


### [146] [Interactions Between Crosscoder Features: A Compact Proofs Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09940)
*Dmitry Manning-Coe,Thomas Read,Anna Soligo,Oliver Clive-Griffin,Chun-Hei Yip,Rajashree Agrawal,Jason Gross*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A new interaction-based framework for crosscoders in dictionary learning. It defines a differentiable interaction penalty that quantifies feature interactions, enabling computationally sparse crosscoders that preserve substantial performance, and revealing semantically meaningful feature clusters and sleeper agent interactions; code is released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explain and quantify how interactions between crosscoder features affect reconstruction and model performance, and provide a formal, actionable framework (including a differentiable penalty) to leverage these interactions for sparsity and interpretability.

Method: Formalize a compact proof of model performance via crosscoders; derive an explicit interaction term for crosscoder features within MLP layers; propose using the interaction term as a differentiable loss penalty; optimize crosscoders with this penalty; analyze clustering by interaction measure and assess sleeper agents.

Result: The interaction term is a well-defined, differentiable quantity that can be used as a penalty; applying it yields highly sparse crosscoders that retain ~60% of full MLP performance when only one feature is kept per datapoint and neuron (vs ~10% for standard crosscoders); clustering by the interaction measure yields semantically meaningful feature groups; sleeper agents exhibit significant feature interactions; code is available at the stated repository.

Conclusion: The interaction measure provides a practical, interpretable tool for regularizing crosscoders, enabling substantial sparsity with limited performance loss and offering insights into feature structure and agent interactions.

Abstract: Dictionary learning methods like Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) and crosscoders attempt to explain a model by decomposing its activations into independent features. Interactions between features hence induce errors in the reconstruction. We formalize this intuition via compact proofs and make five contributions. First, we show how, \textit{in principle}, a compact proof of model performance can be constructed using a crosscoder. Second, we show that an error term arising in this proof can naturally be interpreted as a measure of interaction between crosscoder features and provide an explicit expression for the interaction term in the Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) layers. We then provide three applications of this new interaction measure. In our third contribution we show that the interaction term itself can be used as a differentiable loss penalty. Applying this penalty, we can achieve ``computationally sparse'' crosscoders that retain $60\%$ of MLP performance when only keeping a single feature at each datapoint and neuron, compared to $10\%$ in standard crosscoders. We then show that clustering according to our interaction measure provides semantically meaningful feature clusters, and finally that sleeper agents have significant interactions. Code is available at https://github.com/chainik1125/crosscoders-feature-interactions/tree/arxiv.

</details>


### [147] [Learning Where to Simulate: Generative Active Sampling for Online PDE Surrogate Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09949)
*Pierre Cesar,Sofya Dymchenko,Abhishek Purandare,Bruno Raffin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Online Generative Active Sampling (OGAS) uses a diffusion-based conditional sampler to steer data generation toward configuration regimes that yield high surrogate difficulty, improving tail error statistics for PDE surrogates with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To generalize data-driven PDE surrogates across wide parameter configurations, avoiding under-representation of challenging dynamics typical of uniform sampling.

Method: OGAS trains a fast diffusion model in parallel as a conditional sampler that maps surrogate-imposed difficulty signals to configuration parameters. A prior biased toward high difficulty guides sampling. Online training couples data generation with surrogate learning without delaying the workflow.

Result: Across 2D PDEs (Kuramoto–Sivashinsky, Navier–Stokes, Gray–Scott) with up to 308 parameters and multiple surrogate architectures, OGAS reduces tail errors (above the 99th percentile) and error dispersion compared with uniform sampling, with negligible wall-time overhead. There is a trade-off: average error may worsen slightly.

Conclusion: OGAS consistently improves worst-case reliability of PDE surrogates by adaptively focusing on challenging regimes while maintaining training efficiency, enabling better robustness across diverse PDE configurations.

Abstract: Data-driven PDE surrogates are trained with data produced by numerical PDE solvers. However, when the surrogate's goal is to generalize across a wide range of PDE configurations (e.g., initial conditions and physical coefficients), generating a representative training set is non-trivial. Uniform sampling of configuration parameters often under-represents trajectories exhibiting challenging dynamics, leading to high prediction errors and large error variance in the trained surrogate. Online training, where data generation and surrogate training are coupled, offers a natural advantage by allowing solver parameters to be steered on-the-fly. To efficiently exploit this capability, we introduce Online Generative Active Sampling (OGAS), an active learning method that reactively learns the relationship between configuration parameters and surrogate performance to control the sampling distribution. OGAS trains a fast diffusion model in parallel to the surrogate to act as a conditional sampler, mapping a surrogate-derived difficulty signal (e.g., loss or uncertainty) to configuration parameters. By actively drawing target signals from a prior biased toward high difficulty, OGAS continuously steers data generation toward challenging regimes without delaying the training workflow. We evaluate OGAS across 2D PDEs with distinct challenging dynamics (Kuramoto-Sivashinsky, Navier-Stokes, Gray-Scott) and up to 308 parameters, using multiple surrogate architectures. Across all settings, OGAS consistently improves tail statistics, yielding substantial reductions in errors above the 99th percentile and overall error dispersion compared to uniform sampling. While prioritizing challenging trajectories introduces a trade-off with average error, OGAS effectively ensures worst-case reliability of trained surrogates with negligible wall-time overhead.

</details>


### [148] [Integrating Out, Twice:The Open-System Case That Neural-Network Ensemble Theory Is Missing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09950)
*Jin Lei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A conceptual link between closed neural network ensembles and open-system scattering, with a complete dictionary for the closed case and a predominantly negative result for the open case, implying open dynamics require continuous-spectrum, wave-like behavior not present in standard ML setups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unify neural network ensemble theory and scattering/open-system physics via Gaussian algebra and Schur complements; assess what information survives when projecting to a reduced (open) sector.

Method: Algebraic derivation using distribution moments, Gaussian calculus, and block inversion to build a dictionary between closed-system neural-network objects (e.g., NTK, GP emulator) and scattering-theory analogs; then apply a test-suite to a truncated attention map, token-transfer operator, and sparse router to probe the open case.

Result: Closed-case dictionary established: neural tangent kernel equals Fisher sensitivity kernel; infinite-width limit corresponds to Gaussian-process emulator; lazy-to-feature transition defines a reduced-basis emulator boundary. Open-case results are largely negative; the open export lacks the eliminated sector with continuous spectrum and wave-like dynamics, a structure current ML models do not provide. Uncertainty is epistemic and localized to the closed half of the correspondence; the negative is attributed to fundamental requirements for openness that ML objects do not satisfy.

Conclusion: The note frames a precise boundary: the open case is not captured by the current finite/dissipative ML objects and needs a continuous-spectrum, wave-like open dynamics. The main value is the mapping that locates this limitation, guiding future work on genuinely open-system ML formalisms and non-Hermitian/open dynamics.

Abstract: Averaging a neural network over its random parameters and marginalizing a Gaussian sector are the same operation, the Schur complement of the eliminated block, and when that block is closed it returns a covariance and its inverse. That is all a network ensemble produces, the closed case. The open case is missing, and nuclear reaction theory has it worked out. Projecting a scattering problem onto a chosen set of channels, with the rest carrying probability irreversibly to a continuum, leaves a non-Hermitian effective generator that conserves and itemizes exactly what it loses: the nuclear optical model and its generalized optical theorem. I set the two cases side by side using only the moments of a distribution, the algebra of Gaussians, and block inversion, no field theory, and give the closed-case dictionary in full: the neural tangent kernel is the Fisher sensitivity kernel, the infinite-width Gaussian limit is the Gaussian-process emulator, and the lazy-to-feature transition is the validity boundary of a reduced-basis emulator. I then test the open export on a truncated attention map, a token-level transfer operator, and a sparse expert router, and report a mostly negative result. The conserved flux ledger ports wherever openness is genuinely present, but its distinctive content is absent, an artifact of the chosen partition, or pinned near a floor by the training objective, and the operationally useful uncertainty turns out to be epistemic, living in the closed half of the correspondence, not the open one. The negative has a structural reason this note makes precise: the open case needs an eliminated sector with a continuous spectrum and wave-like, not relaxational, dynamics, which mainstream learning's finite or dissipative objects do not supply. This is a note, not a result; its main finding is that negative one, and its value is the map that locates it.

</details>


### [149] [Hasse Diagrams for Attention: A Partial Order Framework for Designing Transformer Masks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09951)
*Chentao Li,Han Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A theoretical framework links arbitrary attention masks in deep Transformers to information-flow, showing convergence to a DAG/Hasse diagram under sufficient depth; uses this to formulate a minimal common supergraph problem for task-driven mask design and derives two novel masks (Block Two-Stream and Butterfly Attention).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of a systematic, formal understanding of how arbitrary attention masks shape information flow and to enable automatic mask design aligned with multi-task/parallel training objectives.

Method: 1) Prove that multi-layer Transformer information flow converges to a Hasse diagram (a DAG representing a partial order) with sufficient depth. 2) Reformulate parallel training task design as finding a minimal common supergraph of Hasse diagrams and derive a criterion for this supergraph. 3) Provide a constructive pipeline to derive masks from a family of tasks. 4) Use the framework to design two masks: Block Two-Stream Attention (Block-generation mask ensuring training-inference consistency) and Butterfly Attention (fully supervised bidirectional mask).

Result: A complete theoretical framework is established connecting masks to information-flow structures; a convergence result to Hasse diagrams is proven; a criterion for minimal common supergraphs is provided; a constructive method to derive masks is developed; two novel masks are introduced, validating the framework’s capacity to discover new structures.

Conclusion: The framework enables principled, automatic mask design tailored to multiple training tasks, with potential broad applicability to Transformer efficiency and generalization; it also opens avenues for exploring new masks guided by formal information-flow properties.

Abstract: During the training of large Transformer models, attention masks regulate the scope and direction of information flow across a sequence. Numerous mask variants exist, and operators such as FlexAttention already support arbitrary attention masks. Nevertheless, a systematic formal analysis of the information-flow structure induced by arbitrary masks has been missing. This paper develops a complete theoretical framework. We prove that, with sufficient depth, the information flow of a multi-layer Transformer converges to a Hasse diagram -- a directed acyclic graph representing a partial order. Building on this, we recast the design of parallel training tasks as the problem of finding a minimal common supergraph of Hasse diagrams, and we establish a criterion for the minimal common supergraph. This yields a constructive method to derive attention masks directly from a family of tasks. Applying the framework, we design two novel masks: a block-generation attention mask that ensures training-inference consistency (Block Two-Stream Attention), and a fully supervised bidirectional attention mask (Butterfly Attention). These results demonstrate the framework's capacity to discover new structures.

</details>


### [150] [Does Normalization Choice Matter for Causal Large Time-Series Models?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09954)
*Samy-Melwan Vilhes,Gilles Gasso,Mokhtar Z Alaya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Normalization choice in transformer-based large time-series models with causal training and patching strongly affects training convergence and forecasting accuracy; careful design is needed to avoid information leakage and align with non-stationary data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world time series exhibit non-stationarities that degrade predictive performance; normalization stabilizes training but can leak future information during causal training; there is a need to understand how normalization strategies interact with patching and efficient causal training.

Method: Empirical evaluation of normalization strategies in transformer-based LLMs for time-series forecasting with patching and efficient causal strategy; compare standard normalization, causal normalization, and statistics computed from initial observations; assess effects on convergence and forecasting accuracy.

Result: Normalization choice has a substantial effect on both training convergence and forecasting performance; some strategies mitigate leakage-related degradation, while others can harm performance.

Conclusion: Selecting an appropriate normalization strategy is crucial for effective training of large time-series transformers in causal settings; further work should systematically characterize practical implications and establish guidelines.

Abstract: Large models for time-series forecasting have been emerged as a promising paradigm for training models on heterogeneous collections of signals. These models typically rely on causal autoregressive architectures, where each observation is sequentially predicted from past. In practice, real-world time-series exhibit non-stationarities, which significantly influence predictive performance. To mitigate this, normalization is commonly employed. However, in efficient causal settings it might induce information leakage from future observations during training. Recent alternatives, including causal normalization and statistics computed from initial observations, have been proposed to address this issue, but their practical implications remain insufficiently understood. In this work, we evaluate normalization strategies for transformer-based large time-series models trained with patching and efficient causal strategy. We showcase that normalization choice significantly influences both training convergence and forecasting performance.

</details>


### [151] [Temporal Context Conditioning for Seasonality-Aware Precipitation Nowcasting of High-Intensity Rainfall](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09959)
*Gijs van Nieuwkoop,Siamak Mehrkanoon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TA-SmaAt-UNet extends SmaAt-UNet with temporal conditioning via cyclical time encodings to improve radar nowcasting, particularly for high-intensity rainfall, with minimal parameter cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Incorporating simple temporal context (diurnal/seasonal patterns) can improve the realism and reliability of deep-learning precipitation nowcasts, especially for rare events.

Method: Extend the core SmaAt-UNet with temporal conditioning layers that use cyclical encodings of time-of-day and time-of-year to modulate intermediate feature representations; train on KNMI radar precipitation data; conduct a layer conductance analysis to assess usage.

Result: Temporal conditioning is most beneficial for rare, high-intensity events and improves seasonal variability representation and rainfall-intensity distributions; conductance analysis shows the temporal layers are actively used with small parameter cost.

Conclusion: Physically motivated temporal context can improve deep-learning precipitation nowcasts; implementation and training setup are available on GitHub.

Abstract: Precipitation nowcasting is increasingly being approached with deep learning models that learn directly from recent radar observations. Although such models can efficiently capture short-term precipitation motion, they often lack broader contextual information about the meteorological conditions under which rainfall develops. This paper investigates whether lightweight temporal context can improve radar-based nowcasting, particularly for high-intensity rainfall. We propose the Time-Aware Small-Attention U-Net (TA-SmaAt-UNet), which extends the core SmaAt-UNet model with temporal conditioning layers that use cyclical encodings of time-of-day and time-of-year to modulate intermediate feature representations. Experiments on KNMI radar precipitation data show that temporal conditioning is most beneficial for rare, high-intensity precipitation events, while also improving the representation of seasonal variability and predicted rainfall-intensity distributions. A layer conductance analysis further indicates that the added temporal conditioning layers are actively used by the model despite their small parameter cost. These findings suggest that simple, physically motivated temporal context can improve the realism and reliability of deep learning-based precipitation nowcasts. The implementation of our models and training setup is available on \href{https://github.com/gijsvn/TA-SmaAt-UNet}{GitHub}.

</details>


### [152] [HydraCIL: Decoupled Class-Incremental Learning through Prototype-Guided Multi-Head Classifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09960)
*Daniel Vila-Cruz,Laura Morán-Fernández,Verónica Bolón-Canedo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A resource-efficient continual learning approach that freezes the feature extractor and uses per-task lightweight heads selected by prototype similarity, achieving competitive CIL performance with reduced training time and energy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
