Motivation: CIL in real-world embedded/edge devices demands fast adaptation under limited compute and energy constraints, making backbone retraining expensive and impractical.

Method: Freeze the backbone to decouple feature extraction from learning. For each task, extract features once and instantiate a lightweight, task-specific classifier head. During inference, select the appropriate head by measuring similarity to class prototypes.

Result: HydraCIL achieves comparable or superior performance to state-of-the-art CIL methods on benchmarks (CIFAR-100, ImageNet-100, CoRe50, Flowers102) while significantly reducing training time and carbon footprint.

Conclusion: The framework provides a practical, energy-efficient solution for continual learning in constrained environments, enabling rapid adaptation without costly backbone updates.

Abstract: We present HydraCIL, a decoupled continual learning model based on prototype-guided multi-head classifiers, targeting sustainable deployment in embedded and resource-constrained environments. While most Class-Incremental Learning (CIL) methods rely on powerful hardware and long retraining cycles, real-world systems, such as robots or edge AI devices, must adapt quickly with limited resources. HydraCIL addresses this gap by freezing the backbone and decoupling feature extraction from learning. For each task, features are extracted once and a lightweight, task-specific classifier head is created, avoiding costly backbone retraining. At inference, HydraCIL selects the appropriate head via similarity with prototypes. Experiments on CIFAR-100, ImageNet-100, CoRe50, and Flowers102 datasets show that HydraCIL matches or outperforms state-of-the-art CIL methods while significantly reducing training time and carbon footprint, making it a practical solution for continual learning in real-world and embedded settings, where energy efficiency and rapid adaptation are critical.

</details>


### [153] [3SPO: State-Score-Supervised Policy Optimization for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09961)
*Yu Han,Kailing Li,Yang Jiao,Yulin Dai,Yuqian Fu,Linhai Zhuo,Tianwen Qian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: 3SPO is a state-score supervised post-step RL method for LLMs that improves credit assignment and sample efficiency in long-horizon tasks by using dynamic state scores, outperforming GRPO and enabling faster convergence with more exploration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address sparse/delayed rewards and per-step credit assignment in RL for autonomous LLM agents; move beyond trajectory-level optimization.

Method: Compute state scores at each step based on historical success rates; use these scores to supervise step-level policy updates; perform post-step optimization without value function estimation; no extra auxiliary models; theoretical analysis under per-state bandit abstraction; experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen models.

Result: 3SPO outperforms GRPO by +22.6% on ALFWorld and +15.6 points on WebShop; achieves 2.4x more state exploration and 1.8x faster convergence with comparable resources; code available.

Conclusion: 3SPO achieves improved sample efficiency and credit assignment with theoretical guarantees; demonstrates practical gains in long-horizon LLM autonomous tasks.

Abstract: Training large language models (LLMs) as autonomous agents via reinforcement learning (RL) has enabled frontier models to achieve superhuman performance in long-horizon tasks. However, existing RL algorithms operate at the trajectory level, performing policy optimization only after collecting complete episode rollouts. This coarse-grained approach faces fundamental challenges in multi-turn agent settings where rewards are sparse, delayed, and credit assignment across individual steps is critical. In this work, we propose \textbf{State-Score-Supervised Policy Optimization (3SPO)}, a novel RL algorithm that performs post-step policy optimization with dynamic state score supervision. At each step, 3SPO computes the state score based on historical success rates, supervising step-wise credit assignment, adaptive rollout and post-step policy optimization without requiring value function estimation or additional auxiliary models. Theoretically, under a per-state bandit abstraction, we show that the proposed score-supervised allocation mechanism achieves logarithmic allocation regret and provide sample-complexity guarantees for action identification, score distinguishability, and filtering stability. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop with Qwen2.5-1.5B/7B-Instruct show that 3SPO consistently outperforms GRPO by $+22.6\%$ on ALFWorld and $+15.6$ points on WebShop, while using comparable resources to achieve $2.4\times$ more state exploration and $1.8\times$ faster convergence. Code is available at https://github.com/genalyu/3SPO.

</details>


### [154] [Optimality of FSQ Tokens for Continuous Diffusion for Categorical Data with Application to Text-to-Speech](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09962)
*Vadim Popov,Wenju Gu,Tasnima Sadekova,Georgii Aparin,Assel Yermekova*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FSQ tokenization yields superior latent-space structure for continuous diffusion on categorical data, achieving better performance and efficiency in TTS diffusion models compared to LLM-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess latent-space geometry of discrete tokens under KL divergence of diffusion path measures and token-prediction accuracy to identify a tokenization best-suited for continuous diffusion on categorical data; test in real-world tasks.

Method: Theoretical analysis of latent-space structure via KL divergence along diffusion paths; empirical evaluation across tokenizations; selection of FSQ as best; validation by training multiple TTS diffusion models with speech tokens; comparison to strong LLM-based approach; evaluation of size/speed.

Result: FSQ offers latent-space properties most conducive for diffusion; FSQ-based models outperform LLM-based counterparts in accuracy, while being smaller and faster.

Conclusion: FSQ tokenization is the preferred scheme for continuous diffusion with discrete data, enabling efficient and accurate generation; supports broader applicability beyond TTS to other discrete-data diffusion tasks.

Abstract: Continuous diffusion for categorical data is a framework belonging to the diffusion family and aiming at generating discrete data. The scientific interest to such models has been constantly increasing these days because researchers try to achieve a challenging goal of finding reasonable alternatives to autoregressive large language models. In this paper, we study the properties of the structure of the latent space corresponding to discrete tokens expressed in terms of Kullback-Leibler divergence on diffusion path measures and accuracy of the correct token prediction by the optimally trained diffusion model. We find that FSQ tokenization scheme has the latent space structure with the properties that make it best suited for continuous diffusion for categorical data as verified through rigorous theoretical analysis and numerical experiments. To validate our findings in real-life scenario, we train several text-to-speech diffusion models having speech tokens as intermediate acoustic features, and show that the one based on FSQ tokens indeed performs the best, and, moreover, it outperforms its strong LLM-based counterpart, at the same time being significantly smaller and faster.

</details>


### [155] [SHAPO: Sharpness-Aware Policy Optimization for Safe Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10228)
*Kaustubh Mani,Yann Pequignot,Vincent Mai,Liam Paull*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes SHAPO, a sharpness-aware policy optimization method for safe exploration via perturbing actor parameters to gauge epistemic uncertainty; reweights policy gradients to bias learning toward conservative behavior in under-explored regions; shows improved safety and performance across continuous-control tasks, expanding the Pareto frontier.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safe exploration in reinforcement learning for safety-critical domains. Epistemic uncertainty is addressed by making policy updates pessimistic with respect to uncertain regions, aiming to reduce risky actions in under-explored states.

Method: Introduce Sharpness-Aware Policy Optimization (SHAPO) by evaluating gradients at perturbed network parameters (sharpness-aware updates). Analytically show that this reweights policy gradients, boosting the influence of rare unsafe actions while tempering safe actions, leading to conservative behavior in uncertain regions. Empirical evaluation on continuous-control benchmarks comparing safety and performance against baselines.

Result: SHAPO consistently improves both safety and task performance over baselines and expands their Pareto frontiers across several continuous-control tasks.

Conclusion: SHAPO offers a principled way to incorporate epistemic uncertainty into policy updates, yielding conservative, safer exploration without sacrificing performance, and broadening achievable trade-offs.

Abstract: Safe exploration is a prerequisite for deploying reinforcement learning (RL) agents in safety-critical domains. In this paper, we approach safe exploration through the lens of epistemic uncertainty, where the actor's sensitivity to parameter perturbations serves as a practical proxy for regions of high uncertainty. We propose Sharpness-Aware Policy Optimization (SHAPO), a sharpness-aware policy update rule that evaluates gradients at perturbed parameters, making policy updates pessimistic with respect to the actor's epistemic uncertainty. Analytically we show that this adjustment implicitly reweighs policy gradients, amplifying the influence of rare unsafe actions while tempering contributions from already safe ones, thereby biasing learning toward conservative behavior in under-explored regions. Across several continuous-control tasks, our method consistently improves both safety and task performance over existing baselines, significantly expanding their Pareto frontiers.

</details>


### [156] [Interpreting and Steering a Text-to-Speech Language Model with Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10029)
*Nikita Koriagin,Georgii Aparin,Nikita Balagansky,Daniil Gavrilov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A study of latent representations in a single residual stream for text and generated speech in TTS. It trains BatchTopK sparse autoencoders on CosyVoice3's LM backbone and uses a modality-aware auto-interp pipeline to label features by firing context (text prefix, 1s of speech, or both). Recovered features span phonemes, laughter, accent prompts, and speaker gender; causal interventions in the SAE latent space can manipulate laughter probability, alter perceived gender, and adjust speech rate while preserving content. SAE features serve as interpretable objects and as control directions for TTS synthesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and expose the latent representations formed when text and generated speech share a single residual stream in LM-based TTS, and to establish controllable, interpretable directions for synthesis.

Method: Train BatchTopK sparse autoencoders on the language-model backbone of CosyVoice3; develop a modality-aware auto-interpretation pipeline that labels each latent feature by its firing context (text-prefix context, 1-second speech clips, or both); recover interpretable features including phonemes, laughter, accent prompts, and speaker gender; perform targeted interventions in the SAE latent space to test causality on outputs.

Result: Recovered SAE features are interpretable across phonemes, laughter, accent prompts, and speaker gender. Causal steering demonstrates large effects: laughter probability rises from 0.02 to 0.79; perceived speaker gender can be flipped; speech rate can be controlled while preserving spoken content.

Conclusion: SAE features function as both interpretable objects and practical control directions for TTS synthesis, enabling interpretability and manipulation of multimodal representations without compromising core content.

Abstract: Language models increasingly serve as the backbone of text-to-speech (TTS) systems, yet we understand little about the representations they build when text and generated speech tokens share a single residual stream. We train BatchTopK sparse autoencoders on the LM backbone of CosyVoice3 and introduce a modality-aware auto-interp pipeline that labels each feature from where it fires-text-prefix context, 1-second speech clips, or both. The recovered features are interpretable, spanning phonemes, laughter, accent prompts and speaker gender. Steering through the SAE latent space shows these features are causal rather than merely descriptive: targeted interventions raise laughter probability from 0.02 to 0.79, flip perceived speaker gender, and control speech rate while preserving spoken content. SAE features thus serve both as interpretability objects and as control directions for TTS synthesis.

</details>


### [157] [Baseline-Free Policy Optimization for Neural Combinatorial Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10321)
*Carlos S. Sepúlveda,Gonzalo A. Ruz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRPO provides a baseline-free training for neural combinatorial optimization, eliminating REINFORCE's rollout baseline and improving stability on hard instances; it matches strong AM-based baselines with lower variance, and shows competitive performance with P3O on TSP/CVRP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle baseline-induced instability in autoregressive NCO training by removing the baseline, enabling more robust learning on hard instances and simpler training dynamics.

Method: Apply Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) by normalizing advantages within groups of sampled trajectories to remove the need for a baseline. Perform controlled experiments comparing five RL algorithms on TSP and CVRP benchmarks within the RL4CO framework. Include a gradient-update-matched comparison to POMO and evaluate P3O as an additional baselines.

Result: GRPO avoids the training collapse observed with REINFORCE on TSP-100; at matched gradient updates, GRPO achieves solutions within ~2% of POMO without an external baseline; P3O is competitive on TSP but shows higher variability on CVRP.

Conclusion: GRPO is a promising baseline-free alternative for neural combinatorial optimization, particularly in regimes where baseline-dependent training is fragile.

Abstract: Neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) trains autoregressive policies to solve routing problems. The standard training algorithm, REINFORCE with a rollout baseline, requires maintaining and periodically updating a frozen copy of the policy for variance reduction. This baseline introduces a structural vulnerability: on harder instances, a poor baseline produces noisy gradient estimates that can destabilize training. We evaluate Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), an algorithm from large language model alignment that eliminates the baseline entirely by normalizing advantages within groups of sampled trajectories. In a controlled comparison of five RL algorithms on TSP and CVRP benchmarks within the RL4CO framework, we find that: (i) GRPO avoids the training collapse observed with REINFORCE on TSP-100, where performance degrades from cost 9.8 to 52.1 immediately after the warmup phase and does not recover under extended training; (ii) at matched gradient updates, GRPO achieves solution quality within 2% of POMO, a strong AM-based multi-start baseline, while requiring no external baseline; and (iii) P3O, a pairwise preference algorithm also from the alignment literature, is competitive on TSP but shows higher variability on CVRP. These results identify GRPO as a promising baseline-free alternative for NCO, particularly in settings where baseline-dependent training becomes fragile.

</details>


### [158] [Bittensor Agent Arenas as a Trajectory Primitive: Distilling a Shopping Agent from ShoppingBench Subnet Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10064)
*Shardul Bansal,Seth Schilbe,Jarrod Barnes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Incentive-aligned trajectories plus a structural-quality filter improve small-model agentic post-training by generating per-trajectory judged data; achieves substantial ASR gains on ShoppingBench SN15, approaching synthetic baselines with less data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the data bottleneck in post-training agentic systems by creating high-quality, per-trajectory supervised data produced in an incentive-aligned arena, avoiding biases from frontier synthesis and uncontrolled production logs.

Method: Create an incentive-aligned arena (ORO Subnet 15) with race dynamics, LLM reasoning judge, and rotating leak-cluster-guarded tasks. Develop a structural-quality filter that retains only agentic trajectories (where the model issues tool calls) and discards non-agentic sub-task traces. Train Qwen3-4B using a SFT-then-GRPO pipeline matched to ShoppingBench. Use per-step teacher-grounded Dr. GRPO rewards to improve process.

Result: On a leak-cluster-guarded held-out set, ASR rises from 18.0% to 42.7%, close to the 43.6% synthetic-data SFT baseline. Training on a fraction of a single subnet-day output yields a broad pass@k improvement (pass@1 from 34.8% to 53.3%); GRPO reduces remaining headroom, and the sub-task firehose is identified as the main lever for closing the gap.

Conclusion: Incentive-aligned arena data generation combined with a structural-quality filter can substantially boost performance of small-model agentic post-training with limited data, enabling near-parity with synthetic-data baselines. The authors release the filter, corpus splits, and arena mechanics.

Abstract: Small-model agentic post-training is bottlenecked less by the algorithm than by the trajectory substrate it consumes. Leading recipes (RLVR, group-relative RL, rejection-sampled re-SFT) all need multi-turn traces carrying per-trajectory supervision, and the two existing sources fall short: frontier-synthesised data inherits the synthesizer's biases and collapses the long tail, while unfiltered production logs are unjudged and contaminated by shortcut behaviour. We argue that an incentive-aligned agent arena can be engineered to manufacture such trajectories, and demonstrate this on ORO Subnet 15 (SN15), a Bittensor deployment of the ShoppingBench agentic-commerce benchmark. SN15's race mechanism, LLM reasoning judge, and rotating leak-cluster-guarded problem suite yield a corpus with three properties: incentive-aligned diversity, per-trajectory judging, and anti-memorised held-out evaluation. We introduce a structural-quality filter that converts the raw firehose into a trainable corpus by keeping agentic trajectories (the model itself emits the tool calls) and rejecting sub-task trajectories (the model only classifies or narrates over a deterministic search loop), then post-train Qwen3-4B with a recipe matched to the published ShoppingBench SFT-then-GRPO pipeline. On a leak-cluster-guarded held-out partition scored production-strict, the model lifts from the published Qwen3-4B base of 18.0% ASR to 42.7%, within single-problem noise of the synthetic-data SFT-only baseline (43.6%), while training on a fraction of a single day of subnet output. The supervised stack leaves a large pass@8 to pass@1 gap (53.3% vs 34.8%); a per-step teacher-grounded Dr. GRPO reward converts that headroom into process improvement, and we identify the sub-task firehose as the primary lever for closing the gap to the 48.7% SFT+GRPO bar. We release the filter, the corpus splits, and the arena mechanics.

</details>


### [159] [Importance-Aware Scheduling for High-Dimensional Hyperparameter Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10068)
*Ruinan Wang,Ian Nabney,Mohammad Golbabaee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GIF improves sample efficiency in high-dimensional HPO by importance-aware greedy scheduling with warm-started importance estimation, grouping, proportional trial allocation, and a full-space fallback; it outperforms several baselines on high-dimensional benchmarks and remains competitive on lower-dimensional ones, with ablations confirming the contributions of estimation, allocation, and fallback and validating the HIA component.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-dimensional HPO with costly evaluations suffers from slow progress and dilution of effort across many low-impact variables; there is a need for more sample-efficient, scalable optimization strategies that leverage parameter importance.

Method: Greedy Importance First (GIF) algorithm: use a small-sample warm start to estimate parameter importance, form importance-based groups, allocate trials proportionally to groups, and retain a full-space fallback; incorporate an HIA component to recover anisotropy; perform ablations to assess the importance estimation, allocation strategy, and fallback step.

Result: GIF achieves better incumbents with faster convergence than TPE, BOHB, Random Search, and Sequential Grouping on higher-dimensional benchmarks; on Bayesmark, it remains competitive with smaller margins; ablations show that all components contribute to gains; HIA validates anisotropy recovery on analytic benchmarks.

Conclusion: GIF is a simple, plug-compatible approach that improves sample efficiency for high-dimensional HPO by leveraging importance-aware scheduling and robust fallback, with demonstrated gains across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Hyperparameter Optimization (HPO) is essential for building high-performing ML/DL models, yet conventional optimizers often struggle in high-dimensional spaces where evaluations are costly and progress is diluted across many low-impact variables. We propose Greedy Importance First (GIF), an importance-aware scheduling strategy that uses a small-sample warm start to estimate hyperparameter importance, forms importance-based groups, allocates trials proportionally, and retains a full-space fallback. We evaluate GIF under fixed evaluation budgets on five anisotropic analytic functions, Bayesmark, and NAS-Bench-301. On the higher-dimensional benchmarks, GIF reaches better incumbents with faster convergence than TPE, BOHB, Random Search, and Sequential Grouping. On Bayesmark, where the effective dimensionality is smaller, GIF remains competitive but the margins are smaller. Ablation studies show that importance estimation, proportional allocation, and the fallback step all contribute to the gains. We also verify that the HIA component recovers the intended anisotropy on the analytic benchmarks. These results suggest that GIF is a simple and plug-compatible way to improve sample efficiency in high-dimensional HPO.

</details>


### [160] [Spatiotemporal Seismic Hazard Assessment Using VQ-VAE and Seismic Statistical Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10069)
*Wei Quan,Denise Gorse*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Localized seismic forecasting using SSFs and a novel VQ-VAE spatial feature improves prediction, with potential to replace the traditional b-value in feature importance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of previous global prediction approaches by focusing on localised regions around candidate events and by integrating spatial information from 2D seismic maps to improve forecasting performance.

Method: Replicates prior XGBoost-based evaluation of 60 seismic statistical features (SSFs) against 428 tsfresh features on earthquake catalog data from Japan and Chile. Extends to: (1) localised predictions within a 24 km radius, (2) integration of a VQ-VAE-derived feature from 2D seismic maps trained to reconstruct maps, using the reconstruction error as a local stress-related metric. Uses SHAP for feature importance and evaluates predictive performance via AUC.

Result: Localization retains high predictive performance (AUC comparable to previous whole-region results). The VQ-VAE-derived spatial feature provides an additive performance boost and tends to dominate feature usage in SHAP analysis, potentially replacing the traditional b-value.

Conclusion: Combining a curated set of SSFs with a spatially-informed VQ-VAE feature yields strong, localized earthquake forecasting performance and suggests a shift away from reliance on the b-value, highlighting the value of integrating 2D seismic maps into predictive models.

Abstract: In this paper we build upon a previous study in which we demonstrated, using XGBoost and earthquake catalogue data from Japan and Chile, that a set of 60 seismic statistical features (SSFs) had much greater predictive value than a set of 428 generic time series features from the tsfresh package. We here extend this previous work in two key ways, focusing on data from Japan as a large dataset is necessary in order to allow for the training of a deep learning (autoencoder) model. First, we move from whole-region prediction (considering, for each candidate event, the likelihood of an event M $\geq$ 5.0 anywhere in the region in the next 15 days) to localised predictions in which both the region of feature computation and the region of prediction are restricted to a circle of radius 24 km around the candidate event, and we show that performance remains excellent, similar to our previous whole-region study for the same area. Second, we here couple this proven set of SSFs, based on one-dimensional (catalogue) data, with a novel feature based on two-dimensional seismic maps, obtained by training a VQ-VAE model to reproduce such maps as output and identifying a measure of its error in doing so with a localised build-up of crustal stress. We show that while localised prediction based on SSFs can be effective alone, with test AUC values as high as those obtained in the case of Japan in our previous whole-region study, the inclusion of the new natively-spatial VQ-VAE-derived feature, top-ranked by SHAP analysis, can enhance performance and additionally appears to near-wholly replace the traditionally-computed $b$-value in terms of feature usage.

</details>


### [161] [Temporal Sheaf Neural Networks with Dynamic Orthogonal Transport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10071)
*Md Sadek Hossain Asif,Tanzila Khan,Md. Mosaddek Khan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TSNN introduces per-node, time-varying orthogonal frames and transport-based comparisons for temporal link prediction, achieving causal, history-only processing with strong empirical gains on heterogeneous graphs; supported by theoretical links to sheaf and graph Laplacians and energy descent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing continuous-time GNNs typically operate in a shared global embedding space, neglecting node-specific evolving semantics and explicit transport between local frames. There is a need for causal, frame-aware representations that preserve hidden states under frame updates and handle node-role heterogeneity.

Method: Per-node frames parameterized via low-rank Householder products; exact preservation of stored hidden states under frame updates; comparisons occur after transporting node states between local frames; geometric-residual decoder anchored on transported distances with learned residuals; all computations strictly causal using pre-event history; theoretical results: symmetric degree-normalized sheaf Laplacian is orthogonally similar to the symmetric normalized graph Laplacian; the diffusion is a metric-gradient step on the combinatorial sheaf Dirichlet energy with non-expansiveness; frame drift perturbs updates linearly; implementation validated on benchmarks including TGB v2 and DGB.

Result: TSNN matches or surpasses the strongest prior methods on most benchmarks, with the largest gains on graphs exhibiting strong node-role heterogeneity; ablations confirm the distinct benefits of dynamic frames, orthogonal transport, and geometric-residual decoding.

Conclusion: Dynamic, transport-aware local frames enable causal, state-preserving temporal graph representations with theoretical energy-descent guarantees and practical gains on heterogeneous graphs; the approach unifies sheaf-based and graph-based diffusion under an orthogonal transport framework.

Abstract: We introduce Temporal Sheaf Neural Networks (TSNN), a temporal link prediction framework that equips each node with a time-varying orthogonal frame and compares node states only after explicit transport between local coordinate systems. In contrast to existing continuous-time graph models that operate in a shared global embedding space, TSNN models node-specific and evolving interaction semantics through dynamic local frames. The model parameterizes per-node frames via efficient low-rank Householder products, preserves stored hidden states exactly under frame updates, and uses a geometric-residual decoder that anchors predictions on transported distances while learning residual corrections. All computations are strictly causal and use only the pre-event history. We show that the symmetric degree-normalized sheaf Laplacian is orthogonally similar to the symmetric normalized graph Laplacian, with the random-walk normalized form similar in the corresponding degree metric; the full-active, feature-scaled diffusion used by TSNN is exactly a metric-gradient step on the combinatorial sheaf Dirichlet energy, with a degree-free monotone-descent and non-expansiveness guarantee. Frame drift perturbs updates only linearly. Across TGB v2 link-prediction and temporal-heterogeneous leaderboards, together with the DGB benchmark suite, TSNN matches or surpasses the strongest prior methods on most benchmarks, with the largest improvements on graphs exhibiting strong node-role heterogeneity. Ablations confirm the distinct benefit of dynamic frames, orthogonal transport, and geometric-residual decoding.

</details>


### [162] [VFUSE: Virulent Feature Understanding with Sparse autoEncoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10080)
*Michael Yu,Matthew L. Olson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SAE-based mechanistic interpretability auditing of diffusion-driven protein design models for hazard detection, enabling interpretable prediction of risky designs without sacrificing performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address safety concerns in generative protein design by enabling interpretable, hazard-aware auditing of large, open-weight models.

Method: Train sparse autoencoders on diffusion-transformer activations (VFUSE) and apply linear probes to RoseTTAFold3 and RFDiffusion3; extract monosemantic SAE features that correlate with hazardous designs; evaluate hazard detection (AUROC) and compare latent-space probes to original representations.

Result: SAE latent-space probes detect hazardous designs more effectively than models’ original representations without performance loss; monosemantic SAE features fire selectively on hazardous designs achieving AUROC up to 0.84 (q < 1e-13); claims first SAE on an all-atom diffusion model and first feature-level virulence audit of a protein design model.

Conclusion: Presents a novel path for safe, interpretable protein design by combining SAEs with diffusion-model activations, enabling hazard auditing at the feature level and highlighting a basis for mechanistic interpretability in protein generation.

Abstract: Generative models have shown remarkable progress in a variety of domains such as protein design, but such power enables the opaque generation of hazardous proteins. In this work, we introduce VFUSE (Virulent Feature Understanding with Sparse autoEncoders), a mechanistic interpretability approach that trains SAEs on diffusion-transformer activations to audit protein models for hazard-aware features. We apply VFUSE to RoseTTAFold3 and RFDiffusion3, popular open-weight models for protein folding and synthesis. We find that for certain blocks, linear probes detect hazardous designs significantly better when fit in the SAE latent space over the original model's representations: improving interpretability without sacrificing model performance. Furthermore, we identify monosemantic features from the SAE that fire only on hazardous designs at up to AUROC $0.84$ ($q < 10^{-13}$). To our knowledge this is the first SAE trained on an all-atom diffusion model and the first feature-level virulence audit of a protein design model, paving the way towards safe and interpretable protein design.

</details>


### [163] [Divide-and-Conquer Modeling for the CTF-4-Science Lorenz Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10084)
*Shundong Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A divide-and-conquer, task-aligned modeling strategy for the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark improves chaotic forecasting by pairing specialized components with each task group, achieving a public score of 79.63.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address chaotic-system prediction across heterogeneous regimes (clean forecasting, noisy reconstruction, noisy-input forecasting, few-shot learning, and parametric generalization) without forcing a single model class to handle all tasks.

Method: Partition tasks by group and apply specialized components: smoothing-based denoising for noisy full trajectories; NG-RC/NVAR models tuned for long-time attractor forecasting; a fitted Lorenz transition correction restricted to the sensitive clean short-time prefix; and a parametric prefix blend for interpolation.

Result: Final public score of 79.63; demonstrates that bounded, scenario-specific updates can outperform broad model replacement on mixed chaotic benchmarks.

Conclusion: Modular, task-aligned updates can outperform monolithic models in heterogeneous chaotic forecasting settings.

Abstract: This work presents a divide-and-conquer modeling strategy for the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark, which evaluates chaotic-system prediction across twelve hidden scores and five scenario families: clean forecasting, noisy reconstruction, noisy-input forecasting, few-shot learning, and parametric generalization. Rather than forcing one model class to handle all regimes, the final system matched each prediction block to the evaluation behavior of its task group. The main contributions are: smoothing-based reconstruction for noisy full-trajectory denoising; NG-RC/NVAR models tuned for noisy long-time attractor forecasting; a fitted Lorenz transition correction restricted to the sensitive clean short-time prefix; and a parametric prefix blend for the interpolation task. The resulting system with final public score of 79.63 shows that bounded, scenario-specific updates can outperform broad model replacement on mixed chaotic forecasting benchmarks.

</details>


### [164] [Structured Adaptive Tensor Prediction for Streaming Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10085)
*Zhen Qin,Yang Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive tensor regression for streaming matrix-valued time series with MoM and ToM; online SGD; ToM stacking yields lower error and better denoising; fixed-time recovery guarantees under sparsity/low-rank structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Matrix-valued time series (e.g., medical imaging, geophysics) arise in streaming settings; static models and scalar/vector adaptive methods are inadequate; need online forecasting and handling of temporal structure.

Method: Proposes adaptive tensor regression with two formulations: Matrix-on-Matrix (MoM) and Tensor-on-Matrix (ToM). Employs online stochastic gradient descent (SGD); stacks past responses into higher-order tensors to leverage temporal structure; analyzes tracking under time-varying dynamics; proves fixed-time recovery guarantees for ToM under low-dimensional structures (sparsity, low-rankness, and joint models).

Result: ToM demonstrated lower steady-state error and stronger denoising than MoM; stacking across time improves performance; ToM provides favorable tracking behavior and solid theoretical guarantees.

Conclusion: ToM is preferred for streaming matrix-valued prediction due to superior empirical performance and robust theoretical guarantees under low-dimensional structures; extends adaptive forecasting to tensor-valued matrices with online learning.

Abstract: Matrix-valued time series arise in a wide range of applications, such as spatio-temporal data from medical imaging and geophysics. Existing methods are mainly designed for static settings and lack adaptability to streaming and time-varying environments. Adaptive filtering techniques have also been largely limited to data with scalar or vector values, leaving adaptive forecasting for matrix-valued time series inadequately understood. To bridge these gaps, we develop an adaptive tensor regression framework that includes Matrix-on-Matrix (MoM) and Tensor-on-Matrix (ToM) formulations for streaming matrix-valued prediction. The two formulations differ in whether to directly model matrix-valued outputs or to exploit temporal structure via higher-order tensor representations. For the proposed tensor regression framework, we develop stochastic gradient descent (SGD) algorithms for online learning. We show that stacking multiple responses across time into higher-order tensors improves performance; in particular, the ToM achieves lower steady-state error and stronger denoising capability than MoM, motivating our focus on the ToM model. We further characterize the tracking behavior of SGD under time-varying dynamics. From a statistical perspective, we establish fixed-time recovery guarantees for ToM under general low-dimensional structures, including sparsity, low-rankness, and their joint sparselow-rank models.

</details>


### [165] [A Theory on Flow Matching with Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10089)
*Yihan He,Qishuo Yin,Yuan Cao,Jianqing Fan,Han Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Theory for flow matching with neural conditional velocity fields; proves convergence for over-parameterized 2-layer ReLU nets, generalization bounds for the velocity-field objective, and Wasserstein guarantees for generated samples; grounded in multi-task representation learning with unbounded losses; validated on synthetic and real-image benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide a rigorous theoretical foundation for flow matching in neural-parameterized velocity fields and extend generalization analysis to unbounded losses in multi-task learning, with implications for flow-based generative modeling.

Method: Prove gradient-descent convergence in over-parameterized 2-layer ReLU networks; derive generalization bounds for the conditional velocity-field matching objective; establish Wasserstein-distance guarantees for induced-flow samples; base analysis on broad generalization bounds for multi-task representation learning with unbounded losses; validate theory with extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world image data.

Result: Convergence guarantees for training, generalization bounds for the objective, and Wasserstein guarantees for generated samples; connects flow matching to multi-task learning with unbounded losses; experiments corroborate theoretical insights.

Conclusion: The work provides robust theoretical guarantees for flow matching with neural-parameterized velocity fields and demonstrates practical validity across datasets; the generalized bounds may be of independent interest beyond flow-based generative modeling.

Abstract: In this work, we develop theoretical foundation for flow matching with neural-network-parameterized conditional velocity fields. We establish convergence guarantees for gradient descent in the over-parameterized 2-layered ReLU neural network regime. We derive generalization bounds for the conditional velocity-field matching objective. Building on these results, we provide Wasserstein-distance guarantees for the samples generated by the induced flow. Our analysis is based on generalization bound for multi-task representation learning with unbounded losses, which may be of independent interest beyond flow-based generative modeling. These theoretical results are validated through extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world image benchmarks.

</details>


### [166] [Decision-Making under Combinatorial Risk](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10092)
*Yifan Hong,Hongmiao Fan,Chen Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces an investment-allocation task to study combinatorial risk; revealing the PMF reshapes decisions; symbolic regression yields compact PMF-focused models; prospect-theory residual explains PMF-driven behavior; main finding: core combinatorial-risk features guide choices, with lottery valuation emerging primarily when the PMF is displayed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses decision-making under combinatorial risk, where risk arises from multiple components, the full induced distribution is costly to evaluate, and existing single-shot benchmarks may be insufficient.

Method: Experiment with an investment-allocation task that modulates risk increments across components; measures choices; uses symbolic regression to discover compact descriptive models centered on combinatorial-risk features; compares behavior with and without revealing the induced PMF; augments the model with a prospect-theoretic residual.

Result: Participants prefer the option with the larger probability increment; when increments are equal, they favor the option with higher initial success probability. Revealing the induced PMF reduces sensitivity to combinatorial-risk features and decreases choice variance. Discovered models rely mainly on after-investment success probability rather than full PMF; adding a prospect-theory residual accounts for PMF-displayed behavior.

Conclusion: People navigate combinatorial risk via core features (e.g., after-investment probability) and shift toward lottery valuation mainly when the induced PMF is displayed; full evaluation of the induced distribution is not necessary for the observed behavior under typical conditions.

Abstract: Decision-making under risk is typically studied through single-shot lottery choices. Yet many real decisions involve combinatorial risk, where risk arises from multiple risky components, so the lottery over outcomes is induced rather than given outright and can be costly to evaluate exactly. We introduce an investment-allocation task to study decision under combinatorial risk, where investing in a component raises its success probability and thereby reshapes the outcome distribution. Participants favor the option with the larger probability increment, and, when increments are equal, the option with the higher initial success probability. Revealing the induced probability mass function (PMF) substantially changes behavior, making participants less responsive to combinatorial-risk features and reducing choice variance. To explain these patterns, we move beyond standard benchmarks and hand-crafted hypotheses with symbolic regression to discover compact descriptive models. The discovered models rely mainly on combinatorial-risk features, such as the after-investment success probability, rather than exact evaluation of the full induced distribution. Behavior under the displayed PMF is then well explained by augmenting this model with a prospect-theoretic residual model. The results show that people navigate combinatorial risk primarily through its core features, shifting toward lottery valuation only when the induced PMF is displayed.

</details>


### [167] [Unsupervised Style Representation Learning for AI-Text Detection via Paraphrase Inversion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10099)
*Rafael Rivera Soto,Barry Chen,Nicholas Andrews*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unsupervised style representations enable robust LLM-detection without author labels, achieving strong few-shot performance and competitive zero-shot results, with good out-of-distribution generalization and transfer to related tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome dependence on author labels and in-distribution data for style-based detectors, and improve robustness to adversarial attacks and unseen LLMs.

Method: Train a style encoder to reconstruct human-authored text from its machine-generated paraphrase, while freezing a semantic encoder to bias the style encoder toward non-semantic features. Evaluate via a few-shot detector and a zero-shot DeepSVDD-based detector.

Result: In few-shot settings, the method matches or exceeds baselines across benchmarks. In zero-shot settings, it is competitive with fully supervised in-distribution classifiers and generalizes better to unseen LLMs. Learned representations transfer to authorship verification and fine-grained style discrimination without additional training.

Conclusion: Disentangling semantic and stylistic features through paraphrase-based reconstruction yields robust, label-free style representations that support effective detection and broader generalization to related tasks.

Abstract: The rapid development of large language models (LLMs) has raised concerns about misuse such as plagiarism, misinformation, and automated influence operations, motivating the need for robust detectors. Recent work has shown that neural representations of writing style are effective for detection and, crucially, robust to adversarial attacks that defeat most existing detectors. However, current style-based detectors rely on authorship labels for training, and are limited to few-shot inference for detection, requiring in-distribution samples that may not always be available. We learn discriminative style features without authorship labels by training a style encoder to reconstruct human-authored text from its machine-generated paraphrase; freezing a semantic encoder during training biases the style encoder to capture only the non-semantic features needed for reconstruction. We evaluate the learned representations via two detection strategies: a few-shot detector and a zero-shot DeepSVDD-based detector. Across benchmarks, our method matches or outperforms all baselines in the few-shot setting and, in the zero-shot regime, is competitive with fully supervised classifiers on in-distribution test data while generalizing better to unseen LLMs. Beyond detection, the learned representations generalize to unseen tasks, achieving competitive performance on authorship verification and fine-grained style discrimination despite never being trained on either objective.

</details>


### [168] [Nonlinear Estimator: Dual Bayesian Affine Estimators for Parameter Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10111)
*Sasan Vakili,Daniël Woonings,Pradyumna Paruchuri,Peyman Mohajerin Esfahani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes two fixed-point nonlinear estimators for Wiener-type state-space models by coupling two affine MMSE estimators via Dynamic Basis Statistics (DBS); introduces a dual basis-parameter estimator and a dual state-parameter estimator that outperform competitive methods in simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable efficient nonlinear parameter estimation in Wiener-type state-space models by preserving affine MMSE structure while incorporating nonlinear basis information through DBS.

Method: A fixed-point architecture alternates between updating parameter and latent-variable estimators. Two DBS construction strategies yield two nonlinear estimator frameworks: (1) dual basis-parameter estimator combines an affine basis estimator with an affine parameter estimator, and (2) dual state-parameter estimator first computes affine state estimates and covariances, then maps these through a Gaussian DBS operator to obtain DBS estimates. Both admit fixed-point characterizations with updates driven by the other component’s plug-in prior statistics from the previous iteration.

Result: Monte Carlo experiments show the dual basis-parameter estimator achieves parameter MSE comparable to the purely affine parameter estimator, while the dual state-parameter estimator attains the lowest parameter MSE, outperforming both the dual basis-parameter and purely affine estimators as well as sequential Monte Carlo variants of Particle Gibbs and EM schemes.

Conclusion: The dual estimators provide effective nonlinear parameter estimation in Wiener-type state-space models, with the dual state-parameter estimator offering the best parameter accuracy and competitive performance relative to SMC/EM baselines; fixed-point alternating schemes are viable for nonlinear parameter estimation in this setting.

Abstract: This paper presents a nonlinear parameter estimator for Wiener-type state-space models obtained as a fixed-point architecture that couples two affine minimum mean-squared error (MMSE) estimators: one for the unknown parameters and one for latent variables. The architecture retains the functional structure of the optimal affine MMSE parameter estimator while incorporating Dynamic Basis Statistics (DBS) estimates that summarize nonlinear basis-function evaluations. Two DBS construction strategies are developed, leading to two nonlinear estimator frameworks. The dual basis-parameter estimator combines an affine basis estimator with the affine parameter estimator, whereas the dual state-parameter estimator first computes affine state estimates and their covariances, then maps these state-estimate statistics through a Gaussian DBS operator to obtain DBS estimates. Both dual estimators admit fixed-point characterizations that alternate between estimating each component using the updated prior of the other, obtained from that component's plug-in estimate statistics from the previous iteration. The efficacy of the proposed methods is examined via extensive Monte Carlo experiments, showing that the dual basis-parameter estimator attains parameter mean-squared errors comparable to those of the purely affine parameter estimator, while the dual state-parameter estimator achieves the lowest parameter mean-squared error, outperforming both the dual basis-parameter and purely affine parameter estimators, as well as sequential Monte Carlo variants of classical Particle Gibbs and Expectation-Maximization schemes.

</details>


### [169] [FedSteer: Taming Extreme Gradient Staleness in Federated Learning with Corrective Projections and Caching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10124)
*Haoran Zhang,Cainã Figueiredo Pereira,Marie Siew,Xutong Liu,Carlee Joe-Wong,Rachid El-Azouzi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FedSteer builds a low-dimensional gradient subspace from recent client updates to steer stale gradients in federated learning, reducing variance from uneven participation; it uses selective caching to limit memory and projects active gradients onto the subspace, while reusing coordinates for inactive clients. Empirical results show improved stability and accuracy (including >7% gains in some settings).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aggregation variance in federated learning grows when client participation is skewed or inconsistent, causing stale updates to destabilize training.

Method: Maintain a cache of recent client gradients to form a representative gradient subspace (via a low-dimensional basis). For an active client, project its true gradient onto this subspace to obtain optimal coordinates. For inactive clients, reuse these coordinates with the subspace drifted by other active clients. Use selective caching to pick a representative subset of clients to construct the subspace, reducing server memory.

Result: Empirical evaluation indicates FedSteer substantially outperforms baselines, preventing performance collapse under challenging participation patterns and delivering accuracy gains exceeding 7% in other scenarios.

Conclusion: FedSteer provides a principled way to mitigate stale-gradient effects in FL by aligning updates to a shared low-dimensional optimization landscape, balancing memory efficiency with training stability and accuracy.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) is often subject to aggregation variance if clients do not consistently participate in training rounds. While reusing stale model updates from inactive clients is a common technique to reduce this variance, we find that with skewed client participation, the resulting update staleness can become severe enough to destabilize training. To remedy this, we propose FedSteer, a novel method that constructs a gradient subspace from a cache of recent client gradients to serve as a low-dimensional representation of the current optimization landscape. FedSteer projects an active client's true gradient onto this subspace to find a set of optimal coordinates. For an inactive client, FedSteer reuses these coordinates with the now-evolved subspace drifted by other active clients. This process effectively "steers" outdated gradients toward the current global objective. This is complemented by a selective caching strategy that identifies a representative client subset to form the subspace, reducing server memory. Experiments demonstrate that FedSteer significantly outperforms baselines, preventing performance collapse in challenging scenarios while delivering accuracy gains of over 7% in others.

</details>


### [170] [Discovering Interpretable Multi-Parameter Control Policies for Evolutionary Algorithms Using Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10129)
*Tai Nguyen,Phong Le,Carola Doerr,Nguyen Dang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Deep RL can learn effective multi-parameter control for the (1+ (λ, λ))-GA on OneMax; with action-space decomposition, reward shifting, and long-horizon discounting, Double DQN outperforms PPO and yields analyzable trajectories; learned policies can be distilled into a transparent symbolic controller that outperforms baselines across problem sizes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Theoretical analysis of parameter control in evolutionary algorithms has been largely confined to single-parameter settings. Multi-parameter control promises potentially super-constant speedups but is difficult to study; OneMax is a canonical testbed with known speedups. The work aims to produce interpretable, effective multi-parameter control policies via deep RL.

Method: Introduce algorithm-agnostic enhancements for deep RL in multi-parameter EA control: action-space decomposition, reward shifting, and long-horizon discounting. Compare deep RL methods (notably Double Deep Q-Networks vs Proximal Policy Optimization). Apply to the (1+ (λ, λ))-GA optimizing OneMax. Finally, distill the learned behaviors into a symbolic, interpretable policy and evaluate across problem sizes.

Result: Double DQN uniquely avoids policy collapse seen with PPO, producing trajectories suitable for downstream analysis. The extracted symbolic policy is interpretable and delivers exceptional performance, consistently outperforming existing baselines across a wide range of problem sizes.

Conclusion: Multi-parameter control in evolutionary algorithms can be learned and distilled into transparent policies. The distilled policy provides both theoretical interpretability and practical performance gains, illustrating that deep-RL can tackle interpretable, multi-parameter control when complemented with suitable design choices.

Abstract: While deep Reinforcement Learning (deep-RL) has been increasingly applied to parameter control in evolutionary algorithms, rigorous theoretical analysis of parameter control remains largely restricted to single-parameter settings, owing to the difficulty of deriving effective, interpretable multi-parameter policies amenable to formal study. We demonstrate how deep-RL can be leveraged to overcome this barrier, using the (1+($λ$,$λ$))-genetic algorithm optimizing OneMax, one of the few problems where a super-constant speedup of dynamic control has been formally proven, as a representative case study. We first show that standard approaches struggle to converge in this multi-parameter setting, and introduce algorithm-agnostic enhancements targeting action-space decomposition, reward shifting, and long-horizon discounting. With these in place, we compare common deep-RL methods and find that Double Deep Q-Networks uniquely avoid the policy collapse observed in Proximal Policy Optimization, yielding trajectories suitable for downstream analysis. Crucially, we move beyond the ``black-box'' nature of neural networks by distilling the learned behaviors into a transparent, symbolic control policy. This resulting policy does not only offer interpretability for future theoretical analysis but also yields exceptional performance, consistently outperforming existing baselines across a wide range of problem sizes.

</details>


### [171] [Ambiguous Strategic Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10137)
*Ivri Hikri,Nir Rosenfeld*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Explores regulatory partial disclosure in strategic classification using ambiguity (robust mechanism design) to optimize both the classifier and the disclosed set; provides algorithmic and empirical analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivated by policy constraints that force partial disclosure of a classifier, this work studies learning under ambiguity, where a learner must jointly select a disclosure set and a realized classifier, capturing strategic behavior and uncertainty.

Method: Formulates learning under an ambiguity set of classifiers; the learner discloses a set of classifiers and privately realizes one; derives efficient best-response and training algorithms; analyzes strategic learning with robustness to disclosure uncertainty; conducts empirical studies.

Result: Develops efficient algorithms for computing best-responses and training under ambiguity; empirically investigates strategic learning outcomes and how disclosure ambiguity shapes outcomes.

Conclusion: Ambiguity provides a principled way to model regulatory partial disclosure in strategic settings; it influences learning dynamics and outcomes, offering insights into the trade-offs between disclosure robustness and performance; suggests directions for policy design and future work on ambiguity types and equilibria.

Abstract: A common assumption in strategic classification is that the classifier is public knowledge. However, it remains unclear whether, and why, a system would choose to commit to full disclosure. We study a setting in which regulation requires the system to disclose some, but not all, of the information. This induces a learning task in which the learner must jointly optimize the classifier and the uncertainty surrounding it. To this end, we adopt from robust mechanism design the notion of ambiguity, which in our setting allows the learner to reveal a set or range of possible classifiers, while privately choosing which of them to ultimately realize. We investigate how ambiguity affects the learning task, develop efficient algorithms for computing best-responses and training, and empirically explore strategic learning and its outcomes in this novel setting and using our approach.

</details>


### [172] [Compositional Generative Modeling from Decentralized Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10153)
*Mashrur M. Morshed,Vishnu Naresh Boddeti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DCFM enables decentralized compositional learning by enforcing structural constraints on a global set of generative factors without sharing raw data, allowing novel combinations via peer interaction and outperforming federated and Mixture-of-Experts baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In decentralized settings, factors are distributed across siloed datasets. Existing methods either model the union without true compositionality or require data sharing. There is a need to enable emergence of new factor combinations through constrained coordination without exposing data.

Method: Introduce Decentralized Compositional Flow Matching (DCFM), a framework that imposes structural constraints across the global generative-factor set. It uses a flow-matching style objective to align factor distributions across silos while preserving data locality, enabling peers to induce novel combinations.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows DCFM substantially outperforms federated learning and mixture-of-experts baselines on tasks including conditional image generation, robotic spatial planning, and medical attribute co-occurrence modeling.

Conclusion: Structural constraints plus peer interactions unlock true compositionality in decentralized data without raw-data exchange, with cross-domain effectiveness.

Abstract: Learning the compositional nature of the physical world requires joint observation of interacting factors. However, because practical data is often decentralized, these factors are fragmented across isolated silos. Existing decentralized generative approaches focus only on modeling the union of siloed data, overlooking novel combinations implied by the collective whole. To bridge this gap, we introduce Decentralized Compositional Flow Matching (DCFM), a framework that enforces structural constraints across the global set of generative factors, without exchanging any raw data. DCFM enables novel combinations to emerge through peer interactions, even when no single data source can independently support the composition. Empirically, DCFM substantially outperforms federated learning and mixture-of-experts baselines across conditional image generation, robotic spatial planning, and medical attribute co-occurrence modeling.

</details>


### [173] [Quality Is Not a Safety Proxy Under Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10154)
*Sahil Kadadekar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Quality-based shortcuts for safety screening fail to reliably predict dangerous quantized checkpoints across multiple models; a calibrated, direct safety evaluation workflow is required.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess whether quality metrics used in quantized-model pipelines can serve as reliable proxies for safety, and validate a cross-model screening framework that couples quality signals with direct safety testing.

Method: Construct a matched 51-row matrix spanning 6 models, 4 families, a 7-level GGUF ladder, and AWQ/GPTQ INT4 checkpoints; evaluate quality–safety pairings; perform a four-probe mechanistic follow-up on 17 Hugging Face-backed FP16/AWQ/GPTQ cells; analyze entropy, refusal-direction, and calibration; relabel items with Claude Sonnet 4 using a predefined stratified set; calibrate a Refusal Template Stability Index (RTSI) from four drift features; validate in-sample scoring and row-level leave-one-out; compare against baselines (unique-prefix-rate-delta, raw refusal-rate-delta); examine cross-stack transfer with recalibration.

Result: The shortcut fails: all 36 quality–safety pairings show direction split across models; 9 hidden-danger rows and 1 near-hidden-danger row exhibit stable or improved quality while refusal declines by 12–68 percentage points; seven of 11 AWQ/GPTQ rows are hidden-danger. The four-probe follow-up yields weak separators for dangerous rows; increased quantization error concentrates on safety-associated neurons (≈1.39×, p<5×10⁻⁷) but is not regime-specific. Sonnet 4 relabels 11,470 items and agrees with the primary judge on 89.9% of rows (κ=0.873); it changes no hidden-danger labels. RTSI routes 10/10 hidden- or near-hidden-danger rows to direct safety testing (Wilson 95% CI lower bound 0.72), while many non-baseline rows fall into a low-risk bucket in-sample and LOOCV. Best single-feature baselines recover 9/10 and 8/10, respectively, but cross-stack transfer requires recalibration.

Conclusion: Across the studied configurations, retained quality does not obviate the need for direct safety evaluation in quantized checkpoints; a calibrated, behaviorally informed screening pipeline (RTSI) is essential for reliable safety routing.

Abstract: Quantized checkpoints are often screened first with quality metrics and only later, if at all, with direct safety tests. This paper audits that shortcut on a matched 51-row matrix spanning 6 models, 4 families, a 7-level GGUF ladder, and AWQ/GPTQ INT4 checkpoints. In this matrix the shortcut fails: all 36 quality-safety pairings split direction across models, and 9 hidden-danger rows plus 1 near-hidden-danger row show quality stable or improved while refusal falls by 12-68 percentage points. Seven of the 11 AWQ/GPTQ rows are hidden-danger. A four-probe mechanistic follow-up over the 17 Hugging Face-backed FP16/AWQ/GPTQ cells does not rescue it: entropy, refusal-direction, and calibration probes are weak or null separators of dangerous rows, and although probe-identified safety-associated neurons absorb 1.39$\times$ more quantization error overall ($p < 5 \times 10^{-7}$), the effect is not regime-specific. Claude Sonnet 4 relabels 11,470 items in a predefined stratified set, agrees with the primary gemma3:12b judge on 89.9\% of rows ($κ= 0.873$, 95\% CI [0.866, 0.881]), and changes 0/10 hidden-danger cells. A calibrated study-internal behavioral screen -- the Refusal Template Stability Index (RTSI), built from four refusal-template drift features and calibrated on this matrix -- routes 10/10 hidden- or near-hidden-danger rows to direct safety testing (Wilson 95\% CI lower bound 0.72) while leaving 23 of 45 non-baseline rows in a low-risk bucket under both in-sample scoring and row-level leave-one-out validation; on the same matrix, the best single-feature baselines (unique-prefix-rate-delta, raw refusal-rate delta) recover 9/10 and 8/10 respectively at matched bucket size, and cross-stack transfer requires recalibration. For the quantized checkpoints, model families, and safety outcomes studied here, retained quality cannot waive direct safety evaluation.

</details>


### [174] [Learning Entropy and Spatial Adaptation Dynamics of Multilayer Perceptrons for Structural Point Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10170)
*Jan Glaser,Ivo Bukovsky,Marcel Jirina*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Spatial Learning Entropy Maps (SLEM) extend Learning Entropy to the spatial domain in image-based MLPs, highlighting informative locations via weight-adaptation entropy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Shift analysis from static image structure to learning dynamics, providing a learning-centric explainability tool that complements traditional feature-based methods.

Method: Train an MLP to predict a center pixel from its spatial context; compute Learning Entropy from the incremental weight updates during learning on image-derived samples; output Spatial Learning Entropy Maps identifying impactful locations.

Result: SLEM identifies unusual image points/regions that elicit strong weight adaptation, offering a learning-centric complement to conventional feature extraction and explainability methods.

Conclusion: The framework enables learning-driven image/scene analysis and could benefit domains such as computer vision, manufacturing, and robotics.

Abstract: This paper extends the concept of Learning Entropy (LE) from temporal adaptive systems to spatial learning in multilayer perceptron networks (MLPs) applied to image data. Instead of evaluating image structure directly from gradients or covariance operators, as local neighborhood methods do, the proposed approach analyzes the learning process itself through Learning Entropy. An MLP is trained to predict the intensity of a center pixel from its surrounding spatial context, while LE is evaluated from the incremental adaptation of neural weights during learning across image-derived samples. The resulting Spatial Learning Entropy Maps (SLEM) identify unusual image points and regions that induce strong adaptation of the neural network and therefore have an important role in the learning process. The results indicate that spatial Learning Entropy provides a complementary perspective to conventional feature extraction and explainability methods by highlighting spatial locations that are particularly informative for network learning. Spatial Learning Entropy provides a complementary perspective to conventional feature extraction and explainability methods by identifying image points and regions according to their learning impact rather than their local structural properties. The proposed framework may open new directions for learning-driven image or scene analysis in computer vision, manufacturing, and robotics.

</details>


### [175] [Dropout-GRPO: Variational Stochasticity for Continuous Latent Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10184)
*Wooil Jung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dropout-GRPO injects stochasticity into latent reasoning via a single shared Bernoulli mask per rollout, enabling group-relative policy optimization for latent-reasoning LLMs and yielding a GSM8K pass@1 gain (27.29% → 29.01%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO requires diverse rollouts; in continuous latent reasoning models like Coconut the latent phase is deterministic, causing zero group-mean advantage and hindering GRPO; thus a principled stochastic mechanism is needed.

Method: Apply a single Bernoulli mask shared across all latent recurrence steps per rollout (structured dropout); treat each rollout as a posterior sample from a variational distribution over parameters; optimize the expected reward of a Bayesian model-average policy; provide theoretical claims: unbiasedness, variance reduction, well-defined latent gradient.

Result: Theoretical justification and empirical validation; GSM8K shows improvement: pass@1 from 27.29% to 29.01% for Coconut baseline.

Conclusion: This approach offers a practical, theoretically grounded method for enabling post-training GRPO in latent-reasoning LLMs.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) relies on the diversity of $K$ rollouts within each group; otherwise, the group-mean advantage $A^{(k)} = r^{(k)} - μ_r$ collapses to zero. This presents a structural challenge for latent-reasoning models like Coconut, which feed continuous hidden states recurrently in place of discrete chain-of-thought tokens. Because the latent phase is inherently deterministic given the parameters and prompt, multiple rollouts produce identical trajectories, stalling GRPO's progress. Consequently, applying group-relative reinforcement learning to continuous latent reasoning has proven difficult.
  To address this, we propose sourcing the necessary stochasticity through structured dropout. By applying a single Bernoulli mask held constant across all latent recurrence steps for a given rollout, we generate essential trajectory variance. This shared mask effectively treats each rollout as a posterior sample from a variational distribution over parameters, allowing GRPO to optimize the expected reward of a Bayesian model-average policy. We provide both theoretical justification for this method -- including unbiasedness, variance reduction, and the well-definedness of the latent gradient -- and empirical validation. On GSM8K, dropout-GRPO improves a Coconut baseline from $27.29\%$ to $29.01\%$ pass@1, demonstrating the viability of GRPO learning for latent-reasoning models. Our work positions this as a practical, theoretically grounded approach for post-training latent-reasoning LLMs.

</details>


### [176] [MMClima: A Framework for Multimodal Climate Science Data and Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10194)
*Muhammad Umer Sheikh,Hassan Abid,Khawar Shehzad,Ufaq Khan,Muhammad Haris Khan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MMClima is a large-scale multimodal climate QA dataset with 104k+ expert-validated QA pairs spanning articles, video transcripts, and figures; built via automated claim extraction and human-in-the-loop QA synthesis; benchmarks multimodal LMs; includes domain-adapted textual baseline mmclima-70b-txt; dataset, eval pipeline, model weights, and data-creation framework released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Climate science requires AI that can reason across text, dynamic visuals, and scientific figures; existing climate QA benchmarks are small, text-only, and limited in model coverage; a standardized multimodal benchmark is needed for climate research.

Method: Automated claim extraction and QA synthesis from heterogeneous sources (articles, video transcripts, figures) with human-in-the-loop validation; evaluation of state-of-the-art multimodal language models on tasks involving factual recall, visual interpretation, and cross-modal synthesis; fine-tuning on the textual subset to create mmclima-70b-txt.

Result: 104k+ expert-validated QA pairs; benchmarks state-of-the-art multimodal LMs on cross-modal tasks; mmclima-70b-txt outperforms strong open- and closed-source models on textual QA; dataset, evaluation pipeline, fine-tuned weights, and data-creation framework released.

Conclusion: Provides a scalable, standardized multimodal evaluation framework for climate science and a domain-adapted baseline, enabling reproducible research and broader evaluation of climate-focused multimodal AI.

Abstract: Climate change research increasingly requires AI systems that reason across text, dynamic visual content, and scientific figures, yet existing climate QA benchmarks are small, mostly textual, and cover a narrow range of models. We introduce MMClima, a large-scale multimodal climate question answering framework with 104k+ expert-validated question-answer pairs spanning articles, video transcriptions, and figures across five core climate science domains. MMClima is constructed via automated claim extraction and QA synthesis with human-in-the-loop validation to ensure both scale and reliability. Using MMClima, we benchmark state-of-the-art multimodal language models on tasks requiring factual recall, visual interpretation, and cross-modal synthesis. We additionally fine-tune on the textual split to produce mmclima-70b-txt, a domain-adapted baseline that outperforms strong open- and closed-source models on textual QA. We release the dataset, evaluation pipeline, fine-tuned model weights, and data creation framework to support standardized multimodal evaluation for climate science.

</details>


### [177] [Density Ridge Selective Prediction for LLM and VLM Hallucination Detection under Calibration Label Scarcity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10198)
*Nina I. Shamsi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Ridge-based hallucination detection improves AUROC under label scarcity by modeling LLM output as a density-ridge on a 6D kinematic feature map of generation trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address selective prediction for hallucination detection by overcoming limitations of unsupervised detectors (plateauing) and supervised probes (data-hungry, sensitive to calibration labels).

Method: Build a 6D feature map from hidden-state generation trajectories, fit a kernel density estimate, extract density ridges (the response manifold), and score test generations by negated Euclidean distance to the nearest ridge vertex; produce a low-dimensional geometric skeleton of the stochastic output distribution. Evaluated against baselines on seven QA benchmarks using nine text/vision LLMs under a label-scarce protocol (n_cal=200, N=5).

Result: The ridge-based score yields 5–20 point AUROC gains over baselines (Semantic Entropy, SAR, EigenScore, SAPLMA, and log-probability) and shows tempered degradation when calibration labels are scarce.

Conclusion: The density-ridge geometry of LLM output provides a robust, label-efficient hallucination detector that outperforms existing methods across multiple benchmarks and modalities.

Abstract: Hallucination detection in large language and vision-language models is increasingly framed as selective prediction, where a detector assigns a confidence score and abstains when confidence is low. Unsupervised sampling detectors (Semantic Entropy, EigenScore) avoid labels but plateau in quality, while supervised probes (SAPLMA) attain stronger in-distribution scores yet degrade sharply when calibration labels are scarce. We recover the response manifold of an LLM as the density ridge of a kernel density estimate built on a six-dimensional kinematic feature map of hidden state generation trajectories. A test generation is scored by the negated Euclidean distance from its projected feature point to the nearest ridge vertex, yielding a low-dimensional geometric skeleton of the stochastic output distribution. We evaluate against Semantic Entropy, SAR, EigenScore, SAPLMA, and log-probability on seven QA benchmarks (HaluEval-QA, TriviaQA, GSM8K, POPE, ScienceQA, A-OKVQA) using nine text and vision LLMs in a deliberately label-scarce protocol ($n_{\text{cal}}{=}200$ queries, $N{=}5$ generations). Our ridge-based score beats on AUROC with 5-20 points gain, while demonstrating tempered degradation under calibration-label scarcity.

</details>


### [178] [A Continuous-Time Markov Chain Framework for Insertion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10199)
*Dhruvesh Patel,Benjamin Rozonoyer,Soumitra Das,Tahira Naseem,Tim G. J. Rudner,Andrew McCallum*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A diffusion-style denoising objective for Insertion Language Models (ILMs) derived from a continuous-time Markov process on variable-length sequences; unifies prior ILMs under a single framework; competitive with left-to-right and masked diffusion models; adds sampling flexibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current insertion-based language models are largely ad-hoc. A principled, diffusion-based formulation could unify ILMs with diffusion models, clarify the noising/denoising process, and improve sampling flexibility and performance on both synthetic planning tasks and language modeling.

Method: Formulate the noising process as a continuous-time Markov chain over variable-length sequences and derive a diffusion-style denoising objective for ILMs. Show that existing ILM formulations are special cases of this framework. Evaluate empirically on a synthetic planning task and on language modeling tasks, comparing to left-to-right generation and masked diffusion baselines.

Result: The proposed diffusion-based ILM approach retains the advantages of insertion-based generation over left-to-right and masked diffusion models on the planning task. In language modeling, it is competitive with left-to-right generation and masked diffusion models while offering greater sampling flexibility than existing insertion language models.

Conclusion: A principled diffusion-based framework for insertion language models is feasible and advantageous, unifying previous ad-hoc ILM formulations, and delivering competitive performance with additional sampling flexibility.

Abstract: Insertion Language Models (ILMs) offer several advantages over left-to-right generation and mask-based generation. However, existing formulations of insertion-based generation have largely been ad-hoc. In this paper, we derive a diffusion-style denoising objective for ILMs from first principles by formulating the noising process as a continuous-time Markov chain on the space of variable-length sequences. We show that previous formulations of ILMs can be viewed as special cases of this denoising framework. Through empirical evaluation on a synthetic planning task, we show that the proposed approach retains the benefits of insertion-based generation over left-to-right generation and masked diffusion models.
