Prior studies largely rely on heuristics that perform coarse-grained matching. They lack sufficient constraints and ignore distributional alignment, leading to representation drift and limited generalization. Building on Energy-based Models (EBMs), we propose an Energy-based Representation Alignment (ERAlign) framework that projects GNN-encoded graph structure and LLM-derived text embeddings in a shared latent space to achieve distribution consistency. Concretely, layer-wise alignment is quantified by a distance metric and optimized via an EBM objective. By decreasing energy values, our framework yields well-aligned representations for downstream tasks. During training, we introduce Energy Discrepancy (ED) to avoid high sampling costs associated with intractable normalization. ED also carries theoretical guarantees of higher training efficiency and reduced energy landscape distortion. Empirical evaluations on eight TAG datasets demonstrate that ERAlign obtains state-of-the-art performance across varying levels of supervision and cross-task transfer scenarios.

</details>


### [204] [UPLOTS: A Unified Pretrained Language Model for Constrained Time-series Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10466)
*Du Yin,Hao Xue,Jinliang Deng,Yang Yang,Shuang Ao,Arian Prabowo,Flora Salim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A unified prompt-guided transformer for constrained time-series generation across domains (UPLOTS) that uses dynamic multi-dataset loss re-weighting and prompt-to-pattern mapping to learn diverse temporal structures and enable on-demand generation; it shows cross-domain generalization and improved data augmentation with scarce data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current time-series generation methods are fragmented with dataset-specific models, limiting scalability and cross-domain learning of shared temporal patterns. A unified approach with constraint prompts could leverage cross-domain temporal structures and reduce model fragmentation.

Method: Use a single pre-trained transformer backbone guided by learned constraint prompts. Implement dynamic multi-dataset loss re-weighting and a prompt-to-pattern mapping to internalize diverse temporal structures during training, enabling conditional generation at inference across various constraint settings.

Result: Evaluations on four real-world benchmarks with multiple constraint settings (e.g., peak-period, calendar, load-level, volatility patterns) and held-out constraint-combinations show that UPLOTS generalizes beyond the peak-pattern setting and improves data augmentation under scarce real-data regimes; code is available at an anonymized repository.

Conclusion: UPLOTS offers a scalable, cross-domain solution for constrained time-series generation, achieving knowledge sharing across datasets and enabling on-demand pattern-controlled synthesis, with demonstrated generalization and data-augmentation benefits.

Abstract: In time-series generation, existing approaches typically handcraft ortrain a separate model for each dataset, which hinders their scalability and fails to leverage shared temporal structures across domains. To address this fragmentation, we propose UPLOTS, a Unified, Prompt-guided Language model framework fOr constrained Time-Series Generation across diverse domains. Instead of building task-specific models, UPLOTS leverages a single pre-trained transformer backbone guided by learned constraint prompts, enabling on-demand generation with precise pattern control. One key innovation is our dynamic multi-dataset loss re-weighting and prompt-to-pattern mapping, which allows UPLOTS to internalize diverse temporal structures during training and conditionally generate them at inference. We evaluate UPLOTS on four real-world benchmarks and multiple constraint settings, including peak-period, calendar, load-level, and volatility patterns. Additional held-out constraint-combination and downstream forecasting experiments further demonstrate that UPLOTS generalizes beyond the original peak-pattern setting and improves data augmentation under scarce real-data regimes. Our code and baselines are available at anonymous github repo: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/UPLOTS-6C36.

</details>


### [205] [Advancing the State-of-the-Art in Empirical Privacy Auditing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10481)
*Nicole Mitchell,Galen Andrew,Arun Ganesh,Brendan McMahan,Peter Kairouz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes empirical privacy auditing (EPA) for parameter-efficient fine-tuning of LLMs using high-entropy synthetic canaries generated at high temperature to induce strong memorization signals; supplements with a synthetic-data audit via an auxiliary model to estimate leakage; analyzes how model capacity and canary entropy affect memorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantify and mitigate privacy leakage risks in fine-tuned LLMs and synthetic data generation. Existing audits struggle with identifying and measuring leakage; synthetic canaries and synthetic-data audits offer stronger, inspectable signals.

Method: 1) Generate synthetic canaries by high-temperature sampling (T ≥ 0.8) from LLMs using prompts tailored to privacy-sensitive data. 2) Insert canaries into training data to create high-influence outliers for robust membership inference and reconstruction audits. 3) Fine-tune an auxiliary model on synthetic data and audit its ability to recover original canaries to estimate leakage through synthetic data. 4) Systematically study how model capacity and canary entropy influence memorization.

Result: Canaries act as high-identifiability outliers, enabling strong empirical privacy audits. The synthetic-data audit provides a practical estimate of leakage through synthetic data. The framework reveals interaction effects between model capacity and canary entropy on memorization, informing risk assessment and defense strategies.

Conclusion: A practical EPA framework combining high-entropy synthetic canaries and synthetic-data auditing offers robust measurement of privacy leakage in fine-tuned LLMs and data-generation pipelines, with insights into how capacity and canary entropy shape memorization behavior.

Abstract: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) can exhibit problematic memorization of individual training examples. Empirical privacy auditing (EPA) quantifies this risk by measuring realistic data leakage on membership inference (MI) or reconstruction attacks. A key challenge in EPA is designing ``canary'' examples that are mixed with the privacy-sensitive training data. We propose generating synthetic canaries via high-temperature sampling ($T \geq 0.8$) from LLMs, using prompts tailored to the privacy-sensitive training data. These canaries act as high-influence outliers, ensuring high identifiability and hence strong audits. Further, since the canaries are themselves non-private, they are inspectable and can be inserted with repetition without jeopardizing the privacy of the real data. An important use of models fine-tuned on privacy-sensitive data is the generation of synthetic data. This also comes with privacy risk. We introduce a powerful synthetic data audit based on fine-tuning an auxiliary model on the synthetic data. Auditing the auxiliary model for the original canaries then provides a strong estimate of the privacy leakage through the synthetic data. Finally, leveraging our strong auditing methodologies, we perform a systematic investigation into the interacting effects of model capacity and canary entropy on memorization.

</details>


### [206] [Stop Early, Spend Less: Hidden-State Probes as a Practical Recipe for Streaming Moderation of LLM Outputs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10487)
*Huizhen Shu,Xuying Li,Piao Xue*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Token-level safety probes operating on model activations can approximate a strong moderation model with far lower latency and compute, enabling online, token-level filtering during decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce latency and compute of safety filtering in user-facing LLMs by leveraging internal activations to enable preemptive checks, replacing post hoc moderation.

Method: Train lightweight token-level probes on internal activations; produce per-token safety scores; reuse generator activations; no extra forward pass; apply to a mid-layer; aggregate scores for offline/online; deployment recipe; linear component corresponds to a residual-space direction.

Result: Single mid-layer probe recovers most decisions of a strong guard model; orders of magnitude lower compute overhead; sub-millisecond per-token checks; enables halt/modify in streaming; provides practical deployment guidelines; enables activation steering via the linear component.

Conclusion: Internal activation probes offer an efficient surrogate for moderation with low latency and cost, suitable for continuous token-level monitoring during generation, with a practical deployment recipe.

Abstract: Deploying large language models in user-facing systems requires efficient output safety filtering. Existing approaches typically rely on a separate moderation model applied after generation, which doubles inference cost and only detects violations after generation completes. We observe that the signal needed for moderation is already present in the model hidden states. Based on this, we train lightweight token-level probes that operate directly on internal activations, producing per-token safety scores that can be aggregated for both offline evaluation and online intervention. The probe reuses activations from the generator and requires no additional forward pass, enabling sub millisecond per-token safety checks inside the decoding loop. A probe applied to a single mid layer recovers most decisions of a strong guard model, acting as a low cost surrogate optimized for latency rather than accuracy. In streaming settings, it can halt or modify unsafe outputs before they are fully generated, replacing end of sequence moderation with continuous token level monitoring. Compared to post hoc and streaming guard models, our method achieves orders of magnitude lower compute overhead with minimal latency cost. We also provide a practical deployment recipe, including layer selection, aggregation strategy, probing frequency, and triggering thresholds. Finally, we show that the probe linear component corresponds to a direction in residual space, enabling both detection and activation steering at negligible cost.

</details>


### [207] [MoE Enhanced Federated Learning for Spatiotemporal Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10499)
*Zhehao Dai,Xiao Han,Zhaolin Deng,Zijian Zhang,Xiangyu Zhao,Guojiang Shen,Xiangjie Kong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A privacy-preserving personalized federated cross-city traffic prediction framework (MoE-FedTP) using lightweight Mixture-of-Experts improves accuracy for data-scarce cities by modeling city-specific heterogeneity through gated expert fusion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data scarcity in many cities due to limited sensors and uneven development; centralized models raise privacy issues; cross-city transfer faces spatiotemporal heterogeneity that existing federated methods struggle with.

Method: Introduce MoE-FedTP: spatiotemporal neural networks extract features from source and target cities; build expert networks derived from source cities via partial parameter sharing; a gating mechanism dynamically fuses experts to capture diverse traffic dynamics; privacy preserved through federated setup; lightweight MoE design.

Result: Empirical evaluation on four real-world traffic datasets shows MoE-FedTP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art cross-city and federated baselines, improving prediction accuracy for data-scarce cities.

Conclusion: MoE-FedTP effectively handles heterogeneity and privacy in cross-city traffic prediction, demonstrating the benefits of personalized MoE with partial parameter sharing for data-scarce environments.

Abstract: Traffic prediction is fundamental to intelligent transportation systems and urban computing, yet many cities continue to suffer from traffic data scarcity due to limited sensor deployment and uneven urban development. Cross-city knowledge transfer has thus attracted increasing attention, enabling data-rich cities to assist data-scarce ones. However, centralized approaches raise privacy concerns, while existing federated methods struggle with pronounced spatiotemporal heterogeneity across cities. To address these challenges, we propose MoE-FedTP, a personalized federated cross-city spatiotemporal prediction framework based on lightweight Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) networks. MoE-FedTP first employs spatiotemporal neural networks to extract features from both source and target cities, then introduces a set of expert networks derived from different source cities through partial parameter sharing. A gating mechanism dynamically fuses the experts to capture diverse traffic dynamics, achieving fine-grained modeling of urban heterogeneity while preserving privacy. Experiments on four real-world traffic datasets show that MoE-FedTP consistently outperforms state-of-the-art cross-city and federated learning baselines, demonstrating its effectiveness in enhancing prediction accuracy for data-scarce cities.

</details>


### [208] [Representation-Aware Advantage Estimation: Your Reward Model Provides More Than A Scalar Output](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10528)
*Guozheng Li,Xiyan Fu,Yiwen Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Represents a representation-aware advantage estimation (GraphAE) that uses RM hidden states to form graphs for advantage propagation, improving RLHF performance across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scalar rewards from reward models (RMs) are noisy and fail to capture fine-grained preference differences; RM hidden states encode richer semantic and preference information that can be leveraged for better advantage estimation.

Method: Construct a graph per sampled group: nodes are responses, edges reflect similarity in the RM hidden space. Compute advantages via graph propagation to incorporate contextual information from neighbors. GraphAE is lightweight and integrates with existing group-based RLHF algorithms (GRPO, GSPO, RLOO).

Result: Empirical results show consistent improvements across three benchmarks: up to +6.3 on Arena-Hard-v0.1, +8.27 on AlpacaEval 2.0, and +0.22 on MT-Bench, indicating improved sample efficiency and robustness.

Conclusion: Leveraging RM representations through GraphAE enhances RLHF performance and can be readily adopted in existing group-based RLHF pipelines, offering a lightweight path to better sample efficiency and robustness.

Abstract: Current reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) methods primarily rely on scalar rewards from a trained reward model (RM). While effective, scalar rewards are often noisy and fail to capture fine-grained preference differences, whereas RM hidden states encode richer semantic and preference information. We introduce the representation-aware advantage estimation, which leverages RM hidden states and models them as auxiliary signals for better advantage estimation. Specifically, we propose the Graph-based Advantage Estimation (GraphAE), treat each sampled group as a graph, where nodes correspond to responses and edges capture their similarity in the RM hidden space. Then advantages are computed via graph propagation, enabling each sample to incorporate contextual information from its neighbors. GraphAE is lightweight and can be seamlessly integrated into existing group-based RL algorithms. We apply GraphAE to GRPO, GSPO and RLOO, and conduct extensive experiments on different models and benchmarks. Empirical results show consistent improvements across three benchmarks, with gains of up to + 6.3 on Arena-Hard-v0.1, + 8.27 on AlpacaEval 2.0, and + 0.22 on MT-Bench. These results demonstrate that leveraging RM representations leads to more sample efficient and robust RLHF.

</details>


### [209] [Machine Learning Methods for Studying Latent Neural Activity Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10530)
*Shufeng Kong,Fumei Deng,Xinyi Dong,Caihua Liu,Weiwei Chen,Yingheng Wang,Daniel Cao,Azahara Oliva,Antonio Fernandez-Ruiz,Carla Gomes*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive taxonomy and synthesis of latent variable models for neural population dynamics, spanning single-region dynamics, inter-regional communication, behavior-aligned modeling, and modern foundation models, with benchmarks and open challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To consolidate rapid advances in latent variable modeling for neural decoding, provide guidance on evaluation, causal inference, cross-subject generalization, and future research directions.

Method: Literature review and taxonomy across three domains (single-region dynamics, multi-region communication, behavior-aligned modeling), analysis of classical and deep generative approaches, and discussion of large-scale foundation models and evaluation criteria.

Result: A structured framework classifying existing models, highlighting integration with behavior and inter-regional communication, and identifying gaps such as causality/directionality; outlines benchmarks, evaluation criteria, and open challenges for bridging interpretable brain dynamics with reliable neural decoding.

Conclusion: Summarizes key insights, emphasizes benchmarks and open challenges, and anticipates impact of foundation models; calls for methods to identify causal links, directionality, and robust decoding across subjects.

Abstract: Recent developments in brain recording are driving a demand for machine learning tools capable of decoding the latent structure of large populations of neurons. In this paper, we provide a comprehensive survey that outlines the trajectory of Latent Variable Models (LVMs) from early state-space models to more recent deep generative models. We organize the literature into three closely related domains: (1) Single-Region Latent Dynamics, which includes models such as linear dynamical systems to more complex dynamics represented by Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) and Neural Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs); (2) Multi-Region Communication, which employs probabilistic as well as subspace methods to study how information is transferred across different brain areas considering synaptic propagation delays and network connectivity; and (3) Behavior-Aligned Modeling, which seeks to disentangle neural activity related to task performance from other internal states via supervised or contrastive learning. This survey also includes large-scale neural foundation models, such as Transformers and diffusion models, that rely on large-scale pre-training for optimal performance across subjects. Finally, we conclude and discuss benchmarks, evaluation criteria, and open challenges, such as the ability to identify causal links or directionality of communication, to facilitate future research for bridging interpretable brain dynamics with reliable neural decoding.

</details>


### [210] [Flexible Flows for Biological Sequence Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10543)
*Yogesh Verma,Dani Korpela,Harri Lähdesmäki,Vikas Garg*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Structured coupling and latent edit-based control extend Discrete Flow Matching (DFM) for biological sequences, enabling variable-length generation, improved realism, and test-time controllability, achieving state-of-the-art across density estimation, DNA, and peptide sequence tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the mismatch between discrete sequence spaces and biologically plausible generation in DFM, due to uninformative couplings and limited control over length and edits.

Method: 1) Introduce domain-informed structured couplings that bias the source distribution toward plausible sequence regions without changing the flow objective or training procedure. 2) Develop a latent edit-based rate parameterization for variable-length generation via edits conditioned on a shared global latent (latent variable model). 3) Add latent classifier-free guidance to steer generation in continuous latent space, and Dirichlet-prior temperature scaling for test-time control over edits.

Result: State-of-the-art performance across diverse biological sequence tasks, including density estimation, unconditional and conditional DNA sequence generation, and peptide sequence generation.

Conclusion: The approach yields more realistic, controllable sequence generation without modifying the flow objective, extending DFM to variable-length sequences and enabling coherent guidance across latent space for varied biological tasks.

Abstract: Designing functional biological sequences requires navigating vast discrete spaces under strict evolutionary and biophysical constraints. Discrete Flow Matching (DFM) offers a generative framework over such spaces, but existing approaches rely on biologically uninformative couplings and offer limited flexibility for variable-length sequence generation and fine-grained control. We propose a structured coupling that encodes domain-specific preferences among sequence elements, biasing the source distribution toward plausible regions without modifying the flow objective or training procedure. Building on this, we introduce a latent edit-based rate parameterization that models variable-length generation via edit operations conditioned on a shared global latent, akin to a latent variable model, while remaining tractable. We further introduce a latent classifier-free guidance mechanism that steers generation coherently in continuous latent space, along with Dirichlet-prior temperature scaling for test-time control over edit operations. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse biological sequence tasks, including density estimation, unconditional and conditional DNA sequence generation, and peptide sequence generation.

</details>


### [211] [Convergence of Monte Carlo Optimistic Policy Iteration: Beyond Uniform State-Action Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10580)
*Octave Oliviers,Glenn Vinnicombe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Initial-visit MC-O-PI converges to optimality even when updates are uniform only over actions within each state, relaxing the need for uniform initialization over the entire state-action space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the long-standing question of MC-O-PI convergence in unknown-model settings and to relax an impractical prior requirement that episodes be uniformly initiated across all state-action pairs.

Method: Analyze MC-O-PI under per-state action-level uniform updates. Use mean-field dynamics to prove monotone policy improvement, then extend the lock-in argument of the stability-ODE method to show that noise cannot prevent improvement, establishing convergence to optimal policies.

Result: Proves convergence of initial-visit MC-O-PI to optimality under the weaker per-state action uniformity condition; allows arbitrary state visitation frequencies; provides a new analytic route for optimistic policy-iteration algorithms.

Conclusion: The work broadens the applicability of MC-O-PI and introduces a general analytical framework for studying optimistic policy-iteration algorithms, especially in large or unknown state spaces with tractable per-state action sets.

Abstract: The asymptotic behaviour of Monte Carlo optimistic policy iteration (MC-O-PI) is a long-standing open question. When the model of the environment is unknown, as is common in practice, the only known condition that guarantees convergence to optimality is impractical. In its canonical form, this condition requires that the episodes used for policy evaluation be initialised uniformly over the entire state-action space. This paper strictly relaxes that requirement. Specifically, we prove that initial-visit MC-O-PI converges to optimality even when updates are uniform only over the actions within each state. This allows episodes to start in different states at arbitrary frequencies; a realistic implementation when the state space is large or unknown but the action space in each state is manageable. The proof departs from the classical analysis of Tsitsiklis whose central commutativity argument no longer applies when states are updated at different frequencies. Instead, we first show that the mean-field dynamics of MC-O-PI generate monotonically improving policies when updates are uniform over the actions in each state, and then prove that noise cannot consistently prevent this improvement by extending the lock-in argument of the combined stability-ODE method. This approach suggests a new way to study optimistic policy-iteration algorithms in general.

</details>


### [212] [Drawing with Strangers: Population Scaling Drives Zero-Shot Mutual Intelligibility in Emergent Sketching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10582)
*Jooyeon Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Zero-shot mutual intelligibility (ZMI) improves with larger emergent-sketching populations; cross-group convergence occurs despite in-group variation, driven by perceptual grounding to object-level visual resemblance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address interoperability of emergent communication across disjoint, independently trained populations; introduce ZMI as a new generalization axis for socially interoperable agents.

Method: Train multiple independent populations using emergent sketching (drawn strokes) to communicate target images; vary population size; test ZMI between groups without prior exposure; analyze intra-group variation, cross-group convergence, and alignment to objective visuals.

Result: Larger population scales increase ZMI across independent groups; in-group variation grows with size preventing co-adaptation to homogeneity; cross-group variation decreases, indicating convergence toward a universal communicative form; universality is rooted in perceptual grounding to target image resemblance.

Conclusion: ZMI is a distinct generalization axis in emergent communication; scaling populations fosters cross-group interoperability and a perceptually grounded universality, suggesting a path to socially interoperable artificial agents.

Abstract: Generalization in emergent communication has largely focused on novel inputs or linguistic structures, yet the capacity for agents to communicate with strangers from strictly disjoint communities remains relatively unexplored. In this work, we formalize this capability as \textit{zero-shot mutual intelligibility (ZMI)}: successful communication between independently trained populations without prior exposure. Leveraging emergent sketching -- in which agents communicate through sets of drawn strokes -- as a visually grounded modality, we find that scaling the training population substantially improves ZMI across independent groups. Crucially, as we scale the population size, in-group communicative variation increases, preventing co-adaptation into homogeneity. Simultaneously, cross-group variation decreases, indicating a structural convergence toward a certain type of universality. Further analysis reveals that this universality is achieved through perceptual grounding: scaled populations increasingly anchor their emergent sketches on the objective visual resemblance of the target images. Together, these results position ZMI as a distinct axis of generalization in emergent communication and suggest a route toward socially interoperable artificial agents.

</details>


### [213] [NOVA: Symbolic Regression Discovery of Interpretable Car-Following and Lane-Change Models with Driver Heterogeneity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10583)
*Ishak Abassi,Nassim Ali Bouazzouni,Farah Ibelaiden,Nadir Farhi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NOVA is an autonomous symbolic regression framework that learns interpretable car-following and lane-change models from large-scale trajectory data, delivering competitive accuracy with zero-shot transfer and interpretable terms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extract interpretable, physics-informed driving behavior structures directly from raw trajectory data with minimal priors, achieving robustness, transferability, and theoretical interpretability.

Method: A Rust-powered, deterministic symbolic-regression search evaluating over 10k algebraic structures to identify a compact two-term acceleration model using a forward-shifted rolling-mean target. Two preprocessing pipelines are tested. For lane-change, a multinomial logit framework is used. The approach emphasizes zero-shot transfer of learned feature operators between freeway sites and includes a residual-guided extension linked to psychophysical collision-avoidance theory.

Result: On 4.77M driving observations (NGSIM I-80/US-101): RMSE 1.376 m/s^2, R^2 15.57% on the intent-forecasting benchmark, beating SR-LLM (PNAS 2025) by 0.135 m/s^2 RMSE under identical evaluation. Eight experiments reveal a dominant nonlinear term as the backbone of car-following; residual-guided extension connects to psychophysical theory. Features transfer with <3 percentage points R^2 loss across freeway sites. Lane-change: 67.4% balanced accuracy under strict driver holdout on 502 unseen drivers, +29.8 pp over baselines on a three-class problem.

Conclusion: A robust, transferable nonlinear term underpins human car-following, captured by an autonomous symbolic-regression framework that also extends to lane-change modeling. The approach yields interpretable models with strong cross-site transfer and competitive predictive performance, aligning with psychophysical theory for collision avoidance.

Abstract: We present NOVA, an autonomous symbolic regression framework that identifies interpretable car-following and lane-change structures from raw trajectory data with minimal behavioral priors. Applied to 4,765,788 active driving observations from the NGSIM I-80 and US-101 datasets, NOVA's deterministic Rust-powered search engine evaluates over 10,000 candidate algebraic structures and identifies a compact two-term acceleration model under a forward-shifted rolling-mean prediction target. Evaluated under two complementary preprocessing pipelines, NOVA achieves $RMSE = 1.376 m/s^2$ ($R^2 = 15.57\%$) on the intent-forecasting benchmark, outperforming the best recalibrated symbolic-regression baseline (SR-LLM, PNAS~2025) by 0.135 m/s$^2$ in RMSE under an identical evaluation protocol. Across eight independent experiments, a single dominant nonlinear term emerges as a robust backbone of human car-following; a residual-guided extension further links the selected structure to an established psychophysical theory of collision avoidance. The discovered feature operators transfer zero-shot between freeway sites with under 3 pp $R^2$ loss. Extended to lane-change modelling within a multinomial logit framework, NOVA achieves 67.4\% balanced accuracy under strict vehicle-ID holdout on 502 unseen drivers, surpassing existing lane-changing baselines by +29.8 percentage points on a three-class problem.

</details>


### [214] [Towards Diverse Scientific Hypothesis Search with Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10587)
*Haorui Wang,Parshin Shojaee,Kazem Meidani,Kunyang Sun,José Miguel Hernández-Lobato,Teresa Head-Gordon,Jiajun He,Chandan K. Reddy,Chao Zhang,Yuanqi Du*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: We present 4ours, a multi-temperature evolutionary sampling framework for hypothesis generation inspired by parallel tempering, enabling exchange across temperature levels to maintain diversity and improve quality under a fixed validation budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In scientific discovery, validating hypotheses is costly and noisy. Traditional evolutionary search prioritizes optimization, causing diversity collapse and poor exploration. A sampling-based approach with multiple temperatures can maintain exploration while ensuring convergence.

Method: We adapt parallel tempering into an evolutionary framework for hypothesis search. The population is maintained across multiple temperature levels; hypotheses migrate between levels with principled exchange. This sampling-based, temperature-graded search promotes exploration without sacrificing convergence and is applied across molecular discovery, equation discovery, and algorithm discovery.

Result: Across domains, ours improves both hypothesis quality and diversity under the same validation budget and yields candidates that remain robust under more expensive downstream validations, outperforming standard optimization-focused approaches.

Conclusion: Multi-temperature, parallel-tempering-inspired evolutionary search effectively mitigates diversity collapse, enabling robust, diverse, high-quality hypothesis sets under fixed validation budgets across diverse scientific discovery tasks.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are on the rise for accelerating scientific discovery, most recently in advanced tasks such as generating valid scientific hypotheses. Yet in many discovery settings, the goal is not to identify a single best hypothesis since validation can be noisy and expensive, and scientists benefit from a set of high-quality alternative hypotheses that hedge against downstream uncertainty for the best solutions. Nevertheless, commonly used evolutionary search recipes tend to prioritize optimization over exploration in hypothesis generation, and the resulting selection pressure during the search process leads to diversity collapse. Motivated by these limitations, we formulate hypothesis search as a sampling problem, where the objective is to efficiently produce diverse, high-quality hypotheses under a fixed validation budget. Building on this perspective, we propose \ours, an evolutionary framework inspired by the classical parallel tempering algorithm that searches hypotheses at multiple temperature levels and enables principled information exchange across temperatures to improve exploration without disrupting convergence. Across domains including molecular discovery, equation discovery, and algorithm discovery, our approach consistently improves both hypothesis quality and diversity under the same validation budget, and produces candidates that remain robust under more expensive downstream computational validations.

</details>


### [215] [Dirichlet-Guided Group Forecasting for Alleviating Over-smoothing in Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10592)
*Xingyu Zhang,Jingyao Wang,Xin Yu,Zeen Song,Jianqi Zhang,Changwen Zheng,Wenwen Qiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DGF addresses over-smoothing in multi-modal time-series forecasting by modeling multiple mode-conditioned forecasts with Dirichlet-guided sampling, achieving better accuracy, diversity, and dynamical consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forecasting under partial observation and single-realization supervision tends to collapse multiple plausible future modes into a single averaged trajectory, causing over-smoothing and loss of dynamic events such as sharp changes, turning points, and regime shifts.

Method: Propose Dirichlet-Guided Group Forecasting (DGF), a mode-preserving framework that explicitly models multiple mode-conditioned predictive distributions and uncertainty over mode-selection probabilities. It employs a Dirichlet-guided hierarchical sampling mechanism and reward-based optimization to encourage accuracy, dynamic consistency, and mode distinctness.

Result: Extensive experiments on real-world benchmarks show that DGF reduces over-smoothing and improves forecasting accuracy, diversity, and dynamical consistency.

Conclusion: DGF provides a principled approach to multi-modal forecasting by preserving latent dynamical modes, leveraging Dirichlet-guided sampling to maintain mode diversity and accurate, dynamically consistent forecasts.

Abstract: Time series forecasting often suffers from over-smoothing, especially when future dynamics are multi-modal. Forecasts may follow the coarse trend of the observed future, but fail to preserve sharp changes, oscillations, turning points, and regime transitions that define plausible dynamic evolution. In this work, we revisit over-smoothing from the perspective of latent dynamical mode compression: under partial observation and single-realization supervision, multiple plausible future modes can be weakened, merged, or averaged during forecasting. Based on this view, we propose Dirichlet-Guided Group Forecasting (DGF), a mode-preserving forecasting framework that explicitly models multiple mode-conditioned predictive distributions and uncertainty over their selection probabilities. DGF uses a Dirichlet-guided hierarchical sampling mechanism and reward-based optimization to encourage forecasts that are accurate, dynamically consistent, and mode-distinct. Extensive experiments on real-world forecasting benchmarks show that DGF reduces over-smoothing while improving forecasting accuracy, diversity, and dynamical consistency.

</details>


### [216] [Embedding Hybrid Systems into Continuous Latent Vector Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10596)
*Sangli Teng,Hang Liu,Koushil Sreenath*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Embedding a discrete hybrid system into a higher-dimensional Euclidean space with a continuous vector field when the target dimension exceeds twice the state dimension; from this, a latent Neural ODE with consistency losses learns the flow of hybrids from time-series data, outperforming baselines across geometries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of representing intrinsically discontinuous hybrid systems in a differentiable, optimization-friendly framework; enable accurate learning from observations using neural ODEs.

Method: Proves an existence theorem for embedding an n-dimensional hybrid system into an m-dimensional Euclidean space with m>2n and a continuous vector field on the embedded image. Builds a learning approach using a latent Neural ODE with consistency loss in both latent and state spaces to recover the flow of hybrid systems from time-series data.

Result: Theorem guarantees a continuous extrinsic representation; the proposed latent Neural ODE method accurately recovers flows and outperforms existing methods on learning hybrids with varying geometries from time-series data.

Conclusion: Intrinsically discontinuous hybrid dynamics can admit smooth, well-posed extrinsic representations enabling effective learning via constrained latent ODEs; the approach scales to diverse geometries and demonstrates empirical advantages.

Abstract: This work proves that an $n$-dimensional hybrid system can be embedded into an $m$-dimensional Euclidean space equipped with a continuous vector field on its embedded image whenever $m>2n$. This result suggests that an intrinsically discontinuous hybrid system generically admits a continuous extrinsic representation that is well-posed for differentiable optimization. Building on this existence theorem, we show that a latent Neural ODE with consistency loss in both the latent and state space can accurately recover the flow of hybrid systems. Extensive experiments suggest the proposed method outperforms the existing method in learning hybrid systems with varying geometries from only time series data.

</details>


### [217] [Causal Ensemble Agent: Hierarchical Causal Discovery with LLM-guided Expert Reweighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10607)
*Xinyu Li,Yuanyuan Wang,Haoxuan Li,Chuan Zhou,Erdun Gao,Bo Han,Tongliang Liu,Kun Zhang,Howard Bondell,Mingming Gong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CEA: a causal ensemble framework that fuses multiple graph-learning experts via linear opinion pooling and uses an LLM as a meta-referee to reweight near-decision-boundary evidence, yielding improved causal graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Disparate causal discovery algorithms produce conflicting graphs; rich domain information and a data-aligned integration method are lacking. LLM-based direct queries can be unreliable without alignment to data; need robust, interpretable ensemble with meta-analysis.

Method: Aggregate structural insights from diverse statistical discovery experts across multiple graph levels using linear opinion pooling. Employ an LLM as a meta-referee to dynamically reweight expert contributions when aggregate confidence is near the decision boundary, producing a more complete and accurate causal graph.

Result: Experimental validation on synthetic and real-world datasets shows CEA achieves the strongest overall performance across a broad spectrum of causal discovery methods, highlighting the utility of LLMs for meta-analysis in causal discovery.

Conclusion: LLM-assisted meta-analysis via CEA improves robustness and completeness of causal graphs by reconciling diverse expert opinions, outperforming individual methods.

Abstract: Causal discovery aims to uncover causal structures from observational data, which is crucial for real-world decision-making. However, different causal discovery algorithms can produce divergent results that conflict with each other, complicating the identification of accurate causal graphs. Traditional approaches rely on numerical values and statistical assumptions, often ignoring rich domain-specific information, such as feature descriptions, which could also help structure learning. While recent works explore using Large Language Models (LLMs) to infer causal relations via direct queries, such methods can be unreliable due to a lack of alignment with the actual data. To address these limitations, we propose Causal Ensemble Agent (CEA), a novel framework that aggregates structural insights from statistical discovery experts across different graph levels via linear opinion pooling, and uses an LLM as a meta-referee to dynamically reweight experts when the aggregated confidence is close to the decision boundary, thereby composing an improved and more complete causal graph. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that CEA achieves the strongest overall performance across a wide range of causal discovery methods, highlighting the effectiveness of using LLMs for meta-analysis in causal discovery.

</details>


### [218] [Fast and Highly Expressive Policy Learning for Offline Reinforcement Learning via Bootstrapped Flow Q-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10613)
*Thanh Nguyen,Tri Ton,Hongbin Choe,Tung M. Luu,Chang D. Yoo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Bootstrapped Flow Q-Learning (BFQ) enables accurate single-step action generation in diffusion-based offline RL by bootstrapping short flow-path displacements, removing multi-step denoising, and achieving faster, more robust training and inference with strong D4RL results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the computational cost and brittleness of multi-step denoising in diffusion-based offline RL; eliminate reliance on auxiliary networks, policy distillation, or multi-phase training.

Method: Introduce BFQ, a divide-and-conquer approach to the displacement vector along the diffusion flow path. It starts by learning short-range displacements estimable from the Flow Matching marginal velocity and bootstraps these components to learn a noise-to-action mapping in a single step, removing multi-step denoising and auxiliary networks.

Result: BFQ substantially speeds up training and inference, is simpler and more robust, and yields improved performance on D4RL benchmarks compared to multi-step diffusion baselines due to effective single-step action generation.

Conclusion: Single-step action generation is sufficient for high-performance offline RL; BFQ offers a simpler, faster, and more robust alternative to multi-step diffusion Q-learning without auxiliary networks or distillation.

Abstract: Diffusion-based Q-learning has emerged as a powerful paradigm for offline reinforcement learning, but its reliance on multi-step denoising makes both training and inference computationally expensive and brittle. Recent efforts to accelerate diffusion Q-learning toward single-step action generation typically introduce auxiliary networks, policy distillation, or multi-phase training, which frequently compromise simplicity, stability, or performance. To address these limitations, we introduce Bootstrapped Flow Q-Learning (BFQ), a novel framework that enables accurate single-step action generation during both training and inference, without auxiliary networks or distillation procedures. BFQ adopts a divide-and-conquer view of the displacement vector along the flow path: it begins by learning short-range displacements that can be accurately estimated from the Flow Matching marginal velocity, and bootstraps these components to directly learn a noise-to-action mapping in a single step. This formulation eliminates multi-step denoising, resulting in a learning procedure that is substantially faster, simpler, and more robust. Extensive D4RL evaluations show that BFQ improves performance while significantly reducing computational cost compared to multi-step diffusion baselines, demonstrating that single-step action generation suffices for high-performance offline Reinforcement Learning.

</details>


### [219] [Is Fairness Truly Fair? Towards Reliable Lipschitz Fairness in Multi-Task Learning via Fixed-\texorpdfstring{$δ$}{delta} Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10632)
*Junbo Ding,Xin Zang,Chenchen Pan,Donghao Song,Jiaxin Zhu,Danhuai Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReLiF introduces reliability-aware fixed-delta auditing to mitigate threshold confounding in Lipschitz-style individual fairness for multi-task learning, using a shared reference tolerance and a violation-rate controller to balance utility and fairness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Threshold confounding arises when auditing tolerance is derived from each model's own representation distances, causing different algorithms to be compared at different semantic thresholds; there is a need for semantically consistent evaluation and understanding of threshold drift.

Method: Propose ReLiF: a framework that separates evaluation-time fixed-δ auditing from training-time controlled regularization. It uses a shared reference tolerance for comparable auditing and a violation-rate feedback controller to keep the Lipschitz surrogate active without overpowering stochastic training. Includes analysis of threshold drift, reference-tolerance selection, and relationship between the huberized training surrogate and the unsmoothed positive-margin counterpart.

Result: Fixed-δ auditing reveals utility–fairness trade-offs that method-dependent thresholds can obscure; ReLiF achieves competitive utility and reduces aligned bias under shared thresholds on NYUv2 with ResNet50; on clinical benchmarks, it yields controlled fairness-regularized trade-offs; supports fixed-δ auditing as a semantically consistent protocol for evaluating Lipschitz fairness in MTL.

Conclusion: Fixed-δ auditing provides a semantically consistent evaluation protocol for Lipschitz fairness in multi-task learning, and ReLiF offers a reliability-aware framework that achieves balanced utility and fairness while avoiding threshold confounding.

Abstract: Lipschitz-style individual fairness formalizes the idea that semantically similar examples should receive similar predictions, but its evaluation in multi-task learning (MTL) can be confounded by method-induced representation scales. This paper identifies threshold confounding: when the auditing tolerance is derived from each model's own representation distances, different algorithms are compared under different semantic thresholds. A threshold-drift analysis further shows how Bias rankings can change and identifies sufficient conditions for ranking preservation.
  We propose \textbf{ReLiF}, a reliability-aware framework that separates evaluation-time fixed-$δ$ auditing from training-time controlled regularization. ReLiF uses a shared reference tolerance for comparable auditing and a violation-rate feedback controller to keep the Lipschitz surrogate active without letting it dominate stochastic training. This work also develops supporting analysis for threshold drift, reference-tolerance selection, and the relationship between the huberized training surrogate and its unsmoothed positive-margin counterpart.
  Experiments on clinical time-series benchmarks and NYUv2 (NYU Depth V2) dense prediction show that fixed-$δ$ auditing exposes utility--fairness trade-offs that method-dependent thresholds can obscure. On NYUv2 with a ResNet50 backbone, ReLiF achieves competitive utility while substantially reducing aligned bias under shared fixed thresholds. On clinical benchmarks, ReLiF yields controlled fairness-regularized trade-offs, while fixed-$δ$ auditing reveals that task-balancing baselines can sometimes achieve lower bias and that genuine utility--fairness trade-offs persist. These results support fixed-$δ$ auditing as a semantically consistent protocol for evaluating Lipschitz fairness in MTL.

</details>


### [220] [PhysMetrics.Weather: An Evaluation Framework for Physical Consistency in ML Weather Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10642)
*Emma Kasteleyn,Timo Maier,Axel Lauer,Veronika Eyring,Pierre Gentine,Ana Lucic*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces PhysMetrics.Weather, a framework to evaluate ML-based weather prediction models for physical realism using conservation, spectral, and dynamical metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLWP models are data-driven and often evaluated with pixel-level metrics (e.g., RMSE), which may not enforce physical consistency; a framework to quantify physical realism is needed to guide physics-informed architectures and ensure operational reliability.

Method: Proposes a framework that assesses physical realism across three metric domains—conservation, spectral, and dynamical—and provides implementation on GitHub for evaluating MLWP models.

Result: Provides quantitative metrics to evaluate physical realism in MLWP models, guiding the development of physics-informed approaches and informing operational reliability; code and resources available at the GitHub repository.

Conclusion: PhysMetrics.Weather offers a structured approach to ensure MLWP forecasts adhere to physical laws, facilitating better integration of physics constraints into ML models.

Abstract: Machine learning weather prediction (MLWP) models have achieved impressive forecasting performance at a small fraction of the computational costs required for traditional physics-based methods. However, they are primarily (1) data-driven and (2) evaluated using pixel-wide error metrics (e.g., RMSE), so there are no guarantees that their forecasts are consistent with known physical laws. We introduce PhysMetrics.Weather, an evaluation framework that assesses the physical realism of MLWP models across three types of metrics: conservation, spectral, and dynamical. By quantifying physical realism, this tool guides the development of physics-informed architectures and helps evaluate whether MLWP models are reliable for operational use. Our framework is available on Github at https://github.com/Emmakast/PhysMetrics.Weather.

</details>


### [221] [How Does Reasoning Flow? Tracing Attention-Induced Information Flow for Targeted RL in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10646)
*Zhichen Dong,Yang Li,Yuhan Sun,Weixun Wang,Yijia Luo,Zinian Peng,Taiheng Ye,Chao Yang,Wenbo Su,Yu Cheng,Bo Zheng,Junchi Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlowTracer builds an attention-based token graph to trace answer-focused reasoning in LLMs, enforcing flow conservation and reweighting to reveal an information-flow backbone; token importances via flow throughput guide token-level RL rewards, yielding consistent gains across reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Token-level credit assignment in RL for LLMs is weak when using uniform token treatment. Existing signals are local/point-wise and ignore global information propagation; a global, structure-aware credit mechanism could better identify decisive reasoning steps.

Method: Construct an attention-induced DAG with tokens as nodes and edge capacities from aggregated attention; reweight edges to keep only influence reaching the answer region; enforce local flow conservation; extract an information-flow backbone from question to answer; score tokens by flow throughput to derive token importances, used to shape token-level rewards.

Result: A flow-based token credit mechanism that highlights high-impact tokens and bottlenecks in reasoning; token importances improve RL signals and yield performance gains across multiple reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: FlowTracer provides a global, flow-based credit assignment framework for RL in LLMs, enabling targeted token rewards and consistent improvements in reasoning tasks by tracing information flow from questions to answers.

Abstract: Token-level credit assignment remains a key obstacle for reinforcement learning (RL) in large language models (LLMs), where RL recipes typically treat all tokens equally, failing to distinguish decisive reasoning steps from routine formatting or fluent filler. Recent attempts leverage model-internal signals to assign finer-grained credit, but these are often point-wise heuristics that ignore the global structure of information propagation. We propose FlowTracer, an RL framework that traces answer-targeted reasoning flow on an attention-induced directed acyclic graph in which nodes correspond to tokens and edge capacities come from aggregated attention weights and derives token credit from this global structure. The edge capacities are reweighted to retain only the influence that can reach the answer region, while enforcing local flow conservation so intermediate tokens neither lose nor gain effective mass due to path length or irrelevant branches. On this graph, FlowTracer extracts an information-flow backbone connecting the question to the answer and scores tokens by flow throughput, revealing high-impact hubs and aggregation checkpoints that mediate long-range dependencies. These derived importances are used to shape token-level rewards, enabling learning signals to focus precisely on the tokens that route information toward (or away from) correct answers and delivering consistent performance gains across a range of reasoning tasks.

</details>


### [222] [In Defense of Information Leakage in Concept-based Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10669)
*Mateo Espinosa Zarlenga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Challenges the view that concept-irrelevant leakage in concept-based models is always harmful; introduces benign leakage and reframes training to exploit it, preserving performance and intervenability under incomplete concept sets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world concept sets are typically incomplete. For CM interpretability and control, enforcing zero leakage can hurt accuracy and practicality. The work argues leakage can be beneficial if properly constrained.

Method: Reframe the CM training objective to encourage and exploit benign leakage, deriving an optimization framework that balances interpretability, accuracy, and intervenability under concept incompleteness.

Result: Empirically show that models can leverage benign leakage without sacrificing accuracy or the ability to intervene, challenging the necessity of eliminating all leakage.

Conclusion: Benign leakage exists and can be harnessed via objective redesign. Under incomplete concept inventories, allowing controlled leakage improves practicality of CMs without harming performance.

Abstract: Concept-based models (CMs), deep neural networks that ground their predictions on representations aligned with human-understandable concepts (e.g., "round", "stripes", etc.), have been shown to learn representations that leak concept-irrelevant information. As the traditional narrative goes, this leakage is undesirable and should be eradicated as it leads to uninterpretable models. In this paper, we posit that this conventional view of leakage in CMs is not only ill-posed, as the evidence of how leakage makes a model less interpretable is often inconclusive, but also bound to lead to impractical CMs under common real-world constraints. Specifically, we argue that in real-world settings where concept incompleteness is the norm, some leakage is often necessary for constructing accurate and intervenable CMs. To this end, we propose that there is such a thing as benign leakage and show that, by optimizing a reframing of the typical CM training objective, CMs can encourage and exploit this form of leakage without sacrificing accuracy or intervenability.

</details>


### [223] [One Step Closer to Ground Truth: A Multi-Scale Residual-Aware Representation Learning Pipeline for Predicting Time Series Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10678)
*Amrijit Biswas,Mustafa Kamal,Robin Krambroeckers,M. M. Lutfe Elahi,Sifat Momen,Nabeel Mohammed,Shafin Rahman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-stage, model-agnostic framework for transformer-based time-series forecasting that decouples base prediction from residual correction using a meta-corrector to model structured errors across multiple channels, improving robustness and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single-stage transformer forecasts exhibit systematic residual biases due to structural shortcomings, unmodeled stochastic components, and insufficient multi-scale representation; treating residuals as irreducible noise precludes adaptive correction of error patterns.

Method: Base transformer generates initial forecasts; a dedicated meta-corrector models structured residual patterns across multivariate channels, preserves cross-variable dependencies, and iteratively refines residual bias; framed as a hypothesis space expansion enabling end-to-end learning of complex error dynamics.

Result: Evaluated on eight benchmark datasets; achieves state-of-the-art performance with significant improvements in MSE and MAE, demonstrating reduced systematic biases and enhanced robustness to complex temporal dynamics.

Conclusion: The framework mitigates systematic biases, expands the hypothesis space, and improves practicality of transformer-based forecasting through explicit residual modeling and iterative correction.

Abstract: Transformer-based models have emerged as leading paradigms in time-series forecasting in recent years, employing self-attention mechanisms to capture long-range dependencies. Despite their success, these single-stage forecasting architectures exhibit persistent systematic residual biases arising from structural discrepancies, unmodeled stochastic components, or inadequate multi-scale temporal representations. This limitation persists when residuals are treated as irreducible noise, precluding adaptive correction of structured error patterns. To address this limitation, we introduce a two-stage, model-agnostic framework that explicitly decouples forecasting and residual learning into distinct stages of representation learning. A base transformer first generates the initial predictions. Subsequently, a dedicated meta-corrector dynamically models structured error patterns across multivariate channels, preserves cross-variable dependencies, and iteratively refines the residual bias of the base transformer. By formalizing this pipeline as a hypothesis space expansion, our framework addresses approximation limitations inherent in single-stage architectures, removes reliance on restrictive assumptions, and enables end-to-end learning of complex error dynamics. Evaluated on eight popular benchmark datasets using established protocols, our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, with significant improvements in standard metrics (MSE, MAE). The results demonstrate the framework's ability to mitigate systematic biases and enhance robustness to complex temporal dynamics, advancing the practical applicability of transformer-based forecasting models.

</details>


### [224] [PL-KKT-hPINN: Enforcing Nonlinear Equality Constraints on Neural Networks via Piecewise-Linear Projection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10682)
*Fateme Mohammad Mohammadi,Hector Budman,Joshua L. Pulsipher*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PL-KKT-hPINN enforces nonlinear equality constraints via piecewise-linear projection, extending linear-KKT-hPINN, and demonstrates improved constraint satisfaction with comparable accuracy on a CSTR, plus data-efficiency gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PINNs enforce physics as soft penalties, which may violate constraints at inference. Hard-constrained surrogates are desirable in chemical engineering for reliability, safety, and interpretability.

Method: Introduce a piecewise-linear projection that enforces nonlinear equalities; extend KKT-hPINN to nonlinear constraints; apply to a continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) with one and two inputs; compare against a standard neural network; evaluate constraint violation (hard constraints), RMSE, and robustness in low-data regimes.

Result: Compared to an unconstrained NN, PL-KKT-hPINN achieves lower constraint violations while maintaining predictive accuracy; demonstrates improved robustness in low-data settings with lower RMSE; results indicate computational feasibility for surrogate modeling of nonlinear chemical systems.

Conclusion: PL-KKT-hPINN provides a computationally efficient, physically consistent surrogate modeling framework for nonlinear chemical engineering systems by hard-constraining nonlinear equalities; broad potential for process modeling.

Abstract: While physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have shown strong potential for process modeling, physical equations are only enforced as soft constraints during training, and thus, they do not guarantee constraint satisfaction at inference. We propose a framework, called piecewise-linear Karush--Kuhn--Tucker hard-constrained PINNs (PL-KKT-hPINNs), that strictly enforces nonlinear equality constraints through piecewise-linear projection. This extends the KKT-hPINN framewor, which exactly enforces linear equalities through the Karush--Kuhn--Tucker (KKT) conditions associated with orthogonally projecting neural network outputs onto the constraint feasible region. The method is demonstrated on a continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) case study for both one and two inputs. Results show that PL-KKT-hPINN preserves predictive accuracy comparable to that of a standard neural network while achieving substantially lower constraint violations. In addition, the proposed model shows improved robustness in low-data regimes, yielding lower RMSE than the unconstrained neural network for limited training sample sizes. These results demonstrate that PL-KKT-hPINN provides a computationally efficient and physically consistent framework for surrogate modeling of nonlinear chemical engineering systems.

</details>


### [225] [Divide and Cooperate: Role-Decomposed Multi-Agent LLM Training with Cross-Agent Learning Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10684)
*Jaewan Park,Solbee Cho,Jay-Yoon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Divide-and-Cooperate (DAC): a role-decomposed, multi-agent training framework for QA that separates evidence search from answer generation, using abstention-based credit assignment and hard-positive evidence augmentation, implemented with LoRA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional single-policy approaches couple evidence gathering and generation, causing combinatorial policy space and credit assignment issues; need explicit role separation and cross-agent learning signals to improve exploration and robustness.

Method: Two cooperative agents: a searcher and a generator; generator acts as answer producer and evidence sufficiency verifier (abstains if evidence is insufficient). The abstention signal is used as a reward component for the searcher; hard-positive evidence augmentation exposes generator to diverse, challenging evidence; training with role-specific learning signals; implemented with parameter-efficient LoRA modules on a shared backbone.

Result: Empirical evaluation on general and multi-hop QA benchmarks shows strong performance compared to baselines relying on full fine-tuning of monolithic models; DAC with LoRA achieves competitive results with fewer parameters.

Conclusion: Role decomposition with cross-agent learning signals improves credit assignment, exploration, and robustness in multi-hop QA; LoRA-enabled adaptation provides efficiency; the framework outperforms monolithic baselines.

Abstract: Modern language agents which perform multi-step reasoning have shown strong performance in knowledge-intensive question answering. However, existing approaches typically couple evidence acquisition and answer generation within a single policy. This forces a single model to play multiple potentially conflicting roles, inducing a combinatorial explosion in the policy space and hindering efficient exploration. It also introduces a credit assignment problem during training: a search action that retrieves sufficient evidence may still be penalized when generation fails, and vice versa. We propose DAC (Divide and Cooperate), a role-decomposed multi-agent training framework that divides agentic search into two cooperative subtasks, each handled by a dedicated agent trained with role-specific learning signals. The generator serves a dual role as both an answer producer and an evidence sufficiency verifier, abstaining when retrieved evidence is insufficient. This abstention signal is incorporated into the search agent's reward, providing structured cross-agent learning signals that improve credit assignment. Conversely, the searcher exposes the generator to diverse and challenging evidence environments by hard-positive evidence augmentation, improving its robustness. Experiments on general and multi-hop QA benchmarks show that DAC, implemented via parameter-efficient LoRA modules over a shared backbone, achieves strong performance against prior baselines that rely on full fine-tuning of monolithic models.

</details>


### [226] [From Observation to Intervention: A Causal Audit of Expert Importance in Mixture-of-Experts Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10703)
*Leonard Engmann,Christian Medeiros Adriano,Holger Giese*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Population-level routing metrics do not predict token-level causal importance of MoE experts; interventional audits reveal limited predictive power of observational metrics; pruning benefits arise from early-layer redundancy rather than true dispensability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question the validity of inferring interventional conclusions from associational statistics in interpretability; provide a principled interventional audit to assess expert importance in MoE pruning.

Method: Token-level interventional audit across three high-redundancy MoE models; evaluate whether observational routing metrics predict causal expert importance; apply multiple-comparison correction; per-token routing weight control; report effect sizes (Cohen's d) and p-values; identify significant signals.

Result: No observational metric predicted causal expert importance after correction across 60 metric-layer combos; all d < 0.17; only one Bonferroni-significant signal in final layer under routing weight control (d=0.231, p=0.0013).

Conclusion: Early-layer redundancy makes most selection criteria interchangeable; common pruning claims are not supported by observational statistics; interventional audits provide calibrated standards for interpretability claims; pruning succeeds due to redundancy, not metric validity.

Abstract: Interpretability methods routinely use population-level summary statistics over observed model behaviour to license claims about the effects of targeted interventions on specific computations; in Pearl's terms, they treat rung-1 associational evidence as if it supported rung-2 interventional conclusions, a move whose validity is rarely tested. We examine one concrete instance: the use of routing statistics in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) pruning, where utilization rates, activation norms, and routing weight distributions are treated as predictors of which experts can be removed without functional cost. A token-level interventional audit across three high-redundancy MoE architectures (OLMoE-1B-7B-0924, Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite) finds no observational metric predicts causal expert importance after multiple-comparison correction in any model, with effect sizes below Cohen's $d = 0.17$ across all 60 metric-layer combinations. A per-token routing weight control rules out insufficient power, recovering a single Bonferroni-significant signal at OLMoE's final MoE layer ($d = +0.231$, $p = 0.0013$). Existing pruning methods succeed in this regime not by identifying dispensable experts but because early-layer redundancy renders most selection criteria interchangeable. Our results provide an explicit counterexample to the common inferential step from population-level observational summaries to token-level interventional claims about expert importance, and illustrate how interventional audits can calibrate the evidential standards for interpretability claims.

</details>


### [227] [Event-Driven Reinforcement Learning Enables Long-Horizon Control in Semiconductor Fabrication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10705)
*Yavar Yeganeh,Mahsa Shekari,Nicla Frigerio,Daniele Pagano,Andrea Matta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A deep reinforcement learning framework for multi-objective, centralized control of large-scale, event-driven semiconductor manufacturing using an event-driven TD formulation, evaluated with high-fidelity simulations showing throughput and utilization gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle stochastic, highly constrained, high-dimensional fab environments where delayed feedback and long horizons hinder traditional planning; enable scalable, transferable policy optimization for complex adaptive systems.

Method: Formulate as a centralized-agent problem with a core policy coordinating system-wide decisions; model evolution as an interconnected, event-driven temporal process; develop a tailored event-driven temporal-difference learning formulation; integrate with multiple model-free policy optimization algorithms; validate in offline and online settings using high-fidelity simulations across diverse operating scenarios.

Result: Agents trained in offline and online settings achieve significant and consistent improvements in throughput and utilization; performance and generalization analyzed across training phases, clarifying strengths of different RL formulations and algorithms.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates scalability, generality, and transferability for controlling event-driven, complex adaptive systems such as semiconductor manufacturing, with robust gains across varied scenarios.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning promises to optimize sequential decisions in large-scale systems. Semiconductor manufacturing systems are stochastic and highly constrained environments where heterogeneous wafers traverse hundreds of processing steps across extensive equipment networks. These characteristics yield complex, high-dimensional decision problems with delayed feedback and long-horizon requirements, complicating production planning and control. We propose a deep reinforcement learning framework for multi-objective policy optimization at this scale. Specifically, we formulate control as a centralized-agent problem, where a core policy coordinates system-wide decisions, while system evolution is represented as an interconnected temporal process driven by discrete events. Accordingly, we develop a tailored event-driven temporal-difference formulation that remains general and can be integrated with various policy optimization methods under relevant training settings. We investigate several core model-free algorithms incorporated into this framework and evaluate their effectiveness using high-fidelity simulations of diverse, industry-real operating scenarios. Across extensive validation experiments, agents trained in both offline and online settings show significant and consistent gains in throughput and utilization. We further evaluate performance and generalization across training phases, clarifying the relative strengths of alternative reinforcement learning formulations and algorithms. Overall, the results support the scalability, generality, and transferability of the proposed framework for controlling event-driven complex adaptive systems.

</details>


### [228] [Unifying Data, Memory, and Compute Efficiency in LLM training: A Survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10706)
*Vanessa Schmidt,Huy Hoang Nguyen,Cédric Jung,Shirin Salehi,Anke Schmeink*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A constraint-centric survey of LLM efficiency across data, memory, and compute bottlenecks; emphasizes systems-level interaction and compute-aware planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficiency constraints dictate what can be trained/fine-tuned/deployed; existing work treats bottlenecks in isolation. A unified perspective is needed to optimize under realistic resource budgets.

Method: Survey and synthesis of recent progress organized around data efficiency, memory efficiency, and compute budget awareness; review data selection, pruning, curriculum strategies, memory bottlenecks (GPU memory), joint scaling considerations (weight storage, optimizer states, activations), and compute-governed training/inference with stopping/allocation rules.

Result: Demonstrates that different data notions are optimal in different regimes; GPU memory often dominates; effective scaling requires joint management of memory components; compute budgets influence data selection and when to stop or reallocate training/fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Proposes a unified framework where compute-aware data selection, scaling laws, and adaptive inference under resource limits guide training and deployment decisions; encourages resource-conditioned decision-making across the whole ML pipeline.

Abstract: Resource constraints increasingly determine what can be trained, fine-tuned, and deployed in large language models (LLMs), yet efficiency is often studied through isolated techniques rather than as an interacting system of limits. This survey adopts a constraint-centric perspective and organizes recent progress around three coupled bottlenecks: data efficiency (what to train on), memory efficiency (how to fit training), and compute budget awareness (when and where to spend FLOPs). On the data axis, we review selection and pruning methods that maximize learning per token, ranging from scalable proxy signals based on learning dynamics to gradient- and influence-based scoring, as well as difficulty-aware and curriculum-style strategies. We highlight emerging evidence that different notions of good data dominate in different regimes, implying that optimal subsets depend on the task objective and resource budget rather than being universal. On the systems side, we show that GPU memory, not raw compute, is often the dominant bottleneck in fine-tuning, and that effective scaling requires jointly reducing weight storage, optimizer states, and activation memory rather than optimizing any single component in isolation. Beyond memory, we frame training and inference as compute-governed processes in which optimization, data selection, and decoding must explicitly account for finite FLOP budgets. We review evidence for compute-optimal allocation and stopping rules, where computation should be halted or reallocated once marginal performance gains fall below a budget-dependent threshold. Together, these results unify compute-aware data selection, scaling laws, and adaptive inference under a common principle of resource-conditioned decision-making.

</details>


### [229] [Transformer Based Model for Spatiotemporal Feature Learning in EEG Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10718)
*Xinglong Cui,Dian Gu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A Transformer-based EEG classifier (EEG-TransNet) that fuses ResNet+wavelet denoising for preprocessing, a Local Self-Attention Block for regional features, and a Fuzzy-Attention Synchronous Transformer (FAST) for spatiotemporal modeling; it outperforms baselines on BETA, SEED, and DepEEG, with ablations validating the components and depthwise separable deconvolutions for efficiency, and shows strong cross-subject generalization across varying signal lengths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: EEG signals are high-dimensional, noisy, and exhibit complex spatiotemporal dynamics. Effective classification/recognition requires integrated modeling of temporal, regional, and synchronous patterns, with robustness to variable-length signals and subject variability.

Method: EEG-TransNet consists of three modules: (1) preprocessing/feature extraction using ResNet and wavelet-based denoising; (2) Local Self-Attention Block to learn regional features; (3) Fuzzy-Attention Synchronous Transformer (FAST) to capture spatiotemporal dependencies. The decoder uses depthwise separable convolutions to reduce computational cost. Evaluated on three datasets (BETA, SEED, DepEEG) with ablation studies.

Result: The model achieves higher classification accuracy and robustness across varying signal lengths compared with baselines. Ablation shows the Local Self-Attention Block provides significant gains; depthwise separable convolutions in the decoder reduce computation without sacrificing accuracy. The approach generalizes across subjects with minimal performance variation.

Conclusion: EEG-TransNet offers a robust, efficient, and generalizable framework for EEG-based brain activity classification and emotion recognition, suitable for practical applications and subject-generalizable EEG analysis.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) is a widely adopted technique for monitoring brain activity, offering valuable insights into neurological states due to its high temporal resolution and cost-effectiveness. To enhance the analysis of complex EEG data, we propose EEG-TransNet, an architecture designed to capture temporal, regional, and synchronous features of EEG signals. EEG-TransNet introduces three key modules: 1) a preprocessing and feature extraction module leveraging ResNet and wavelet-based denoising, 2) a Local Self-Attention Block for regional feature learning, and 3) a Fuzzy-Attention Synchronous Transformer (FAST) to model spatiotemporal dependencies. Through extensive experiments on three EEG datasets (BETA, SEED, and DepEEG), the proposed model consistently outperforms other methods in terms of classification accuracy and robustness across varying signal lengths. Ablation studies confirm the contribution of the Local Self-Attention Block in improving performance, and the inclusion of depthwise separable convolutions in the decoder reduces computational complexity while maintaining high accuracy. EEG-TransNet's ability to generalize across subjects with minimal performance variation highlights its potential as a robust tool for EEG-based brain activity classification and emotion recognition tasks.

</details>
