### [230] [Pre-AF 13: An Interpretable Atrial Fibrillation Risk Score Mined from Discharge Reports](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10725)
*Olga Shakhmatova,Dmitrii Kriukov,Daniil Larionov,Nikita Khromov,Iaroslav Bespalov,Alexander Zolotarev,Kirill Grishchenkov,Ekaterina Ivanova,Miron Kuznetsov,Ilya Sochenkov,Elizaveta Panchenko,Artem Shelmanov,Dmitry V. Dylov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ML models using EHR data (including NLP-extracted features) predict 24-month AF risk in cardiovascular disease patients without prior AF, outperforming traditional risk scores. A simple 13-feature model (Pre-AF 13) and a linear bedside score (Pre-AF 9) offer interpretable risk stratification, with SHAP-identified age and left atrial volume as key predictors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses limited discrimination of existing AF risk scores in high-risk CVD patients and aims to provide medium-term (24-month) AF risk prediction using routinely collected hospital data with interpretable ML.

Method: Retrospective single-center study using EHR from National Research Cardiology Center (Russia). Includes adults with CVD and no prior AF, hospitalized ≥2 times (2012-2019). NLP pipeline converted discharge reports into 73 structured features (rule-based parser + transformer NER). Models: full (73 features), simple (reduced subset, 13 features), and linear Pre-AF 9. Evaluated via ROC AUC for 24-month and entire follow-up; compared to CHARGE-AF, C2HEST, MHS, HAVOC; SHAP used for interpretation.

Result: Cohort: 80,576 records from 45,000 patients; 17,562 included; 1,438 AF events (8.19%). Full model AUC 0.735 (24-month) and 0.696 (entire). Simple model AUC 0.725 and 0.696. All non-linear models outperformed the clinical scores (AUC 0.53–0.64). Pre-AF 13 uses 13 features; SHAP highlights age and left atrial volume as dominant predictors. Linear Pre-AF 9 stratifies 24-month AF incidence from ~7% to 36%.

Conclusion: Interpretable ML models using routinely collected EHR data can identify high-AF-risk CVD patients and outperform established clinical risk scores, with practical bedside-score variants.

Abstract: Background. Atrial fibrillation (AF) is the most prevalent cardiac arrhythmia and a major determinant of prognosis. Established AF risk scores rely on factors (older age, hypertension) nearly ubiquitous among patients with cardiovascular disease (CVD), offering limited stratification in this high-risk group. Most target long-term (5-10 year) rather than medium-term prediction. We developed interpretable ML models predicting AF risk over a 24-month and entire follow-up horizon in CVD patients using routinely collected hospital data.
  Methods. Single-center retrospective study of electronic health records from the National Research Cardiology Center (Russia) for patients aged >=18 with CVD but without pre-existing AF, hospitalized more than once between January 2012 and May 2019. A custom NLP pipeline transformed unstructured discharge reports into 73 structured features, combining a rule-based parser with transformer-based NER. Using LightAutoML we built a full model (73 features), a simple model (reduced subset), and a linear model for a bedside risk score. Performance was assessed by ROC AUC, compared with CHARGE-AF, C2HEST, MHS, and HAVOC, and interpreted via SHAP.
  Results. Of 80,576 records from 45,000 patients, 17,562 met inclusion criteria; 1,438 (8.19%) developed AF. The full model reached ROC AUC 0.735 (24-month) and 0.696 (entire follow-up); the simple model was nearly identical (0.725, 0.696). All non-linear models outperformed the four clinical risk scores (ROC AUC 0.53-0.64). The simple model uses 13 features and is named Pre-AF 13. SHAP identified age and left atrial volume as dominant predictors. A linear risk score (Pre-AF 9) stratified observed 24-month AF incidence from ~7% to 36%.
  Conclusion. Interpretable ML models built from routinely collected EHR data identify high-AF-risk CVD patients, outperforming established clinical risk scores.

</details>


### [231] [SPACR: Single-Pass Adaptive Training of Uncertainty-Aware Conformal Regressors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10734)
*Soundouss Messoudi,Sylvain Rousseau,Sébastien Destercke*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SPACR introduces a single-pass, differentiable training objective for uncertainty-aware regression that yields valid prediction intervals at multiple confidence levels without batch-splitting. It aligns training with conformal goals, reducing computation vs. post-hoc CP methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conformal Prediction (CP) is powerful but typically post-hoc, causing misalignment between training and the goal of narrow, reliable intervals and requiring batch-split or retraining for different confidence levels. A differentiable approach can integrate uncertainty guarantees into training and provide multi-level intervals efficiently.

Method: Directly train an uncertainty-aware regressor with a differentiable loss that jointly optimizes interval efficiency (width) and validity. Operates in a single pass, requires no batch-splitting during training, and does not fix confidence levels a priori. During inference, yields valid intervals at multiple confidence levels from one model. Compared to standard CP and DOICR, SPACR avoids retraining and provides tighter intervals with better coverage-efficiency balance.

Result: Empirical evaluation on diverse datasets shows SPACR achieves tighter prediction intervals and better coverage-efficiency trade-offs than standard CP and DOICR, while significantly reducing computational costs due to the single-pass training and avoidance of retraining for new confidence levels.

Conclusion: SPACR enables a single trained model to produce valid, multi-level prediction intervals with improved efficiency, avoiding batch-splitting and retraining requirements typical of conformal methods, and offering substantial computational savings.

Abstract: Conformal Prediction (CP) provides robust uncertainty guarantees for predictive models, but is typically applied post hoc, which misaligns model training with the conformal goal of producing efficient (i.e, narrow) intervals. We propose SPACR (Single-Pass Adaptive Conformal Regressor), a novel method for directly training uncertainty-aware regressors within a differentiable loss. SPACR jointly optimizes efficiency and validity without batch-splitting or a predefined confidence levels during training. As a result, a single SPACR model yields valid prediction intervals at multiple confidence levels during inference, avoiding the costly retraining required by methods like DOICR. Experiments on diverse datasets show that SPACR consistently gives tighter intervals and better coverage-efficiency trade-offs compared to standard CP and DOICR, while significantly reducing computational costs.

</details>


### [232] [N-GRPO: Embedding-Level Neighbor Mixing for Enhanced Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10768)
*Xukun Zhu,Hang Yu,Peng Di,Linchao Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: N-GRPO uses Semantic Neighbor Mixing to diversify LLM rollout trajectories within GRPO, achieving improved math reasoning and OOD generalization without semantic disruption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the trade-off between token-level sampling redundancy and embedding-level noise, by preserving semantic integrity while enabling exploration of diverse solution paths.

Method: Integrate Semantic Neighbor Mixing into GRPO. Construct input representations by blending embeddings of an anchor token with its nearest semantic neighbors, injecting diversity while staying on the local semantic manifold.

Result: Empirical evaluations on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen models across sizes show consistent improvements over strong baselines on math reasoning benchmarks and robust OOD generalization.

Conclusion: Semantic Neighbor Mixing offers a principled, semantically faithful exploration strategy within GRPO, outperforming token-level sampling and embedding-noise approaches; demonstrates potential for broad application in policy optimization for LLMs.

Abstract: The success of Large Language Models in mathematical reasoning relies heavily on the generation of diverse and valid solution paths during the rollout phase. However, current rollout techniques face a fundamental trade-off: token-level sampling often yields redundant trajectories that differ only in rephrasing, while embedding-level methods utilizing random noise frequently disrupt semantic consistency. To resolve this, we introduce N-GRPO, a novel exploration strategy integrated into the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) framework. Rather than relying on token-level sampling or native embedding-level noise, our approach leverages Semantic Neighbor Mixing. This mechanism dynamically constructs input representations by mixing the embeddings of an anchor token and its nearest semantic neighbors, thereby injecting diversity while strictly adhering to the local semantic manifold. Experimental evaluations on the DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen models across different sizes show that N-GRPO not only achieves consistent improvements over strong baselines on math reasoning benchmarks but also exhibits robust generalization capabilities on out-of-distribution tasks.

</details>


### [233] [Inverse Probability Weighting and Age-of-Information Aggregation for Decentralized Federated Learning under Partial Reception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10774)
*Chanuka A. S. Hewa Kaluannakkage,Rajkumar Buyya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes DFL-AA, an adaptive AoI-weighted decentralized FL method that uses inverse probability weighting and EWMA-based channel estimation to correct selection bias and staleness in lossy wireless networks, with theoretical and empirical validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In decentralized federated learning over lossy wireless networks, updates from poor-quality links are underrepresented due to partial receptions (selection bias) and asynchronous updates cause stale information. Uniform gossip with local-fill reconstruction introduces bias; completeness-based weighting worsens it.

Method: Introduce DFL-AA: combine Inverse Probability Weighting to correct selection bias with online EWMA-based channel estimation for link quality, and Age-of-Information-based weighting to mitigate staleness; operates without global synchronization.

Result: Theoretical analysis shows DFL-AA removes link-quality distortion in expectation; experiments demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines across varying loss rates, network sizes, and heterogeneous wireless conditions.

Conclusion: DFL-AA effectively mitigates selection bias and update staleness in decentralized federated learning over lossy networks and provides robust performance without requiring global synchronization.

Abstract: Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) over lossy wireless networks faces two key challenges: selection bias, where updates from poor-quality links are systematically underrepresented due to partial model reception, and update staleness, where asynchronous nodes contribute outdated information. We show that uniform gossip aggregation with local-fill reconstruction introduces persistent link-quality-induced bias, while completeness-based weighting further amplifies this effect. To address these challenges, we propose DFL-AA (Decentralized Federated Learning with Adaptive AoI-weighted Aggregation), which combines Inverse Probability Weighting with online EWMA-based channel estimation to correct selection bias and Age-of-Information-based weighting to mitigate staleness without requiring global synchronization. We theoretically show that DFL-AA removes link-quality distortion in expectation and experimentally demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines across varying loss rates, network sizes, and heterogeneous wireless conditions.

</details>


### [234] [Can we trust our models? Epistemic calibration in second-order classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10777)
*Arthur Hoarau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Epistemic calibration is a stronger notion than classical calibration for uncertainty estimates. The paper defines epistemic calibration, introduces the Expected Epistemic Calibration Error (EECE) as a consistent estimator of True Epistemic Calibration Error (TECE), proves an impossibility theorem under the hypothesis, and shows empirically that epistemic calibration differentiates UQ methods with similar predictive accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical calibration only assesses predicted probabilities, not the reliability of epistemic uncertainty in second-order models; need a principled measure of whether epistemic uncertainty reflects true dispersion around labels, especially for high-stakes decisions.

Method: Formalize epistemic calibration; define TECE and EECE; prove consistency of EECE estimator; prove an impossibility theorem under epistemic calibration hypothesis; empirical evaluation across various UQ methods.

Result: EECE consistently estimates TECE; epistemic calibration discriminates between methods with similar accuracy; indicates substantial differences in epistemic reliability not captured by standard metrics.

Conclusion: Epistemic calibration is a coherent, meaningful criterion for uncertainty quantification; EECE provides a practical estimator; recommended for evaluating and comparing UQ methods; reveals limitations of classical calibration.

Abstract: Uncertainty estimation is critical for deploying machine learning models in high-stakes settings. However, classical calibration only assesses the reliability of predicted probabilities and does not evaluate whether epistemic uncertainty estimates are themselves trustworthy. This limitation is particularly relevant for second-order classification models. We introduce epistemic calibration, a principled criterion that measures whether reported epistemic uncertainty faithfully reflects the dispersion of model predictions around the ground truth. We show that epistemic calibration is a strictly stronger notion than classical calibration and captures failure modes invisible to standard metrics. We relate this work to the existing literature through an impossibility theorem that holds under the epistemic calibration hypothesis. To operationalize this concept, we propose the Expected Epistemic Calibration Error (EECE), which we prove to be a consistent estimator of a True Epistemic Calibration Error (TECE). Experiments across a broad range of uncertainty quantification methods show that epistemic calibration is a coherent and meaningful criterion and reveal substantial differences across methods, despite similar predictive performance.

</details>


### [235] [Closing the Modality Gap in Zero-Shot HAR: Contrastive Training and Separability-Optimized Prototypes on IMU Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10789)
*Anik Ghosh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Discriminative textual activity descriptions and contrastive alignment markedly improve zero-shot IMU-HAR, with strong unseen-class performance and macro-F1 emphasis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the modality gap between sensor embeddings and semantic class representations in ZSL for IMU-based HAR; determine how encoder training objectives shape alignments and performance.

Method: Systematic evaluation of seven configurations (three inference methods × two training pipelines) on PAMAP2 with 14 seen and 4 unseen activities; subjects 108/109 held out. A TCN trained with cross-entropy over Sentence-BERT prototypes using label-name vs discriminative activity descriptions. Incorporates contrastive training and inverted softmax correction. Benchmarks unseen-class accuracy and macro-F1; analyzes effect of text richness on prototype separability.

Result: Sensor embeddings achieve mean cosine similarity to text prototypes: 0.30 with label-name targets; 0.69 with discriminative descriptions. The best setup (contrastive training + inverted softmax) yields 73.2% accuracy and 0.583 macro-F1 on unseen classes, vs 58.3% accuracy and 0.34 macro-F1 for label-name baseline. Richer descriptions reduce inter-prototype separability due to shared biomechanical vocabulary but maintain benefits of alignment. Macro-F1 is recommended over accuracy due to class imbalance.

Conclusion: Modality alignment is a training-time phenomenon driven by encoder objectives; using discriminative textual descriptions and contrastive training significantly improves ZSL-HAR performance on unseen classes. The results generalize across inference methods; emphasize macro-F1 reporting and acknowledge descriptiveness-versus-separability trade-off.

Abstract: Zero-shot learning (ZSL) for inertial measurement unit (IMU)-based human activity recognition (HAR) faces a central challenge: bridging the gap between sensor embeddings and semantic class representations. We systematically evaluate seven configurations combining three inference methods with two training pipelines on the PAMAP2 dataset, using 14 seen and 4 unseen activity classes with subjects 108 and 109 held out for testing. We find that the modality gap is a training-time phenomenon governed by the encoder objective. A temporal convolutional network (TCN) trained with cross-entropy over label-name Sentence- BERT prototypes yields sensor embeddings with a mean cosine similarity of 0.30 to the corresponding text prototypes, while replacing the label-name prototype targets with discriminative activity descriptions raises this to 0.69. This alignment improvement transfers consistently across all three inference methods. The strongest result combines contrastive training with inverted softmax correction, achieving 73.2% accuracy and 0.583 macro F1 on unseen classes, compared to 58.3% accuracy and 0.34 macro F1 for the label-name baseline. A secondary finding is that richer text descriptions reduce inter-prototype separability in Sentence-BERT space, because shared biomechanical vocabulary causes the language model to compress the prototype cloud. This effect does not negate the benefits of contrastive alignment provided prototype descriptions retain sufficient discriminative vocabulary. We also demonstrate that overall accuracy is a misleading primary metric when test-set class distributions are imbalanced, and recommend macro-averaged F1 as the standard reporting metric for ZSL-HAR benchmarks.

</details>


### [236] [CITRAS-FM: Tiny Time Series Foundation Model for Covariate-Informed Zero-Shot Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10798)
*Yosuke Yamaguchi,Issei Suemitsu,Yuki Kajihara,Wenpeng Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Tiny covariate-aware time-series foundation model for zero-shot forecasting with real-time CPU inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computational cost and expand support for univariate/multivariate series and covariates in zero-shot forecasting; address limited covariate handling in existing TSFMs.

Method: Patch-based, decoder-only Transformer with a cross-variate module using Shifted Attention; introduces CovSynth to synthesize realistic covariates from decomposed target components; designed for CPU-efficient inference at sub-second latency.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot accuracy among sub-10M TSFMs on fev-bench across 100 tasks; CPU inference under 0.1 seconds, demonstrating strong accuracy–latency trade-off.

Conclusion: CITRAS-FM provides strong real-time deployability without sacrificing accuracy; CovSynth enables covariate-aware pretraining in covariate-scarce settings.

Abstract: Pretrained time series foundation models (TSFMs) have enabled zero-shot forecasting on unseen target series. However, existing TSFMs often incur high computational cost and provide limited support for diverse variable types, often failing to account for covariates that exogenously influence target variability. To address these challenges, we propose CITRAS-FM, a tiny 7M-parameter TSFM that supports univariate, multivariate, and covariate-informed zero-shot forecasting with real-time CPU inference. Built on a patch-based, decoder-only Transformer, CITRAS-FM introduces Shifted Attention into the cross-variate module to effectively exploit known covariates accessible throughout the forecast horizon. Moreover, to enable covariate-aware pretraining despite the scarcity of covariate-rich corpora, we propose CovSynth, which synthesizes realistic covariates from decomposed components of target series. Experiments on fev-bench, spanning 100 tasks across various settings, demonstrate that CITRAS-FM achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot accuracy among sub-10M TSFMs while delivering sub-0.1-second CPU inference, offering a strong balance between forecasting accuracy and real-time deployability.

</details>


### [237] [Boosting ECG Classification Performance by Pre-training with Synthesized Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10802)
*Naoki Nonaka,Jun Seita*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Domain-knowledge-based synthetic ECGs generated by Gaussian composition improve abnormal ECG classification, especially AFLT, with larger gains when real data are scarce; three of four targets benefit.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome data scarcity in medical DNNs due to privacy constraints and rarity of conditions by using synthetic data derived from domain knowledge as pre-training or augmentation.

Method: Gaussian-composition synthesis for single-lead II ECGs; encode each heartbeat with Gaussian P, Q, R, S, T components; generate synthetic data for AF, AFLT, PVC, WPW; evaluate across ten DNN architectures using synthetic-to-real transfer.

Result: Synthetic data improved abnormal ECG classification for three of four conditions; AFLT showed the largest average gain of 33.2%; improvements are more pronounced with smaller real-world datasets.

Conclusion: Knowledge-driven synthetic ECGs are a viable pre-training resource to mitigate data scarcity in medical ECG analysis and can reduce dependence on large real datasets.

Abstract: Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) typically require extensive datasets for effective training. In the medical domain, acquiring large-scale data is often challenging due to privacy concerns and the rarity of certain diseases. To address this data scarcity, we investigate the efficacy of training DNN models using synthetic data, generated based on domain-specific medical knowledge. Specifically, we develop a knowledge-driven Gaussian-composition synthesis algorithm for single-lead II ECGs, in which each heartbeat is represented by Gaussian-shaped P, Q, R, S, and T wave components. Using this simulator, we generate synthetic data for four abnormal electrocardiogram (ECG) classes: atrial fibrillation (AF), atrial flutter (AFLT), premature ventricular complex (PVC), and Wolff-Parkinson-White Syndrome (WPW). We evaluate the utility of this synthetic data by conducting abnormal ECG classification using ten different DNN architectures. Our results demonstrate that synthetic-to-real training improves classification performance for three of the four target abnormalities, with the largest architecture-averaged gain of $33.2\%$ observed for AFLT. Further analysis reveals that the performance enhancement from synthetic data is more pronounced with smaller real-world datasets. These findings suggest that domain-knowledge-based synthetic ECGs can serve as a useful pre-training resource, particularly in scenarios where real-world data are limited or difficult to obtain.

</details>


### [238] [K-Forcing: Joint Next-K-Token Decoding via Push-Forward Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10820)
*Zhiwei Tang,Yuanyu He,Yizheng Han,Wangbo Zhao,Jiasheng Tang,Fan Wang,Bohan Zhuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: K-Forcing enables joint next-k-token decoding by distilling an autoregressive model into a conditional push-forward mapping, achieving 2.4–3.5× speedups with k=4 and modest quality loss, suitable for high-load batch serving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive decoding is sequential and memory-bound; existing accelerations either require special conditions or do not target industrial-scale, high-load batch serving. A method that speeds up generation while staying compatible with standard AR infrastructure is needed.

Method: Train a conditional push-forward mapping that takes independent uniform noise and outputs a joint k-token sequence, via progressive self-forcing distillation from an AR teacher. The mapping preserves fixed-length outputs and reuses the AR backbone; the prediction window is gradually expanded during training.

Result: With k=4, the method achieves ~2.4–3.5× speedup across batch sizes on LM1B and OpenWebText, with modest quality degradation compared to the AR teacher.

Conclusion: K-Forcing is a practical approach to accelerate autoregressive generation in industrial deployments, offering significant latency reductions while remaining compatible with standard AR serving architectures; it is particularly well-suited for high-load batch inference.

Abstract: Autoregressive (AR) language modeling is the dominant paradigm for text generation, yet its sequential token-by-token decoding makes inference memory-bound and inefficient. Existing acceleration approaches, such as speculative decoding and diffusion language models, can yield speedups under certain conditions but do not directly address high-load batch serving--the scenario most critical for industrial-scale deployment. We introduce K-Forcing, a push-forward language modeling paradigm for joint next-k-token decoding. K-Forcing distills an existing AR model into a conditional push-forward mapping--one that transforms independent uniform noise variables into a joint sample of multiple future tokens in a single forward pass. This design preserves fixed-length outputs, reuses the AR teacher backbone, and remains compatible with standard AR serving infrastructure. We train this mapping via progressive self-forcing distillation, which gradually expands the prediction window while enabling the student to closely match the sequence distribution of the AR teacher. We evaluate K-Forcing on LM1B and OpenWebText using a standard causal Transformer backbone. When aggressively configured to generate k = 4 tokens per forward pass, K-Forcing delivers approximately 2.4-3.5x speedup across different batch sizes, while incurring modest quality degradation relative to its AR teacher. As inference increasingly dominates the lifetime compute cost of modern LLMs, K-Forcing offers a promising route toward accelerating AR generation under real-world high-load deployment.

</details>


### [239] [Encoding the Euler Characteristic Transform](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10824)
*Nello Blaser,Odin Hoff Gardaa,Lars M. Salbu,Elena Xinyi Wang,Bastian Rieck*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a continuous encoding for the Euler Characteristic Transform (ECT) by tokenizing per-vertex net Euler-characteristic changes across directions, with a two-stage ECC→ECT pipeline and six architectures; empirically yields better classification accuracy on five of six benchmarks, showing encoding choice matters more than model capacity, and that continuous encoding outperforms discretization in several settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Replace discretized ECC encodings in ECT with a continuous token-based representation to reduce inductive bias and investigate how encoding interacts with architectural choices across diverse geometric data modalities.

Method: Define per-direction, per-vertex tokens for net Euler-characteristic changes; an ECC encoder maps each direction's ECC curve to a fixed-length vector; an ECT representation aggregates these across directions. Compare six architectures (ranging from a structure-agnostic feedforward to convolutional and complex-valued models enforcing planar-rotation equivariance) across six benchmarks (point clouds, graphs, cubical complexes, meshes). Perform ablations to attribute gains to the encoding rather than transformer capacity.

Result: Continuous encoding improves accuracy on five of six datasets. Control experiments indicate the gains arise from the tokenization itself rather than added model capacity. The particular representation architecture matters less than the encoding; inductive biases matter more when using continuous encoding, with feedforward performing best under continuous encoding but being less robust under discretization than convolutional architectures.

Conclusion: A continuous ECT encoding can substantially improve performance, with the encoding design playing a more crucial role than architectural capacity. The benefits of inductive biases depend on the encoding, suggesting a need to align model biases with the continuous tokenization approach for geometric learning.

Abstract: The Euler Characteristic Curve (ECC) records the Euler characteristic of a linearly embedded cell complex as a function of filtration height in a given direction, and the Euler Characteristic Transform (ECT) is the injective shape descriptor obtained by collecting ECCs over many directions. How the ECT is encoded for a neural network is itself an inductive bias, conventionally fixed by discretizing each ECC. We introduce a continuous encoding: for each direction and each vertex it records the net Euler-characteristic change attributed to that vertex, producing a per-direction token sequence that a small transformer maps to a feature vector. We separate the resulting pipeline into two stages on orthogonal axes: an ECC encoder that acts within each direction, mapping its curve to a fixed-length vector, and an ECT representation that acts across directions, aggregating the per-direction vectors into one. We study six ECT representation architectures spanning a range of inductive biases, from a structure-agnostic feedforward baseline to convolutional and complex-valued models that preserve equivariance under planar rotations. Across six classification benchmarks covering point clouds, graphs, cubical complexes, and meshes, the continuous encoding improves accuracy on five of six datasets, and control experiments attribute the gain to the tokenization itself rather than to the added transformer capacity. The representation architecture matters less than the encoding, and the payoff from its inductive biases depends on the encoding: a feedforward network performs best under continuous encoding but is less robust under discretization than convolutional architectures.

</details>


### [240] [MODIP: Efficient Model-Based Optimization for Diffusion Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10825)
*Zakariae El Asri,Philippe Gratias-Quiquandon,Nicolas Thome,Olivier Sigaud*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MODIP enables offline-to-online fine-tuning of diffusion policies by leveraging a world model and MPC to generate high-quality supervised targets, while using terminal-state values and policy-independent TD targets to improve efficiency. It preserves BC simplicity while achieving improved performance over BC and competitive results with RL-fine-tuning and strong model-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion policies are effective for imitation learning but are hard to fine-tune with reinforcement learning due to the multi-step denoising process; there is a need for stable, data-efficient online adaptation from offline data.

Method: In MODIP, a world model guides policy adaptation. Model predictive control (MPC) generates high-quality trajectories within the world model, which serve as supervised targets to fine-tune the diffusion policy. To improve efficiency, MPC planning uses a terminal state value instead of a policy-dependent value, and critics are trained with policy-independent TD targets.

Result: Empirical evaluation on D4RL (MuJoCo, Kitchen) and RoboMimic shows that MODIP improves diffusion policies beyond behavior cloning, and is competitive with or superior to diffusion-policy RL fine-tuning methods and strong model-based baselines such as TD-MPC2.

Conclusion: MODIP provides a data-efficient, stable offline-to-online fine-tuning framework for diffusion policies by integrating a world model and MPC-guided supervised updates, achieving improved performance and efficiency over BC and competitive with advanced baselines.

Abstract: Diffusion policies (DPs) have emerged as expressive policy representations for robot learning, often used with imitation learning methods such as behavioral cloning (BC). However, while their success has largely been confined to BC, direct reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning remains challenging because actions are generated through a multi-step denoising process. In this work, we propose MODIP, a framework for the offline-to-online fine-tuning of DPs. Rather than directly applying RL to the DPs, MODIP leverages a world model (WM) to guide policy adaptation and keeps the simplicity and stability of BC. We utilize model predictive control (MPC) to generate high-quality trajectories within the WM, and use them as supervised targets for fine-tuning the DP. To make MPC planning efficient, MODIP uses a terminal state value instead of a policy-dependent state-action value, reducing inference time. Additionally, MODIP trains critics with policy-independent TD targets, reducing training time. Experiments on D4RL (MuJoCo, Kitchen) and RoboMimic tasks show that MODIP improves diffusion policies beyond BC, and is competitive with or outperforms diffusion policy RL fine-tuning methods and strong model-based baselines such as TD-MPC2.

</details>


### [241] [Geometrically Averaged Hard Target Updates for Linear Q-Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10835)
*Donghwan Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a λ-target update for linear Q-learning, averaging m-periodic target updates with geometric weights to interpolate between one-step updates and projected Q-value iteration; analyzed in a deterministic switching-system framework; extensible to stochastic RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stabilize Q-learning with function approximation by smoothing target updates, providing a unified, tunable mechanism that covers known schemes as special cases.

Method: Define the λ-target update as a geometric-averaged, m-periodic target-update map. Analyze using a switching-system model for linear Q-learning, first in a deterministic setting, with discussion on extending to stochastic reinforcement learning.

Result: Characterizes the λ-target update as a parametric family that bridges one-step and projected-Q learning, with theoretical insights into stability via the switching-system perspective; endpoint cases recover known update schemes.

Conclusion: Offers a unified framework for target updates in linear Q-learning, potentially improving stability; suitable for extension to stochastic settings and broader function approximators.

Abstract: Periodic hard target updates are among the most common stabilization devices in modern deep Q-learning. Recent studies suggest that target updates can improve stability in Q-learning with function approximation, including linear function approximation. We introduce and analyze the so-called $λ$-target update, obtained by averaging the $m$-periodic target update maps with $λ$-geometric weights $(1-λ)λ^{m-1}$, $λ\in [0,1]$. The endpoint $λ=0$ recovers the one-period target update, while the continuous endpoint $λ\uparrow1$ recovers projected Q-value iteration. We study this mechanism for Q-learning with linear function approximation, namely linear Q-learning, using a switching-system model and related tools. For clarity, the paper treats a deterministic version; the formulation extends to stochastic reinforcement-learning settings.

</details>


### [242] [When Do Autoregressive Sequence Models Forecast Physical Wavefields? A Controlled Study on Synthetic Seismograms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10868)
*Waleed Esmail,Stuart Russell,Jana Klinge,Alexander Kappes,Christine Thomas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Long-horizon autoregressive forecasting of oscillatory wavefields is limited by error accumulation; multi-token prediction is the dominant stabilizer; horizon-embedding head and STFT-based coherence loss provide small gains; stability is highly sensitive to a context window near the full P-S interval; residual polarity inversions indicate the need for phase-aware objectives; study frames rollout stability rather than benchmarking architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand rollout stability of autoregressive models on oscillatory physical signals by performing controlled intra-architecture ablations, isolating design contributions, and assessing stability under free-running rollout with significance testing.

Method: Synthetic three-component seismograms used as a physically structured testbed; evaluation of the SeismoGPT autoregressive forecaster; intra-architecture ablations with paired significance tests; comparisons between multi-token versus single-token predictions; analysis of a horizon-embedding hybrid head; evaluation of a cross-horizon STFT-magnitude coherence loss; assessment of generalization as a function of context-ratio (near the P-S interval).

Result: Multi-token prediction accounts for nearly the entire stability improvement over a single-token baseline (median NCC gain ≈ +0.040); horizon-embedding head and STFT coherence loss yield small but consistent gains; rollout stability is highly sensitive to a context-ratio threshold near one (roughly the full P-S interval); below this threshold rollout generalization collapses; the dominant residual error is polarity inversion, which magnitude-based spectral losses cannot penalize, highlighting the need for phase-aware objectives.

Conclusion: The work frames rollout stability on oscillatory wavefields as a controlled study rather than a general benchmark, identifying multi-token prediction as the primary stabilizer and phase-related issues as the natural next step for improving long-horizon forecasts.

Abstract: Long-horizon autoregressive forecasting of oscillatory physical signals, such as seismograms, gravitational-wave strain, and similar wavefields is limited by error accumulation: as a causal model is fed its own outputs over hundreds of steps, small per-step errors compound into phase drift that pointwise metrics fail to detect. We ask when such rollout stays stable, using synthetic three-component seismograms as a physically structured testbed and the \textsc{SeismoGPT} autoregressive forecaster as the model under study. Through controlled, intra-architecture ablations evaluated on free-running rollout with paired significance tests, we isolate the contribution of each design choice. Multi-token prediction is the dominant stabilizer, accounting for almost the entire improvement over a single-token baseline ($+0.040$ median NCC); a horizon-embedding hybrid prediction head and a cross-horizon STFT-magnitude coherence loss each add a small but consistent further gain. Performance depends sharply on a context-ratio threshold near one, roughly the full P-S interval of observed signal, below which rollout generalization collapses. The dominant residual failure is a polarity inversion that a magnitude-based spectral loss cannot, by construction, penalize, identifying phase-aware objectives as the natural next step. We frame this as a controlled study of rollout stability on oscillatory wavefields, not a benchmark of forecasting architectures.

</details>


### [243] [Optimal Post-Training Quantization Scales and Where to Find Them](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10890)
*Juan Amboage,Pablo Monteagudo-Lago,Ian Colbert,Giuseppe Franco,Nicholas Fraser*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PiSO improves post-training quantization by explicitly optimizing channel-wise weight scales using calibration data, obtaining closed-form minimizers within finite scale intervals; extends to group-wise quantization and interleaves with error correction; yields consistent perplexity/zero-shot accuracy gains, especially at lower bit-widths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address accuracy degradation in PTQ for large language models by replacing data-free heuristics with principled, data-driven scale optimization.

Method: Partition the scale search space into a finite set of intervals; on each interval, derive a closed-form minimizer for the objective under round-to-nearest quantization; apply channel-wise (and group-wise) scales; interleave scale optimization with error correction; evaluate on Llama and Qwen models across sizes and target bit-widths using calibration data.

Result: Consistent improvements in perplexity and downstream zero-shot accuracy across tested models and bit-widths; larger gains as quantization becomes more challenging (lower bit-widths); benefits observed both standalone and when combined with error correction.

Conclusion: PiSO provides principled, data-driven scale optimization for PTQ that yields robust gains in LLM quantization, particularly in challenging, low-bit scenarios, and supports extension to group-wise quantization with effective error-correction interleaving.

Abstract: Post-training quantization (PTQ) compresses large language models by mapping weights to low-bit representations. The scaling factor that defines the quantization grid is typically chosen using simple, data-free heuristics. In this work, we present PiSO (Piecewise Scale Optimization), an algorithm that leverages calibration data to compute the optimal channel-wise weight scales exactly and efficiently under round-to-nearest quantization. PiSO partitions the scale search space into finitely many intervals on which the objective admits a closed-form minimizer. We extend PiSO to group-wise quantization via principled heuristics and propose effective strategies for interleaving scale optimization with error correction. Experiments on Llama and Qwen models across multiple model sizes and target weight bit-widths demonstrate consistent improvements in perplexity and downstream zero-shot accuracy, both standalone and combined with error correction. In particular, we observe increased benefits as the target bit-width narrows and quantization becomes more challenging.

</details>


### [244] [Flash-GMM: A Memory-Efficient Kernel for Scalable Soft Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10896)
*Gal Bloch,Ariel Gera,Matan Orbach,Ohad Eytan,Assaf Toledo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A fused Triton kernel (Flash-GMM) enables single-pass GMM computation on GPUs by avoiding the materialization of the full responsibility matrix, delivering 20x speedup and enabling training on datasets ~100x larger on a single device; integrated into IVF for ANN search, demonstrating soft GMM as a drop-in replacement for k-means with multi-cluster border assignment and efficiency gains; kernel is open source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GMM training and evaluation on large-scale data is memory- and computation-intensive, largely due to the full responsibility matrix. Reducing memory traffic and kernel overhead can unlock GPU-scale training and practical deployment in ANN pipelines.

Method: Develop a fused Flash-GMM kernel in Triton that computes GMM responsibilities on-the-fly in a single GPU pass, avoiding materialization of the full responsibility matrix. Integrate the kernel into the IVF coarse quantizer for ANN, enable soft clustering to replace k-means, and use GMM responsibilities to assign border vectors to multiple clusters.

Result: Achieves ~20× speedup over existing implementations; enables training on datasets >100× larger on one GPU. In ANN, soft GMM clustering serves as a drop-in for k-means; border vectors can be assigned to multiple clusters. Attains fixed recall targets with up to 1.7× fewer distance computations, or +2 to +12 recall@10 at equivalent compute.

Conclusion: Flash-GMM provides a scalable, open-source solution for fast probabilistic clustering on GPUs, expanding the practicality of GMMs in large-scale learning and ANN pipelines, and enabling effective soft clustering with reduced computation.

Abstract: We present \textbf{Flash-GMM}, a fused Triton kernel for efficient computation of Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) over large-scale data in a single GPU pass. By eliminating the need to materialize the full responsibility matrix in GPU memory, Flash-GMM achieves a \textbf{20$\times$} speedup over existing implementations and enables training on datasets more than \textbf{100$\times$} larger than previously feasible on one device. To demonstrate its impact, we integrate Flash-GMM into the IVF coarse quantizer for approximate nearest-neighbor (ANN) search. We show that soft GMM clustering is now a viable drop-in replacement for $k$-means, and that GMM responsibilities can be leveraged to assign border vectors to multiple clusters. Our approach reaches fixed recall targets with up to $1.7\times$ fewer distance computations, or equivalently, yields $+2$--$12$ recall@10 at matched computational cost. We release the kernel as an open-source project.

</details>


### [245] [Conservation Laws from Data Symmetry in Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10913)
*Jakob Galley,Vahid Shahverdi,Axel Flinth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Analytic, non-polynomial losses: data symmetries generically do not create extra invariants during gradient flow. For MSE with data augmentation, extra conserved quantities can arise. A framework of tensorizable networks explains this phenomenon, unifying linear, polynomial nets and Lightning Attention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how inherent data symmetries influence dynamical invariants (integrals of motion) of gradient-flow training, and when such symmetries produce conserved quantities.

Method: Theoretically analyze gradient-flow dynamics under analytic, non-polynomial loss; prove generically no new integrals from data symmetries. For MSE, examine scenarios with data augmentation. Introduce and develop a framework of tensorizable networks, where parameter-input dependencies separate via an intermediate representation, and demonstrate how these networks capture the observed invariants.

Result: 1) For analytic and non-polynomial losses, data symmetries do not generically yield extra conserved quantities during gradient flow. 2) For MSE loss, certain data augmentation setups yield additional conserved quantities. 3) The tensorizable networks framework explains and unifies these phenomena, encompassing linear, polynomial networks and Lightning Attention.

Conclusion: Data symmetries are not universally sources of conserved quantities in gradient-flow dynamics for generic analytic losses; however, with MSE and augmentation, conserved quantities can emerge. Tensorizable networks provide a unifying language to describe when and how these invariants arise across architectures.

Abstract: We explore whether intrinsic symmetries of the training data lead to conserved quantities during gradient-flow training of neural networks. Under the assumption that the loss function is analytic and non-polynomial, we prove that data symmetries generically do not induce any additional integrals of motion. For mean squared error (MSE) loss, on the other hand, there are situations in which data augmentation yields extra conserved quantities. We build a framework, utilizing \emph{tensorizable networks} to describe this phenomenon. Tensorizable networks are a family of architectures whose dependence on parameters and inputs can be separated using an intermediate representation. They include linear and polynomial networks, as well as Lightning Attention.

</details>


### [246] [Recoverable but Not Stationary:Local Linear Structures in Weights and Activations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10929)
*Irina Piontkovskaia,Sergey Nikolenko*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Linear task directions in trained networks are local and evolving rather than global; strong local low-rank task-gradient structure exists, but a fixed-task-plane model fails as the basis drifts quickly; a trajectory-prefix basis captures most of LoRA recovery; random search is theoretically justified in high dimensions; activation steering aligns with labelled-contrast vectors; results generalize across synthetic Transformers and LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether and how linear structures that control learned behavior exist, their scale, and whether they are global across parameter and activation spaces or local and evolving.

Method: Experiments on a synthetic multitask transformer and LoRA adapters on DistilGPT-2 / GPT-2; development of a Gaussian local-linear random-search theory; measurement of activation steering via a gradient step and its cosine similarity to a labelled-contrast steering vector; validation on Qwen-0.5B BoolQ statements; cross-model validation on synthetic transformers and LLMs.

Result: Found strong local low-rank task-gradient structure but rejected the fixed-task-plane hypothesis; the useful basis drifts within ~100 steps; the first recovery updates form a trajectory-prefix basis capturing ~77% of the LoRA recovery displacement; random-search theory justifies effectiveness of random parameter search in high dimensions; a single gradient step produces an activation shift with cosine similarity ~0.58 to a CAA steering vector, with similar effects on Qwen-0.5B BoolQ; results generalize across synthetic Transformers and LLMs.

Conclusion: Linear structures in trained networks are not fixed global task directions but evolving local geometries that persist partially across parameter and activation spaces.

Abstract: Task vectors, LoRA, activation steering, and random search around pretrained weights all suggest that learned behaviour can be controlled by linear directions. We ask which linear structures actually exist and on what scale. In a synthetic multitask transformer and LoRA adapters on DistilGPT-2 / GPT-2 we find strong local low-rank task-gradient structure but reject the fixed-task-plane hypothesis: static bases miss the recovery direction, and the useful basis drifts substantially within 100 steps. However, the first recovery updates form a trajectory-prefix basis capturing 77% of the LoRA recovery displacement. We develop random search theory with a Gaussian local-linear theorem that justifies the effectiveness of random parameter search even in very high dimensions. We also study the relation between parameter perturbations and activation steering: a single gradient step produces an activation shift with 0.58 cosine to a labelled-contrast CAA steering vector, with a similar steering effect on Qwen-0.5B BoolQ statements. We validate our results with experiments on synthetic Transformers and LLMs. Our results suggest that linear structures in trained networks are not global task directions, but evolving local geometries that partially persist across parameter and activation spaces.

</details>


### [247] [CLP: Collocation-Length Prediction for Zero-Loss Adaptive Multi-Token Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10935)
*Xuezhen Xie,Zhiqiang Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Backbone-as-Architect with CLP enables faster multi-token prediction (MTP) for LLMs by ensuring the backbone LM head generates the first token and MTP heads handle subsequent tokens. CLP is a tiny span-level predictor (single linear layer, ~4.6K–7.7K params) that decides how many additional tokens can be safely accepted per step, avoiding large gate networks. On Qwen2.5 models (0.5B, 1.5B, 7B), CLP achieves ~1.20x–1.29x speedups for 1.5B and ~1.14x–1.20x for 7B with zero quality degradation, while gate-based methods fail to accelerate or severely degrade output. Shorter horizons (k=2) boost MTP head accuracy by ~24% on large models, identifying MTP head accuracy as the binding constraint on acceleration and proposing a scaling-aware roadmap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive decoding is the bottleneck in LLM inference. While multi-token prediction (MTP) can accelerate decoding, existing MTP designs suffer from head-backbone competition: the MTP head competes with the backbone LM head for the first token, causing repetitions and incoherence. A design that separates responsibilities is needed to preserve output quality while accelerating inference.

Method: Introduce Backbone-as-Architect: the backbone LM head always generates the first token; MTP heads handle only subsequent tokens. Develop CLP (Collocation-Length Predictor): a lightweight span-level predictor implemented as a single linear layer (4.6K–7.7K params) to decide how many extra tokens to accept at each step; evaluate with shorter horizon k=2 on Qwen2.5 models (0.5B, 1.5B, 7B).

Result: CLP yields 1.20x–1.29x speedup on 1.5B and 1.14x–1.20x on 7B with zero quality loss (repetition ratio < 0.02). Gate-based MTP approaches either fail to accelerate (≈1.07x) or produce severe degradation (repetition ratio > 0.5%). MTP head accuracy improves by ~24% for large models with k=2, indicating head accuracy as the bottleneck for acceleration.

Conclusion: MTP acceleration is constrained by MTP head accuracy. The Backbone-as-Architect with CLP provides a scalable, low-parameter solution that achieves speedups without sacrificing quality; a scaling-aware roadmap is proposed to further improve MTP head accuracy and acceleration.

Abstract: Large language model inference is bottlenecked by autoregressive decoding, where each token requires a full forward pass. Multi-token prediction (MTP) offers a promising acceleration path, but existing approaches suffer from a fundamental architectural flaw: the MTP head for the first token competes with the backbone's own language model (LM) head, leading to severe quality degradation when predictions are accepted. We identify this head-backbone competition as the root cause of repetitive and incoherent outputs in prior MTP-based acceleration methods. To address this, we propose Backbone-as-Architect, a design principle where the backbone LM head always generates the first token, and MTP heads are responsible only for subsequent tokens. Building on this principle, we introduce CLP (Collocation-Length Predictor), a lightweight span-level decision layer that predicts how many additional tokens can be safely accepted at each decoding step. CLP uses only a single linear layer (4.6K--7.7K parameters), replacing the over-engineered 1M-parameter gate networks used in prior work. Experiments on Qwen2.5 models (0.5B, 1.5B, 7B) show that CLP achieves 1.20x--1.29x speedup on 1.5B and 1.14x--1.20x on 7B, with zero quality degradation (repetition ratio < 0.02), while gate-based approaches fail to accelerate (1.07x) or produce severely degraded outputs (repetition ratio > 0.5%). We further demonstrate that shorter prediction horizons (k=2) recover 24% higher MTP head accuracy on large models, establishing a scaling-aware design principle. We identify MTP head prediction accuracy as the binding constraint on acceleration and establish a clear roadmap for future improvements.

</details>


### [248] [A Systematic Approach for Selecting Trajectories for Data Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10938)
*Adam Nordling*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Five systematic trajectory-augmentation selection strategies (Outlierness, Diversity, Representativeness, Uncertainty, and Random baseline) were evaluated across four datasets with linear and nonlinear models. Systematic selection offers stable gains over random in many cases, especially Outlierness and Uncertainty, but benefits are conditional on dataset properties (sparsity, density, velocity). Optuna hyperparameter optimization identified dataset-specific augmentation parameters. Visual and qualitative analyses show systematic methods repair topology in sparse data but may inject noise in high-quality dense data; perturbation techniques can diverge in high-velocity domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap in understanding which augmented trajectories maximize predictive performance while preserving spatio-temporal coherence, beyond naive random geometric perturbation.

Method: Develop and compare five selection strategies (Outlierness, Diversity, Representativeness, Uncertainty, Random) for trajectory augmentation. Evaluate across four datasets (animal behavior – Foxes and Starkey; maritime AIS; urban traffic – Car) using a mix of linear and nonlinear models. Integrate an Optuna-based hyperparameter optimization loop to identify best augmentation parameters within the search space. Use UMAP for qualitative topology analysis and assess performance stability across regimes including high-velocity domains.

Result: Systematic strategies yield advantages over random baselines but are not universally superior. Outlierness and Uncertainty provide more stable gains, while systematic augmentation improves topology in sparse datasets but can introduce noise in dense data. High-velocity domains reveal limitations of standard perturbations due to divergence in feature space. Optuna identifies dataset-specific augmentation parameters, underscoring that augmentation value is conditional on data regime.

Conclusion: Augmentation effectiveness is conditional and dataset-dependent. Systematic selection strategies can outperform random baselines in certain regimes (notably sparse data and certain feature regimes) but require careful consideration of data density, velocity, and topological integrity. Practitioners should use systematic selection as a complementary approach and rely on dataset-aware hyperparameter tuning to maximize benefits.

Abstract: Trajectory data augmentation is a promising approach to mitigate data scarcity in machine learning applications, but its utility has been limited by the complexity of preserving spatio-temporal coherence. Although prior work demonstrated the viability of geometric perturbation, it relied on naive random selection, leaving a critical gap in understanding which trajectories should be augmented for maximal benefit. This thesis addresses this gap by developing a systematic and scalable framework to evaluate five systematic selection strategies: Outlierness, Diversity, Representativeness, Uncertainty, and Random selection. These strategies were rigorously tested across four datasets covering animal behavior (Foxes and Starkey), maritime traffic (AIS), and urban traffic (Car) using a suite of linear and non-linear machine learning models. As part of this evaluation, an Optuna-based hyperparameter optimization loop was integrated to empirically identify the best-performing augmentation parameters for each dataset within the explored search space. The results indicate that, while systematic selection is not a universal solution, it offers distinct advantages over the random baseline. Systematic strategies, particularly Outlierness and Uncertainty, demonstrated higher stability and were less prone to performance degradation observed with random sampling in dense datasets. However, the findings also reveal that the value of augmentation is strictly conditional. Visual analysis via UMAP demonstrates that while systematic augmentation successfully repairs topological fragmentation in sparse datasets, it can act as a corrupting noise signal in high-quality, dense datasets. Furthermore, the study identified physical limitations in high-velocity domains, where standard perturbation techniques lead to divergence in feature space...

</details>


### [249] [Express Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10944)
*Albert Gong,Annabelle Michael Carrell,Raaz Dwivedi,Lester Mackey*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Express converts non-causal attention approximations into causal ones with provable guarantees, achieving a log^{3/2}(n)/s error using only O(s) memory and O(s^2 log^2(n)) compression overhead, when paired with Thinformer. An I/O-aware Triton implementation yields speedups over FlashAttention 2 and helps alleviate four long-context bottlenecks in language modeling: prefill, KV cache compression, and long-form decoding under memory or compute constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Efficient, provable causal attention for long sequences. Non-causal attention approximations are fast but lack guarantees; classical causal attention is accurate but expensive. A bridge is needed to obtain the benefits of fast approximations while maintaining causal correctness and theoretical guarantees, suitable for long-context language modeling.

Method: Introduce Express to convert a non-causal attention approximation into a causal approximation with matching approximation guarantees. Combine Express with the Thinformer approximation to derive a bound of log^{3/2}(n)/s on the causal error, while using O(s) memory and O(s^2 log^2(n)) compression overhead. Implement an I/O-aware Triton kernel to optimize performance and evaluate on long-context language modeling tasks.

Result: The approach yields improved causal attention guarantees beyond the previous best, with the specified error bound and resource metrics. The Triton implementation delivers substantial speedups relative to FlashAttention 2, and the method mitigates key resource bottlenecks in the language modeling pipeline.

Conclusion: Express, in tandem with Thinformer and an optimized Triton implementation, provides a practical, provable, and scalable solution for efficient causal attention on long sequences, enabling improved long-context prefill, KV cache compression, and memory- and compute-constrained long-form decoding.

Abstract: We introduce a new tool, Express, for converting a non-causal attention approximation into a causal approximation with matching approximation guarantees. When combined with the state-of-the-art Thinformer approximation, Express improves upon the best known causal attention guarantees, delivering $\log^{3/2}(n)/s$ approximation error with only $O(s)$ memory and $O(s^2 \log^2(n))$ compression overhead for a sequence of length $n$. We pair these developments with an efficient I/O-aware Triton implementation, demonstrate substantial speedups over FlashAttention 2, and use Express to overcome four resource bottlenecks in the language modeling pipeline: long-context prefill, KV cache compression, long-form memory-constrained decoding, and long-form compute-constrained decoding.

</details>


### [250] [Population-Aware Physics-Informed Neural Particle Flow for Bayesian Update](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10959)
*Batu Candan,Simone Servadio*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Population-aware PINPF (PA-PINPF) augments each particle update with a permutation-invariant population representation, improving Bayesian transport over standard PINPF; feature-based encoding yields the strongest gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard PINPF updates particles independently, neglecting global population information. Incorporating the entire particle set via Deep Sets can capture population-level transport geometry and improve posterior transport in Bayesian inference tasks.

Method: Introduce PA-PINPF with two encoders: PA-PINPF-State (summary of particle states) and PA-PINPF-Feature (full local physics-informed features: position, pseudo-time, measurements, likelihood, score). Uses permutation-invariant Deep Sets, preserves physics-informed residual objective, requires no ground-truth posterior samples.

Result: Both PA-PINPF variants outperform particle-wise PINPF; the feature-based encoding provides the strongest improvement. Validation on range-measurement tasks and nonlinear time-difference-of-arrival posterior transport.

Conclusion: Population-level physics features enable global information for learned Bayesian particle transport, enhancing transport geometry while preserving unsupervised physics-informed learning.

Abstract: Physics-informed neural particle flow (PINPF) learns a deterministic transport field that moves particles from a prior distribution toward a Bayesian posterior while enforcing the governing probability-evolution equation. However, the standard PINPF velocity model processes particles independently and therefore does not explicitly condition its transport decisions on the empirical particle population. This paper introduces population-aware PINPF (PA-PINPF), which augments each particle update with a permutation-invariant Deep Sets representation of the full particle set. We investigate two population encoders. PA-PINPF-State summarizes the particle states, whereas PA-PINPF-Feature summarizes the complete local physics-informed feature vectors, including particle position, pseudo-time, measurement information, likelihood values, and score information. The latter allows the population context to represent not only particle-cloud geometry, but also the population-level Bayesian transport geometry. The methods retain the original unsupervised physics-informed residual objective and require no ground-truth posterior samples during training. Experiments on range-measurement tasks and nonlinear time-difference-of-arrival posterior transport demonstrate that both population-aware variants improve over particle-wise PINPF, while feature-population encoding provides the strongest performance. These results show that population-level physics features provide useful global information for learned Bayesian particle transport.

</details>


### [251] [Beyond Uniform Token-Level Trust Region in LLM Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10968)
*Renjie Mao,Xiangxin Zhou,Lvfang Tao,Yixin Ding,Yu Shi,Yongguang Lin,Yuheng Wu,Honglin Zhu,Qian Qiu,Wenxi Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CPPO introduces token-level masking with position-aware thresholds and a cumulative prefix budget to align RL updates with autoregressive, finite-horizon policy-improvement, improving stability and reasoning accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PPO-style trust-region methods apply uniform thresholds across tokens, ignoring autoregressive dynamics; early deviations cause drift and late tokens constrained unnecessarily; evaluating tokens independently ignores cumulative prefix drift.

Method: Two coupled mechanisms: (1) position-weighted thresholds applying stricter constraints at early positions; (2) a cumulative prefix budget tracking historical deviations to prevent further divergence along the prefix; implemented as a token-level masking rule aligned with finite-horizon bound.

Result: Empirical results show CPPO enhances training stability and significantly improves reasoning accuracy across various model scales.

Conclusion: The cumulative-prefix approach mitigates autoregressive-specific degradation, enabling more reliable policy optimization for LLM reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become standard for improving LLM reasoning. However, existing PPO-style trust-region mechanisms remain position-agnostic by enforcing uniform thresholds across all tokens independently. This pointwise treatment conflicts with autoregressive generation in two critical ways. First, uniform thresholds ignore autoregressive asymmetry. Early-stage deviations produce compounding sequence-level drift, causing static thresholds to under-regulate early divergence and excessively constrain late-stage exploration. Second, evaluating token-level divergence in isolation overlooks cumulative prefix drift, granting the same divergence allowance regardless of how far the conditioning history has already deviated from the rollout policy. To address this limitation, we propose CPPO (Cumulative Prefix-divergence Policy Optimization), a token-level masking rule that aligns updates with a finite-horizon policy-improvement bound via two coupled mechanisms. First, a position-weighted threshold imposes stricter limits at early positions whose effects persist longer, relaxing constraints for late-stage tokens. Second, a cumulative prefix budget tracks historical deviations, dynamically restricting further token-level deviation to prevent compounding errors along the prefix. Empirically, CPPO enhances training stability and significantly improves reasoning accuracy across various model scales.

</details>


### [252] [Learning Doubly Sparse Explicitly Conditioned Transforms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10975)
*Tudor Pistol*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a structured, data-adaptive transform as product of a fixed canonical matrix and a refining sparse component; uses inexact proximal methods with a closed-form projection; achieves state-of-the-art results on doubly sparse transform learning with lower cost, faster convergence, and improved local-minima behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need for sparse representations that adapt to data while retaining the speed and stability of analytical transforms; fixed transforms (DFT/DCT) are suboptimal for restrictive signal structures; condition number can guide the trade-off between generalization and approximation error; novelty lies in an explicitly conditioned transform formulation.

Method: Model the transform as T = C * S, where C is a fixed canonical matrix and S is a data-adaptive sparse component; learn via an inexact proximal optimization framework with a newly derived closed-form projection operator; algorithm emphasizes fast, stable updates.

Result: Empirically achieves state-of-the-art performance on doubly sparse transform learning; compares favorably with the dense variant while requiring significantly lower computational cost, with faster convergence and improved avoidance of bad local minima.

Conclusion: Preserves the advantages of fast, analytical transforms while introducing controllable data adaptivity; the formulation appears novel and practical for applications in data compression, denoising, and feature extraction due to efficiency gains and potentially better representations.

Abstract: Finding convenient spaces in which certain hypotheses regarding an assumed sparse structure of natural signals hold true has become a desirable result in recent research, its implications being reflected in areas such as data compression, noise reduction and feature extraction. While the extensively used analytical transforms, such as DFT or DCT, already provide efficient algorithms and robust sparse representations, they assume a fixed prior about the data, failing to accurately capture the specific structure of more restrictive classes of signals. To address this, the concept of a data-adaptive, learnt transform has been introduced in the literature, allowing for the reduction of a residual term in the transform domain. More recent studies have shown that the condition number serves as a good metric in this context, where the desired outcome alternates between a generalizing tendency and one that achieves minimal approximation error. Motivated by these considerations, we introduce the learning of a structured, explicitly conditioned transform formulated as the product of a fixed canonical matrix and a refining data-adaptive sparse component. This approach seeks to preserve the advantages of fast and stable analytical transforms, while introducing controllable adaptivity to the data. No references that concern this specific formulation have been identified so far, indicating its novelty. The proposed algorithm is motivated within the framework of inexact proximal methods, leveraging a newly derived closed-form projection operator. Empirical observations demonstrate state-of-the-art results on the doubly sparse transform learning problem and comparable performance with its dense variant at significantly lower computational costs and sometimes faster convergence and better avoidance of bad local minima.

</details>


### [253] [Data-Driven Runway and Taxiway Exits Prediction of Landing Aircraft: A Case Study at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11017)
*Alex Porcayo,Yutian Pang,Maria Thomas,John-Paul Clarke*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A two-stage data-driven decision aid for KATL arrival taxi-in; Stage I predicts the runway exit, Stage II predicts crossing the active departure runway at a designated point versus using the end-around taxiway. Gradient-boosting models (XGBoost/LightGBM) outperform others across east/west flows. Stage I accuracy 0.86–0.89 (macro-F1 0.40–0.50); Stage II accuracy 0.70–0.74 (macro-F1 0.28–0.55). Approach speed drives exit choice; ramp destination, departure rate, crossing rate, and exit choice predict crossing vs end-around. Minority classes are hard due to feature-space overlap (shown by t-SNE/UMAP). Framework aims for calibrated, explainable predictions with human-in-the-loop control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve efficiency and safety of high-throughput airport surface operations by supporting controller decisions on taxi-out routing and exit selection using data-driven, interpretable predictions that mirror controller workflow.

Method: Two-stage classification framework. Stage I predicts runway exit for arriving aircraft; Stage II predicts whether, given the exit, the aircraft will cross the active departure runway at a designated point or take an end-around taxiway. Training uses ASDE-X surface trajectories, aircraft characteristics, ramp destinations, short-horizon traffic rates, and weather across multiple look-back windows. Benchmark across nine classifiers (e.g., Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost) with metrics including accuracy, macro-F1, precision-recall, confusion matrices, Brier score, and Expected Calibration Error. Feature-importance analysis performed; t-SNE/UMAP used to assess class separability.

Result: Stage I: 0.86–0.89 accuracy; macro-F1 0.40–0.50. Stage II: 0.70–0.74 accuracy; macro-F1 0.28–0.55. XGBoost and LightGBM outperform Random Forest across flows. Approach speed is the key predictor of exit. For crossing vs end-around: departure rate, crossing rate, ramp destination, and west flow exit are strong predictors. Minority classes remain harder due to feature-space overlap.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides calibrated, explainable predictions to support controller situational awareness while preserving human responsibility for routing decisions; it integrates with controller workflow to potentially improve surface operation efficiency at high-throughput hubs.

Abstract: Airport surface operations increasingly constrain performance at high-throughput hubs. This study examines arrival taxi-in decisions at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport (KATL) and proposes a two-stage, data-driven decision aid that mirrors controller workflow. Stage I predicts the runway exit selected by an arriving aircraft. Stage II predicts whether, given that exit, the aircraft will cross the active departure runway at a designated point or use the end-around taxiway. Models are trained using ASDE-X surface trajectories, aircraft characteristics, ramp destinations, short-horizon traffic rates, and weather across multiple look-back windows. We benchmark nine classifiers, including Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, and CatBoost, and evaluate accuracy, macro-F1, precision-recall behavior, confusion matrices, Brier score, and Expected Calibration Error. Across east and west flows, XGBoost and LightGBM outperform Random Forest. Stage I achieves 0.86-0.89 accuracy with macro-F1 scores of 0.40-0.50, while Stage II achieves 0.70-0.74 accuracy with macro-F1 scores of 0.28-0.55. Feature-importance analysis shows that approach speed is the main driver of exit choice. Departure rate, crossing rate, ramp destination, and, for west flow, the selected exit are the strongest predictors of crossing versus end-around routing. Minority classes remain harder to predict because of feature-space overlap, as shown by t-SNE and UMAP analyses. The proposed framework supports controller situational awareness through calibrated, explainable predictions while preserving human responsibility for final routing decisions.

</details>


### [254] [Flow-DPPO: Divergence Proximal Policy Optimization for Flow Matching Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11025)
*Bowen Ping,Xiangxin Zhou,Penghui Qi,Minnan Luo,Liefeng Bo,Tianyu Pang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Flow-DPPO replaces ratio clipping in flow-based denoising RL with a divergence proximal constraint, leveraging Gaussian per-step policies for exact KL, leading to better stability and rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ratio clipping in PPO is a poor proxy for true policy divergence in flow models, causing uneven constraints across trajectory regions and hindering training stability.

Method: Introduce Flow-DPPO: replace ratio clipping with a divergence proximal constraint; exploit Gaussian per-step policies to compute KL exactly; use an asymmetric divergence mask to block gradients only when moving outside the trusted region and exceeding divergence threshold.

Result: Flow-DPPO achieves higher rewards, improved KL-proximal efficiency, reduced catastrophic forgetting, more balanced multi-objective optimization, and stable multi-epoch training where ratio clipping degrades performance.

Conclusion: Flow-DPPO provides a more appropriate divergence constraint for flow-based RL in denoising tasks, improving performance and stability over ratio clipping; implementation and models are available online.

Abstract: Recent work has demonstrated that online reinforcement learning (RL) can substantially improve the quality and alignment of flow matching models for image and video generation. Methods such as Flow-GRPO and CPS cast the denoising process as a Markov Decision Process and apply PPO-style ratio clipping to enforce a trust region. However, we argue that ratio clipping is structurally ill-suited for flow models: the probability ratio between new and old policies is a noisy, single-sample estimate of the true policy divergence, leading to over-constraining in some regions of the trajectory and under-constraining in others. We propose Flow-DPPO (Flow Divergence Proximal Policy Optimization), which replaces ratio clipping with a divergence proximal constraint. A key observation is that the per-step policy in flow models is Gaussian, enabling exact and cheap computation of the KL divergence between old and new policies. Flow-DPPO employs an asymmetric divergence mask that blocks gradient updates only when they simultaneously move away from the trusted region and violate the divergence threshold. Experiments show that Flow-DPPO achieves higher rewards with better KL-proximal efficiency, alleviates catastrophic forgetting, promotes balanced multi-objective optimization, and enables stable multi-epoch training where ratio clipping degrades. Code and models are available at https://github.com/Tencent-Hunyuan/UniRL/tree/main/FlowDPPO.

</details>


### [255] [AuRA: Internalizing Audio Understanding into LLMs as LoRA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11033)
*Bo Cheng,Lei Shi,Zhanyu Ma,Yuan Wu,Jun Xu,Jiuchong Gao,Jinghua Hao,Renqing He*

Main category: cs.LG
