TL;DR: AuRA distills audio encoding into an LLM by aligning a LoRA-tuned LLM with an ASR encoder (teacher) via a lightweight audio embedding and layer-wise distillation, enabling tighter speech-language integration with parallel end-to-end inference and reuse of pretrained components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional speech-language systems rely on cascaded ASR-LLM pipelines, end-to-end speech-language models, or bridge-based adaptations, which incur transcript latency, multimodal training cost, or sequential coupling. A compact method to internalize speech representations within the LLM would improve efficiency and latency.

Method: Use ASR encoder as teacher and a LoRA-adapted LLM as student. Feed same speech input through a lightweight audio embedding layer to both, then perform layer-wise distillation to align student hidden states with teacher representations, effectively transferring audio encoding into the LLM-side adaptations.

Result: AuRA outperforms cascaded systems, speech-to-LLM adaptation baselines, and large multimodal models on multiple speech-language benchmarks in both effectiveness and efficiency.

Conclusion: Distilling audio encoding into the LLM enables tighter speech-language coupling, end-to-end parallel inference, and reuse of pretrained components, providing a practical and efficient alternative to existing cascaded or monolithic multimodal approaches.

Abstract: Recent efforts to extend large language models (LLMs) to speech inputs typically rely on cascaded ASR-LLM pipelines, end-to-end speech-language models, or bridge/distillation-based adaptation. While these routes respectively reuse strong pretrained components, enable native speech-language interaction, or offer lightweight adaptation, they often suffer from transcript-interface latency, costly multimodal training, or sequential speech-language coupling. To address these limitations, we present AuRA, a method that distills audio encoding capability into the LLM. Specifically, AuRA feeds the same speech input to an ASR encoder (as a teacher) and a LoRA-adapted LLM (as a student) through a lightweight audio embedding layer, and uses layer-wise distillation to align the student's hidden states with corresponding teacher representations, thereby internalizing speech representations into lightweight LLM-side adaptations. Compared with cascaded and serial bridge methods, AuRA enables tighter speech-language joint modeling and efficient parallel end-to-end inference, while also reusing pretrained speech and language models rather than requiring large-scale multimodal training. On multiple speech-language benchmarks, AuRA consistently outperforms cascaded systems, speech-to-LLM adaptation baselines, and large-scale speech-language and multimodal models in both effectiveness and efficiency.

</details>


### [256] [Flexible Kernels for Protein Property Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11057)
*Martin Jankowiak,Yerdos Ordabayev,Rudraksh Tuwani,Henry N. Ward,Hunter Nisonoff,James M. McFarland,Gevorg Grigoryan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A kernel-based, data-efficient GP framework for predicting protein properties that leverages evolutionary substitution matrices and structure-aware substitutions, outperforming embedding-based baselines and enabling multi-task learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predicting protein properties from sparse data is challenging; need models that leverage evolutionary signals and structure information to improve data efficiency and transfer across tasks.

Method: Propose a class of sequence kernels combining evolutionary substitution matrices with local linearity; use Gaussian processes; learn structure-aware substitution matrices and incorporate structure information from foundation models; apply to multi-task learning.

Result: GP kernels with these designs outperform foundation-model embedding baselines and local learning methods; structure-conditioned kernels improve multi-task learning across multiple protein properties.

Conclusion: Structure-aware, evolution-informed sequence kernels enable data-efficient, cross-property modeling of protein landscapes and integrate structure information effectively.

Abstract: Despite its importance to applications in protein design, predicting protein properties like binding affinity and thermostability from sparse experimental data remains a significant challenge. Accordingly, we introduce a class of sequence kernels that exploit evolutionary substitution matrices as well as local linearity and demonstrate that the resulting Gaussian processes provide data-efficient models of protein property landscapes, frequently outperforming alternatives that rely on foundation model embeddings. Furthermore--by learning what are in effect structure-aware substitution matrices--we show that our kernels can readily incorporate structural information from foundation models. We demonstrate that these structure-conditioned kernels are well suited to multi-task learning across multiple protein property landscapes and can decisively outperform local supervised learning methods.

</details>


### [257] [GRAFT: Gain-Recalibrated Adapters for Transformer-Based Neural Population Activity Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11066)
*Xiangsheng Ge,Yang Xie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GRAFT separates reusable temporal dynamics from a recalibratable neuron interface in a Transformer-based neural population model, enabling cross-day recalibration with minimal parameter updates and achieving state-of-the-art co-bps on MC Maze NLB'21 and strong cross-day performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard read-in/readout tied to fixed neuron sets limits long-term brain-computer interface reuse; a reusable temporal backbone paired with a recalibratable neuron interface can improve data efficiency across days.

Method: A Transformer backbone models neural population activity; a neuron interface governs entry/exit of recorded neurons into the shared backbone; auxiliary gain and positional mechanisms support within-Transformer neural activity modeling. Evaluation includes MC Maze under NLB'21 and cross-day calibration across scaled MC Maze datasets (Large/Medium/Small), with updates limited to ~9.21% of parameters.

Result: On MC Maze NLB'21 protocol, GRAFT achieves 0.3866 co-bps as an ensemble, setting a new state of the art for the primary co-bps metric among public/reported NLB'21 results. In a cross-day protocol from MC Maze to scaled MC Maze datasets, updating only 9.21% of parameters yields co-bps of 0.3749 (Large), 0.3112 (Medium), and 0.3152 (Small) with restricted target-day support sets.

Conclusion: Interface-backbone separation enables both strong Transformer-based neural population modeling and data-efficient cross-day recalibration.

Abstract: Neural population activity models can recover rich temporal structure from binned spikes, but their read-in and readout layers often remain tied to a fixed set of recorded neurons. This coupling limits reuse in long-term brain-computer interfaces, where recorded neuron identities, counts, and response statistics can change across days. We introduce GRAFT, a Transformer-based neural population activity model that separates reusable temporal dynamics from a recalibratable neuron interface. The neuron interface controls how recorded neurons enter and leave the shared backbone, and auxiliary gain and positional mechanisms support neural activity modeling inside the Transformer. On MC Maze under the standard NLB'21 protocol, GRAFT reaches 0.3866 co-bps as an ensemble, setting a new state of the art on the primary co-bps metric among public and reported NLB'21 results. In a cross-day protocol constructed from the NLB'21 MC Maze dataset series, GRAFT recalibrates from MC Maze to the scaled MC Maze datasets (Large/Medium/Small) by updating only 9.21% of parameters, reaching 0.3749, 0.3112, and 0.3152 co-bps with restricted target-day support sets. These results show that the same interface-backbone separation supports both strong Transformer-based neural population activity modeling and data-efficient cross-day recalibration.

</details>


### [258] [Exploring the Design Space of Reward Backpropagation for Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11075)
*Ruoyu Wang,Boye Niu,Xiangxin Zhou,Yushi Huang,Tongliang Liu,Chi Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlowBP introduces a unified surrogate-trajectory framework for reward-based alignment of text-to-image flow models, bounding memory and gradient chaining, with three instantiations (FlowBP-Sparse, FlowBP-Bridge, FlowBP-Lagrange) that outperform direct-gradient baselines on multiple metrics across several model/setting combos.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Direct reward backpropagation is sample-efficient but suffers from memory and gradient-pathologies; existing connector-based methods decouple sampling and optimization but risk gradient quality when the short path misapproximates the full rollout. A unified, design-objective backward trajectory can reconcile sampling/optimization while bounding resources.

Method: Define backward surrogate built from cached forward rollout; keep no-gradient cached rollout for sampling; enable four design choices: reward-model input, active set, integration weights, bridge coupling; instantiate three variants with different reconstruction/bridging/quadrature strategies; memory is bounded by active-set size; limit gradient chaining to at most one Jacobian factor; recover prior methods as special cases.

Result: Empirical evaluation on SD3.5-M, FLUX.1-dev, and FLUX.2-Klein-base across preference, quality, and compositional metrics shows FlowBP variants outperform direct-gradient baselines on most metrics.

Conclusion: FlowBP provides a flexible, memory-bounded, surrogate-trajectory framework for reward backpropagation in text-to-image alignment, with three concrete variants that achieve consistent gains and subsume prior methods as special cases.

Abstract: Aligning text-to-image flow matching models with human preferences via direct reward backpropagation is sample-efficient but hampered by two well-known pathologies: activations cannot be stored across the full sampling trajectory at modern model scale, and chained Jacobian products across steps inflate the reward gradient as it travels back to early indices. Connector-based methods, such as LeapAlign, address these issues by replacing the full backward trajectory with a short pinned path, highlighting a useful decoupling between sampling and optimization. However, the quality of the resulting gradient depends on how accurately this short path approximates the full rollout, especially over long intervals. We propose FlowBP, a unified surrogate-trajectory framework that treats the backward trajectory itself as the design object. FlowBP keeps a no-gradient cached rollout for sampling, then builds a lightweight backward surrogate from cached and selectively re-forwarded velocities. This view separates four choices: the reward-model input, active set, integration weights, and bridge coupling, and recovers prior direct-gradient methods as particular settings. Within this framework, we instantiate three variants: FlowBP-Sparse uses sparse Euler reconstruction, FlowBP-Bridge adds controlled bridge coupling, and FlowBP-Lagrange raises the order of leap quadrature. All three bound memory by the active-set size and limit gradient chaining to at most one Jacobian factor. Across SD3.5-M, FLUX.1-dev, and FLUX.2-Klein-base on preference, quality, and compositional metrics, the three variants improve over direct-gradient baselines on most metrics.

</details>


### [259] [Unifying Local Communications and Local Updates for LLM Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11081)
*Pietro Cagnasso,Eugene Belilovsky,Edouard Oyallon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GASLoC is a decentralized, gossip-based pre-training method for LLMs that generalizes outer optimizers to support adaptive optimizers, local steps, and sparse randomized peer communication, achieving superior performance to other decentralized methods in single-step-per-communication settings and competitive results with DiLoCo when multiple local steps are used, with clear advantages under heterogeneous bandwidth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address communication bottlenecks in large-scale distributed LLM pre-training by moving away from synchronous All-Reduce to decentralized, gossip-based communication that tolerates heterogeneity and supports adaptive optimization.

Method: Introduce GASLoC, a decentralized pre-training algorithm that leverages the outer-optimizer concept to enable a practical gossip-based framework compatible with adaptive optimizers, allows local optimization steps, and uses sparsified, random peer communication. Evaluate across multiple topologies and compare to state-of-the-art decentralized methods (e.g., DiLoCo) under single-step and multi-step local updates, including heterogeneous bandwidth scenarios.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows GASLoC outperforms existing decentralized algorithms in single communication step settings across varied topologies. When employing multiple local steps, GASLoC achieves performance competitive with DiLoCo. In heterogeneous bandwidth settings, GASLoC significantly outperforms DiLoCo.

Conclusion: A decentralized, gossip-based training framework with outer-optimizer support can effectively accelerate LLM pre-training, tolerate heterogeneity, and maintain competitive performance with existing decentralized approaches, especially when leveraging multiple local steps and sparse communication.

Abstract: Communication-efficient pre-training of LLMs is increasingly important as training draws on compute distributed across clusters, data centers, and lower-bandwidth links. Many practical methods reduce communication frequency but still rely on synchronous All-Reduce operations that maintain identical model states and tie progress to global collectives. This can become a bottleneck when bandwidth or worker speed is heterogeneous. We introduce GASLoC, a novel decentralized pre-training algorithm that generalizes the notion of communication acceleration to the recently popular "outer optimizer" to allow a practical gossip-based training framework that is compatible with adaptive optimizers, allows for local optimizer steps, and can utilize sparse randomized peer communication. Empirically, on a number of standard LLM training tasks, we demonstrate that GASLoC outperforms state-of-the-art decentralized algorithms in single step per communication setting for a number of topologies and, unlike existing decentralized methods in the LLM setting, it allows to obtain performance competitive with DiLoCo when utilizing multiple local steps. In the heterogeneous bandwidth setting we demonstrate the advantage of GASLoC showing that it can significantly outperform DiLoCo.

</details>


### [260] [Test-Time Gradient Guidance of Flow Policies in Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11087)
*Zhiyuan Zhou,Andy Peng,Charles Xu,Qiyang Li,Tobias Springenberg,Kevin Frans,Sergey Levine*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QGF (Q-Guided Flow) is a test-time policy-improvement RL method that guides a pre-trained expressive flow policy using the value gradient, avoiding policy updates during deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Expressive continuous policies (e.g., diffusion/flow models) scale poorly when trained with RL due to instability from backprop through denoising and specialized objectives. A simple test-time improvement could provide competitive performance without training-time RL instability.

Method: Pre-train a reference flow policy via behavioral cloning and a value function critic. At test time, use the critic's value gradient to steer actions of the reference policy to higher-value outcomes, with no additional policy learning.

Result: QGF outperforms prior test-time RL methods on single-task and goal-conditioned offline RL benchmarks with high-dimensional action spaces; competitive with state-of-the-art training-time algorithms while cheaper to run; scales favorably with model size by avoiding actor-critic instability.

Conclusion: Test-time policy optimization with a pre-trained expressive policy and a value gradient offers a practical, scalable alternative to training-time RL, delivering strong performance without unstable policy training.

Abstract: Expressive continuous control policies, such as diffusion and flow models, form the backbone of recent advances in scaling imitation learning for simulated and real robot control. While they are known to scale stably in the supervised imitation learning setting, incorporating them into reinforcement learning (RL) pipelines for policy improvement has proven more difficult. It often requires specialized training objectives or backpropagating through denoising processes, which cause well-known issues with stability and affect scalability. In this paper we study the question of whether simple policy improvement schemes at test time alone, leaving stable supervised policy training intact, can be a competitive alternative which sidesteps these issues. To this end, we propose QGF (Q-Guided Flow), an RL algorithm that performs policy optimization entirely at test time. QGF works by pre-training both a reference flow policy (via a standard behavioral cloning objective) and a value function critic and, at test time, using the value gradient to guide the reference policy to generate higher-value actions without any additional policy learning. Empirically, QGF outperforms prior test-time RL methods on single-task and goal-conditioned offline RL benchmarks with high-dimensional action spaces, and is competitive with state-of-the-art training-time algorithms while being much cheaper to run. Moreover, it exhibits favorable scaling with model size by avoiding the instability of actor-critic training, offering a practical and effective alternative RL algorithm with expressive policies.

</details>


### [261] [Limitations of Learning Tanh Neural Networks with Finite Precision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11104)
*Philipp Grohs,Matěj Trödler*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Finite-precision limits prevent learning tanh networks faster than Monte Carlo rates for L^p error using m samples, unless the budget grows exponentially with network size; localized bumps built via iterated tanh activations drive the lower bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand fundamental learnability limits of tanh neural networks under finite precision and to extend known results from ReLU to smooth activations, clarifying how precision, sampling, and architecture interact.

Method: Construct sharply localized bump functions using iterated tanh activations and analyze adaptive randomized learning algorithms with m samples under finite-precision arithmetic; derive lower bounds showing no rate better than O(m^{-1/p}) unless sampling budgets grow exponentially with network parameters.

Result: Established a lower bound: in finite precision, no adaptive randomized algorithm with m samples can achieve convergence faster than the Monte Carlo rate O(m^{-1/p}) in L^p, unless the sampling budget scales exponentially with network size; demonstrated the limitation for tanh networks analogous to ReLU results.

Conclusion: Finite precision imposes fundamental learnability constraints for function classes with localized bumps; to surpass Monte Carlo rates for tanh networks, one must incur exponential growth in sampling budget relative to network complexity; the work generalizes prior ReLU-based results to smooth activations and highlights precision-driven barriers in practical learning scenarios.

Abstract: We investigate limitations of learning $\tanh$ neural networks from point evaluations under finite-precision computations and $L^p$ accuracy guarantees, building on Berner, Grohs, and Voigtländer (2023). Our approach is based on a novel construction of sharply localized bump functions via iterated $\tanh$ activations. Using this mechanism, we show that, in a finite-precision setting, no adaptive randomized algorithm based on $m$ samples can achieve a convergence rate higher than the Monte Carlo rate $O(m^{-1/p})$ in the $L^p$ norm, unless the sampling budget grows exponentially with the size of the network parameters and architecture. The results reveal fundamental limitations imposed by finite precision on the learnability of classes containing localized bump functions, extending previous results for ReLU networks to the $\tanh$ setting.

</details>


### [262] [Data-Driven Dynamic Assortment in Online Platforms: Learning about Two Sides](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11118)
*Rahul Roy,Nur Sunar,Jayashankar M. Swaminathan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dynamic two-sided assortment on a platform with unknown customer and seller choice parameters; introduces a data-driven algorithm with polylog regret and a matching lower bound.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designing revenue-optimizing strategies for two-sided service platforms where key choice-model parameters are unknown and must be learned online.

Method: Propose a data-driven learning algorithm that simultaneously estimates both sides' multinomial logit parameters while selecting assortments; analyze regret against a clairvoyant benchmark.

Result: Worst-case regret grows polylogarithmically with time; a matching information-theoretic lower bound is established, implying rate-optimality.

Conclusion: This work is the first to address dynamic assortments with incomplete information on both sides; it provides a provably efficient learning-and-earning framework for two-sided platforms.

Abstract: We study a dynamic assortment problem on a two-sided service platform with incomplete information and heterogeneous customers in a discrete-time setting. In each period, a customer arrives seeking service, and the platform chooses an assortment of sellers to display. The customer then proposes a transaction to at most one seller in the assortment according to a multinomial logit choice model. After a fixed number of periods, sellers review the proposals they have received and each chooses at most one customer according to another multinomial logit choice model, after which the cycle repeats. A key challenge is that the platform does not know the choice-model parameters of either customers or sellers in advance. To our knowledge, this is the first study of a dynamic assortment problem in which both sides' choice parameters are unknown. We develop a data-driven algorithm that learns these parameters while optimizing the platform's objective over time. We evaluate performance using regret, which measures revenue loss relative to a clairvoyant benchmark that knows all parameters and customer arrivals in advance. We show that the algorithm's worst-case regret grows polylogarithmically over time, and we derive a matching lower bound, establishing its rate optimality.

</details>


### [263] [TRACE: A Unified Rollout Budget Allocation Framework for Efficient Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11119)
*Heming Zou,Qi Wang,Yun Qu,Yuhang Jiang,Lizhou Cai,Yixiu Mao,Ru Peng,Xin Xu,Weijie Liu,Kai Yang,Saiyong Yang,Xiangyang Ji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRACE introduces a tree-structured rollout allocation method to improve reward contrast in multi-turn RL for LLMs by allocating budget to prompts and intermediate prefixes, guided by a predictor of success probability; it yields efficiency gains and performance improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL with verifiable rewards often suffers from low reward contrast due to outcome-only rewards and low-variance feedback across multi-turn prompts; there is a need to leverage turn-level information to improve sample efficiency.

Method: Model each ReAct-style turn as a semantically distinct node and allocate rollout budget from prompts to intermediate prefixes, forming tree-structured rollouts. Use a shared predictor to estimate conditional success probability at anchors to guide allocation.

Result: TRACE achieves competitive performance and efficiency gains on agentic benchmarks, notably improving Qwen3-14B Multi-Hop QA average accuracy by 2.8 points at equal sampling cost, relative to competitive baselines.

Conclusion: A unified rollout allocation framework that enriches outcome feedback via tree-structured rollouts and prefix-level guidance, improving reward contrast and policy-update signals in multi-turn agentic RL.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is a promising approach for enhancing reasoning and agentic behavior in large language models. However, rollout-intensive policy optimization is often limited by insufficient reward contrast, arising when overly simple or complex prompts generate low-variance feedback and when outcome-only rewards assign the same terminal assessment to every decision in a multi-turn rollout. Past efforts have focused on allocating available rollout resources to promising prompts, yet they only leverage sample informativeness at the prompt level and neglect variation in prefix-level informativeness across turns within the same rollout. This work targets multi-turn agentic RL by modeling each ReAct-style thought-action-observation turn as a semantically distinct node, allowing budget allocation to extend from prompt roots to turn-level prefixes with further continuations, which naturally forms tree-structured rollouts. We introduce Tree Rollout Allocation for Contrastive Exploration (TRACE), a unified rollout allocation framework that enhances reward contrast within a fixed sampling budget. Technically, TRACE allocates rollout budget to both prompt roots and intermediate prefixes that are most likely to yield mixed terminal rewards. A shared generalizable predictor estimates conditional success probability at these anchors from prefix histories to guide this allocation. The resulting adaptive tree structure enriches outcome-only feedback and amplifies the policy-update signal. Empirically, TRACE achieves competitive performance and efficiency gains on typical agentic benchmarks, e.g., improving Qwen3-14B Multi-Hop QA average accuracy by 2.8 points over competitive baselines at equal sampling cost.

</details>


### [264] [Overcoming Rank Collapse in Feedback Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11123)
*Gauthier Boeshertz,Razvan Pascanu,Claudia Clopath*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FA has a lower-rank error signal than BP, limiting exploration; two strategies—Muon optimizer (weight-update orthogonalization) and hidden activity normalization (activation orthogonality)—improve FA’s scalability to deeper nets, e.g., +9% accuracy on CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18, highlighting higher-dimensional update geometry as key to scaling FA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the biological plausibility gap for backpropagation by exploring FA, identify why FA underperforms at scale, and propose mechanisms to increase update dimensionality.

Method: Empirical comparison of BP and FA on CIFAR-10 focusing on the effective rank of the error signal. Propose and evaluate two mechanisms to increase update dimensionality—Muon optimizer that orthogonalizes weight updates and hidden activity normalization to promote activation orthogonality—across larger architectures/benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18).

Result: FA error exhibits substantially lower rank than BP, constrained to a low-dimensional subspace. Both proposed mechanisms yield consistent improvements over FA baselines in larger models; CIFAR-100 with ResNet-18 shows a 9 percentage-point accuracy gain.

Conclusion: Low-dimensional gradient dynamics is a primary obstacle to scaling FA; inducing higher-dimensional update geometry via orthogonalized updates and activation orthogonality is a promising route to scaling alternatives to backpropagation.

Abstract: Backpropagation (BP) is widely viewed as biologically implausible, in part because it requires feedback weights to be the transpose of forward weights for error propagation. Interestingly, when training a network with fixed random feedback weights to circumvent this issue, learning aligns the forward weights with the feedback weights, leading the backpropagated error signal to become an approximation of the standard gradient used by BP. This process, called Feedback Alignment (FA), occurs in MLPs and very shallow CNNs but does not scale well to deeper architectures. In this work, we first investigated differences between BP and FA models, trained on CIFAR10, specifically focusing on the effective rank of the signal. We found that the FA error has a considerably lower rank and hence is constrained to a lower-dimensional subspace compared to BP, limiting exploration of the parameter space. Motivated by this observation, we evaluated two mechanisms for increasing the effective dimensionality of FA: Muon, an optimiser that orthogonalises weight updates; and hidden activity normalisation, which promotes activation orthogonality. Across larger architectures and benchmarks, we find that these methods consistently improve over FA baselines, for example, on CIFAR100 with a Resnet-18, accuracy increases by 9 percentage points. Our results identify low-dimensional gradient dynamics as a key obstacle to scaling FA and suggest that inducing higher-dimensional update geometry is a promising route toward scaling alternatives to backpropagation.

</details>


### [265] [Robust Regression of General ReLUs with Queries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11130)
*Ilias Diakonikolas,Daniel M. Kane,Mingchen Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An interactive, query-efficient learner for agnostic ReLU regression under Gaussian data achieves near-optimal label complexity: error O(opt)+ε with d polylog(1/ε) + Õ(min{1/p,1/ε}) label queries, and query access is necessary to beat passive learning; pool-based active learning demands Ω(d/ε) labels unless using many unlabeled examples.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce label complexity in agnostic, non-homogeneous ReLUs under Gaussian distribution by leveraging interactive query access; establish near-optimality and necessity of queries for improving over passive learning.

Method: Develop a computationally efficient interactive learner that uses black-box label queries. Prove an error bound of O(opt)+ε with a label/query complexity of d polylog(1/ε) + Õ(min{1/p,1/ε}). Complement with unconditional lower bounds showing near-optimality and show that query access is essential for improvements over passive learning, including pool-based active learning lower bounds.

Result: First computationally efficient interactive learner achieving O(opt)+ε with label complexity d polylog(1/ε) + Õ(min{1/p,1/ε}). Demonstrates near-optimal query complexity bounds and that query access is necessary to surpass passive learning; pool-based active learning requires Õ(d/ε) labels unless there are super-polynomial unlabeled examples.

Conclusion: Query access is essentially necessary to improve label complexity over passive learning in this setting; interactive learning enables near-minimal label usage for agnostic ReLU learning under Gaussian, with proven lower bounds guiding the limits of improvement.

Abstract: We study the task of agnostically learning general (as opposed to homogeneous) ReLUs under the Gaussian distribution with respect to the squared loss. In the passive learning setting, recent work gave a computationally efficient algorithm that uses $poly(d,1/ε)$ labeled examples and outputs a hypothesis with error $O(opt)+ε$, where $opt$ is the squared loss of the best fit ReLU. Here we focus on the interactive setting, where the learner has some form of query access to the labels of unlabeled examples. Our main result is the first computationally efficient learner that uses $d polylog(1/ε)+\tilde{O}(\min\{1/p, 1/ε\})$ black-box label queries, where $p$ is the bias of the target function, and achieves error $O(opt)+ε$. We complement our algorithmic result by showing that its query complexity bound is qualitatively near-optimal, even ignoring computational constraints. Finally, we establish that query access is essentially necessary to improve on the label complexity of passive learning. Specifically, for pool-based active learning, any active learner requires $\tildeΩ(d/ε)$ labels, unless it draws a super-polynomial number of unlabeled examples.

</details>


### [266] [First-Order Trajectory Matching: Fast Ensemble Predictions of Chaotic, Turbulent, Stochastic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11138)
*Shreya Jha,Timo Schorlepp,Nicholas Geissler,Jules Berman,Benjamin Peherstorfer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FTM is a surrogate-modeling approach that learns the first-order local transport (probability current velocity) from stochastic trajectories by symmetric motion matching, preserving time marginals and enabling current-like quantities with low rollout cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To directly infer current velocity from trajectory data without estimating drift, diffusion, or scores, enabling stable, trajectory-aware ensemble predictions and current-related metrics like flux and barrier-crossing currents.

Method: FTM matches symmetric first-order motion between trajectory increments to learn the probability current velocity. It yields a one-step, simulation-free loss that separates discretization error from sampling variance, and ensures stability under proper balance of temporal resolution and sample size.

Result: Empirically, FTM delivers trajectory-aware ensemble predictions with low deterministic rollout cost across stochastic systems and PDEs, and captures current-like quantities such as fluxes, circulations, and barrier-crossing currents while preserving time marginals.

Conclusion: FTM offers a stable, efficient surrogate modeling framework for stochastic dynamics that avoids drift/diffusion/score estimation, directly learning the current velocity from trajectories and enabling a range of trajectory-based analyses.

Abstract: We introduce First-Order Trajectory Matching (FTM), a surrogate-modeling method that learns the first-order local transport of probability mass from trajectories of stochastic systems. By matching the symmetric first-order motion of trajectories, FTM learns the probability current velocity, whose flow preserves time marginals to match ensemble averages, while also capturing current-like trajectory quantities such as fluxes, circulations, and barrier-crossing currents. FTM learns the current velocity directly from trajectories, avoiding drift, diffusion, and score estimation. Our stability analysis separates discretization error from sampling variance and shows that the one-step simulation-free FTM loss is stable when temporal resolution and sample size are properly balanced. Across stochastic dynamical systems and PDE examples, we empirically demonstrate that FTM provides trajectory-aware ensemble predictions at low, deterministic-rollout cost.

</details>


### [267] [OncoTraj: a public benchmark for longitudinal resistance prediction in EGFR-mutant non-small-cell lung cancer on osimertinib](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11144)
*Abhijoy Sarkar,Aarchi Singh Thakur*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OncoTraj is a public benchmark for first-line osimertinib resistance in EGFR-mutant NSCLC, aggregating 813 patients from three sources, defining three tasks (12-month progression classification, time-to-progression regression, dominant resistance mechanism classification), with leakage-audited train/validation/test splits and baselines. v1 features are single-snapshot tissue NGS, yielding no better-than-chance performance; results confirm a TP53 co-mutation association with higher progression; guides v2 toward serial ctDNA and design improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no public benchmark to train/evaluate models on longitudinal trajectories of resistance to first-line osimertinib in EGFR-mutant NSCLC. A standardized, leakage-audited dataset with clear tasks and baselines enables reproducible method development and fair comparisons.

Method: Harmonization of three real-world clinical-genomic sources (MSK-CHORD, AACR GENIE BPC NSCLC, FLAURA molecular-resistance supplement) into OncoTraj, with three predefined tasks: (A) 12-month progression binary classification, (B) time-to-first-progression regression, (C) six-class dominant resistance mechanism classification. Provides train/validation/test splits with leakage auditing, an open-source evaluation harness, and six reference baselines (majority baseline, logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, LSTM, multi-task transformer).

Result: With v1 features (single-timepoint snapshot), all tasks perform at chance on within-source evaluation, indicating input modality limits (single-snapshot NGS) rather than algorithmic limits. The dataset shows TP53 co-mutation raises 12-month progression from 29% to 59% cohort-wide. OncoTraj yields a leakage-audited baseline and highlights design requirements for a v2 using serial ctDNA.

Conclusion: OncoTraj establishes a reproducible benchmark for osimertinib resistance in EGFR-mutant NSCLC, demonstrates modality limitations of single-snapshot data, and provides concrete guidance for developing v2 with serial ctDNA-enriched data to improve predictive power.

Abstract: Resistance to first-line osimertinib in EGFR-mutant non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is the canonical example of predictable clonal evolution under therapeutic pressure, yet no public benchmark exists for training or evaluating computational models on the corresponding longitudinal patient trajectories. We introduce OncoTraj, a public benchmark of 813 EGFR-mutant NSCLC patients receiving first-line osimertinib, harmonized from three real-world clinical-genomic sources: MSK-CHORD (672 patients), AACR Project GENIE BPC NSCLC (34 patients), and the FLAURA molecular-resistance supplement (107 patients). OncoTraj defines three locked tasks: (A) binary classification of progression by a fixed 12-month landmark, (B) regression of time-to-first-progression in days, and (C) six-class classification of the dominant resistance mechanism. We release the harmonized dataset, patient-level train/validation/test splits with an audited no-leakage guarantee, an open-source evaluation harness, and six reference baselines spanning a majority-class predictor, logistic regression, random forest, XGBoost, an LSTM, and a multi-task transformer. With v1's single-timepoint snapshot features, no task clears chance on clean within-source evaluation: the uniformity of this ceiling across every model class localizes the limit to the input modality (single-snapshot tissue NGS rather than serial ctDNA), not the algorithm. The benchmark does recover a reproducible literature-consistent association: TP53 co-mutation raises the 12-month progression rate from 29% to 59% cohort-wide. OncoTraj establishes a reproducible, leakage-audited baseline and converts the modality limit into concrete design requirements for a serial-ctDNA-enriched v2.

</details>


### [268] [Efficiently Learning Drifting Halfspaces with Massart Noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11149)
*Mingchen Ma,Guyang Cao,Jelena Diakonikolas,Ilias Diakonikolas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Efficient online learning of drifting margin-separable halfspaces under Massart noise; achieves error η+˜O(Δ^{1/3}/γ); realizable setting yields improved rates; information-theoretic optimum is Δ^{1/2} but computationally, Δ^{1/3} scaling is unavoidable for low-degree tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explore learning under non-stationary drift and random classification noise for margin-based linear classifiers; quantify the tradeoffs between noise η, drift Δ, and margin γ; provide both algorithmic results and lower bounds to separate information and computation.

Method: Develop a computationally efficient online learner for margin-separable halfspaces with Massart noise; derive error bound η+˜O(Δ^{1/3}/γ); adapt techniques to the realizable setting to improve error; prove lower bounds indicating an information–computation tradeoff, showing Δ^{1/3} scaling for low-degree polynomial tests.

Result: Algorithm achieves error η+˜O(Δ^{1/3}/γ). In the realizable setting, the technique yields an improved error rate over prior work. Lower bounds show that, while information-theoretic optimum scales as Δ^{1/2}, Δ^{1/3}-scaling is unavoidable for low-degree polynomial tests (even with random classification noise).

Conclusion: The work delineates the gap between what is information-theoretically possible and what is computably achievable for drifting Massart-noise halfspace learning, providing near-optimal algorithmic performance and concrete lower bounds that constrain low-degree testing approaches.

Abstract: We study the problem of learning a drifting concept in the presence of Massart noise. In this framework, an online learner has access to a history of independent samples whose labels are noisy versions of a target concept that may change from round to round. The goal is to output, in each round, a hypothesis with small prediction error. We study the complexity of this learning problem for the fundamental class of margin-separable linear classifiers (halfspaces). On the positive side, we give a computationally efficient learner achieving error $η+ \tilde O(Δ^{1/3}/γ)$, where $η$ upper bounds the Massart noise rate, $Δ$ is the drift rate, and $γ$ is the margin. Interestingly, in the realizable setting, an adaptation of our techniques yields an efficient learner with an improved error rate over prior work. On the lower-bound side, we provide formal evidence of an information-computation tradeoff, strongly suggesting that our algorithm's performance is essentially optimal. Specifically, while the information-theoretically optimal error scales with $Δ^{1/2}$, we prove that $Δ^{1/3}$-scaling is unavoidable for low-degree polynomial tests, even in the special case of random classification noise.

</details>


### [269] [COGENT: Continuous Graph Emulators with Neural Ordinary Differential Equations for Long-Term Physical Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11162)
*Zesheng Liu,Maryam Rahnemoonfar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: COGENT combines a history-aware graph encoder with a continuous-time latent neural ODE to forecast irregular-geospatial-mesh physics over long horizons, with a residual decoder enabling arbitrary-time querying and stable, non-autoregressive multi-step predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon physical forecasting on irregular geospatial meshes requires stable, scalable models that can query states at arbitrary future times, overcoming error accumulation and fixed-step limitations in autoregressive discrete-time models.

Method: A graph-based history encoder produces node-wise context vectors that initialize/condition a latent Neural ODE. The ODE dynamics are driven by interpolated future forcings and relative rollout time. A residual decoder maps latent trajectories to future states. Training stability is aided by rollout-horizon sampling and progressive rollout scheduling. Evaluation uses transient ice-sheet simulations from the Ice-sheet and Sea-level System Model.

Result: COGENT achieves improved long-range stability over autoregressive graph baselines and supports querying states at arbitrary future times, indicating effectiveness of continuous latent dynamics on irregular meshes.

Conclusion: Continuous graph Neural ODEs are a promising approach for scalable, stable physical forecasting on irregular geospatial meshes, particularly when long-horizon predictions and arbitrary-time querying are required.

Abstract: In this work, we present COGENT, a continuous graph emulator with Neural Ordinary Differential Equations for long-term physical forecasting on irregular geospatial meshes. COGENT encodes a finite history of system states and associated forcing fields and external forcings with a graph-based history encoder, producing node-wise context vectors that capture both local spatial interactions and temporal evolution. These context vectors initialize and condition a latent Neural Ordinary Differential Equation whose dynamics are driven by interpolated future forcings and explicit relative rollout time. By modeling the forecast trajectory as a continuous latent dynamical system, COGENT can generate predictions at arbitrary future times rather than being restricted to a fixed temporal discretization. A residual decoder maps the resulting latent trajectories back to future physical states, enabling direct multi-step forecasting without repeatedly feeding predicted states back into the model. This formulation combines graph-based spatial representation, history-conditioned latent dynamics, and continuous-time rollout in a unified framework for mesh-based physical simulation emulation. In order to stabilize training with long-horizon supervision, we also propose effective rollout-horizon sampling and a progressive rollout-horizon scheduling strategy. We evaluate COGENT on transient ice-sheet simulations generated by the Ice-sheet and Sea-level System Model, demonstrating improved long-range stability over autoregressive graph baselines. These results suggest that continuous graph Neural ODEs provide a promising methodology for scalable physical forecasting on irregular geospatial meshes, particularly in applications that require stable long-horizon predictions and the ability to query system states at arbitrary times.

</details>


### [270] [Algorithmic and Minimax Complexities in Kernel Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11171)
*Yunbei Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GP-UCB and DEC frameworks can be unified under an MAIR/information-theoretic lens in kernel bandits; algorithmic complexity can exceed class-wide minimax or DEC guarantees in overparameterized models; a safeguarded master can merge their strengths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reconcile two seemingly distinct theoretical viewpoints (GP-UCB’s algorithmic GP prior and DEC’s robust class-wide envelopes) by casting them in a common algorithmic-information framework for RKHS bandits and studying their interaction via MAIR envelopes.

Method: Introduce a unified MAIR framework with heterogeneous PSD algorithmic priors; generalize both GP-UCB analysis and MAMS/DEC guarantees; propose a safeguarded master algorithm that combines their advantages; construct kernel-bandit instances to illustrate the benefits of algorithmic information over class-wide certificates.

Result: The framework unifies and extends GP-UCB analysis and MAMS; shows that algorithmic information and class-wide minimax/DC certificates capture different quantities; demonstrates in kernel bandits that algorithmic complexity can be more informative in overparameterized settings.

Conclusion: Algorithmic information and class-wide minimax coefficients answer different questions; kernel-bandit settings expose the distinction clearly, advocating a hybrid approach that leverages both perspectives.

Abstract: Gaussian-process upper confidence bound (GP-UCB) and decision-estimation-coefficient (DEC) methods may appear, at first sight, to belong to different theories. This paper places the two viewpoints in a common algorithmic-information language for frequentist RKHS bandits. GP-UCB fixes an algorithmic, rather than true, Gaussian-process prior and exploits realized-trajectory complexity together with computational tractability, whereas MAMS optimizes a robust class-wide MAIR/DEC envelope. Through the unified MAIR framework and heterogeneous positive-semidefinite algorithmic priors, we generalize both the GP-UCB analysis and the MAMS algorithm, propose a safeguarded master that combines their advantages, and provide a kernel-bandit construction showing that algorithmic complexity can be more informative than class-wide minimax or DEC certificates in overparameterized models. The resulting message is that algorithmic information and class-wide minimax coefficients answer different questions and can lead to different gaps; kernel bandits provide a clean setting in which this distinction becomes mathematically visible.

</details>


### [271] [Predicting Future Behaviors in Reasoning Models Enables Better Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11172)
*Evgenii Kortukov,Piotr Komorowski,Florian Klein,Paula Engl,Gabriele Sarti,Seong Joon Oh,Sebastian Lapuschkin,Wojciech Samek*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Future Probe Controlled Generation (FPCG) uses activation probes that predict future LRMs' behavior from intermediate steps, enabling text-level steering with minimal output quality loss, addressing limitations of prior detection-based steering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard test-time steering modifies hidden representations via detection features that predict already-generated behavior, which poorly predicts future outputs; a more robust steering target is needed.

Method: Train activation probes to forecast future behavior likelihood from reasoning steps, create a future-behavior predictor, generate multiple candidate sentences, and select the one with highest predicted future likelihood; compare against activation-steering baselines.

Result: Probes achieve 64–91% accuracy in predicting future behavior; FPCG yields steering with almost no quality degradation and succeeds in settings where activation steering fails.

Conclusion: Distinguishing detection and prediction features enables nuanced control of LRM behavior; future-oriented prediction features are more effective targets for steering than detection features.

Abstract: Deployed large reasoning models (LRMs) often behave unexpectedly. Test-time steering controls LRM outputs by intervening on their hidden representations, but it can degrade output quality. We argue that prior steering work implicitly relies on internal features that detect behavior in already generated text. We show that these detection features are poor predictors of future behavioral outcomes, and thus not the natural intervention target. Instead, we train activation probes to predict future behavior likelihoods from intermediate reasoning steps. These probes predict the most likely behavior with 64%-91% accuracy, revealing a separate type of internal prediction features. Building on these prediction features, we introduce a text-level steering method, Future Probe Controlled Generation. FPCG samples multiple candidate sentences and chooses the best one according to a probe predicting the future behavior likelihood. This enables steering with almost no output quality degradation. FPCG also enables steering in several evaluations where activation steering fails. These results show that distinguishing detection and prediction features enables a more nuanced approach to controlling LRM behaviors.

</details>


### [272] [EEVEE: Towards Test-time Prompt Learning in the Real World for Self-Improving Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11182)
*Weixian Xu,Shilong Liu,Mengdi Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EEVEE is the first multi-dataset test-time prompt learning framework for LLM agents that uses a router to cluster heterogeneous inputs and co-evolve router and prompts for robust task adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world LLM deployments face heterogeneous, multi-dataset input streams causing cross-dataset interference; existing single-dataset prompt methods lack robustness and adaptability.

Method: EEVEE introduces a routing module that partitions inputs into task clusters and assigns corresponding prompt configurations; it employs a router-prompt co-evolution strategy with interleaved router and prompt optimization to handle mutual dependency.

Result: Across multiple datasets, EEVEE improves robustness under heterogeneous streams and maintains single-benchmark efficiency, achieving average multi-benchmark gains of 10.38 and 24.32 points over Qwen3-4B-Instruct and DeepSeek-V3.2, and surpassing SOTA GEPA and ACE by up to 37.2% and 48.2%.

Conclusion: EEVEE demonstrates enhanced resilience to cross-dataset interference in test-time prompt learning while preserving efficiency and single-benchmark performance.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose EEVEE, the first multi-dataset test-time prompt learning framework for LLM agents, enabling test-time prompt learning under real-world task streams. Existing methods are largely designed for single-dataset settings, while real-world applications require models to handle heterogeneous input streams drawn from multiple datasets, domains, and task distributions, limiting their practical applicability. To mitigate cross-dataset interference, EEVEE introduces a router that partitions incoming inputs into task clusters and assigns them to suitable prompt configurations. This design is optimized via a router-prompt co-evolution strategy, which employs interleaved router and prompt learning phases to address their mutual dependency. Experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate that the framework improves robustness under heterogeneous data streams while maintaining single-benchmark learning capability and efficiency. Specifically, EEVEE improves average multi-benchmark scores by 10.38 and 24.32 points over Qwen3-4B-Instruct and DeepSeek-V3.2, surpassing SOTA methods GEPA and ACE by up to 37.2% and 48.2%.

</details>


### [273] [A Unifying Lens on Supervised Fine-Tuning Through Target Distribution Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11189)
*Tong Xie,Yuanhao Ban,Yunqi Hong,Sohyun An,Yihang Chen,Cho-Jui Hsieh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reframes supervised fine-tuning as target distribution design; introduces Q-target framework to decide how much to rely on the observed token and how to distribute remaining probability mass; proposes Target-SFT that optimizes directly against the target distribution; shows improvements across ten reasoning datasets/models; emphasizes broader design space for SFT objectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SFT uses a strict one-hot target which can misrepresent token uncertainty, noise, or misalignment with the model's prior. Leveraging pretrained priors via flexible target distributions may improve performance and calibration.

Method: Introduce the Q-target framework that decomposes SFT into (1) reliance on the observed token and (2) distribution of remaining probability mass over alternatives. Build Target-SFT to construct training objectives directly from the desired target distribution.

Result: Empirical evaluation shows Target-SFT consistently outperforms baselines across ten reasoning dataset-model settings, indicating robustness of the target-distribution design approach.

Conclusion: Target-based SFT design is a fundamental principle that broadens the objective space for SFT and can better integrate model priors and uncertainty into supervision.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) typically maximizes the likelihood of every token in a demonstrated trajectory. However, an observed token can be non-unique, noisy, or misaligned with the model prior. Strictly fitting toward this one-hot target may be suboptimal, especially when the pretrained model encodes a rich knowledge prior. In this work, we reinterpret SFT as target distribution design: instead of studying only the loss objective, we analyze the token-level target that the loss drives the model to match. We introduce the Q-target framework, which decomposes SFT supervision into two explicit choices: (1) how strongly to rely on the observed token, and (2) how to allocate the remaining probability mass over alternatives. This perspective unifies many existing SFT variants as implicit choices of the target distribution Q. Building on this view, we propose Target-SFT which constructs the training objective directly from the desired target distribution. This method consistently outperforms across the ten reasoning dataset-model settings evaluated, showing the effectiveness of this target-based approach. Overall, our formulation reveals a more fundamental design principle for SFT training and opens a broader search space for SFT objectives.

</details>


### [274] [When to Align, When to Predict: A Phase Diagram for Multimodal Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11190)
*Ilay Kamai,Hugues Van Assel,Aviv Regev,Hagai B. Perets,Randall Balestriero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Unified linear framework for cross-modal alignment (CA) and cross-modal prediction (CP) revealing four regimes and a practical diagnostic to choose the right objective before training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide practitioners with a systematic understanding of when CA or CP succeeds or fails in multimodal settings and to diagnose real-world datasets to select the appropriate training objective, especially in scientific domains with heterogeneous instruments and cross-modal nuisance correlations.

Method: A spiked signal-plus-noise model with structured cross-modal nuisance correlation is analyzed to derive separation ratios for CA and CP. CA corresponds to a whitening of each modality and fails when cross-modal nuisance is strongly correlated; CP uses one-sided whitening and is governed by source-modality quality. These yield a phase diagram with four regimes (Both, CA only, CP only, Neither). A data-driven procedure uses a small labeled subsample to place real datasets in the diagram and identify the preferred objective and prediction direction before cross-modal training. Evaluation on synthetic data, stereo-vision benchmarks, image-caption data, and real astrophysical data validates the predictions, including a Neither regime where cross-modal training is harmful and nonlinear effects are captured.

Result: Derivation of separation ratios and a phase diagram that partitions multimodal tasks into Four regimes. A practical diagnostic procedure enables selecting CA, CP, or neither before training. Empirical validation across diverse datasets supports the theory, including cases where cross-modal training degrades performance.

Conclusion: The framework enables practitioners to diagnose multimodal problems and choose the appropriate training objective pre-training, potentially saving resources and improving performance in complex multimodal domains.

Abstract: Cross-modal alignment (CA) and cross-modal prediction (CP) are the dominant paradigms for multimodal representation learning, yet there is no systematic understanding of when each succeeds, when each fails, and when cross-modal training helps at all -- a gap that leaves practitioners, especially in scientific domains like biomedicine or astrophysics, with heterogeneous instruments and multiple levels of organization and measurement, unable to diagnose why standard methods underperform the best single modality. We develop a unified linear framework that addresses both questions. Under a spiked signal-plus-noise model with structured cross-modal nuisance correlation, we derive separation ratios for both objectives that expose complementary failure modes: alignment whitens each modality and fails when nuisance is strongly correlated across views; prediction encodes whatever is cross-predictable through a one-sided whitening, with recovery governed by source-modality quality. The resulting phase diagram partitions multimodal problems into four regimes: Both, CA only, CP only, and Neither. We present a data-driven procedure to locate real-world datasets in this diagram using a small labeled subsample, identifying the preferred objective and prediction direction before any cross-modal training. Experiments on synthetic data, stereo-vision benchmarks, image-caption pairs, and real astrophysical data validate the predictions in the nonlinear regime, including the Neither regime where cross-modal training is actively harmful. Our framework lets practitioners diagnose their multimodal problem and choose the right objective before committing to training. Code to reproduce the results is available at https://github.com/IlayMalinyak/mm_align_vs_pred.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [275] [Business World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10044)
*Cecil Pang,Hiroki Sayama*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes the Business World Model (BWM), an executable internal simulator for autonomous business planning that ties business states, dynamics, constraints, and actions to semantic business entities, enabling goal-driven planning and counterfactual reasoning under uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI automation focuses on predefined tasks; there is a need for intelligent systems that can plan, optimize, and execute business initiatives from high-level strategic objectives in uncertain environments.

Method: Introduce BWM architecture: a business-semantics-centric representation linking states, dynamics, and actions to business entities; integrate semantic data representations, probabilistic machine learning models, deterministic business rules, and an explicit action space; enable simulation of alternative action sequences, estimation of future outcomes, and trade-off evaluation under uncertainty; provides an executable internal simulator for planning and counterfactual reasoning.

Result: Present a conceptual framework and architecture for an executable internal simulator, enabling simulation of alternative actions, forecasted outcomes, and trade-off analysis; no empirical evaluation yet, but a foundation for autonomous, goal-driven business systems.

Conclusion: BWM consolidates existing components into a coherent, executable platform that supports goal-driven planning and execution of business initiatives, establishing a foundational step toward autonomous business systems.

Abstract: Businesses are increasingly adopting AI-enabled tools to improve productivity, reduce costs, and enhance products and services. However, the transformative potential of AI extends beyond automating predefined tasks: it lies in enabling intelligent systems to plan, optimize, and execute business initiatives from high-level strategic objectives. This paper introduces the concept and architecture of a business world model (BWM), a world model specialized for business and organizational environments. Inspired by world models in artificial intelligence, cognitive science, and control theory, a BWM encodes business states, dynamics, constraints, objectives, and feasible action space to support autonomous decision-making. We propose a business-semantics-centric formulation in which business states, dynamics and actions are linked to key business entities. Within this framework, agents can simulate alternative action sequences, estimate their effects on future business outcomes, and evaluate trade-offs under uncertainty. The proposed architecture integrates semantic data representations, probabilistic machine learning models, deterministic business rules, and explicit action space into a coherent structure for planning and counterfactual reasoning. Although its individual components are not new, the contribution of BWM lies in organizing them as an executable internal simulator for business initiatives. This work establishes a conceptual foundation for autonomous business systems capable of moving from instruction-based execution toward goal-driven planning and execution.

</details>


### [276] [Deployment-Time Memorization in Foundation-Model Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10062)
*Lei,Chen,Guilin Zhang,Kai Zhao,Dalmo Cirne,Andy Olsen,Xu Chu,Zeke Miller,Alet Blanken,Amine Anoun,Jerry Ting*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Foundation-model agents' deployment-time memory shapes personalization utility, extraction risk, and deletion fidelity. The paper defines a privacy-utility frontier via Personalization Recall (PR) and Adversarial Extraction Rate (AER), and analyzes three knobs: summarization aggressiveness, retrieval breadth (k), and deletion mode, plus a Forgetting Residue Score (FRS). Empirical results show that targeted summarization reduces leakage while preserving personalization, but deletion strategies influence residue and recoverability of derived memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Memorization in deployed agents is not only in model weights but in persistent memory at deployment time. There is a gap in understanding how joint memory-design choices affect utility, risk, and erasure across memory lifecycles.

Method: Define PR and AER to characterize personalization and leakage. Introduce FRS to quantify recoverability of deleted information. Sweep memory-design knobs: summarization aggressiveness, retrieval breadth (k), and deletion mode. Evaluate on LongMemEval with canary extraction tasks using Gemma 3 12B and GPT-4o-mini, comparing canary leakage and personalization recall across configurations.

Result: Key-fact summarization reduces canary extraction by 76% on Gemma 3 12B and 64% on GPT-4o-mini while preserving nearly full personalization recall. After compression, increasing k does not restore leakage. Raw-only deletion leaves derived summary copies recoverable in ~20% of cases; full-pipeline purge or tombstone redaction drives worst-tier residue to zero. The Forgetting Residue Score enables tracking residual access across memory tiers.

Conclusion: Persistent agent memory must be evaluated as a first-class memorization mechanism, assessed by what it enables recall, what it exposes to extraction, and what it can truly erase.

Abstract: Foundation-model agents are increasingly long-lived systems that remember users across interactions, making memorization an explicit deployment-time function rather than solely a property of model weights. Existing work addresses parametric memorization or audits fixed memory configurations, but does not characterize how memory-design choices jointly shape personalization utility, extraction risk, and deletion fidelity. We study this surface as deployment-time memorization, formulating agent memory as a privacy-utility frontier measured by Personalization Recall (PR) and Adversarial Extraction Rate (AER), and sweeping three memory-design knobs: summarization aggressiveness, retrieval breadth (k), and deletion mode. We further introduce the Forgetting Residue Score (FRS) to quantify whether deleted information remains recoverable from derived memory tiers. On LongMemEval, key-fact summarization reduces canary extraction by 76% on Gemma 3 12B and 64% on GPT-4o-mini while preserving nearly all personalization recall; critically, once content is compressed away, increasing k no longer restores leakage. The same compression, however, induces a deletion-fidelity failure: raw-only deletion leaves derived summary copies recoverable in approximately 20% of instances, and only full-pipeline purge or tombstone redaction drives worst-tier residue to zero. Together, these results establish that persistent agent memory must be evaluated as a first-class memorization mechanism -- assessed by what it helps agents recall, what it makes extractable, and what it can truly erase.

</details>


### [277] [Exploratory Responsiveness and Adaptive Rigidity under AI-Assisted Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10086)
*Balaraju Battu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A theoretical framework linking AI-assisted optimization to exploratory adaptation on rugged landscapes. AI can substitute for exploration under low adaptive responsiveness, causing metastable trapping and premature convergence, but can amplify exploration in regimes with higher responsiveness. Long-run outcomes depend on AI capability and the architecture of human–machine interaction and institutional structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how predictive AI assistance shapes long-run adaptability and exploratory behavior in complex adaptive systems, including when it aids or hinders exploration, and the policy implications of these dynamics.

Method: A dynamical-systems theoretical model tying cognitive, institutional, and technological subsystems on rugged epistemic landscapes. Introduces a central state variable—adaptive responsiveness—and analyzes convergent predictive regimes versus exploration-enhancing regimes, deriving a substitution parameter that governs AI's impact on exploration.

Result: The model yields two broad regimes: (1) exploratory substitution, where AI reduces exploratory engagement and leads to metastable trapping, hysteresis, premature convergence, and exploration-collapse; (2) exploration enhancement, where AI amplifies exploratory search and adaptive mobility. The substitution effect is responsiveness-dependent: weak exploratory routines are more vulnerable, while high adaptive responsiveness can be leveraged to expand exploration. Long-run effects hinge on AI capability, institutional structure, developmental context, and human–machine interaction architecture.

Conclusion: Long-run adaptive effects of AI arise not only from AI capabilities but also from how institutions are structured and how humans interact with AI. Designing AI systems and institutions to preserve or boost adaptive responsiveness is essential to avoid rigidity and to harness AI for sustained exploration and mobility across rugged landscapes.

Abstract: This paper develops a theory of exploratory adaptation under AI-assisted optimization. The central argument is that the long-run adaptive effects of AI systems depend critically on how predictive assistance interacts with exploratory responsiveness itself. We formalize this mechanism using a dynamical framework in which cognitive, institutional, and technological systems evolve over rugged epistemic landscapes characterized by multiple locally reinforced configurations. A central state variable in the model is adaptive responsiveness, which measures the capacity of a system to traverse unfamiliar conceptual and institutional trajectories under changing conditions. Under convergent predictive regimes, AI systems substitute for exploratory engagement, reducing adaptive responsiveness and generating metastable trapping, hysteresis, premature convergence, and exploration-collapse dynamics in which systems become locally efficient but globally rigid. The framework also identifies contrasting exploration-enhancing regimes in which AI systems amplify exploratory search, conceptual traversal, and adaptive mobility. The effective substitution parameter is therefore responsiveness-dependent: systems possessing weak exploratory routines are more vulnerable to exploratory substitution, whereas systems already possessing high adaptive responsiveness may use AI assistance to expand exploratory mobility across rugged landscapes. The long-run adaptive effects of AI consequently depend not only on AI capability itself, but also on institutional structure, developmental context, and the architecture of human-machine interaction.

</details>


### [278] [Predictive Assistance and the Temporal Dynamics of Exploratory Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10094)
*Balaraju Battu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A geometric dynamical framework links predictive AI assistance to exploratory cognition, showing that stabilization from predictions reduces exploration, induces hysteresis in learning trajectories, and whose developmental timing crucially shapes future exploration; it yields testable predictions on exploratory entropy and convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how exogenously provided predictive assistance reshapes problem-solving dynamics and the geometry of strategy space, beyond classical exploratory search.

Method: Develop a geometric dynamical system where attention/strategy evolve under stabilizing drift, endogenous perturbations, and learning gating; model predictive assistance as exogenous exploratory compression that stabilizes trajectories; analyze implications for exploration, curvature, hysteresis, and development.

Result: 1) Predictive stabilization reduces responsiveness to intrinsic perturbations, lowering exploratory variability impact; 2) Curvature in the cognitive landscape accumulates and relaxes asymmetrically, causing hysteresis and delayed recovery after assistance withdrawal; 3) Timing of stabilization critically shapes development, with early intervention narrowing future exploration prior to diversification; yields testable predictions on exploratory entropy, premature convergence, and delayed recovery.

Conclusion: Predictive systems can fundamentally reshape the geometry of exploratory cognition, with measurable consequences for dynamics, development, and recovery once assistance ends.

Abstract: Classical theories of cognition describe problem solving as exploratory search through structured problem spaces in which repeated interaction gradually compresses search into efficient representational structures. Predictive artificial intelligence systems introduce a distinct regime in which stabilization may occur before exploratory diversification unfolds, supplying solutions and decision trajectories prior to internally generated search. This paper develops a geometric dynamical framework in which attention evolves over a landscape of strategies shaped by stabilizing drift, endogenous exploratory perturbation, and responsiveness-gated learning. Predictive assistance is modeled as a process of exogenous exploratory compression that stabilizes trajectories before self-generated exploration broadens the accessible regions of strategy space. The framework yields three main results. First, sustained predictive stabilization reduces exploratory responsiveness by attenuating the effective influence of intrinsic perturbations even when exploratory variability remains present. Second, curvature accumulates and relaxes asymmetrically, producing hysteresis and delayed recovery of exploratory mobility after assistance withdrawal. Third, developmental outcomes depend critically on the timing of stabilization, with early intervention narrowing future exploratory traversal before broad representational diversification has occurred. The framework generates empirically testable predictions concerning exploratory entropy, premature convergence, and delayed recovery following predictive stabilization. More broadly, the results suggest that predictive systems may reshape the geometry of exploratory cognition itself.

</details>


### [279] [From Senses to Decisions: The Information Flow of Auditory and Visual Perception in Multimodal LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10147)
*Wish Suharitdamrong,Muhammad Awais,Xiatian Zhu,Sara Atito*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AVLLMs route audio and visual tokens along established pathways, adapt routing for interleaved items via parallel streams, and can discard tokens post-transfer to LLM for efficiency; findings replicate across models (Qwen2.5-Omni, Video-SALMONN2 Plus) at 3B/7B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To illuminate the internal information routing in audio-visual LLMs, addressing a gap in understanding how multimodal signals shape final predictions, with implications for interpretability and efficiency.

Method: Empirical analysis tracing information flow inside AVLLMs across two input configurations: (1) audio-visual video, and (2) multiple interleaved audio-visual items. Tested across multiple models and scales (Qwen2.5-Omni and Video-SALMONN2 Plus at 3B and 7B). Utilized flow tracing and ablations to assess token discardibility and impact on predictions.

Result: For audio-visual video, information flows along the established VLM/VideoLLM pathway with modality contributions proportional to task reliance. With interleaved items, routing shifts to parallel streams. Audio-visual and other tokens can be discarded after transfer to the LLM with minimal or even positive impact, generalizing across tasks/datasets.

Conclusion: Provides the first coherent picture of how AVLLMs orchestrate sound and sight, enabling interpretability, design, and efficiency improvements in audio-visual and broader MLLMs; supports hypotheses on why these flow structures emerge and guides future work.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can listen and see, but how do audio and visual signals actually travel through the network to shape an answer? Despite their growing role in research and real-world applications, the internal pathways through which audio and visual tokens influence the final prediction remain poorly understood. In this study, we examine audio-visual information flow inside Audio-Visual Large Language Models (AVLLMs), tracing how AVLLMs route, utilize, and integrate audio and visual information across two input configurations, audio-visual video and multiple interleaved audio-visual items. We find that for audio-visual video, AVLLMs follow the sequential information flow pathway established for VLMs and VideoLLMs, with audio and visual contribution flowing along this pathway in proportion to the task's reliance on each modality. In settings with multiple interleaved audio-visual items, this routing shifts to different parallel streams. Furthermore, we demonstrate that audio-visual and other token types can be discarded once their information is transferred to LLM, with minimal impact on the model's prediction or even slight improvement, generalizing across multiple tasks and datasets, enabling more efficient inference. These findings hold across multiple models and scales, Qwen2.5-Omni and Video-SALMONN2 Plus at 3B and 7B scales, leading to hypotheses on why these flow structures emerge. Together, these results deliver the first coherent picture of how AVLLMs orchestrate sound and sight inside the network and lay the groundwork for the next wave of interpretability, design, and efficiency advances in audio-visual and broader MLLMs.

</details>


### [280] [Less Context, Better Agents: Efficient Context Engineering for Long-Horizon Tool-Using LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10209)
*Abhilasha Lodha,Mahsa Pahlavikhah Varnosfaderani,Abir Chakraborty,Abhinav Mithal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Selective context-engineering via pruning recent tool interactions plus automated summarization markedly boosts automation performance and efficiency in enterprise expense itemization using GPT-5, achieving 91.6% complete itemization with reduced context size and runtime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address context overflow, stale-state errors, and high inference cost caused by verbose enterprise tool responses in autonomous agents. Evaluate strategies on Microsoft Dynamics 365 Finance and Operations with automated expense itemization tasks.

Method: Four GPT-5 configurations tested on a 50-task hotel expense benchmark: no user model, full conversation history, context pruned to the last 5 tool call/response pairs, and pruning with automated summarization. Results averaged over 5 runs. User model held constant for context-engineering. Cross-model comparison with Claude Sonnet 4.5. Analyses include confidence intervals, effect sizes, sensitivity over pruning/summary windows, failure analysis, results across five expense types grouped into three categories.
