Result: No-user-model baseline achieves 8.0% complete itemization. Full history yields 71.0% completion but uses ~1.48M tokens and 14.56 hours. Last-5 calls prune yields 79.0% completion with ~0.53M tokens and 5.39 hours. Summarization plus pruning achieves best: 91.6% complete itemization and 99.64% average amount itemized, with ~0.55M tokens and 5.79 hours. Additional cross-model evidence supports findings.

Conclusion: For this class of enterprise tool-use workflows, selective retention of recent tool interactions plus compact summarization improves reliability and efficiency compared with full-history retention.

Abstract: Large language models deployed as autonomous agents for enterprise workflows face a key challenge: verbose tool responses from enterprise systems can cause context overflow, stale-state errors, and high inference cost. We study this problem in automated expense itemization in Microsoft Dynamics 365 Finance and Operations using Model Context Protocol tools. We evaluate four GPT-5 configurations on a 50-task hotel expense benchmark: no user model, full conversation history, context pruned to the last 5 tool call/response pairs, and pruning with automated summarization. Results are averaged across 5 independent runs, with the user model held constant for the context-engineering comparison. The no-user-model baseline achieves only 8.0% complete itemization. Full-context retention improves completion to 71.0%, but consumes 1,480,996 tokens and 14.56 hours per benchmark. Pruning to the last 5 tool calls improves completion to 79.0% while reducing token use to 535,274 and runtime to 5.39 hours. Adding summarization achieves the best result: 91.6% complete itemization and 99.64% average amount itemized, with 553,374 tokens and 5.79 hours. We further report confidence intervals, effect-size analysis, sensitivity over pruning and summary windows, failure analysis, results across five expense types grouped into three categories, and cross-model evidence with Claude Sonnet 4.5. These results show that, for this class of enterprise tool-use workflow, selective retention of recent tool interactions plus compact summarization can improve both reliability and efficiency compared with full-history retention.

</details>


### [281] [Minimalist Genetic Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10237)
*Leonardo Trujillo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MGP reframes genetic programming as a syntactic derivation task using the MERGE operator from Minimalist Program, building symbolic expressions incrementally from a lexicon; it bypasses traditional evolutionary search and, with a suitable lexicon, recovers exact ground-truth models on symbolic regression problems where standard GP struggles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address GP's reliance on evolutionary search and issues like bloat by adopting a minimalist linguistic computation (MERGE) as the core mechanism for program induction, aiming to uncover the essential building blocks of expressions.

Method: Introduce Minimalist Genetic Programming (MGP) that uses a binary set-formation operator MERGE to incrementally construct complex syntactic structures from a lexicon of atomic objects, guided by a Markovian derivation process rather than evolution; evaluated on symbolic regression benchmarks known to be challenging for standard GP due to bloat.

Result: With an appropriately chosen lexicon of atomic syntactic objects, MGP consistently discovers the exact ground-truth model on the tested symbolic regression problems, where standard GP struggles to replicate it; demonstrates the viability of a syntactic-derivation approach to program induction.

Conclusion: Minimalist Program-inspired synthesis can inform program induction; MGP shows promising potential and warrants further exploration of syntactic-derivation approaches as alternatives to evolutionary search in GP.

Abstract: Genetic programming (GP) is based on two important insights. First, that any learning task can fundamentally be posed as a program induction problem, where the goal is to construct a symbolic hierarchical model that is expressed as a syntax tree. Second, to pose this task as a search problem, and use evolution to locate the desired model. Since it was proposed, GP has produced notable results in a wide range of tasks and problem domains. This work presents an alternative view by modifying the second core insight of GP, posing the problem as a syntactic derivation task instead. In particular, this paper presents Minimalist Genetic Programming (MGP), an algorithm that like GP is biologically inspired, but instead of evolution it takes inspiration from the Minimalist Program to human language, in which syntax is understood as an optimal solution to the problem of linking two other mental systems. In minimalism, the core computational process is a binary set formation operator called $MERGE$, than can be used to incrementally construct complex syntactic structures using a simple Markovian process. MGP is able to discover the core building blocks of the symbolic expressions, and to incrementally combined them using $MERGE$. The proposed system is benchmarked on symbolic regression tasks that are known to be difficult to solve with standard GP systems because of the propensity for bloat. Results show that when a proper lexicon of atomic syntactic objects are chosen, MGP is able to consistently produce the exact ground truth model on a set of symbolic regression where standard GP struggles to do the same. The insights provided by minimalism are shown to be relevant to the problem of program induction, and should be explored further based on the potential exhibited by MGP in this work.

</details>


### [282] [Regimes: An Auditable, Held-Out-Gated Improvement Loop Demonstrated on LongMemEval with ActiveGraph](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10241)
*Yohei Nakajima*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes event-sourced, auditable agent runtimes to enable trusted autonomous improvement loops; demonstrates with Regimes on ActiveGraph, showing repeatable failures, validated patches, and promotions; reports modest held-out accuracy gains on LongMemEval-S and outlines durable contributions and open questions about failure routing and prompt-based discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous improvement loops are fragile when failures and improvements live in external scaffolding. An event-sourced, deterministic agent state that is a projection of an append-only log enables auditable, replayable, and controllable improvements where promotions/discards are themselves events.

Method: Implement event-sourced agent runtime (ActiveGraph) where the agent’s state is a deterministic projection of an append-only event log. Build Regimes that diagnose failed evaluations, propose repairs at pipeline points, and promote after static checks, sandboxed execution, in-sample evaluation, and held-out validation. The loop is target-agnostic and applies to multiple tasks via a common interface. Use LongMemEval-S with five seeded splits to evaluate reader-prompt repairs.

Result: Regimes identifies reader-prompt repairs that improve final held-out accuracy by +0.05 to +0.10 in four splits and +0.01 in one split; two splits reach statistical significance. The approach emphasizes an auditable substrate, a held-out gated loop, a taxonomy of failure regimes routing failures to pipeline locations, and the hypothesis that prompts can serve as discovery probes.

Conclusion: ActiveGraph provides an auditable substrate enabling controlled improvement loops; the held-out gating mechanism and failure-regime taxonomy enable traceable improvements; the work introduces the prompt-as-discovery-probe hypothesis and highlights remaining questions about the marginal value of routed failures.

Abstract: Autonomous improvement loops are hard to trust because the improvement process is usually external scaffolding bolted onto the agent: failures go unlogged, diagnoses cannot be replayed, and promote-or-discard decisions land in a side database rather than the agent's own history. We show that an event-sourced agent runtime removes that friction and turns controlled improvement into a first-class workflow. When the agent's state is a deterministic projection of an append-only event log, failures are recorded, a run replays exactly from its log, candidate patches scope to typed pipeline seams, gates are auditable, and every promotion or discard is itself an event. We demonstrate this with Regimes, a loop on the ActiveGraph runtime that diagnoses failed evaluations, proposes a repair at a pipeline point, and promotes it only after static checks, sandbox execution, in-sample evaluation, and held-out validation. The loop is target-agnostic: the same control flow runs against different tasks through a common interface. On LongMemEval-S the dominant failure is not retrieval but reconciliation: the evidence is already in the assembled context, yet the reader answers incorrectly. Across five seeded held-out splits, Regimes discovers reader-prompt repairs that improve final held-out accuracy by +0.05 to +0.10 in four splits and +0.01 in one over-promotion split; two splits are individually significant (seed 5 unadjusted for its sequential promotion structure), and the pooled count is descriptive only, since the splits share one 500-question pool. The durable contributions are ActiveGraph as an auditable substrate that makes controlled improvement loops tractable, the held-out-gated loop it supports, the failure-regime taxonomy routing each failure to a pipeline location (whose marginal value over an unrouted baseline is the primary open question), and the prompt-as-discovery-probe hypothesis.

</details>


### [283] [RealMath-Eval: Why SOTA Judges Struggle with Real Human Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10254)
*Yiteng Mao,Kenan Xu,Yijia Lyu,Wenhao Li,Jianlong Chen,Xiangfeng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A new benchmark RealMath-Eval assesses real-world high-school math solutions to evaluate LLM judges; results show a gap: LLMs struggles to evaluate authentic student reasoning compared to synthetic data; surface-style transfer fails to close the gap; synthetic data may not reflect real reasoning diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To examine whether LLM-based evaluation of student mathematical reasoning generalizes from synthetic solutions to real, diverse student responses, addressing the gap between solving capability and evaluating reasoning.

Method: Assemble RealMath-Eval with 224 real-world high-school exam responses annotated by experts; compare LLM judges against expert grading; use a control where judges evaluate synthetic LLM-generated solutions; perform semantic embedding analysis to study error structure; probe generative probabilities for information-theoretic surprisal; test surface-level style transfer as a possible mitigation.

Result: LLM judges exhibit high MSE (~2.96) against expert grading on real student work; in a synthetic-control setting, judges are more accurate (MSE ~1.17) and consistent; synthetic errors collapse into low-dimensional linear subspaces, while human errors occupy a broader space; human reasoning shows higher surprisal, i.e., more out-of-distribution transitions for models; surface-style transfer fails to close the evaluation gap.

Conclusion: Relying on synthetic data in LLM evaluation pipelines can misrepresent the diversity of authentic student reasoning; robust evaluation requires authentic data benchmarks and methods that capture the heterogeneity of real student thinking.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved near-perfect performance in \emph{solving} high-school mathematics, their ability to \emph{evaluate} the diverse reasoning processes of real human students remains under-examined. To bridge this gap, we introduce \textbf{RealMath-Eval}, a rigorously annotated benchmark of 224 real-world exam responses from high schools. Our initial evaluation reveals that even state-of-the-art LLM judges struggle significantly on this task, exhibiting a high Mean Squared Error ($\sim$2.96) against expert human grading. To probe a plausible explanation, we contrast this performance with a control setting where the same judges evaluate synthetic LLM-generated solutions. We identify a stark ``Evaluation Gap'': judges are considerably more accurate and consistent on synthetic text (MSE $\sim$1.17) but struggle to generalize to authentic student reasoning. Through semantic embedding analysis, we find that synthetic errors suffer from a ``structural collapse'' into predictable, low-dimensional linear subspaces, whereas human errors form a more diverse error space. Furthermore, generative probability probes suggest that human reasoning involves significantly higher information-theoretic surprisal, indicating that student reasoning transitions are more out-of-distribution for current models. Finally, we find that surface-level style transfer fails to close this gap. Our findings suggest that current LLM evaluation pipelines relying heavily on synthetic data may not adequately capture the diversity of authentic student mathematical reasoning.

</details>


### [284] [Supervised Fine-tuning with Synthetic Rationale Data Hurts Real-World Disease Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10279)
*Buxin Su,Bingxuan Li,Cheng Qian,Yiwei Wang,Jin Jin,Bingxin Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Rationale-based supervised fine-tuning harms ADRD prediction performance vs label-only fine-tuning, despite high-quality rationales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate whether synthetic rationales during supervised fine-tuning improve clinical prediction models and to identify when such rationale-based supervision is beneficial or detrimental in high-stakes, longitudinal health data (five-year ADRD prediction).

Method: Large-scale controlled study with 504 configurations across model families and data scales; compare rationale-based SFT against label-only fine-tuning; assess rationale quality with human experts; examine few-shot inference-time demonstrations as a baseline.

Result: Rationale-based SFT consistently and substantially worsens predictive accuracy relative to label-only fine-tuning; degradation persists regardless of model family, data scale, or base reasoning orientation; not explained by rationale quality; few-shot inference-time demonstrations can improve, but not training targets.

Conclusion: Root cause identified as a structural conflict between narrative plausibility and discriminative optimization; caution against assuming rationale-based supervision will help in high-stakes clinical prediction; suggests need for deeper understanding of when and how to use rationale-based supervision.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning with synthetic rationale data is widely assumed to improve language model performance on clinical prediction tasks by teaching models not just what to predict but why. We test this assumption on five-year Alzheimer's disease and related dementias (ADRD) prediction from longitudinal health histories. Across a large-scale controlled experiment of 504 configurations, we find that rationale-based SFT consistently and substantially hurts prediction performance relative to label-only fine-tuning. The degradation persists across model families and data scales, and is not resolved by using a reasoning-oriented base model. Crucially, the failure is not explained by poor rationale quality: human expert annotation confirms that the generated rationales are medically accurate and faithfully grounded in patient-specific evidence, and few-shot experiments show that the same rationales improve performance when used as inference-time demonstrations rather than training targets. We identify the root cause as a structural conflict between narrative plausibility and discriminative optimization. We hope our work paves the path toward a more precise understanding of when and how rationale-based supervision helps and when it does not, guiding the responsible development of language models for high-stakes clinical prediction.

</details>


### [285] [Sim2Schedule: A Simulator-Guided LLM Framework for Autonomous Open-Pit Mine Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10286)
*Mustavi Ibne Masum,Thiago Eustaquio Alves de Oliveira,Mahzabeen Emu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Simulator-guided LLM agents generate near-optimal, interpretable mining schedules in a zero-shot, secure setting, matching 94-99% of MILP NPV with linear-time scaling, offering a scalable alternative to MILP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-pit scheduling is constrained by geotechnical precedence, processing coupling, and dynamic capacity; MILP is optimal but computationally prohibitive and inflexible in dynamic environments; there is a need for scalable, real-time-adaptive methods that preserve solution quality.

Method: Develop a simulator encoding geotechnical precedence, extraction-processing coupling, and dynamic capacity constraints. An LLM acts as an autonomous decision-maker, generating actions at each step within a zero-shot, closed, data-secure environment (no cloud inference, no fine-tuning). The framework yields complete extraction and processing schedules. A novel MILP formulation, incorporating realistic constraints, serves as a performance benchmark.

Result: The LLM framework recovers 94–99% of MILP-optimal NPV across varying instance scales and periods, with computation time scaling linearly with problem size/time.

Conclusion: Simulator-constrained LLM agents offer a practical, scalable alternative to classical optimization for long-horizon industrial scheduling under complex constraints, enabling interpretable schedules without cloud-based inference or retraining.

Abstract: Open-pit mine scheduling is a critical process for maximizing economic return under complex geotechnical and operational constraints. While Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) provides mathematically optimal baselines, its exponential computational complexity and inability to adapt in real time limit its practical deployment in dynamic industrial environments. This work introduces a simulator-driven Large Language Model (LLM) scheduling framework in which the LLM acts as an autonomous decision-making agent, guided at each step by a custom simulator that encodes geotechnical precedence, extraction-processing coupling, and dynamic capacity constraints directly into the action generation mechanism. Operating entirely zero-shot within a closed, data-secure environment, the framework produces complete, interpretable extraction and processing schedules without cloud-based inference, domain-specific fine-tuning, or retraining. To provide a trustworthy performance benchmark, a novel MILP formulation is developed that incorporates realistic operational and geotechnical constraints. Evaluated across mining instances of varying scale and time periods, the LLM-based framework recovers between 94\% and 99\% of the MILP optimal NPV while scaling linearly in computation time. These results position simulator-constrained LLM agents as a practical and scalable alternative to classical optimization for long-horizon industrial scheduling under complex operational constraints.

</details>


### [286] [From Context-Aware to Conflict-Aware: Generalizing Contrastive Decoding for Knowledge Conflict in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10298)
*Runze Jiang,Taiqiang Wu,Yan Wang,Bingyu Zhu,Longtao Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces conflict-aware decoding for LMs that dynamically weighs prior vs. context using a power-family interpolation, exposes regime asymmetry, and presents ARR (Adaptive Regime Routing) to improve resistance. Also proposes TriState-Bench, a model-aware evaluation protocol with three conflict states, and provides code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliably combining external retrieved/augmented context with the model's parametric priors; existing context-aware methods tend to overtrust context and fail when it is noisy or wrong.

Method: Proposes an affine (logit) combination of prior and context to form a power-family of regimes with asymmetry; analyzes extrapolation vs interpolation behavior; introduces TriState-Bench for three-state evaluation (correction, resistance, agreement); develops Adaptive Regime Routing to switch between regimes dynamically at each step.

Result: The framework reveals inherent bias in single-regime methods; ARR substantially improves resistance (EM) from below 6 to 16–33 without harming correction or agreement; code available.

Conclusion: Conflict-aware decoding addresses fundamental asymmetry between priors and context; ARR enables robust, per-step regime routing; TriState-Bench provides a comprehensive evaluation framework for conflict states; empirical code released.

Abstract: When large language models generate from retrieved or augmented contexts, conflicts between external context and parametric priors remain a central reliability bottleneck. Existing contrastive decoding methods follow a \emph{context-aware} paradigm that unilaterally amplifies context over parametric priors, overwriting correct priors when the context is erroneous. We generalize this to the \textbf{conflict-aware} paradigm that dynamically allocates authority between prior and context based on conflict signals, rather than presupposing context trustworthiness. We show that the affine combination of prior and context logits yields a \textbf{power family} with an inherent \textbf{regime asymmetry}: extrapolation amplifies errors unboundedly when the prior is correct, interpolation under-corrects when the context is correct, and no static regime covers both. Existing contrastive decoding methods are instances of this family, mostly extrapolative. To evaluate both conflict directions, we propose TriState-Bench, a model-aware evaluation protocol that calibrates per-model prior knowledge to measure three conflict states: correction, resistance, and agreement. To resolve the asymmetry, we propose Adaptive Regime Routing (ARR), which routes between regimes at each step, lifting resistance EM from below 6 to 16--33 without sacrificing correction or agreement. Our code is available at https://github.com/keith-Jiang/conflict-aware-decoding.

</details>


### [287] [What Spatial Memory Must Store: Occlusion as the Test for Language-Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10299)
*Doeon Kwon,Junho Bang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Geometry-guided memory weighting outperforms recency-based blends for spatial queries; separating recall from visibility is essential; a ray-voxel DDA readout markedly improves visibility estimation; preregistered pilots validate measurement approach; a full human multi-world study is planned for future work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether anchoring memories to world coordinates (memory palace) adds value beyond a linear blend of proximity, recency, and importance, and to isolate and validate the separate roles of recall (occlusion-driven memory) and visibility (perception of stored geometry).

Method: Three pilot experiments. (1) Pre-registered recall comparing a geometry-led weighting against a shipped linear blend, measuring Delta-Hit@5 with nonparametric tests. (2) Separation of recall vs visibility: use a ray-versus-voxel DDA re-pointed from the gaze ray to evaluate visibility; compare text/live FoV vs cone+DDA. (3) Live-visibility preregistration (SPMEM-OCC-LIVE-v1) across eight scripted worlds with automated oracle scoring, 96 behind-wall targets, assessing false-visible; compute exact McNemar statistics. 

Result: 1) Geometry-led weighting wins recall: ΔHit@5 = +0.3208 (p<1e-15); shipped blend: ΔHit@5 = -0.0375 (p=0.306). 2) Visibility readout: text and live FoV both score 0.000 on 849 behind-wall targets; cone+DDA scores 0.982 (p<1e-6); coordinate recall improves cosine similarity (1.000 vs 0.533, n=150). 3) Live preregistration: false-visible reduced from 1.000 to 0.000; McNemar p=2.5e-29; eight scripted worlds, automated oracle scoring. 

Conclusion: Occlusion necessitates geometry, but the contribution is in measurement and isolation of what spatial memory stores versus how it is read. These pilots enable a frozen confirmatory study (SPMEM-ZERO-REAL-PREREG-v1); a full human-authored multi-world study with blind raters remains future work.

Abstract: Language-agent "memory palace" systems anchor each memory to a world coordinate, on the intuition that geometry adds something text cannot. We make that intuition testable and report three results. First, the memory-palace default of folding spatial proximity into a linear blend beside recency and importance does not help and can hurt: in a pre-registered recall experiment the shipped blend fails its own frozen test (mean Delta-Hit@5 -0.0375, Wilcoxon p=0.306), sitting at a position-blind baseline, while a geometry-led weighting wins decisively (+0.3208, p<10^-15): geometry must lead recall when the query regime is spatial. Second, memory recall and visibility must be separated: recall is occlusion-blind by design (you correctly remember the next room behind a wall), while visibility is a perception predicate over stored geometry that the live system never computed. A one-line ray-versus-voxel digital differential analyzer (DDA), re-pointed from the gaze ray the agent already casts, supplies it: text and the live FoV cone both score 0.000 on 849 behind-wall targets while cone-plus-DDA reaches 0.982 (exact McNemar p<10^-6); coordinate recall separately resolves near-duplicate locations a cosine null cannot (1.000 vs 0.533, n=150). Third, the visibility predicate is confirmed live under a git-committed pre-registration (SPMEM-OCC-LIVE-v1: eight scripted worlds, automated oracle scoring, 96 behind-wall targets, false-visible 1.000->0.000, pooled exact McNemar p=2.5x10^-29), a run that surfaced and fixed a real relay anchor defect. We concede that occlusion-needs-geometry is near-tautological; the contribution is the measurement and isolation, separating what spatial memory must store from how it is read. These pilots power a frozen confirmatory study (SPMEM-ZERO-REAL-PREREG-v1); the full human-authored multi-world study with blind raters remains future work.

</details>


### [288] [Mobility Anomaly Generation using LLM-Driven Behavior with Kinematic Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10314)
*Yueyang Liu,Joon-Seok Kim,Andreas Züfle*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: End-to-end generative framework to synthesize realistic trajectory anomalies for ground-truth datasets using LLM-driven anomalies, map-constrained routing, and context-aware GPS noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ground-truth anomalous trajectory data are scarce due to rarity, high collection costs, and privacy constraints; scalable synthetic data with realism is needed for robust anomaly detection research.

Method: Inject semantic anomalies via LLM agents into baseline simulated trajectories; perform map-constrained routing reconstruction to recalibrate physical transitions; apply context-aware spatial noise to emulate heterogeneous GPS degradation; produce annotated ground-truth trajectories.

Result: Conceptual framework with no reported empirical results in the abstract; proposed methodology rather than validated findings.

Conclusion: Proposes a scalable pipeline to generate large-scale, annotated trajectory anomalies that bridge synthetic and real data, enabling more rigorous study of anomaly detection in human mobility.

Abstract: Although the study of human trajectory anomalies is critical for advancing spatial data mining, empirical research remains severely hindered by a pervasive lack of ground-truth datasets. Despite the availability of several real-world and simulated human trajectory collections, these datasets exclusively capture normal mobility patterns and lack annotated anomalies. This specific scarcity is fundamentally driven by the inherent statistical rarity of anomalous events, precluding the feasibility of conventional observational methods. Compounding this challenge, the systematic acquisition of large-scale mobility data is strictly bottlenecked by prohibitive costs and stringent privacy regulations. To overcome these fundamental limitations and establish a reliable human trajectory anomalies dataset with annotated ground truth, we introduce a novel, end-to-end generative framework designed to synthesize realistic trajectory anomalies at scale. Our architecture bridges the gap between purely synthetic mobility data and complex real-world physical constraints by operating directly on baseline simulated trajectories. We employ Large Language Model (LLM) agents to systematically inject semantically meaningful behavioral anomalies such as irregular out-of-distribution check-ins and skipped routine visits. To ensure rigorous spatial validity, the system leverages map-constrained routing reconstruction to recalculate the physical transitions between these LLM agent-modified staypoints. Moreover, to narrow the simulation-to-reality gap, we augment the resulting trajectories with a context-aware spatial noise model, parameterized by environmental and location-specific variables, to accurately emulate heterogeneous GPS sensor degradation.

</details>


### [289] [Self-Distillation Policy Optimization via Visual Feedback: Bridging Code and Visual Artifacts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10334)
*Haoyu Dong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Visual-SDPO: a self-distillation policy-optimization framework using rendered visual feedback as privileged context to improve code-generated visuals; introduces Visual-Grounded Code Credit Weighting and a sequence-level Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) term; evaluated on chart, UI/web, and slide generation with the Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct backbone, attaining >10 absolute-point gains over zero-shot and >2.4-point gains over GRPO, with fewer training steps and no inference-time cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Code-generated visuals often exhibit salient defects due to non-differentiable renderers and coding-before-observing-render; there is a need for supervision that ties visual quality back to the originating code.

Method: A weight-sharing teacher–student distillation framework where rendered visual feedback is treated as privileged context to guide the teacher; Visual-Grounded Code Credit Weighting attributes defects to code statements and amplifies the distillation signal on those statements; a sequence-level GRPO term encourages executable, high-quality rollouts; errors in non-executable outputs remain learnable via the self-distillation path by passing execution errors as privileged context to the teacher; uses a unified Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct backbone; applied to chart, web/UI, and slide generation (ChartMimic, Design2Code, AeSlides).

Result: Visual-SDPO improves over zero-shot baselines by more than 10 absolute points in the primary metric and surpasses GRPO by at least 2.4 points across chart-to-code, UI-to-code, and slide-generation benchmarks; achieves these gains with fewer training steps and no added inference-time cost.

Conclusion: The work presents a cohesive self-distillation framework that leverages visual feedback and defect attribution to improve code-generated visuals across multiple modalities, achieving notable improvements efficiently; it suggests strong potential for generalization to other code-to-visual generation tasks.

Abstract: Code-generating large language models (LLMs) increasingly produce visual artifacts such as charts, web pages, and slides by writing programs that are executed by non-differentiable renderers, committing to code before observing the render. As a result, otherwise executable code often yields artifacts with visually salient defects, including overlapping elements, clipped text, broken alignment, low contrast, and overflow. We study visual-feedback self-distillation for code-generated visual artifacts. We propose Visual-SDPO, a self-distillation policy-optimization framework that treats rendered visual feedback as privileged context for a weight-sharing teacher and distills this feedback into a coding student. To make supervision spatially targeted rather than uniform, we introduce Visual-Grounded Code Credit Weighting, which traces each detected defect back to the code statements responsible for the affected elements and amplifies the distillation signal on those statements. A sequence-level GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) term complements the dense token-level objective by rewarding executable, visually high-quality rollouts, while failed executions remain learnable through the self-distillation path by passing execution errors as privileged context to the teacher. We instantiate Visual-SDPO for chart, web/UI, and slide generation with a unified Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct backbone. Across chart-to-code, UI-to-code, and slide-generation benchmarks (ChartMimic, Design2Code, and AeSlides), Visual-SDPO improves over the zero-shot base by more than 10 absolute points in the primary metric and over GRPO by at least 2.4 points, with fewer training steps and no added inference-time cost.

</details>


### [290] [Reasoning or Memorization? Direction-Aware Diversity Exploration in LLM Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10346)
*Jiangnan Xia,Yucheng Shi,Yu Yang,Kishan Panaganti,Zhenwen Liang,Ninghao Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DiRL is a direction-aware reinforcement learning framework that biases exploration toward reasoning-driven updates by deriving a reasoning-memorization direction from model representations, weighting gradient features, and shaping rewards within GRPO, yielding improved results on math and general reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing exploration methods mix reasoning-driven exploration with memorization shortcuts, risking prioritizing memorization over genuine reasoning. There is a need to anchor exploration to a direction that separates reasoning from memorization.

Method: Extract a direction from model representations that distinguishes reasoning from memorization. Build direction-weighted gradient features to characterize rollout updates. Shape rewards to amplify reasoning-aligned exploration and suppress memorization-aligned variations. Integrate DiRL with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO).

Result: Empirical evaluations on mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks show significant improvements over existing exploration methods, indicating that direction-aware exploration enhances reasoning progress rather than memorization.

Conclusion: DiRL offers a principled mechanism to steer exploration toward genuine reasoning improvements. By anchoring updates to a learned reasoning-memorization direction and integrating with GRPO, it yields stronger reasoning performance and robustness across benchmarks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has become a key paradigm for eliciting reasoning abilities in large language models, where exploration is crucial for discovering effective solution trajectories. Existing exploration methods typically encourage diversity in semantic or gradient spaces, without distinguishing what drives this diversity. A trajectory may appear novel because it follows a new reasoning process, or because it varies memorized patterns and shortcuts. Rewarding both cases equally may steer exploration toward memorization rather than genuine reasoning improvement. In this paper, we propose DiRL, a Direction-Aware Reinforcement Learning framework that anchors exploration to an internal reasoning-memorization direction of the policy. Specifically, DiRL extracts this direction from model representations, constructs direction-weighted gradient features to characterize rollout updates, and shapes rewards to amplify reasoning-aligned exploration while suppressing memorization-aligned variations. DiRL integrates seamlessly into standard Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Extensive experiments on mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of DiRL, showing significant improvements over various existing exploration methods.

</details>


### [291] [ReflectiChain: Epistemic Grounding in LLM-Driven World Models for Supply Chain Resilience](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10359)
*Jia Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: REFLECTICHAIN introduces a Generative Supply Chain World Model (SC-WM) with a 6-dim graph-latent space and double-loop learning to integrate LLMs and RL for epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty handling; evaluated on Semi-Sim benchmark showing improved rationale consistency, resilience, and anti-fragility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the epistemic gap between policy interpretation by large language models (LLMs) and policy optimization by reinforcement learning (RL) in heterogeneous supply networks with unstructured constraints; address uncertainty (epistemic and aleatoric) and robust decision-making under perturbations.

Method: A Generative SC-WM encoding heterogeneous supply networks into a 6-dimensional graph-latent space with physical conservation, plus a Double-Loop Learning framework separating epistemic uncertainty (KL-trust-region-bounded policy adaptation) from aleatoric uncertainty (stochastic latent rollouts). Evaluation on Semi-Sim: a 10-node semiconductor benchmark with SIR risk propagation, 6 perturbation types, and 10 policy constraint templates.

Result: Significant improvement in Rationale Consistency Score by 33.0% (p<0.0001, d=2.78); maintains 82.3% operability under adversarial shocks; exhibits anti-fragile behavior with +40.2% gain under moderate pressure. Identifies three operational epistemic mechanisms (uncertainty separation, knowledge-boundary detection, empirical Bayesian policy updating) and discusses five limitation categories.

Conclusion: REFLECTICHAIN effectively couples LLM-guided reasoning with RL-based optimization through a generative SC-WM and double-loop learning to address epistemic and aleatoric uncertainty in supply chains, offering improved rationale consistency and resilience; the work outlines epistemic mechanisms and limitation categories for future research.

Abstract: AI agents in supply chains face a fundamental epistemic gap: large language models (LLMs) interpret policies but lack physical grounding, while reinforcement learning (RL) optimizes flows but is semantically blind to unstructured constraints. We introduce REFLECTICHAIN, bridging this gap through a Generative Supply Chain World Model (SC-WM) - encoding heterogeneous supply networks into a 6-dim graph-latent space with physical conservation - and Double-Loop Learning that separates epistemic uncertainty (KL-trust-region-bounded policy adaptation) from aleatoric uncertainty (stochastic latent rollouts). On Semi-Sim, a 10-node semiconductor benchmark with SIR risk propagation, 6 perturbation types, and 10 policy constraint templates, REFLECTICHAIN improves Rationale Consistency Score by 33.0% (p < 0.0001, d = 2.78), maintains 82.3% operability under adversarial shocks, and exhibits anti-fragile behavior (+40.2% gain under moderate pressure). We identify three operational epistemic mechanisms - uncertainty separation, knowledge-boundary detection, and empirical Bayesian policy updating - and discuss five limitation categories.

</details>


### [292] [Belief-Space Control for Personalized Cancer Treatment via Active Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10376)
*Deniz Sargun,H. Bugra Tulay,C. Emre Koksal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Cancer treatment is framed as belief-space planning via active inference, unifying goal-directed control and information gathering under measurement budgets; applied to AACR GENIE data to achieve patient categorization and high treatment efficacy under real constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RL struggles with oncology settings where treatments permanently alter transition dynamics and where measurement budgets limit observations; a principled framework is needed to integrate control and information acquisition under uncertainty.

Method: Formulate cancer treatment as a belief-space planning problem using active inference; derive an expected free-energy objective that combines goal-directed behavior and information gathering under measurement constraints; implement on real clinical data from AACR GENIE Biopharma Collaborative dataset.

Result: Demonstrates simultaneous patient categorization (stratification) and high treatment efficacy within real measurement and treatment constraints.

Conclusion: Active inference-based belief-space planning offers a valid and promising approach for cancer treatment under partial observability and budget constraints, with demonstrated feasibility on large-scale clinical data.

Abstract: Cancer treatment is at the core a sequential decision-making problem with partial observability, latent patient heterogeneity, and explicit constraints on the budget for medical measurements. Unlike standard Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches that control state trajectories, cancer treatments permanently modify patients' transition dynamics, changing how states evolve over time. We model cancer treatment as a belief-space planning problem using active inference, deriving an expected free-energy objective that unifies goal-directed control and information acquisition under measurement budgets without. We implement this framework using real clinical cancer data from the AACR Project GENIE Biopharma Collaborative dataset. Results on clinical data demonstrate a simultaneous patient categorization and high treatment efficacy, under real measurement and treatment constraints.

</details>


### [293] [Beyond Static Evaluation: Co-Evolutionary Mechanisms for LLM-Driven Strategy Evolution in Adversarial Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10389)
*Haoran Li,Zengle Ge,Ziyang Zhang,Xiaomin Yuan,Yui Lo,Qianhui Liu,Bocheng An,Dongke Rong,Jiaqun Liu,Annan Li,Jianmin Wu,Dawei Yin,Dou Shen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FAMOU introduces evaluator co-evolution, hierarchical deep evaluation, and weakness pressure to enable robust LLM-driven code evolution in adversarial multi-agent games; achieves state-of-the-art results on MCTF 2026 with strong generalization and real-world transfer; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address evaluation landscape shifts and stagnation when evolving agents face increasingly capable opponents; develop robust, adaptive evaluators and strategies that remain informative as strategies evolve.

Method: Three mechanisms: evaluator co-evolution (incorporate discovered champions into the opponent pool); hierarchical deep evaluation (multi-game, statistically reliable assessments instead of single-game scores); weakness pressure (up-weight the most difficult opponents to break through plateaus). Implemented within FAMOU, a framework aligned with the OpenEvolve/ShinkaEvolve lineage for LLM-driven code evolution. Experimental setup includes MCTF 2026 3v3 maritime capture-the-flag, two backbone LLMs, baselines, ablations, and analysis of emergent tactics; release of code/evaluation tooling.

Result: FAMOU consistently outperforms baselines; highest combined score 0.526; 61.7% win rate against unseen opponents; ablations confirm each mechanism contributes; LLM mutations produce novel tactical structures (lookahead, adaptive interception); hardware round-robin 1st, simulation 3rd at AAMAS 2026 MCTF Competition.

Conclusion: The combination of adaptive evaluation, robust multi-game assessment, and dynamic opponent weighting enables generalizable, high-performing LLM-driven code evolution in adversarial settings, with evidence of novel algorithmic innovations and real-world transfer; resources released to the community.

Abstract: Recent advances in LLM-driven code evolution have enabled automated discovery by iteratively generating and improving programs. However, applying these methods to adversarial multi-agent games introduces a fundamental challenge: the evaluation landscape shifts as strategies improve, causing fixed evaluators to become unreliable and evolution to stagnate. We propose three mechanisms to address this challenge: evaluator co-evolution, which incorporates discovered champions into the opponent pool; hierarchical deep evaluation, which replaces noisy few-game scores with statistically reliable assessments; and weakness pressure, which dynamically up-weights the most difficult opponents to break through plateaus. We implement these mechanisms within FAMOU, a framework built upon the same foundation-model code-evolution paradigm as OpenEvolve and ShinkaEvolve. On the MCTF 2026 3v3 maritime capture-the-flag task, FAMOU consistently outperforms both baselines under two backbone LLMs, achieving the highest combined score (0.526) and the best generalization to unseen opponents (61.7% win rate), while ablations confirm that each mechanism contributes to performance. Notably, the LLM mutation process generates tactical structures entirely absent from the seed strategies -- including lookahead search and adaptive interception -- demonstrating that code-level evolution can produce nontrivial algorithmic innovations in adversarial settings. The FAMOU-evolved strategy further achieved 1st place in the hardware round-robin and 3rd in simulation at the AAMAS 2026 MCTF Competition, validating its real-world transferability. The optimized implementation and corresponding evaluation codes developed through our evolutionary process are available at: https://github.com/1xiangliu1/FAMOU-CoEvo

</details>


### [294] [Instruction Finetuning DeepSeek-R1-8B Model Using LoRA and NEFTune](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10392)
*Wu Yuerong,Mingni Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LoRA-adapted DeepSeek-R1-8B with NEFTune improves financial NER micro-F1 to 0.912 on seven entity types, outperforming several baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Financial NER requires domain-specific patterns; general LLMs often misclassify financial entities, motivating domain-adaptive training and lightweight fine-tuning.

Method: Convert each sentence into an instruction-input-output triple; insert lightweight LoRA adapters into Transformer layers; apply NEFTune by adding uniform noise to embedding vectors during training; evaluate on a corpus of 1693 annotated sentences across seven entity types (Company, Date, Location, Money, Person, Product, Quantity).

Result: Micro-F1: 0.901 with LoRA alone; 0.912 with NEFTune; outperforms Llama3-8B, Qwen3-8B, Baichuan2-7B, T5, and BERT-Base baselines.

Conclusion: Combining LoRA adaptation with NEFTune on an open-source LLM yields strong financial NER performance, illustrating effective domain-specific prompting and lightweight fine-tuning for specialized NLP tasks.

Abstract: Financial named-entity recognition (NER) is essential for translating unstructured financial reports and news into structured knowledge graphs. However, general-purpose large language models (LLMs) often misclassify financial entities or ignore domain-specific patterns. This paper investigates the use of DeepSeek-R1-8B, a recent open-source large language model, combined with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and Noisy Embedding Fine-Tuning (NEFTune) for financial NER. Each annotated sentence in our corpus of 1693 samples is converted into an instruction-input-output triple. We insert lightweight LoRA matrices into the Transformer layers and apply NEFTune to improve generalisation by adding uniform noise to embedding vectors during training. Experiments show that the LoRA-adapted DeepSeek-R1-8B achieves a micro-F1 of 0.901 on seven entity types (Company, Date, Location, Money, Person, Product and Quantity), and adding NEFTune further boosts the micro-F1 to 0.912, outperforming Llama3-8B, Qwen3-8B, Baichuan2-7B, T5 and BERT-Base baselines.

</details>


### [295] [STAGE-Claw: Automated State-based Agent Benchmarking for Realistic Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10394)
*Sirui Liang,Bohan Yu,Peiyu Wang,Shiguang Guo,Wenxing Hu,Pengfei Cao,Jian Zhao,Cao Liu,Ke Zeng,Xunliang Cai,Kang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: STAGE-Claw is a state-based benchmarking framework for evaluating personal agents in realistic environments. It automates task creation, validation, and ground-truth wiring from task hints, and uses final-state correctness as the evaluation metric across 40 tasks and 11 models, addressing scalability gaps in existing benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks rely on sandboxed artifacts, static task design, and coarse scoring, which hinder realistic evaluation and scalability of personal agents. There is a need for automated generation of realistic tasks and state-based evaluation to better reflect real-world agent usage.

Method: Given a task hint, STAGE-Claw automatically generates and validates a complete benchmark task including environment setup, task prompts, ground truth, and verification programs. Agents are evaluated in realistic state-based environments, with performance measured by the correctness of the final system state rather than just textual responses. The study builds a 40-task benchmark and evaluates 11 frontier models, analyzing task scores, costs, tool-call reliability, and failure patterns.

Result: A 40-task benchmark of realistic personal-agent scenarios is created and used to evaluate 11 frontier models. The framework provides metrics on task scores, resource costs, tool-call reliability, and common failure modes, demonstrating scalable, state-based evaluation of agents in realistic settings.

Conclusion: STAGE-Claw offers a scalable, state-based framework for evaluating personal agents in realistic user scenarios, enabling automatic generation of benchmarks and ground-truth verification to drive progress in robust agent evaluation.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used to power personal agents for everyday applications, but evaluating these agents remains a challenge. Existing benchmarks still rely on sandboxed artifacts, static task design, and coarse scoring, which hinder scalability and limit progress toward reliable personal-agent evaluation. This paper introduces STAGE-Claw, an automated framework for building and evaluating realistic personal-agent scenarios in state-based personal-computing environments. Given a task hint, STAGE-Claw automatically creates and validates a realistic benchmark task with its environment, task prompts, ground truth, and related verification programs. Agents are then evaluated in realistic operating environments, where performance is measured by the correctness of the final system state rather than only the textual response. Using STAGE-Claw, this paper creates a benchmark with 40 challenging real scenario agent tasks, evaluates 11 frontier models, and analyzes their task scores, costs, tool-call reliability, and common failure patterns. Overall, STAGE-Claw offers a scalable, state-based way to evaluate agents in realistic user scenarios.

</details>


### [296] [A Unified Multi-Modal Framework for Intelligent Financial Systems: Integrating Reinforcement Learning, High-Frequency Trading, and Game-Theoretic Approaches with Cross-Modal Sentiment Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10412)
*Fanrong Liu,Zhang Yuwei,Mingni Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A unified, multi-domain financial AI framework integrating PPO-based robo-advisory, time-series predictors for high-frequency trading, in-context learning for advisory, game-theoretic banking strategies, and cross-modal sentiment embeddings, achieving significant performance gains over isolated systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome fragmentation where AI methods in finance are developed in isolation, limiting synergy and real-world applicability across portfolio management, trading, advisory, banking competition, and sentiment analysis.

Method: Combine: Proximal Policy Optimization for robo-advisory; advanced time-series models for high-frequency trading; in-context learning for dynamic investment advisory; game-theoretic approaches for competitive banking scenarios; unified embeddings for cross-modal sentiment analysis. Prove convergence of integrated optimization problem; validate on diverse financial datasets and real-world scenarios.

Result: Empirical gains include 23.7% portfolio optimization improvement, 31.2% reduction in HFT prediction error, 18.9% improvement in investment recommendation accuracy, 27.4% faster Nash equilibrium convergence, 15.6% improvement in sentiment analysis via cross-modal fusion; demonstrates superior performance vs specialized systems.

Conclusion: The framework provides both theoretical convergence guarantees and practical blueprint for comprehensive intelligent financial systems adaptable to complex, interconnected markets; potential across multiple institutions.

Abstract: The rapid evolution of financial technology demands sophisticated artificial intelligence systems capable of handling diverse challenges across multiple domains simultaneously. This paper presents a groundbreaking unified framework that seamlessly integrates Proximal Policy Optimization for robo-advisory systems, advanced time-series prediction models for high-frequency trading, in-context learning mechanisms for dynamic investment advisory, game-theoretic approaches for competitive banking scenarios, and unified embeddings for cross-modal financial sentiment analysis. Our comprehensive framework addresses the critical gap in existing literature where these technologies have been developed in isolation, failing to leverage their synergistic potential. Through extensive experimentation across multiple financial datasets and real-world scenarios, we demonstrate that our integrated approach achieves superior performance compared to specialized single-domain systems. Specifically, our framework shows a 23.7% improvement in portfolio optimization metrics, reduces prediction error in high-frequency trading by 31.2%, enhances investment recommendation accuracy by 18.9%, optimizes competitive banking strategies with a 27.4% increase in Nash equilibrium convergence speed, and improves sentiment analysis accuracy by 15.6% through cross-modal fusion. The theoretical foundation of our work establishes convergence guarantees for the integrated optimization problem, while our empirical results validate the practical applicability across diverse financial institutions. This research not only advances the state-of-the-art in financial AI but also provides a blueprint for developing comprehensive intelligent systems that can adapt to the complex, interconnected nature of modern financial markets.

</details>


### [297] [Soul Computing: A Theoretical Framework and Technical Architecture for Intelligent Agents with Independent Consciousness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10413)
*Jinshan Zhang,Xishi Zhou,Qiu Peng,Jianwei Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The abstract proposes 'Soul Computing' as a paradigm shift from traditional virtual humans toward AI entities with self-identity, clarifying its scope and distinctions from related fields, and arguing for an 'Intensional' core to enable AI agency beyond tool use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve core conceptual ambiguities at the AI–digital human interface, including differences between next-generation intelligent agents and virtual humans, methods for constructing digital entities with self-identity, and the technical/ethical challenges facing this domain.

Method: Conceptual and theoretical analysis: tracing evolutionary views of consciousness and memory; reassessing the value of multimodal digital fragments for reverse reconstruction of mental worlds; formal delineation of narrow vs broad Soul Computing; clarifying boundaries with Affective Computing, Historical Reconstruction, and Mortal Computation; arguing for an architectural shift to an Intensional core.

Result: Formal taxonomy and boundaries of Soul Computing introduced; distinctions from related fields clarified; a fundamental architectural requirement (Intensional core) proposed to enable transition from toolhood to living agency; framework laid for future design and ethical considerations.

Conclusion: Soul Computing represents a paradigmatic move toward AI entities with internal intentionality and identity. Realizing this requires explicit architectural commitments to intensionality, careful differentiation from extensional systems, and ongoing attention to ethical implications as AI approaches living-like agency.

Abstract: Breakthroughs in large language models and multimodal generation technologies have propelled the digital reconstruction of human mental traits, emotional patterns, and long-term memory from science fiction toward engineering practice. Yet current research and industry practices at the intersection of AI and digital humans remain hampered by fundamental conceptual ambiguities: the essential differences between next-generation intelligent agents and traditional virtual humans, the construction pathways for digital entities possessing self-identity, and the core technical and ethical challenges confronting this domain all demand urgent clarification. This paper systematically examines the transformative logic underlying the transition from traditional virtual humans to the ``Soul Computing'' paradigm, driven by frontier AI technologies. We first analyze the evolutionary patterns of human consciousness and memory mechanisms, reassessing the core value of massive multimodal digital fragments in the reverse reconstruction of individual mental worlds. On this basis, we formally delineate the academic connotations of narrow and broad Soul Computing for the first time, clarifying its academic boundaries and essential distinctions from Affective Computing, Historical Reconstruction, and Mortal Computation. We argue that Soul Computing systems must architecturally construct an ``Intensional'' core rather than serving as purely ``Extensional'' functional carriers, thereby enabling the fundamental transition of AI from toolhood to living agency.

</details>


### [298] [Trace2Policy: From Expert Behavior Traces to Self-Evolving Decision Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10457)
*Junli Zha,Jinbo Wang,Chao Zhou,Xiang Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Trace2Policy uses error-driven iterative skill refinement (EISR) to recover and improve human decision rules for compliance tasks, producing Python-compiled rules that outperform LLM prompts and baseline LLM deployments, with cost-efficient Auto-EISR and cross-domain transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In auditing, compliance, and contract review, tacit enterprise rules underperform in skewed-base-rate settings. The work argues that rule quality, not model capability, is the primary performance lever, and aims to systematically recover and upgrade tacit rules.

Method: Maintain a human-readable rule document as the optimization target. In each round, run the rules on a validation set, cluster errors by root cause into MISSING, WRONG, or CONFLICT, apply targeted patches, and commit only those that pass a regression gate. Compare execution forms (LLM prompts vs deterministic Python). Evaluate across multiple LLMs, an Auto-EISR variant, and transfer to public benchmarks (LegalBench, BPIC 2012).

Result: Eight EISR rounds raise performance from ~70% (one-shot distillation) to 79.6% when rules are compiled into deterministic Python (zero LLM inference). In production, Python-compiled content runs 9.8 percentage points higher than LLM prompts. A 22-day deployment at a major logistics carrier (3,349 audit cases) shows the compiled pipeline at 72.7%, outperforming the pure-LLM baseline it replaced; re-enabled LLM fallback degrades accuracy. Auto-EISR delivers refinement at $5–$10 per cycle versus ~70 expert-hours and transfers to four public benchmarks without re-engineering.

Conclusion: For skewed-base-rate, rule quality is the dominant performance lever. The EISR-driven, rule-centric pipeline yields substantial gains with no inference-time LLMs, while offering cost-effective deployment and cross-domain transfer, suggesting broad applicability to compliance-sensitive decision tasks.

Abstract: Decision rules that enterprise experts apply tacitly -- in auditing, compliance, and contract review -- can be systematically recovered and improved through iterative error analysis. We present \textbf{Trace2Policy}, whose core mechanism -- \textbf{EISR} (\textbf{E}rror-driven \textbf{I}terative \textbf{S}kill \textbf{R}efinement) -- maintains a human-readable rule document as its optimization target: each round executes the rules on a validation set, clusters errors by root cause into MISSING, WRONG, or CONFLICT types, applies targeted patches, and commits only those that pass a regression gate. \textbf{For this class of compliance-sensitive, skewed-base-rate decision tasks, we identify rule quality -- not model capability -- as the dominant performance lever}: across five LLMs, one-shot distillation plateaus near $\sim$70\% on the deployed pool, while eight EISR rounds lift the same rules to 79.6\% when compiled into deterministic Python -- zero LLM calls at inference. \textbf{Execution form compounds the gain: in production, the same EISR-refined content runs 9.8~pp higher as compiled Python than as an LLM prompt, a form-and-engineering bundle the 22-day deployment matured together.} Deployed for 22 days at a major logistics carrier (3,349 audit cases), the compiled pipeline outperforms the pure-LLM baseline it replaced (72.7\%); on these calibrated, skewed-base-rate workloads, re-enabling LLM fallback monotonically degrades accuracy. An LLM-driven variant, \textbf{Auto-EISR}, reproduces this refinement at \$5--\$10 per cycle versus $\sim$70 expert-hours, and transfers to four public benchmarks spanning legal reasoning (LegalBench) and process-mining decisions (BPIC 2012) without re-engineering.

</details>


### [299] [ComBench: A Benchmark for Rigorous Proof Reasoning and Constructive Realization in Olympiad-Level Combinatorics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10479)
*Shunkai Zhang,Haoran Zhang,Yun Luo,Qianjia Cheng,Haodi Lei,Yizhuo Li,Runzhe Zhan,Zhilin Wang,Bangjie Xu,Yucheng Su,Xinmiao Han,Xiaoye Qu,Dongrui Liu,Zhouchen Lin,Yu Qiao,Ning Ding,Yafu Li,Yu Cheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces ComBench, a 100-item Olympiad-level combinatorics benchmark with analysis- and construction-centric problems, coupled with rubric-based proof grading and deterministic construction verification; results show frontier models are not saturated and reveal a separation between rigorous proof reasoning and constructive realization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To diagnose and quantify the combinatorial reasoning abilities of large language models, addressing observed gaps in Olympiad-style problem solving.

Method: Assemble 100 human-annotated problems, split into analysis-centric and construction-centric sets. Use rubric-guided proof grading and deterministic verification of constructions. Evaluate frontier open- and closed-source models and compare capabilities across problem types.

Result: Best average performance 65.4% (Avg) and 75.3% (Best@4). Findings include a distinction between Rigorous Proof Reasoning and Constructive Realization; Kimi-K2.6 trails GPT-5.5 on analysis-centric proof grading but surpasses on construction-centric Best@4; Existence and Construction problems are the hardest across frontier models.

Conclusion: ComBench exposes non-saturation of current models and distinct reasoning-construction capabilities, highlighting areas for future model development and benchmarking strategies that separately target proof grammar and constructive realization.

Abstract: Combinatorics is central to Olympiad-level mathematical problem solving, requiring deep discrete reasoning, creative constructions, and rigorous structural insight. Recent evidence suggests that even today's strongest frontier models remain uneven on Olympiad combinatorics, revealing a gap in creative mathematical reasoning. We introduce ComBench, an Olympiad-level combinatorics benchmark for evaluating and diagnosing the combinatorial reasoning capabilities of large language models. ComBench contains 100 human-annotated competition-level problems organized around two complementary settings: analysis-centric problems, which primarily require rigorous mathematical arguments, and construction-centric problems, which require explicit constructions in addition to correctness justifications. The evaluation protocol combines rubric-guided proof grading with deterministic construction verification, exposing cases where proof quality and construction validity diverge. Experiments on frontier open- and closed-source models show that ComBench is far from saturated: the strongest model reaches 65.4% overall Avg. and 75.3% overall Best@4. We further find that Rigorous Proof Reasoning and Constructive Realization are distinct capabilities: Kimi-K2.6 trails GPT-5.5 on analysis-centric proof grading but surpasses it on construction-centric Best@4, while Existence and Construction problems remain consistently hardest across representative frontier models.

</details>


### [300] [A complementary study on PlanGPT: Evaluation with defined Performance Metrics and comparison with a planner](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10489)
*Youssef Abdelkader,Humbert Fiorino,Damien Pellier*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PlanGPT does not outperform a Greedy search in automated planning; the study replicates PlanGPT's experiments, evaluates Plan Cost and Plan Generation Time, and finds PlanGPT no better than a traditional heuristic planner.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess the relevance and validity of PlanGPT for planning with LLMs, verify the original paper's plan coverage claims, and provide a more thorough comparative evaluation against traditional planners.

Method: Replication/complementary study: re-run PlanGPT experiments on planning tasks, measure Plan Cost and Plan Generation Time, compare PlanGPT outputs to a traditional planner for the same tasks and metrics, and re-examine PlanGPT's reported plan coverage.

Result: PlanGPT does not demonstrate superiority over a Greedy search strategy; in other words, the LLM-based planner offers no clear advantage over a simple heuristic approach under the evaluated metrics.

Conclusion: LLM-based planning via PlanGPT may not offer advantages over traditional heuristic planners; results call for cautious interpretation and broader validation of LLM planners for planning tasks.

Abstract: Automated Planning is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence (AI) where the main objective is generating a sequence of actions, known as a plan, that helps us reach a goal state from an initial state. A planning problem is defined by a set of objects, an initial state and a desired goal state. The objective is to compute a plan that'll lead us from the inital state to the goal state. Programs that generate plans are called planners.
  In this paper, we did a complementary study to the state-of-the-art LLM called PlanGPT which was released last year. We redid some experiments to verify whether planning with LLMs is \textbf{pertinent} and \textbf{worthwhile}. We also check whether the results obtained in the official PlanGPT paper for plan coverage were correct, and we also performed a more comprehensive study on PlanGPT's performance: in our paper PlanGPT's performance was evaluated using two metrics: Plan Cost and Plan Generation Time. The results of planGPT were compared to those produced by a traditional planner for the same plans and same metrics. We discovered that PlanGPT is no better than a Greedy search strategy.

</details>


### [301] [A Reliable Fault Diagnosis Method Based on Belief Rule Base Consider Robustness Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10500)
*Mingyuan Liu,Dan Yin,Zongzong Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BRB-based fault diagnosis with robustness analysis and three constraint strategies; validated on WD615 diesel engine and CWRU bearings; shows improved accuracy and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sensor readings are imperfect and uncertain; robustness in fault diagnosis models is essential to maintain accuracy under variability and noise.

Method: Analyze BRB model robustness, propose three robustness constraint strategies to optimize BRB fault diagnosis, and validate on two real-world datasets (WD615 engine, CWRU bearings).

Result: The proposed BRB method with robustness constraints improves both accuracy and robustness in fault diagnosis tasks.

Conclusion: Incorporating systematic robustness analysis and constraint-based optimization into BRB fault diagnosis yields more reliable performance in the presence of sensor uncertainty.

Abstract: In equipment operation, the implementation of fault diagnosis is essential to ensure the continuity and safety of production equipment, improve operational efficiency and reduce maintenance costs. Since sensor readings are widely used for fault diagnosis, their reliability directly affects the results of fault diagnosis. A new fault diagnosis method is proposed to address the two problems of robustness assessment and robustness optimization of fault diagnosis models. For this purpose, a reliable fault diagnosis method based on a belief rule base (BRB) considering robustness analysis is proposed. Firstly, the robustness analysis of the BRB model is carried out systematically. Secondly, three robustness constraint strategies are proposed to optimize the robustness of the BRB fault diagnosis model. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed model is verified by taking the fault diagnosis of WD615 diesel engine and Case Western Reserve University bearings as an example, and the experiments show that the proposed model improves both accuracy and robustness.

</details>


### [302] [Cross-Modal Knowledge Distillation without Paired Data: Theoretical Foundation and Algorithm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10504)
*Trong Khiem Tran,Anh Duc Chu,Quang Hung Pham,Phi Le Nguyen,Trong Nghia Hoang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A cross-modal distillation framework that learns from unpaired data by aligning teacher-student feature and label distributions, with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical gains across multimodal benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CMKD typically relies on paired, aligned multi-modal data, which is costly to obtain. The work targets the more realistic, unpaired setting by adopting a distributional perspective.

Method: Defines a cross-modal distributional relationship and identifies two governing quantities—feature alignment (representation-level) and label alignment (prediction-level). Proposes a principled distillation framework that aligns distributions rather than samples, with theoretical guarantees.

Result: Extensive experiments show significant improvements over prior work in both unpaired and paired data settings across diverse multimodal benchmarks.

Conclusion: Distribution-level cross-modal distillation is effective and practical for settings lacking paired data, bridging modality gaps through principled alignment of representations and predictions.

Abstract: Cross-modal knowledge distillation (CMKD) studies how a (large) teacher model trained on one type of data (e.g., images) can guide a (smaller) student model building on another type of data (e.g., text/audio). Existing CMKD methods often require paired multi-modal data with aligned semantics, but obtaining such paired data are often costly and impractical. To mitigate this limitation, we develop a new CMKD framework for the more challenging setting where paired data are unavailable. In particular, we establish a cross-modal distributional relationship between teacher and student models, which reveals two fundamental quantities governing effective distillation: feature alignment and label alignment. These quantities characterize semantic discrepancy between modalities at the levels of representation and prediction distributions, respectively. Motivated by this insight, we propose a principled framework, with theoretical guarantees, that enables effective cross-modal knowledge distillation by aligning distributions rather than individual samples. Extensive experiments across a wide range of multimodal benchmarks show that our framework is highly effective in both unpaired and paired data settings, improving significantly over prior work.

</details>


### [303] [HIPIF: Hierarchical Planning and Information Folding for Long-Horizon LLM Agent Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10507)
*Juncheng Diao,Zhicong Lu,Peiguang Li,Yongwei Zhou,Changyuan Tian,Qingbin Li,Rongxiang Weng,Jingang Wang,Xunliang Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HIPIF introduces Hierarchical Planning and Information Folding to tackle long-context interference in long-horizon LLM agents by structuring tasks around explicit subgoals, folding completed histories, and using hierarchical reflection with subgoal-focused rewards; validated on three agentic benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs in long-horizon, multi-turn agent tasks suffer from degraded performance due to growing histories that obscure the global task state. Existing approaches (fine-grained credit assignment, hierarchical RL) fail to directly address long-context interference. A human-inspired solution combining subgoal decomposition and progress summarization is needed to stabilize planning and execution.

Method: Propose HIPIF: end-to-end training that organizes long-horizon execution around explicit subgoals while folding completed subgoal histories to reduce long-context interference. Implement hierarchical reflection and subgoal-oriented process rewards to guide subgoal generation, transition, and execution without relying on extra models or task-specific expert data.

Result: Extensive experiments on three publicly available agentic benchmarks demonstrate the validity of HIPIF, showing improved handling of long-horizon tasks under long-context conditions.

Conclusion: HIPIF provides a scalable, end-to-end framework for stabilizing subgoal-based planning and execution in long-horizon LLM agents by combining hierarchical planning, information folding, and reflective rewards, validated across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities as autonomous agents across a wide range of tasks, their performance often degrades in multi-turn long-horizon agentic tasks. Existing methods have made progress through fine-grained credit assignment to alleviate long-horizon sparse rewards and hierarchical reinforcement learning to decompose tasks and reduce long-term dependency. However, these methods still do not directly address long-context interference, in which continuously growing histories weaken the agent's ability to track the global task state and impair subsequent reasoning and decision-making. Inspired by the way humans handle complex tasks through subgoal decomposition and completed progress summarization, we propose Hierarchical Planning and Information Folding (HIPIF) for long-horizon LLM agent learning. HIPIF trains the agent end-to-end to organize long-horizon execution around explicit subgoals while folding completed subgoal histories to reduce long-context interference. Furthermore, to stabilize subgoal-based planning and execution, HIPIF combines hierarchical reflection and subgoal-oriented process rewards to guide subgoal generation, transition, and execution, without relying on costly auxiliary models or task-specific expert trajectories. Extensive experiments on three publicly available agentic benchmarks demonstrate the validity of our method.

</details>


### [304] [ActiveMem: Distributed Active Memory for Long-Horizon LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10532)
*Yunhan Jiang,Wenbin Duan,Shasha Guo,Liang Pang,Xiaoqian Sun,Huawei Shen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ActiveMem decouples memory from reasoning by employing a high-level planner and a distributed active memory, yielding state-of-the-art accuracy with lower overhead on long-horizon tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Centralized memory in LLM agents causes a trade-off between context size and information loss. Inspired by the prefrontal cortex–hippocampus divide in humans, ActiveMem aims to mitigate this bottleneck by separating memory management from reasoning.

Method: Introduce a heterogeneous framework where a high-level Planner uses distilled semantic gists for reasoning while a lightweight, distributed memory system actively accumulates and consolidates these gists in parallel throughout the task.

Result: ActiveMem achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with significantly reduced overhead on BrowseComp-Plus and GAIA benchmarks, demonstrating the effectiveness of distributed active memory for long-horizon reasoning.

Conclusion: Decoupling memory from reasoning via distributed active memory improves long-horizon reasoning performance and efficiency, avoiding the pitfalls of centralized memory architectures.

Abstract: Memory is essential for enabling large language model (LLM) agents to handle long-horizon reasoning tasks. Existing memory mechanisms are largely centralized, typically organizing retrieved information and interaction history within a single model context. This design imposes a fundamental trade-off: scaling reasoning trajectories risks context overload, whereas aggressive content pruning may result in irreversible information loss. Seeking a better trade-off, we draw inspiration from human cognitive systems, especially the functional complementarity between the prefrontal cortex (executive control) and the hippocampus (memory management), suggesting that such a trade-off need not be inherent, but may instead stem from centralized memory organization. To this end, we propose ActiveMem, a heterogeneous framework that decouples agent memory from the core reasoning process. Specifically, a high-level Planner utilizes distilled semantic gists to execute reasoning, while a lightweight, distributed memory system operates in parallel to actively accumulate and consolidate these gists throughout the task. Experiments on BrowseComp-Plus and GAIA show that ActiveMem achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with significantly reduced overhead, demonstrating the effectiveness of distributed active memory for long-horizon reasoning.

</details>


### [305] [One Token per Multimodal Evidence: Latent Memory for Resource-Constrained QA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10572)
*Zhi Zheng,Ziqiao Meng,Hao Luan,Wei Liu,Wee Sun Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Latent Memory replaces raw evidence with latent tokens generated by a small compressor LLM/VLM; enables retrieval and generation in latent space, reducing token usage while maintaining QA performance; trained with reconstruction, contrastive, and distillation objectives; competitive on text QA and best on WebQA image-grounded QA; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
