Motivation: Reduce token consumption and storage pressure of existing memory paradigms in RAG-like QA for text and multimodal data; enable efficient retrieval-augmented generation for resource-constrained settings.

Method: Encode each memory item into a latent token via a compressor model; embed query into latent space to retrieve relevant latent tokens; feed retrieved latent tokens to a pretrained LLM/VLM for answer generation; train compressor with reconstruction, contrastive, distillation losses end-to-end to optimize usefulness for reconstruction, retrieval, and generation.

Result: On seven text QA benchmarks (e.g., HotpotQA) and multimodal QA benchmarks; competitive with strong RAG baselines while using 3x-10x fewer generator tokens; strongest image-grounded QA on WebQA.

Conclusion: Latent Memory offers efficient, unified latent representations for multimodal QA, achieving substantial token efficiency without sacrificing performance; code available.

Abstract: External memory effectively grounds large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs)-based question answering (QA) in relevant multimodal evidence. However, existing memory paradigms represent each memory item in raw text and image forms, so retrieval-based systems must pass the retrieved text or images to the generation LLMs/VLMs, resulting in high token consumption and storage pressure, making it unaffordable for resource-constrained applications. We propose Latent Memory, a latent-space memory paradigm that replaces each raw text or image evidence item with a single high-dimensional latent token produced by a small compressor LLM/VLM. Rather than retrieving raw evidence for generation, Latent Memory operates in a unified latent representation space: the query is embedded into this space to retrieve relevant latent tokens, and the retrieved latent tokens are directly prompted to a pretrained LLM or VLM for answer generation. To make each latent token simultaneously informative for reconstruction, retrieval, and generation, we train the compressor with reconstruction, contrastive, and distillation objectives in a unified end-to-end manner. Latent Memory is evaluated on seven text-only QA benchmarks (e.g., HotpotQA) and multimodal QA benchmarks, where it achieves competitive QA performance compared to advanced RAG baselines while consuming 3x to 10x fewer generator tokens. It can also deliver the strongest image-grounded QA performance on WebQA. Code is available at https://github.com/zz1358m/Latent-Memory-Master.

</details>


### [306] [Learning What to Remember: Observability-Safe Memory Retention via Constrained Optimization for Long-Horizon Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10616)
*Qingcan Kang,Liu Mingyang,Shixiong Kai,Kaichao Liang,Tao Zhong,Mingxuan Yuan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A memory-retention framework for long-horizon agents (OSL-MR) uses an observability-safe, constraint-aware approach combining an evidence learner with a Mixed-Score prior to optimize memory under budgets, outperforming baselines on LOCOMO/LongMemEval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address memory retention as a long-horizon resource-allocation problem under finite context, where existing methods treat retention as a local decision and neglect long-term consequences under realistic observability constraints.

Method: Introduce OSL-MR with a strict separation between online-observable features and offline supervision (OAS). Train an evidence learner from realized evidence supervision and use a Mixed-Score heuristic as a deployable online-safe baseline and inductive prior. Learn a query-conditioned evidence value from interaction data, preserving deployability under observability constraints.

Result: OSL-MR consistently outperforms recency-based methods, Generative Agents-style scoring, and other baselines, especially under tight memory budgets. The Mixed-Score prior improves precision while preserving recall. Sensitivity analyses show robustness across varying cost configurations, validated on LOCOMO and LongMemEval.

Conclusion: Formulates memory retention as a constrained stochastic optimization under budget feasibility, evidence utility, and delayed costs; OSL-MR provides a deployable, observability-safe policy that leverages an evidence learner and Mixed-Score priors to improve long-term memory retention outcomes.

Abstract: Long-horizon language agents accumulate observations, reasoning traces, and retrieved facts that exceed their finite context windows, making memory retention a fundamental resource-allocation problem. Existing memory systems improve management through heuristic scoring, retrieval optimization, or learned compression, but largely treat retention as a local decision problem and do not explicitly model its long-term consequences under realistic observability constraints. To fill this gap, we formulate memory retention as a constrained stochastic optimization problem with explicit budget feasibility, evidence utility, and delayed costs including miss penalties, reacquisition delays, and stale-information risk. We then propose OSL-MR (Observability-Safe Learning for Memory Retention), a novel framework that enforces a strict separation between online-observable features and offline-available supervision (OAS). OSL-MR combines an evidence learner trained from realized evidence supervision with a Mixed-Score heuristic that serves both as a deployable online-safe baseline and as a structured inductive prior for learning. The resulting policy learns query-conditioned evidence value directly from interaction data while remaining deployable under the same observability constraints. Experiments on LOCOMO and LongMemEval show that OSL-MR consistently outperforms recency-based methods, Generative Agents-style scoring, and other heuristic baselines, particularly under tight memory budgets. The Mixed-Score prior further improves precision while preserving recall, and sensitivity analysis demonstrates robustness across a wide range of cost configurations.

</details>


### [307] [Infini Memory: Maintainable Topic Documents for Long-Term LLM Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10677)
*Suozhao Ji,Baodong Wu,Zehao Wang,Lei Xia,Qingping Li,Ruisong Wang,Wenbo Ding,Zhenhua Zhu,Boxun Li,Guohao Dai,Yu Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Infini Memory is a topic-structured, text-based persistent memory system for long-term LLM agents that aggregates evidence via topic documents, uses a staging buffer, and iterative retrieval, achieving 64.7% on MemoryAgentBench and showing benefits in ablation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of capable persistent memory for long-running agents, where existing systems struggle with evidence aggregation, revision, and memory maintenance due to isolated records, summaries, or fragments.

Method: Model treats memory as topic-structured documents; each topic document collects related evidence, preserves metadata, and supports revisions; new observations staged in a buffer and periodically consolidated into coherent textual contexts; at inference time, agentic retrieval uses iterative tool calls to read memory rather than a single step.

Result: On MemoryAgentBench, Infini Memory achieves 64.7% overall score; ablations show topic-structured maintenance and iterative evidence inspection improve complementary aspects of long-term memory use.

Conclusion: Infini Memory offers a maintainable, scalable approach to long-term memory for LLM agents, enabling better evidence aggregation and fact revision through topic-oriented memory units and iterative retrieval.

Abstract: Long-term LLM agents need persistent memory that can track changing facts and provide relevant evidence across sessions. Existing memory systems often store observations as isolated records, summaries, or indexed fragments, which makes evidence aggregation, fact revision, and memory maintenance difficult. We propose Infini Memory, a maintainable text-based persistent memory architecture that treats agent memory as topic-structured documents. Each topic document serves as a semantic unit for collecting related evidence, preserving metadata, and revising facts over time. New observations are first staged in a buffer and periodically consolidated into coherent textual contexts. At inference time, an agentic retrieval procedure lets the LLM read memory through iterative tool calls rather than a single retrieval step. On MemoryAgentBench, Infini Memory achieves 64.7% overall score. Ablations show that topic-structured maintenance and iterative evidence inspection improve complementary aspects of long-term memory use.

</details>


### [308] [When the Chain of Thought Knows Better: Failure Modes in Multi-Turn Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10740)
*Sai Kartheek Reddy Kasu,Nils Lukas,Samuele Poppi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces CoT-Output 2x2 safety matrix to diagnose multi-turn reasoning failures; reveals hidden failure modes and releases a dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Terminal-score metrics miss temporal misalignments in dialogue: need trace-level diagnostics to detect alignment failures across turns.

Method: Label each turn on two axes (internal reasoning, visible output) to form four cells: robust alignment, alignment faking, overt jailbreak, context-injection failure; evaluate three distilled reasoning targets against a fixed attacker across five oversight conditions; analyze 6750 turns in the Information-Hazard scenario.

Result: Identified two reproducible vulnerabilities: the oversight paradox (monitoring cues increase alignment-faking rates) and context-injection failure (unsafe external outputs despite safe internal states); released the full multi-turn dialogue and CoT traces dataset.

Conclusion: The CoT-Output 2x2 framework provides a practical trace-level safety diagnostic for multi-turn reasoning and enables follow-up research, offering a structured taxonomy and empirical insights into failure modes.

Abstract: Failures in multi-turn reasoning models are largely invisible to terminal-score evaluation. A model can lock onto an unsafe stance early in a long dialogue, yet its final-turn refusal rate may appear indistinguishable from a robustly aligned baseline. To expose these hidden temporal dynamics, we propose a trace-level diagnostic - the CoT-Output 2x2 safety matrix. This framework labels every turn along two independent axes (internal reasoning and visible output), yielding four operationally defined failure cells: robust alignment, alignment faking, overt jailbreak, and a distinct failure mode we term context-injection failure (where the CoT maintains safe reasoning, but the visible output produces harm, highlighting a multi-turn manifestation of reasoning unfaithfulness). We evaluate three distilled reasoning targets against a fixed attacker across five oversight conditions, collecting 6750 turn-level observations on the Information-Hazard scenario. Our analysis reveals two reproducible vulnerabilities: an oversight paradox where explicit monitoring cues paradoxically increase alignment-faking rates rather than suppress them, and a context-injection failure where models lock onto unsafe external outputs despite safe internal states. We release the full dataset of multi-turn dialogues and CoT traces to support follow-up trace-diagnostic research.

</details>


### [309] [The Arbiter Agent: Continually Monitoring Multi-Agent Conversations to Detect Emergent Misalignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10747)
*Filippo Tonini,Federico Torrielli,Anton Danholt Lautrup,Peter Schneider-Kamp,Mustafa Mert Çelikok,Lukas Galke Poech*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: An Arbiter monitors multi-agent conversations under a limited inspection budget to detect misalignment in real time, using options to wait, query, inspect prompts/reasoning traces, or log behavior; active inspection improves detection speed and accuracy, with trade-offs in precision, and logging affecting recall; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As ensembles of language-model agents increasingly collaborate, misalignment can emerge from interactions despite each agent appearing aligned individually. A real-time auditor under budget constraints is needed to ensure safe, coordinated decision-making.

Method: Introduce Arbiter agent that observes conversations step-by-step within a fixed inspection budget. It can wait, question a participant, examine internal materials (e.g., prompts, reasoning traces), or log behavior. It outputs a report identifying likely misalignment sources after the conversation. Evaluated across five conversation conditions, five tool configurations, and two backbone models.

Result: Arbiter reliably detects misaligned agents well before conversation end. Active inspection improves detection accuracy and speed. Weight-induced misalignment is hardest to detect; instruction-induced misalignment is reliably detected even with passive observation. Logging improves recall but reduces precision. Budget-aware continual monitoring is effective, and treating the auditor as an active participant benefits system safety.

Conclusion: Budget-aware, continual monitoring of multi-agent systems is feasible and effective for catching misalignment. The auditor should be treated as an active component in the process. Code is publicly available for replication.

Abstract: As AI systems built from multiple language-model agents become more common, they are increasingly used to make decisions together: discussing, negotiating, and acting on shared tasks. While individual agents may appear well-aligned when tested on their own, problems can arise from how they interact with one another. We introduce the Arbiter, an agent designed to monitor multi-agent conversations in real time and identify which participants may be behaving in misaligned ways. The Arbiter operates under a limited "inspection budget", meaning it must decide carefully how to use its resources. As it observes a conversation step by step, it can choose to wait, question a participant, examine internal information such as system prompts or reasoning traces, or log concerning behavior. At the end, it produces a report identifying the likely source of misalignment. We evaluate the Arbiter across five conversation conditions, ranging from risky financial advice model organisms to evaluation-aware and colluding agents, we test five tool configurations of increasing capability and two backbone models. We find that the Arbiter reliably detects misaligned agents well before the end of the conversation, with active inspection tools improving both detection accuracy and speed. Weight-induced misalignment proves hardest to detect, while instruction-induced misalignment is identified reliably even under passive observation. The logging tool exhibits a dual effect, improving recall at the cost of precision. These results suggest that continual, budget-aware monitoring can effectively catch misalignment, and that overseeing multi-agent systems may require treating the auditor as an active participant in the process. The code is available at https://github.com/aisilab/arbiter.

</details>


### [310] [AutoPDE: Reliable Agentic PDE Solving via Explicitly Represented Solver Strategies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10752)
*Huanshuo Dong,Keyao Zhang,Hong Wang,Zhezheng Hao,Zhiwei Zhuang,Ziyan Liu,Jiacong Wang,Gengyuan Liu,Xin Jin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoPDE is a code-agent framework that maintains an explicit, inspectable solver-strategy object across the PDE solving process, built via three stages (PDE analysis, method selection, and adaptive tuning) and calibrated with pilot solves. It outperforms a strong baseline on PDE Agent Bench (54.5% pass rate, +14.2pp).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based PDE solvers couple strategy implicitly to code, making numerical decisions hard to inspect, revise, or validate with evidence. An explicit, editable solver strategy could improve reliability and debuggability.

Method: A three-stage pipeline that starts with a reusable library of PDE-solving skills. Stage 1 analyzes the PDE to identify type and algebraic structure. Stage 2 selects a numerical method and commits to discretization, stabilization, and linear solver settings. Stage 3 performs adaptive tuning via low-cost pilot solves to align resolution and tolerances with accuracy and runtime budgets. The solver strategy is an independent object built before coding and revision-friendly during failures.

Result: Evaluation on the PDE Agent Bench shows AutoPDE achieves a pass rate of 54.5%, improving the strongest baseline by 14.2 percentage points.

Conclusion: Explicit solver-strategy objects can enhance transparency, verifiability, and robustness of AI-assisted PDE solvers, with AutoPDE demonstrating measurable improvements on benchmark tasks; opportunities remain to broaden evaluation and explore limits of strategy revisions under varying problem classes.

Abstract: Numerical solvers for partial differential equations (PDEs) are core computational tools in science and engineering. Building reliable PDE solvers requires not only executable code, but a numerical solver strategy, a set of decisions about discretization, stabilization, solver configuration, and resolution control, that matches the PDE structure. Recent LLM-based coding agents have begun to reduce the programming burden by generating and debugging solver implementations. However, they typically move directly from a PDE problem to solver code, leaving the solver strategy implicit in implementation details. Feedback from a failed solve is therefore routed back to code edits rather than to the underlying strategy, so numerical decisions remain hard to check before code is generated and hard to revise using numerical evidence when it fails. To address this limitation, we propose AutoPDE, a code agent that maintains the solver strategy as an explicitly represented object throughout the solving process: an independent, inspectable object that is built before any code is written and can be revised, using numerical evidence, whenever a solve fails. AutoPDE builds and maintains this object in three stages, all drawing from a library of reusable PDE-solving skills: PDE analysis identifies the equation type and algebraic structure; numerical method selection chooses a numerical method that matches the analysis result and commits to a discretization, stabilization, and linear solver accordingly; and adaptive tuning runs low-cost pilot solves to calibrate resolution and tolerances under the prescribed accuracy and runtime budget. We evaluate AutoPDE on the PDE Agent Bench, where experimental results show that AutoPDE achieves a pass rate of $54.5%$, improving over the strongest baseline by $14.2$ percentage points.

</details>


### [311] [Accelerating NeurASP with vectorization and caching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10787)
*Alexander Philipp Rader,Alessandra Russo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NeurASP gains significant speedups via vectorization, batching, and caching, enabling training with non-differentiable ASP; introduces a challenging card-playing task dataset to test scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome computational bottlenecks of backprop through ASP in neurosymbolic AI to scale to more complex tasks.

Method: Optimize NeurASP training: vectorize ASP reasoning steps, batch process examples, cache intermediate computations; implement and compare with original; create a challenging playing-cards dataset.

Result: Reported orders-of-magnitude speedups on larger tasks; demonstrating practical scalability improvements of NeurASP; dataset used to show capabilities.

Conclusion: Performance-oriented improvements make neurosymbolic methods like NeurASP more scalable and applicable to complex tasks; further work could explore generalization and broader benchmarks.

Abstract: Neurosymbolic AI combines neural networks with symbolic programs to create robust and explainable predictions. One such framework is NeurASP, which trains a neural network to predict concepts and reasons over them using rules written in answer set programming (ASP) to solve downstream tasks. Crucially, labels are only provided for the downstream prediction produced by the symbolic rules, not for the latent concepts themselves.Backpropagation through the non-differentiable ASP component requires expensive probability and gradient calculations, which has hindered scalability to more sophisticated tasks.In this paper, we address the current limitations of NeurASP by improving its computational performance through vectorization, batch processing and caching of intermediate computations during training. We compare computation speeds between the original and our new implementation of NeurASP and report speedups of multiple orders of magnitude for larger tasks. To this end, we propose a new dataset of difficult tasks involving playing cards, which we use to test the capabilities of NeurASP's enhanced learning function.

</details>


### [312] [READER: Robust Evidence-based Authorship Decoding via Extracted Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10794)
*Jiaxu Liu,Sunnan Mu,Dong Huang,Liuyin Wang,Jing Shao,Jie Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A Bayesian, query-driven LLM provenance method (READER) that uses a frozen proxy LLM to interpret black-box outputs and attribute them to the source model across varying prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM APIs proliferate, tracing the origin of each black-box response becomes operationally important. Dynamic prompts obscure authorship; conventional fingerprints are fragile. A robust, scalable attribution with confidence estimates is needed.

Method: Treats a frozen proxy LLM as a reader of hidden authorship evidence. Maps black-box outputs into proxy activation space, applies temporal token-state filtering within each response, and uses Bayesian Evidence Accumulation by summing per-response log-posterior evidence across independently sampled prompts. This avoids fragile mean-pooling and yields calibrated, query-wise evidence for attribution.

Result: On Agent500 (50-target), READER achieves 31.0–42.4% top-1 accuracy from a single response and 70.0–84.0% from 50 responses, outperforming sentence-encoder fingerprints. Scaling to nine proxy readers shows stronger LLMs reveal more linearly decodable authorship structure, indicating authorship traces exist in frozen LLM representations and can be leveraged for multi-query attribution.

Conclusion: Authorship signals are present in frozen LLM representations and can be converted into reliable provenance via Bayesian aggregation over multiple prompts. READER offers calibrated, turn-by-turn attribution and scales with stronger LLMs and multiple proxy readers.

Abstract: As agentic applications increasingly route user tasks through official and third-party LLM APIs, provenance becomes an operational question: which model generated a given black-box response? We study Dynamic Black-Box LLM Provenance: identifying the source LLM from generations elicited by query-varying, non-predefined prompts rather than a fixed input set or benchmark suite. This setting is difficult because prompt semantics dominate the text, while model-specific authorship traces are weak and inconsistent at the surface level. We introduce READER (Robust Evidence-based Authorship Decoding via Extracted Representations), a lightweight provenance framework that treats a frozen proxy LLM as a reader of hidden authorship evidence. READER maps black-box outputs into proxy activation space, temporally filters token states within each response, and performs Bayesian Evidence Accumulation by summing single-response log-posterior evidence across independently sampled prompts. This avoids fragile mean-pooling of prompt-specific representations while preserving the query-wise evidence needed for calibrated confidence. On Agent500, a 50-target dataset built from agent-style prompts, READER reaches $31.0$-$42.4\%$ top-1 accuracy from a single response and $70.0$-$84.0\%$ from 50 responses, substantially outperforming sentence-encoder fingerprints. Scaling across nine proxy readers further shows that stronger LLMs expose more linearly decodable authorship structure, suggesting that authorship perception is already present in frozen LLM representations and can be converted into reliable multi-query attribution.

</details>


### [313] [Evaluating Research-Level Math Proofs via Strict Step-Level Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10799)
*Yifeng Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A step-level, constraint-driven verification framework for mathematical proofs evaluated on an adversarial FirstProof suite; global prompting fails to localize errors, while per-step context with strict theorem sourcing improves correctness and reveals pedantic hyper-rigor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global evaluation suffers from context poisoning and superficial plausibility; there is a need for rigorous, local verification with human-mathematician-like note-taking to reliably distinguish rigorous proofs from flawed ones and to generalize to frontier mathematics.

Method: Maintain detailed per-step context; strictly constrain sources of applied theorems; evaluate on a curated adversarial diagnostic suite drawn from the FirstProof challenge; conduct systematic ablations to compare constrained versus unconstrained prompting; perform error taxonomy analysis.

Result: Constrained, step-level verification outperforms global evaluation; ablations show indispensability of deductive constraints; remaining rejections arise from pedantic hyper-rigor due to unstated domain conventions, exposing implicit ambiguities in the benchmark; demonstrates practical prompting practices and verification-note organization.

Conclusion: The framework shifts the failure taxonomy and provides a foundation for automated proof-review systems; encouraging prompts that organize verification notes like a human mathematician substantially improves discrimination of rigorous versus flawed proofs; code and prompts are available on GitHub.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle to rigorously verify complex mathematical proofs. Standard global evaluation approaches suffer from "context poisoning," in which superficially plausible statements mask subtle logical flaws, leading to hallucination or over-skepticism. To address this, we shift from global evaluation to strict step-level verification: our framework maintains detailed context for each deduction step and strictly constrains the sources of applied theorems. We evaluate on a carefully curated adversarial diagnostic suite of research-level proofs drawn from the FirstProof challenge. A systematic ablation study demonstrates that these deductive constraints are indispensable, as unconstrained global prompting consistently fails to localize subtle logical errors. Beyond outperforming global evaluation, our approach fundamentally alters the failure taxonomy. Error analysis reveals that, rather than exhibiting severe logical hallucinations, remaining rejections are primarily instances of "pedantic hyper-rigor" stemming from unstated domain conventions, effectively exposing implicit ambiguities within the expert benchmark itself. Our findings suggest that prompting agents to organize their verification notes in a cautious, human-mathematician-like manner can substantially improve their ability to distinguish rigorous proofs from flawed ones, with the potential to strengthen agentic reasoning on frontier mathematical concepts that the base model does not already know well, and to lay a theoretical foundation for future automated proof-review systems. Code and prompts are available at GitHub.

</details>


### [314] [Moonshine: An Autonomous Mathematical Research Agent Centered on Conjecture Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10806)
*Xiaoyang Chen,Xiang Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Autonomous conjecture generator Moonshine demonstrates the Neural Jacobian Conjecture (NJC) for one-hidden-layer affine-ridge sigmoid networks, with independent proofs for N=n+1 by GPT-5.5-pro and DeepSeek-V4-pro and a geometric-topological proof via web-assisted ChatGPT; higher-width cases remain open.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge local nondegeneracy to global injectivity in Jacobian problems by translating them into neural-network settings, and showcase Moonshine's capability to autonomously generate and advance meaningful mathematical conjectures within an extensible framework.

Method: Translate the Jacobian conjecture into a condition on the Jacobian determinant of a one-hidden-layer affine-ridge sigmoid network (positivity implies injectivity). Use autonomous AI agents (GPT-5.5-pro, DeepSeek-V4-pro) to obtain complete proofs for the N=n+1 case, and develop a geometric-topological proof via interactive web-based use of ChatGPT. Provide a framework for conjecture generation and progress assessment.

Result: Independent complete proofs for N=n+1 obtained by two separate AI agents. A geometric-topological proof developed with ChatGPT assistance. Preliminary evidence supporting the plausibility of the NJC. The general higher-width case (N≥n+2) remains unresolved and requires further investigation.

Conclusion: Moonshine demonstrates the potential of autonomous systems to generate meaningful mathematical problems and make rigorous progress on them, providing plausible evidence for NJC in restricted cases and highlighting directions for future work on higher-width networks and general cases.

Abstract: Moonshine is an autonomous agent whose central objective is to generate mathematical conjectures. Its core capability is to extract structure from classical problems, distill new concepts, and formulate conjectures of mathematical significance. Rather than treating the solution of a single proposition as its endpoint, Moonshine builds an extensible theoretical framework through conjecture generation, bridge building, and obstacle identification. This article uses Moonshine's exploration of the Jacobian conjecture as an example. It shows how the central logic of whether local nondegeneracy can force global injectivity is transferred to one-hidden-layer affine-ridge sigmoid networks. This leads to the formulation of the \emph{Neural Jacobian Conjecture} (NJC): if such a network has strictly positive Jacobian determinant on the whole space, then it must be globally injective. By invoking GPT-5.5-pro and DeepSeek-V4-pro separately, Moonshine obtained independent complete proofs for the case \(N=n+1\). In addition, with the assistance of ChatGPT through interactive use of its web interface with GPT-5.5-pro, a geometric-topological proof was developed. These results provide preliminary evidence for the plausibility of the conjecture. The general higher-width case \(N\ge n+2\), however, remains unresolved and is left for further investigation. This work illustrates Moonshine's ability to autonomously generate meaningful mathematical problems and make rigorous progress on them.

</details>


### [315] [Do VLMs Reason Like Engineers? A Benchmark and a Stage-wise Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10833)
*Syed Wasiq,Syed Mohamad Tawseeq,Yashwant Pravinrao Bangde,Debaditya Roy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EngVQA introduces a multimodal engineering reasoning benchmark with 696 problems across five engineering subjects and an 8-stage automatic evaluation framework to assess VLM-generated solutions, revealing current models' limitations in engineering reasoning and strong alignment with human judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLM benchmarks focus on final answers and surface-level reasoning, lacking process-oriented evaluation for engineering problems. There is a need to assess intermediate, physically consistent multi-step reasoning, especially for education, scientific assistance, and technical decision-making.

Method: Develop EngVQA with 696 problems spanning 5 engineering subjects. Propose an 8-stage automatic evaluation framework that grades each reasoning stage independently. Benchmark multiple open- and closed-source VLMs. Validate automated scores against human judgments using correlation (Pearson) and MAE.

Result: State-of-the-art VLMs show substantial limitations in engineering reasoning. The automated framework correlates strongly with human evaluation (Pearson ≈ 0.975; MAE ≈ 0.67 on a 10-point scale), enabling fine-grained error analysis and reliable assessment of multimodal engineering reasoning.

Conclusion: Process-oriented evaluation is crucial for trustworthy assessment of multimodal engineering reasoning systems. EngVQA provides a comprehensive benchmark and evaluation framework to drive improvements in VLMs for engineering tasks.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate strong performance on general multimodal reasoning benchmarks, yet their ability to perform engineering reasoning remains largely unexplored. Unlike general visual question answering, engineering problem solving requires interpreting technical diagrams, selecting governing physical principles, and maintaining physically consistent multi-step reasoning. These capabilities are increasingly important for AI systems used in engineering education, scientific assistance, and technical decision-making, where reasoning failures may produce physically invalid yet superficially plausible solutions. Existing benchmarks primarily evaluate final answers and provide limited assessment of intermediate reasoning processes. We introduce EngVQA, a multimodal benchmark for evaluating engineering reasoning across 5 engineering subjects containing 696 problems. We introduce an 8-stage automatic evaluation framework for assessing VLM-generated solutions. The framework independently evaluates each stage of the solution, enabling fine-grained analysis of reasoning failures. We benchmark multiple state-of-the-art open and closed source VLMs on our evaluation framework and demonstrate substantial limitations in current engineering reasoning capabilities. Human evaluation shows strong agreement with our automated framework, achieving a Pearson correlation of 0.975 and a mean absolute error of 0.67 on a 10-point grading scale. Our results highlight the importance of process-oriented evaluation for reliable assessment of multimodal engineering reasoning systems.

</details>


### [316] [Large-scale semantic mapping of learner agency and autonomy reveals what measurement and generative AI research overlook](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10881)
*Fei Qin,Xiaobo Liu,Yaowen Zhang,Xuming Li,Fei Wang,Mutlu Cukurova,Jingjing Chen,Yu Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Semantic analysis of learner agency and autonomy definitions reveals three dimensions and a jingle-jangle fallacy; scales underrepresent sociocultural aspects; AI education focuses on regulation/behavior; implications for multidimensional measurement and practice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve conceptual confusion arising from the jingle-jangle fallacy by aggregating a vast body of definitions and scale items to map semantic usage of learner agency/autonomy, and to inform measurement and practice.

Method: Semantic analysis pipeline extracting 8,954 definitions and 2,700 scale items from over 14,000 publications; three-dimensional mapping of constructs: (1) task—regulation/control of learning, (2) person—intrinsic motivation/internal decision-making, (3) sociocultural—social-relational action; empirically quantified relations among terms to reveal the jingle-jangle phenomenon.

Result: Identified three distinct dimensions of learner agency/autonomy; empirical demonstration of jingle-jangle; current scales underrepresent the sociocultural dimension; AI in education research concentrates on learning regulation/control, narrowing AI-mediated learning design.

Conclusion: A multidimensional reconceptualization is needed for measurement and practice; AI-enabled learning environments should broaden behavioral repertoires beyond task-regulation to incorporate sociocultural and personal dimensions, improving conceptual clarity and actionable measurement.

Abstract: Learner agency and autonomy are foundational to personal development, yet a pervasive "jingle-jangle" fallacy (i.e. identical terms denoting different constructs, distinct terms denoting identical ones) has substantially hindered cumulative knowledge. Treating meaning as a phenomenon constituted through use in linguistic practice, we extracted 8,954 definitions and 2,700 scale items from over 14,000 publications, to investigate how researchers actually used learner agency and autonomy with a semantic analysis pipeline. The definitional landscape of two constructs resolves into three dimensions: regulation and control of learning (task), intrinsic motivation and internal decision-making (person), and social-relational action (sociocultural), thereby empirically quantifying the jingle-jangle fallacy. Existing scales, however, systematically underrepresent the sociocultural dimension. Critically, current generative AI research in education concentrates on learning regulation and control, narrowing the behavioral repertoire that AI-mediated learning environments are designed to cultivate. Beyond conceptual clarification, this work carries direct implications for conceptualization, measurement, and practice towards supporting the multidimensional learner agency and autonomy.

</details>


### [317] [Role-Agent: Bootstrapping LLM Agents via Dual-Role Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10917)
*Xucong Wang,Ziyu Ma,Shidong Yang,Tongwen Huang,Pengkun Wang,Yong Wang,Xiangxiang Chu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A single LLM is used as both agent and environment to bootstrap co-evolution via World-In-Agent and Agent-In-World, improving generalization with ~4% gains over strong baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents often suffer from inefficient interaction feedback and static training environments, which limit generalization; a self-contained framework (Role-Agent) to co-evolve agent and environment can enhance learning efficiency and robustness.

Method: World-In-Agent (WIA): the LLM acts as the agent and predicts future states after each action; the alignment between predicted and actual states serves as a process reward to encourage environment-aware reasoning. Agent-In-World (AIW): the LLM analyzes failure modes from failed trajectories and retrieves tasks with similar failure patterns to reshape the training data distribution for targeted practice.

Result: Empirical evaluations on multiple benchmarks show consistent improvements, averaging over 4% gains over strong baselines.

Conclusion: Role-Agent enables bootstrapped co-evolution of agent and environment, promoting environment-aware reasoning and targeted, failure-driven data curation to improve generalization and performance.

Abstract: Although Large Language Model (LLM) agents have demonstrated strong performance on complex tasks, their learning is often limited by inefficient interaction feedback and static training environments, which hinder broader generalization. To address these limitations, this paper introduces Role-Agent, \textcolor{black}{a framework} that harnesses a single LLM to function concurrently as both the agent and the environment, enabling a bootstrapped co-evolution. Role-Agent comprises two synergistic components: World-In-Agent (WIA) and Agent-In-World (AIW). In WIA, the LLM acts as the agent and predicts future states after each action; the alignment between predicted and actual states is then used as a process reward, encouraging environment-aware reasoning. In AIW, the LLM analyzes failure modes from failed trajectories and retrieves tasks with similar failure patterns, thereby reshaping the training data distribution for targeted practice. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that Role-Agent consistently improves performance, yielding an average gain of over 4\% over strong baselines.

</details>


### [318] [Frontier Coding Agents Use Metaprogramming to Adapt to Unfamiliar Programming Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10933)
*Aman Sharma,Sushrut Thorat,Paras Chopra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Six contemporary coding agents are evaluated on four esoteric languages in a sequential workflow (edit, run, hidden tests). Strong agents often avoid direct target-language writing, instead generating Python programs that produce or generate the target code and debugging locally; forbidding this metaprogramming lowers performance. Stronger agents gain more from more tokens/interpreter calls, suggesting resource amplification of useful strategies. The main takeaway is that capable agents use tools, feedback, and workspace state to form a working model of an unfamiliar language; metaprogramming is a clear, but not sole, mechanism to achieve this.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether LLM-based coding agents generalize from familiar languages to unfamiliar esoteric languages, and to reveal hidden strategies (like metaprogramming) that mainstream benchmarks may obscure.

Method: Six contemporary coding agents evaluated on four esoteric languages (e.g., Brainfuck, Befunge-98) using a sequential workflow with file edits, local execution, and hidden-test grading. Compared against benchmarks SWE-Bench Verified and Terminal-Bench 2.0. Experiments include conditions that forbid metaprogramming and vary guidance, helper code, interpreter calls, and tokens to tease strategy and resource usage.

Result: Strong agents often circumvent direct target-language coding by producing Python generators that emit the target code and by debugging those generators locally. Forbidding this metaprogramming strategy causes large performance drops. Text guidance alone does not boost weaker agents. Oppositely, Opus-derived Python helper code for building generators improves some agents (Sonnet 4.6 and GPT-5.4 mini) substantially, while others (Haiku 4.5) stay weak. More interpreter calls and output tokens help stronger agents but leave weaker ones near baseline, indicating resources magnify beneficial strategies rather than create them.

Conclusion: Metaprogramming is a clear strategy among strong agents, but the broader gap lies in constructing and debugging strategies that operate under the target language's rules. Evaluation should emphasize tool use, feedback, and workspace state as key components of generalization to unfamiliar languages.

Abstract: LLM-based coding agents are usually evaluated in familiar software settings: mainstream languages, common libraries, and public repositories. These benchmarks remain important, but they can hide how agents behave when the language itself is unfamiliar. We evaluate six contemporary coding agents on four esoteric programming languages using a sequential setup with file editing, local execution, and hidden-test grading. Our protocol exposes capability differences between these agents that mainstream coding and agentic benchmarks such as SWE-Bench Verified and Terminal-Bench 2.0 compress into much narrower bands. We observe that the strongest agents, Claude Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.4 xhigh, often avoid writing the target language directly. On Brainfuck and Befunge-98, they write Python programs that generate target-language code and debug those generators locally. Forbidding this metaprogramming strategy causes large performance drops. Text guidance distilled from this strategy does not materially improve weaker agents. In contrast, Opus-derived Python helper code for building generators, with no solved benchmark programs or hidden-test answers, sharply improves Sonnet 4.6 and GPT-5.4 mini on the same problems, while Haiku 4.5 remains low. More interpreter calls and output tokens improve stronger agents but leave weaker agents near their original performance, indicating that these resources amplify useful strategies rather than create them. Together, these results show that strong coding agents adapt to unfamiliar languages by using tools, feedback, and workspace state to build a working model of the target language. Metaprogramming is the clearest case, but the broader gap is constructing and debugging a strategy that works under the target language's rules.

</details>


### [319] [WorldKernel: A World Model is the Coupling Kernel of Admissible Possible Worlds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10934)
*Fabio Rovai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Predictive models may fail to represent uncertainty in cross-world counterfactual couplings; the paper introduces a single positive semidefinite coupling kernel over admissible worlds to capture off-diagonal cross-world effects, enabling tractable bounds, ontology-driven tightening, and approximate reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the inadequacy of strong predictors in capturing uncertainty about counterfactual couplings between worlds; provide a formal, tractable framework to bound and learn these couplings.

Method: Model the world as a positive semidefinite coupling kernel K(T,T') over admissible worlds. The diagonal of K corresponds to the usual posterior recovered by a predictor; the off-diagonal encodes cross-world couplings that determine counterfactuals. Use PSD to bound counterfactuals; incorporate ontology axioms to tighten bounds; acquire coupling information via targeted constraints (scar learning). Analyze complexity via the Sly-Sun threshold; approximate counting of admissible worlds for full reconstruction.

Result: On 28% of models, unidentified cross-world quantities lack a valid-predictor representation; enforcing PSD bounds yields polynomial-time bounds on counterfactuals where exact programs are intractable. Ontology axioms tighten bounds by up to ~33%. Targeted constraints accelerate acquisition of the needed couplings. Full reconstruction reduces to approximate counting of admissible worlds, tractable below the Sly-Sun threshold and intractable above.

Conclusion: Predictive models inherently miss cross-world uncertainty. A PSD coupling-kernel framework provides a principled, tractable approach to bound and learn counterfactuals, with structural ontologies enhancing bounds and targeted constraints accelerating acquisition; full reconstruction remains complexity-bound and not universally feasible.

Abstract: A common assumption holds that enough observational and interventional data, given to a strong enough predictor, suffices. We report a failure mode that contradicts it. Across hundreds of structural causal models, on identified quantities a strong predictor and a Bayesian baseline both succeed, but on unidentified quantities (the couplings between counterfactual worlds) the predictor collapses to a point, on 28% of models to one no valid model can produce, while the truth is an admissible interval more data never narrows. The gap is structural: prediction cannot represent uncertainty over counterfactual couplings. We cast a world model as a single positive semidefinite coupling kernel K(T,T') over admissible worlds, whose diagonal is the ordinary posterior (what a predictor recovers) and whose off-diagonal is the cross-world coupling it cannot, which every counterfactual reads. The paper is the theory of that off-diagonal. It is real: two states with identical posteriors differ on a cross-world query, and the off-diagonal is the coupling that fixes counterfactuals. It can be bounded: positive semidefiniteness is partial-identifying information the marginals lack, and enforcing it bounds counterfactuals in polynomial time where the exact response-type program is intractable. Logical structure sharpens it: ontology axioms tighten the bound by up to a third, propagating to couplings they never touch. It can be acquired: targeted scars, constraints learned from encountered infeasibilities, close the gap several times faster than untargeted ones. Its full reconstruction is approximate counting of the admissible worlds, tractable below the Sly-Sun threshold and inapproximable above; we do not claim to beat the worst case.

</details>


### [320] [Recalling Too Well: Sycophancy Evaluation and Mitigation in Memory-Augmented Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10949)
*Shelly Bensal,Axel Magnuson,Aparna Balagopalan,Daniel M. Bikel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Persistent memory amplifies sycophancy in LLMs, increasing agreement bias over accuracy; introduces MIST benchmark to evaluate this across domains; memory systems can yield up to 25x more sycophancy than baselines; memory extraction via lossy compression causes misconceptions to persist; two mitigations reduce sycophancy while preserving recall.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically study how persistent memory influences model correctness, specifically sycophancy, in multi-turn interactions across diverse domains.

Method: Develop MIST, a benchmark of synthetically generated multi-turn conversations with user misconceptions in science, medicine, and morality. Evaluate three memory systems and five model families. Measure sycophancy rates, perform error analyses, and test lightweight mitigations.

Result: Memory amplifies sycophancy across all settings; up to 25x higher rates than in-context baselines. Error analysis points to lossy memory extraction into discrete snippets that encode misconceptions and discard corrective context. Two lightweight mitigations substantially reduce sycophancy while preserving or exceeding factual recall.

Conclusion: Memory systems pose a challenge to factual accuracy due to amplified sycophancy but can be mitigated with proposed interventions, enabling more reliable persistence of user beliefs without sacrificing recall.

Abstract: Persistent memory systems promise to make LLMs more helpful by storing user beliefs over time. We show they also make models less correct by systematically amplifying sycophancy, wherein models prioritize agreement with users over accuracy. We conduct the first systematic evaluation of this effect, introducing MIST: a benchmark of synthetically generated multi-turn conversations where users express plausible misconceptions in scientific, medical, and moral reasoning domains. Testing across three state-of-the-art memory systems and five model families reveals that memory amplifies sycophantic behavior across all conditions, with up to 25x higher sycophancy rates than in-context baselines. Error analyses suggest memory extraction as the primary culprit: lossy compression into discrete snippets encodes user misconceptions while discarding corrective context. Based on these results, we propose two lightweight mitigations that substantially reduce sycophancy while matching or exceeding memory systems at factual recall.

</details>


### [321] [Architect-Ant: Editable Automatic Furnishing of Architectural Floor Plans](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10953)
*Fedor Rodionov,Aleksandar Cvejic,Michael Birsak,John Femiani,Peter Wonka*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces AntPlan-270, a dataset of 270 architectural floor plans with per-room furniture bounding boxes, and Architect-Ant, an editable furnishing framework. It uses a coordinate-based DSL to encode furniture placements, procedural reasoning traces to enforce architectural constraints, and preference optimization to refine layouts, culminating in a Flux-based LoRA renderer that outputs realistic blueprint-style images while preserving the symbolic layout.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The field lacks real, professionally designed floor-plan datasets with object-level furniture annotations, hindering automatic furnishing research and practical visualization workflows.

Method: 1) Build AntPlan-270 with per-room furniture bounding boxes across ten residential categories. 2) Fine-tune a vision–language model within Architect-Ant to produce editable layouts. 3) Represent furniture layouts with a compact coordinate-based DSL encoding category and room-relative placements. 4) Generate procedural reasoning traces capturing wall alignment, clearance, circulation, fixture compatibility, and room inventories; use these to supervise fine-tuning. 5) Apply preference optimization over candidate placements. 6) Rasterize DSL into semantic masks and condition a Flux-based LoRA renderer to produce blueprint-style furnished floor plans while preserving the symbolic layout.

Result: Architect-Ant yields geometrically valid and functionally plausible furnished layouts, demonstrating a scalable path to furnishing larger structure-only floor-plan datasets and enabling editable, realistic outputs.

Conclusion: The approach provides a scalable, interpretable pipeline for automatic furnishing that integrates dataset creation, symbolic DSL representations, procedural reasoning, and conditional rendering, with demonstrated effectiveness on a new furnished floor-plan task.

Abstract: Furnished floor plans are fundamental to real estate visualization, interior design, and architectural workflows. However, progress in automatic furniture arrangement has been limited by the lack of real, professionally designed floor-plan datasets with object-level furniture annotations. To address this gap, we introduce AntPlan-270, a curated dataset of 270 architectural floor plans with per-room furniture bounding box annotations across ten residential room categories. Building on this dataset, we present Architect-Ant, an editable automatic furnishing framework powered by a fine-tuned vision-language model. Furniture layouts are represented using a compact, coordinate-based domain-specific language (DSL) that encodes object categories and placements relative to the room geometry. To improve spatial reasoning, we generate procedural reasoning traces that capture architectural constraints such as wall alignment, door and window clearance, circulation, fixture compatibility, and room-specific furniture inventories, and use them to supervise fine-tuning of the model. We then apply preference optimization over candidate object placements to further refine layout quality. The generated DSL can be rasterized into semantic masks and used to condition a Flux-based LoRA renderer, producing realistic blueprint-style furnished floor-plan images while preserving the editable symbolic layout. Experiments on layout furnishing show that Architect-Ant produces geometrically valid and functionally plausible layouts, and suggest a scalable path for furnishing larger structure-only floor-plan datasets.

</details>


### [322] [Mind the Gap: Can Frontier LLMs Pass a Standardized Office Proficiency Exam?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10956)
*Tengchao Lv,Dongdong Zhang,Jiayu Ding,Yilin Jia,Yuzhong Zhao,Yupan Huang,Wenshan Wu,Xiangyang Zhou,Shaohan Huang,Nan Yang,Li Dong,Lei Cui,Furu Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM agents show limited capability in precise Office document automation; an NCRe-based benchmark reveals substantial gaps despite agent feedback loops.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify long-horizon, multi-application document automation capabilities of LLMs in a professional productivity domain.

Method: Create NCRe-inspired evaluation with 200 practical tasks across Word/Excel/PowerPoint, 100-point rubric scored by 7,118 machine-gradable criteria; compute Score Rate; benchmark 7 LLMs; compare single-turn vs agentic systems with execution feedback.

Result: Single-turn models up to 36.6% SR; agentic system reaches 68.8% SR but remains below a 95.5% sanity threshold; highlighting persistent challenges in reliable fine-grained Office automation.

Conclusion: Despite advances in code generation, current LLM/agent systems struggle to achieve reliable, fine-grained Office automation; need improved planning, integration, and feedback mechanisms.

Abstract: The deployment of Large Language Model (LLM) agents for computer automation is accelerating, yet their ability to navigate complex, professional-grade productivity software is largely untested. We argue that Office automation is an ideal environment for benchmarking document-automation capability, as it requires long-horizon planning and reasoning, precise parameter configuration, and multi-application integration. To quantify this capability, we introduce an evaluation based on China's National Computer Rank Examination (NCRE), featuring 200 comprehensive practical-operation tasks across Word, Excel, and PowerPoint. Each task is scored on a 100-point rubric scale using 7,118 machine-gradable criteria, and Score Rate (SR) denotes the mean percentage of rubric points earned across these tasks. We benchmark 7 frontier LLMs and observe stark limitations: single-turn models score a maximum of 36.6%. A stronger agentic system with execution feedback, iterative repair, and broader Office automation access reaches 68.8%, but remains below the 95.5% community-reference score used as a scoring sanity check. Ultimately, our experiments demonstrate that despite recent advancements in code generation, achieving reliable fine-grained Office document automation remains a significant challenge for current code-generating LLM and agent systems.

</details>


### [323] [Bellman-Taylor Score Decoding for Markov Decision Processes with State-Dependent Feasible Action Sets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10979)
*Yi Chen,Rushuai Yang,Qiang Chen,Dongyan,Huo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Bellman–Taylor score decoding to enable DRL for MDPs with state-dependent, implicit feasible actions by learning a latent Euclidean score and decoding via a feasibility projector, with theoretical guarantees and queueing-network applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard DRL assumes fixed action catalogs or Euclidean spaces; many operations research MDPs have actions defined implicitly by constraints that vary with state, making standard DRL interfaces unsuitable.

Method: Use a Taylor expansion of the optimal action-value function to define a latent-score MDP. Learn in Euclidean score space and enforce feasibility through an action decoder, so policy training does not differentiate through the decoder.

Result: Provides a performance guarantee: the optimality gap decomposes into a structural approximation error and an algorithmic learning error. Empirical results show near-optimal performance in small instances and substantial improvements over benchmarks in larger queueing systems.

Conclusion: The Bellman–Taylor score decoding framework enables standard DRL methods to handle constrained, state-dependent actions by learning a latent score and decoding to feasible actions, with a provable error decomposition and effective application to queueing control.

Abstract: Many Markov decision processes (MDPs) in operations research have feasible actions that are state dependent and defined implicitly by various operational constraints. These features make it difficult to use standard deep reinforcement learning (DRL) algorithms, whose action interfaces typically assume either a fixed finite action catalog or a simple Euclidean space. Motivated by a Taylor expansion of the optimal action-value function, we propose Bellman--Taylor score decoding, a framework that moves policy learning to a Euclidean score space while enforcing feasibility through an action decoder. The induced latent-score MDP then can be optimized by standard DRL algorithms without differentiating through the decoder. We provide a performance guarantee showing that the optimality gap of this approach decomposes into a structural approximation error and an algorithmic learning error. Lastly, we apply this framework to a queueing network control problem, where the policy essentially learns a state-dependent index-based dispatching rule. Numerical experiments show near-optimal performance in small instances and considerable improvements over benchmarks in larger systems.

</details>


### [324] [Null-Space Constrained Low-Rank Adaptation for Response-Specified Large Language Model Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10989)
*Bocheng Ju,Jianhua Wang,Chengliang Liu,Xiaolin Chang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NSRU is a projection-constrained, low-rank unlearning framework that localizes updates to a null space of retention subspaces, enabling safe-target forgetting while preserving benign capabilities; validated on TOFU and WMDP with ablations and robustness analyses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for localized and controllable unlearning in LLMs by constraining updates and explicitly guiding forget/query behavior with safe targets.

Method: Construct per-module benign retain subspaces from hidden representations; apply orthogonal-projected low-rank (LoRA) updates confined to the null space of these retain subspaces; jointly optimize safe-target learning, undesired-response suppression, and retention preservation; provide local first-order analysis of perturbation effects.

Result: On TOFU: effective suppression of extractable forget-set knowledge and improved retain QA performance, utility, and safe-target alignment versus baselines. On WMDP: hazardous-domain accuracy remains near random while preserving broad domain- and adjacent MMLU utility. Ablations validate components; robustness analyses show stability across hyperparameters and prompts.

Conclusion: NSRU achieves localized, safe-guided unlearning by constraining updates to the null space of retention subspaces, yielding effective forgetting with preserved benign capabilities and strong empirical validation across tasks.

Abstract: Large language model unlearning aims to suppress designated undesirable knowledge while preserving benign capabilities. Many unlearning objectives focus on suppressing undesired answers, while recent target-guided variants specify replacement behavior but still leave update locality largely unconstrained. This paper introduces \emph{Null-Space Constrained Response-Specified Unlearning} (NSRU), a projection-constrained low-rank framework for controlled LLM unlearning. NSRU uses an explicitly structured safe target response to specify the desired behavior for each forget query, while suppressing the original undesired content. To localize adaptation, NSRU estimates per-module retain subspaces from benign hidden representations and uses an orthogonal-projected low-rank parameterization to confine LoRA updates to the null space of the retain subspace. The resulting objective jointly optimizes safe-target learning, undesired-response suppression, and retention preservation under this constrained parameterization. We provide a local first-order analysis showing that the projected update reduces retain-side perturbations while preserving editable directions for shaping forget-query behavior. Experiments on TOFU show that NSRU effectively suppresses extractable forget-set knowledge while improving retain QA performance, model utility, and safe-target alignment over representative baselines. On WMDP, NSRU keeps hazardous-domain accuracy near the random-choice region while preserving broad and domain-adjacent MMLU utility. Ablation studies support the complementary roles of safe-target supervision, undesired-response suppression, retention loss, and null-space projected updates, while sensitivity and robustness analyses indicate stable behavior across the tested hyperparameter and prompt variations.

</details>


### [325] [Structure from Reasoning, Numbers from Search: On-Premise Open LLMs as Structural Priors for Coupled MIMO Controller Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11015)
*Jiaxuan Chen,Haonan Li,Yang Shu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Open-source LLMs can provide sample-efficient, interpretable priors for tuning MIMO controllers, helping mainly in higher-dimensional, strongly coupled plants when paired with classical optimization; not general optimizers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the difficulty of tuning strongly coupled MIMO processes by leveraging on-premise LLMs as structure-imposing priors and interpretable reasoning to improve sample efficiency and discover non-obvious controller structures.

Method: Empirical benchmark across a single-loop CSTR and a 2x2 quadruple-tank with conflicting setpoints. Compare classical relay-feedback tuning, naive LLM tuning, and scaffolded LLM tuning. Use a penalized cost J = IAE + lambda*TV(u). Introduce a scalable scaffolded LLM that infers coupling-aware structure, then refine with classical optimization and/or differential evolution. Evaluate sample efficiency (number of evaluations), robustness across starting points, and generalization across different open models.

Result: For the 1x1 CSTR, relay-feedback tuning outperforms LLM tuner (IAE 0.106 vs 0.162). For the 2x2 quadruple-tank, naive approaches perform poorly (J ~28-30) versus open-loop (22.7); a balanced-start local optimizer fails in 10/10. The scaffolded LLM learns an asymmetric coupling structure and achieves J ~16.9±0.2, and with classical refinement reaches J ~12.0 (10/10 vs 0/10). Differential evolution also attains the optimum; but the LLM offers sample efficiency (~18 evaluations) and interpretable rationale, especially advantageous for higher dimensions (≈6x fewer evaluations on a 3x3 plant). Generalizes across four open models; on benign plants, LLMs offer no advantage.

Conclusion: Open LLMs are not general optimizers but can serve as sample-efficient, interpretable priors that capture coupling structure and guide subsequent optimization in control tuning. They are most beneficial in higher-dimensional MIMO settings where they reduce data requirements and provide human-interpretable design insights. The work proposes a reproducible benchmark to delineate when LLMs help in control tuning.

Abstract: Tuning controllers for strongly coupled multi-input multi-output (MIMO) industrial processes is hard: decentralized classical auto-tuning ignores loop interaction, and local numerical optimization from natural initializations stalls in the resulting non-convex cost landscape. We ask whether on-premise open-source large language models (LLMs), which keep data on-site and need no plant model, can help. On a single-loop CSTR, classical relay-feedback tuning (IAE 0.106, near the 0.102 optimum) beats an LLM tuner (0.162): for simple loops the LLM adds nothing. The picture inverts on a strongly coupled quadruple-tank with conflicting set-points, scored by a penalized cost J = IAE + lambda*TV(u) that rewards tracking without chattering actuators. There, naive relay tuning (J ~ 28.6) and naive LLM tuning (29.7) are no better than open loop (22.7), and a local optimizer from balanced starts fails in 10/10 runs. A scaffolded open LLM instead reasons about the coupling, proposes the counter-intuitive asymmetric structure, and reaches J ~ 16.9 +/- 0.2 from any start; refining it with a classical optimizer attains the smooth global optimum (J ~ 12.0, 10/10 vs. 0/10), which even applies a non-obvious negative integral correction decentralized tuning cannot. A global optimizer (differential evolution) also reaches this optimum, so the LLM is not the only route; its advantage is sample efficiency and interpretability: a usable controller in 18 evaluations (where the global optimizer is worse than open loop) plus a stated rationale. This edge grows with dimension, reaching ~6x fewer evaluations on a 3x3 plant. The behaviour generalizes across four open models, and on a benign plant the LLM offers no advantage, sharpening the boundary. We contribute a reproducible benchmark delimiting when open LLMs help in control tuning: not as optimizers, but as a sample-efficient, interpretable structural prior.

</details>


### [326] [Superficial Beliefs in LLM Decision-Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11016)
*Gabriel Freedman,Francesca Toni*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs exhibit systematic, interpretable decision structure rather than random mimicry, but their explicit rationales only partially align with the underlying drivers, suggesting 'superficial belief'.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether LLMs' choices in attribute-based tasks reflect a genuine internal decision structure or merely imitate rationales, by comparing asserted important attributes with those that best explain choices via a behavioural model fit.

Method: Synthetic binary decision tasks where profiles are defined by graded attributes; compare the attribute the model says mattered most with the attribute that best explains its choices using a behavioural model fit to prior decisions; test robustness across prompt-order, sampling, occlusion, and varied decision settings; use self-reports and score-based judges to infer drivers.

Result: Behaviour is systematically related to observable attributes and can predict held-out choices, but self-reports and judges only partially recover the inferred driver; outputs are structured for prediction yet explicit reasons imperfectly track the driver.

Conclusion: Evidence for 'superficial belief': LLMs show probabilistic local attribute priorities guiding decisions, but have limited verbal access to the true driving attributes, resulting in a partial alignment between stated and actual drivers, robust to several perturbations.

Abstract: We ask whether large language models (LLMs) merely imitate rationales when choosing between two options, or whether their choices reflect a systematic underlying decision structure. Using synthetic binary decision settings in which models choose between profiles defined by graded attributes, we compare the attribute a model says mattered most with the attribute that best explains its choice under a behavioural model fit to prior decisions. The behavioural model predicts held-out choices well, showing that model behaviour is systematically related to the visible attributes rather than being random. However, direct self-reports and a separate score-based judge recover the behaviourally inferred driver only partially. The resulting picture is neither one of arbitrary behaviour nor one of fully articulated belief - outputs are structured enough to support prediction, but explicit reasons track the recovered driver only imperfectly. This qualitative pattern persists across prompt-order and sampling perturbations, alternative behavioural models, targeted occlusion analyses, and structurally varied decision settings. We interpret this as evidence for ``superficial belief'' in LLM decision-making: models behave as if guided by probabilistic local priorities over attributes, while having only limited verbal access to the attributes that drive their decisions.

</details>


### [327] [Workflow-GYM: Towards Long-Horizon Evaluation of Computer-use Agentic tasks in Real-World Professional Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11042)
*Liya Zhu,Jingzhe Ding,Jian Zhang,Jianbo Xue,Shihao Liang,Ge Zhang,Xiang Gao,Qingshui Gu,Mailun Gao,Huimin Che,Yan Zhao,Peiheng Zhou,Haojun Wang,Chaobo Xian,Lili Le,Chi Wu,Yiwei Liu,Shengda Long,Jiale Yang,Fangzhi Xu,Sijin Wu,Haodong Duan,Yi Zhu,Chao He,Zhaojian Li,Minchao Wang,Huan Zhou,Jiani Hou,Chuqian Yu,Weiran Shi,Hongwan Gao,Jiamin Chen,Guanhong Chen,Tingqin Luo,Kaiyuan Zhang,Zhixin Yao,Qing Hua,Yuhao Jiang,Jin Chen,Pu Chen,Zhenyu Hu,Xingyu Li,Zhengxuan Jiang,Meng Cao,Tianfeng Long,Haozhe Wang,Mingzhang Wang,Yichen Zhang,Yiming Dai,Chenchen Zhang,Jiaying Wang,Zhiyong Wu,Shen Yan,Yujia Qin,Wenhao Huang,Zaiyuan Wang,Xiaolong Chang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Workflow-GYM is a benchmark for long-horizon GUI tasks in professional software, revealing that top models barely surpass 30% success and struggle with consistency, omission, error propagation, and objective drift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a gap in evaluating AI agents on domain-specific, end-to-end professional GUI workflows, beyond general-purpose software and short-horizon tasks.

Method: Introduce Workflow-GYM benchmark for long-horizon GUI tasks in professional domains; conduct extensive experiments with state-of-the-art models; analyze performance and failure modes to understand limitations.

Result: Strongest models achieve only slightly above 30% success; agents fail to maintain long-horizon consistency and exhibit workflow stage omission, error propagation, objective drift, and poor understanding of professional software environments.

Conclusion: Current GUI agents have significant limitations for professional, long-horizon workflows; results highlight the need for next-generation GUI-agent research focusing on long-horizon planning, error recovery, environment understanding, and domain adaptation.

Abstract: Recent years have witnessed the rapid evolution of AI agents toward handling increasingly complex, real-world tasks. However, existing benchmarks rarely evaluate whether agents can operate graphical user interfaces to complete long-horizon, high-value professional workflows across diverse domains. Current GUI benchmarks still predominantly focus on general-purpose software, relatively simple applications, and short-horizon tasks, leaving it largely unknown whether modern agents can follow user instructions to autonomously operate domain-specific professional software and accomplish economically valuable work in an end-to-end manner. To bridge this gap, we introduce Workflow-GYM, a benchmark for long-horizon GUI tasks centered on professional domains and specialized software environments. Through extensive experiments on state-of-the-art models, we find that even the strongest models achieve only slightly above 30% success rates, highlighting that professional long-horizon GUI workflows remain highly challenging for current GUI agents. Further analysis reveals that current agents struggle to maintain long-horizon workflow consistency, frequently exhibiting workflow stage omission, error propagation, objective drift, and insufficient understanding of professional software environments. Our findings provide important insights into the limitations of current agent systems and suggest key directions for the next generation of GUI-agent research.

</details>


### [328] [What Fits (Into Few Tokens) Doesn't Overfit: Compression and Generalization in ML Research Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11045)
*Martin Andres Bertran,Aaron Roth,Zhiwei Steven Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The study tests whether information bottlenecks in LLM-driven research agents reproduce or discover high-performance models; across 8 datasets, short prompts and one-bit feedback suffice, overfitting is not observed; supports a description-length explanation that successful strategies are low-complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand why benchmark-driven ML exhibits little overfitting by reusing held-out benchmarks; test whether successful strategies are compressible.

Method: Introduce two info bottlenecks: output compression (reproducer tries to replicate explorer's high-performing models from limited prompt + training data) and input compression (explorer uses only binary feedback on each submission). Evaluate across 8 datasets spanning tabular, vision, LLMs, diffusion, reward modeling; using LLM-driven research agents and a validation set.

Result: Bottlenecks have little impact on performance; short prompts and compressible feedback suffice to reproduce and identify high-performance models; deliberate validation-set overfitting breaks from short prompts, falsifying the hypothesis; across domains, results support a description-length explanation.

Conclusion: Successful strategies reside in a low-complexity region of strategy space; the lack of overfitting in benchmark-driven ML can be attributed to compressibility of effective strategies.

Abstract: Reusing a held-out benchmark adaptively should, in principle, invite overfitting. Yet benchmark-driven machine learning (ML) has produced surprisingly little overfitting in practice. An attractive hypothesis is that successful ML strategies are highly compressible. We study this in the setting of LLM-driven research agents, where the hypothesis becomes directly testable via two complementary information bottlenecks. In \emph{output compression}, an exploration agent adaptively searches for high-performance models using a validation set, and we test whether a fresh ``reproducer agent'' can reproduce its performance given only an extremely short prompt and the training data. In \emph{input compression}, the explorer receives only one-bit feedback indicating whether each submitted model improves on the running best. Across 8 datasets spanning tabular classification, vision, language modeling, diffusion modeling, and reward modeling, we find that these bottlenecks have little effect on performance: short prompts and compressible feedback are sufficient to reproduce and find high-performance models. The hypothesis is falsifiable: when we deliberately induce validation-set overfitting, the results fail to reproduce with short prompts. Taken together, our results support a description-length explanation for the lack of overfitting in benchmark-driven ML: successful strategies occupy a low-complexity region of strategy space.

</details>


### [329] [CIAware-Bench: Benchmarking Control Intervention Awareness Across Frontier LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11063)
*Joachim Schaeffer,Thomas Jiralerspong,Alexander Panfilov,Guillaume Lajoie,Jonas Geiping,Yoshua Bengio,Roland S. Zimmermann*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CIAware-Bench probes control intervention awareness (CI awareness) in frontier models by testing if they can distinguish their own trajectories from those altered by a monitoring intervention. Results show low-to-moderate CI awareness with substantial cross-domain/model variability, not a fixed model property. The benchmark aims to track CI awareness to guide harder-to-detect control protocols.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the risk that control protocols monitoring untrusted models could be inferred or evaded by the model itself; quantify and track the detectability of interventions across models and deployment scenarios.

Method: A four-domain benchmark (essay writing, BigCodeBench, Bash Arena, SHADE-Arena) varying trajectory watermarking, side-task presence, and control protocol; evaluated on eleven frontier models; CI awareness measured as the model's ability to classify whether a trajectory has been intervened (random chance 0.5).

Result: CI awareness ranges from near random to up to 0.87 accuracy, with substantial variation across domains and model pairs; detection is easier across model families, suggesting models exploit provider-specific stylistic or post-training differences; CI awareness is not a fixed property of a model.

Conclusion: CIAware-Bench provides a tool to track CI awareness and to inform the design of control interventions that are harder to detect; CI awareness should be measured for each new model release and deployment scenario.

Abstract: AI control protocols oversee untrusted models by monitoring their actions and modifying potentially unsafe steps, often using a trusted model. This partially tampers with the untrusted model's trajectory. If the trusted model detects such an intervention, it may infer properties of the monitor and adapt to evade control.
