Abstract: Humanoid robots have achieved strong locomotion capabilities, but reliable navigation on versatile terrains remains challenging because obstacle avoidance must be coordinated with dynamically feasible motion. In this work, we present GuideWalk, a unified end-to-end framework that integrates traversability-aware navigation guidance with terrain-adaptive locomotion teacher for humanoid navigation. Specifically, we introduce a navigation module that provides explicit velocity guidance, decoupling obstacle avoidance from terrain conditions to enable robust planning across diverse environments. We propose a composite teacher distillation scheme, where goal-directed commands and dynamically consistent actions are aggregated and distilled into a single policy. To further improve robustness, the distilled policy is refined with reinforcement learning and an auxiliary behavior cloning objective, which promotes exploration while preserving desirable teacher behaviors. Experiments demonstrate that GuideWalk achieves stable and effective navigation while maintaining stable humanoid locomotion.

</details>


### [357] [Act on What You See: Unlocking Safe Social Navigation in Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10495)
*Qingzi Wang,Xiyang Wu,Guangyao Shi,Dianwei Chen,Xianfeng Yang,Dinesh Manocha*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Pretrained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models already encode pedestrian-object distinctions and future collision signals; simple behavior cloning fails to translate these cues into safe actions. SALSA is a two-stage, annotation-free post-training framework—social behavioral alignment and temporal safety alignment—that aligns latent representations with action generation using counterfactuals and automatically generated future-risk supervision. It achieves large safety gains on SCAND and in real-world deployment, significantly reducing near-collisions and improving social counterfactual accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the mismatch between rich social cues encoded in pretrained VLA models and the need for anticipatory, socially appropriate actions in robot navigation. Existing behavior cloning does not exploit latent signals for safe action, motivating a post-training alignment that makes the model act on its own representations.

Method: Two-stage post-training: (1) social behavioral alignment - connects intermediate-layer social features to the action head using counterfactual human-object scene pairs to avoid visual saliency shortcuts; (2) temporal safety alignment - uses automatically generated future-risk supervision to enable anticipatory collision avoidance. Training is annotation-free and leverages pretrained VLA representations.

Result: On SCAND and real-world deployment, SALSA reduces near-collisions by 86.4% and increases social counterfactual accuracy from 53% to 93%, indicating effective translation of latent cues into safer social navigation behaviors.

Conclusion: Pretrained VLA policies can be safely adapted for social navigation by aligning their latent representations with the action generator, enabling anticipatory and socially aware behavior without extensive annotations.

Abstract: Safe social navigation requires robots to distinguish people from ordinary obstacles and to react before danger becomes imminent. We show that pretrained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models already encode pedestrian-object distinctions and future collision signals in their internal representations, but behavior cloning fails to translate these signals into socially appropriate actions. To address this mismatch, we propose SALSA, a two-stage annotation-free post-training framework: (1) social behavioral alignment bridges intermediate-layer social features to the action head and trains on counterfactual human-object scene pairs to break visual saliency shortcuts; (2) temporal safety alignment provides automatically generated future-risk supervision to enable anticipatory collision avoidance. On SCAND and real-world deployment, SALSA reduces near-collisions by 86.4% and improves social counterfactual accuracy from 53% to 93%, demonstrating that safer social navigation can be achieved by teaching VLA policies to act on representations they already possess. These results show that pretrained VLA policies can be adapted for safer social navigation by better aligning their latent representations with action generation.

</details>


### [358] [Uncovering Vulnerability of Vision-Language-Action Models under Joint-Level Physical Faults](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10501)
*Minsoo Jo,Taeju Kwon,Junha Chun,Youngjoon Jeong,Taesup Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A lightweight fault-aware residual calibrator (J-PARC) improves robustness of VLA policies to joint-level faults without sacrificing fault-free performance by inferring a latent fault regime from joint dynamics and conditioning a shared calibrator on it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Embodiment faults in real robots alter the action-to-motion interface, undermining learned closed-loop mappings in Vision-Language-Action models. Faults are joint-specific and can degrade success even when tasks remain physically feasible, necessitating a method that adapts at execution time.

Method: Proposes Joint-level Physical-fault Aware Residual Calibrator (J-PARC): freezing the VLA policy and adding a lightweight residual calibrator. It infers a latent joint-fault regime from recent joint dynamics and conditions the calibrator on this regime to produce adaptive action corrections across faulty joints.

Result: Empirical results show J-PARC improves task success under various joint-level faults and maintains performance in fault-free settings; reveals joint-dependent degradation that cannot be explained by feasibility alone.

Conclusion: Joint-level fault-aware residual calibration is an effective, low-overhead approach to increase the robustness of VLA policies in real robots by anticipating and compensating for embodiment faults.

Abstract: Deploying Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models in real robotic systems requires robustness not only to semantic and perceptual variations, but also to embodiment-side faults that change how actions are physically realized. Real robots can experience joint-level changes caused by actuator degradation, hardware faults, safety limits, collision damage, or wear-induced friction. These faults are critical because they alter the action-to-motion interface of a policy, disrupting the learned closed-loop relationship between commanded actions, realized motion, and subsequent observations. In this work, we study realistic joint-level physical faults and show that VLA models are vulnerable when predicted actions are executed through a perturbed robot body. Our analysis reveals joint-dependent effects, with heterogeneous degradation in task success across affected joints. We also show that performance drops cannot be attributed solely to physical infeasibility, since feasible faults such as increased joint friction can still substantially reduce success rates and induce closed-loop execution mismatch. Motivated by these findings, we propose Joint-level Physical-fault Aware Residual Calibrator (J-PARC), a lightweight residual calibration framework built on top of a frozen VLA policy. J-PARC infers a latent joint-fault regime from recent joint dynamics and conditions a shared residual calibrator on this regime, enabling adaptive action correction across faulty joints. Experiments show that J-PARC improves robustness under joint-level faults while preserving fault-free environment performance.

</details>


### [359] [VeriSpace: Spatially Grounded Action Verification for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10568)
*Guiyu Zhao,Longteng Guo,Junyou Zhu,Jun Fu,Yanghong Mei,Bin Cao,Jie Jiang,Xingjian He,Jing Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes VeriSpace, a 3D-aware test-time action verifier for vision-language-action robotic manipulation, improving decision reliability via dual-path 3D-injected scene encoding and spatial reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: One-shot action prediction in VLA can lead to costly errors (grasp failure, collisions, task stalls). Test-time verification that can propose and evaluate multiple candidate actions is desirable, but reliable verification is hard due to subtle geometric differences and the need to measure progress toward goals.

Method: Two components: (1) Dual-Path 3D-Injected Scene Encoding that preserves visual semantics and explicit 3D geometry; (2) Spatially-Grounded Action Reasoning that assesses each action by spatial relations, geometric validity, and expected task progress. The verifier is designed to be compatible with existing VLA policies.

Result: Experiments on public benchmarks and real-world robotic manipulation tasks show VeriSpace consistently improves decision reliability over both base VLA policies and prior verification-based methods, with gains in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings.

Conclusion: VeriSpace enables more reliable discrimination between subtle yet outcome-critical action candidates and can be integrated with existing VLA systems to improve robustness in manipulation tasks.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have shown strong promise for robotic manipulation, but their reliability at test time remains limited by one-shot action prediction, where even small action errors can cause grasp failure, collision, or incorrect task progression. A natural alternative is to equip VLA systems with test-time verification, allowing multiple candidate actions to be proposed and evaluated before execution. However, reliable action verification is challenging because it requires not only distinguishing subtle geometric differences between candidate actions, but also assessing whether an action makes meaningful progress toward the task goal. We present VeriSpace, a 3D-aware action verifier for test-time action selection in VLA systems. VeriSpace evaluates candidate actions through two key components: Dual-Path 3D-Injected Scene Encoding, which constructs a scene representation that jointly preserves visual semantics and explicit 3D geometry, and Spatially-Grounded Action Reasoning, which evaluates each action by reasoning over task-relevant spatial relations, geometric validity, and expected goal progress. Together, these components enable more reliable discrimination between subtle yet outcome-critical action candidates while remaining fully compatible with existing VLA policies. Experiments on public benchmarks and real-world robotic manipulation tasks show that VeriSpace consistently improves decision reliability over both underlying VLA policies and prior verification-based methods, yielding substantial gains in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings.

</details>


### [360] [AgenticNav: Zero-Shot Vision-and-Language Navigation as a Tool-Calling Harness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10577)
*Yijian Li,Changze Li,Hantian Shi,Jiaying Luo,Jiyuan Cai,Ming Yang,Tong Qin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AgenticNav replaces traditional waypoint predictors with an agentic interface exposing action, depth, and memory as callable tools for zero-shot VLN-CE, achieving state-of-the-art results on R2R-CE and demonstrating real-world zero-shot generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLN-CE methods rely on learned waypoint predictors that constrain action space and fail to leverage depth effectively; memory strategies either overload prompts with irrelevant data or rely on cross-episode retrieval, weakening zero-shot performance.

Method: Introduce an agentic navigation harness that exposes three tools: an action tool for direct pixel-level target selection in RGB observations, a depth tool for on-demand metric distances, and a memory toolset comprising a compact trajectory map and a recall mechanism to revisit past observations without bloating prompts.

Result: On the R2R-CE benchmark, AgenticNav achieves new SOTA among zero-shot methods with the same VLM backbone; real-world validation shows stronger zero-shot generalization; ablations indicate the action tool outperforms waypoint predictors, while depth and memory tools provide additional gains.

Conclusion: Framing VLN-CE as an agentic interface between VLMs and environments enables flexible action, selective depth queries, and concise memory, yielding superior zero-shot performance and robust generalization.

Abstract: Zero-shot vision-and-language navigation in continuous environments (VLN-CE) has recently become feasible with large vision-language models (VLMs). However, existing methods typically rely on learned waypoint predictors to propose navigable actions. This severely limits the model's action space and fails to leverage depth inputs effectively. Moreover, memory is commonly handled by accumulating long textual or visual histories with substantial irrelevant context, or by retrieving cross-episode experiences, which weakens the zero-shot setting. In this paper, we rethink zero-shot VLN-CE as an agentic interface between the VLM and the environment, and present AgenticNav, a lightweight navigation harness that exposes action, depth, and memory as callable tools. Instead of choosing from predicted waypoints, the action tool allows the VLM to directly select a target pixel in RGB observations, converting it into executable motion. Depth is exposed through an on-demand pixel-depth tool, enabling the VLM to request precise metric distances only where they matter. For memory, AgenticNav provides a compact map image summarizing the historical trajectory, paired with a recall tool that allows the VLM to selectively revisit past visual observations without overwhelming the prompt context. On the R2R-CE benchmark, AgenticNav establishes new state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance among zero-shot methods given the same VLM backbone. Real-world validation further highlights its zero-shot generalization compared to prior methods. Ablations show that our action tool design outperforms traditional waypoint predictors, and that depth tool and agentic memory further contribute to navigation performance.

</details>


### [361] [LieIPM: Lie Group Interior Point Method for Direct Trajectory Optimization of Rigid Bodies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10579)
*Sangli Teng,Ruiqi Zhang,Tzu-Yuan Lin,William A Clark,Mark Mueller,Ram Vasudevan,Maani Ghaffari,Koushil Sreenath*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A structure-aware trajectory optimization framework on matrix Lie groups for rigid body motions, using a Lie Group Interior Point Method (LieIPM) with variational integrators. It preserves group geometry and topology, avoids rotation singularities, and shows robustness and faster convergence than generic solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard constrained trajectory optimization in Euclidean space ignores the manifold structure of rigid body motions, causing singularities and ill-conditioning. A geometry-preserving approach on matrix Lie groups can improve robustness and convergence.

Method: Second-order rigid body models on Lie groups; Newton-type updates exploiting Lie group structure; line-search Lie Group Interior Point Method (LieIPM) for manifold constraints; use Lie group variational integrators; derive closed-form intrinsic derivatives that exploit group symmetries; preserves rotation topology by construction.

Result: Numerical experiments indicate superior robustness and faster convergence compared to general-purpose solvers and structure-exploiting optimal control methods.

Conclusion: The proposed framework preserves the topology of rotations and avoids singularities, delivering more robust and efficient constrained trajectory optimization on manifolds.

Abstract: Designing dynamically feasible trajectories for rigid bodies is a fundamental problem in robotics. While direct methods are widely used, the existing constrained optimizers typically operate in Euclidean space and ignore the manifold structure of rigid body motions. This mismatch may introduce singularities or lead to poorly conditioned optimization problems. To bridge this gap, we develop a structure-aware framework for constrained trajectory optimization directly on matrix Lie groups. Our approach is based on the second-order rigid body models utilizing Lie group structures, which enables efficient Newton-type updates while preserving the underlying geometry. Building on this model, we propose a line-search Lie Group Interior Point Method (LieIPM) to handle constraints on the manifolds. We instantiate the framework for rigid body motion planning using Lie group variational integrators and derive closed-form intrinsic derivatives that exploit group symmetries. The LieIPM preserves the topology of rotation motions by construction and avoids singularities. Numerical results demonstrate superior robustness and faster convergence compared to general-purpose solvers and structure-exploiting optimal control methods.

</details>


### [362] [Dexterous Point Policy: Learning Point-based Dexterous Hand Policies from Human Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10614)
*Beomjun Kim,Seong Hyeon Park,Seunghoon Sim,Seungjun Moon,Sanghyeok Lee,Jinwoo Shin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Dexterous Point Policy trains dexterous manipulation policies directly from human videos by using a unified 3D keypoint representation for both observations and actions, enabling transfer to real robots without robot demonstrations and achieving substantially higher success than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the robot-embodiment gap and reduce data collection cost for dexterous manipulation by leveraging abundant human videos and a unified 3D keypoint space.

Method: Extract 3D keypoints of task-relevant objects and human hands from videos; train an autoregressive transformer over these keypoints; align at the keypoint level (wrist and fingertips) to enable policy transfer from humans to robots; no robot demonstrations required.

Result: On real-robot tasks including pick-and-place and tool use, achieved 75.0% success, vastly outperforming a state-of-the-art VLA baseline at 1.0%; demonstrated strong generalization to unseen multi-object scenarios and novel object categories.

Conclusion: A keypoint-based, cross-embodiment approach can enable data-efficient dexterous manipulation transfer from human videos to robots, reducing or eliminating the need for robot-specific demonstrations and offering robust generalization.

Abstract: Robotic foundation models pre-trained on human demonstration videos have shown promise, but a significant embodiment gap remains when the resulting policies are deployed on real robots. A common remedy is to fine-tune these models on robot-specific demonstrations. However, robot data collection can be prohibitively expensive and time-consuming, which is particularly acute in dexterous manipulation, e.g., teleoperating a multi-fingered hand for even a single atomic task can take days. To address this, we introduce Dexterous Point Policy, a framework that learns dexterous manipulation policies directly from human videos and requires no robot demonstrations. Our core insight is that a unified 3D keypoint representation can bridge human and robot embodiments when used for both observations and actions. Specifically, we extract 3D keypoints of task-relevant objects and human hands from raw videos, and train an autoregressive transformer over these keypoints. We observe that at the keypoint level, specifically the wrist and fingertips, human and robot behaviors closely align, enabling direct policy transfer. On a suite of real-robot tasks spanning pick-and-place and tool use, Dexterous Point Policy attains 75.0% success, whereas a state-of-the-art VLA baseline reaches only 1.0%. Furthermore, our method generalizes strongly to unseen scenarios, including multi-object environments and novel object categories.

</details>


### [363] [Planar-Sector LOS Guidance for Interception of Agile Targets with Lifting-Wing Quadcopters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10639)
*Linkai Liu,Kun Yang,Han Zou,Chen Min,Shuli Lv,Shuai Wang,Quan Quan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PS-LOS is a visibility-preserving, maneuverability-aware guidance framework for autonomous long-range interception using a lifting-wing quadcopter with a monocular camera. It constrains lateral image error while relaxing longitudinal error, yielding ~50% more thrust near the line of sight and successful intercepts up to 138 m.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous visual interception is challenged by unpredictable target motion, limited sensing, and strong coupling between target visibility and interceptor maneuverability. Conic LOS constraints force symmetric margins, reducing maneuverability and thrust; empirical behavior of FPV pilots suggests uneven visibility margins across image directions.

Method: Introduce Planar-Sector Line-of-Sight (PS-LOS) guidance for a strapdown monocular-camera-equipped lifting-wing quadcopter. PS-LOS tightens lateral (sideways) image error while relaxing longitudinal error within a safe field-of-view, preserving visibility and freeing acceleration capabilities. Implementation includes a delay-compensated state estimator and a nonlinear guidance-and-control architecture to achieve LOS-only interception without direct depth measurements.

Result: Extensive outdoor experiments show autonomous interception of agile targets with large amplitude, high-frequency, and wind-disturbed motion. The system achieved successful interceptions at ranges up to 138 m while maintaining continuous visual tracking, and PS-LOS provides approximately 50% more available thrust near the LOS than conventional conic constraints.

Conclusion: PS-LOS is a visibility-preserving, maneuverability-aware guidance framework for long-range visual interception of agile aerial targets, validated by real-world outdoor flight tests.

Abstract: Autonomous visual interception of agile aerial targets is challenging due to unpredictable target motion, limited sensing, and the strong coupling between target visibility and interceptor maneuverability. Most existing strapdown-camera interception methods preserve visibility using conic line-of-sight (LOS) constraints that keep the target near the image center. While safe, such symmetric constraints unnecessarily restrict maneuverability and can significantly reduce the usable thrust for pursuit. Motivated by the observation that aggressive FPV pilots do not maintain equal visibility margins in all image directions, this paper proposes a Planar-Sector Line-of-Sight (PS-LOS) guidance framework for autonomous interception using a lifting-wing quadcopter equipped with only a strapdown monocular camera. PS-LOS tightly constrains lateral image error while relaxing longitudinal image error within a safe field-of-view margin, preserving visibility while releasing maneuverability for acceleration-intensive pursuit. Under the lifting-wing quadcopter model, PS-LOS provides nearly 50% more available thrust near the LOS direction than conventional conic LOS constraints. To realize LOS-only interception without direct depth measurements, a delay-compensated state-estimation framework and a nonlinear guidance-and-control architecture are developed for lifting-wing quadcopters. Extensive outdoor flight experiments demonstrate autonomous interception of agile targets exhibiting large-amplitude, high-frequency, and unpredictable motion under real wind disturbances. The proposed system achieves successful interceptions at ranges up to 138 m while maintaining continuous visual tracking throughout the engagement. The results validate PS-LOS as a visibility-preserving, maneuverability-aware guidance framework for long-range visual interception of agile aerial targets.

</details>


### [364] [UniDexTok: A Unified Dexterous Hand Tokenizer from Real Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10683)
*Dong Fang,Youjun Wu,Yuanxin Zhong,Rui Zhang,Yunlong Wang,Xiaosong Jia,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Unified Dexterous Hand Model (UDHM) maps human/robot hand states to a shared 22-DoF interface, enabling a retargeting-free, embodiment-conditioned state tokenizer (UniDexTok) that achieves sub-millimeter reconstruction accuracy and strong cross-embodiment generalization, zero-shot, and few-shot performance improvements over UniHM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dexterous hands have highly diverse kinematics and joint definitions, making a universal state representation difficult and hindering data reuse for joint training.

Method: Introduce UDHM to standardize states into a 22-DoF interface; develop UniDexTok, a retargeting-free tokenizer that learns embodiment-conditioned discrete tokens from standardized joint states, enabling a unified representation across heterogeneous hands without retargeting or simulation data.

Result: Compared to UniHM, UniDexTok reduces MPJAE from 15.63° to 0.16° and MPJPE from 18.51 mm to 0.18 mm, i.e., ≈99% error reductions; reconstruction shifts from centimeter-scale to sub-millimeter scale. Cross-embodiment data improves target reconstruction; shows strong zero-shot and few-shot reconstruction on unseen hands.

Conclusion: UniDexTok provides a unified representation for heterogeneous dexterous hands and enables cross-embodiment transfer and generalization, outperforming the baseline UniHM.

Abstract: Dexterous hands are essential for fine-grained manipulation, but their hardware designs vary substantially across embodiments. Differences in kinematics, joint definitions, and degrees of freedom make it difficult to define a shared state representation compared with parallel grippers. As a result, dexterous-hand data remains fragmented and difficult to use for joint training. In this work, we propose the Unified Dexterous Hand Model (UDHM), which maps human and robot hand states into a shared 22-DoF semantic interface. Based on UDHM, we introduce UniDexTok, a retargeting-free state tokenizer that learns embodiment-conditioned discrete tokens from standardized real joint states. UniDexTok provides a unified representation for heterogeneous dexterous hands without relying on retargeting or simulation data. Compared with the recent baseline UniHM, UniDexTok reduces MPJAE from 15.63 degrees to 0.16 degrees and MPJPE from 18.51 mm to 0.18 mm, corresponding to error reductions of 98.98% and 99.03%, respectively. These results improve reconstruction from centimeter-scale to sub-millimeter accuracy. Experiments further show that data from other embodiments improves target-embodiment reconstruction accuracy, demonstrating the benefit of cross-embodiment tokenization. UniDexTok also shows strong zero-shot and few-shot reconstruction ability when new dexterous hands are introduced.

</details>


### [365] [Self-Supervised Relevance Modelling in Autonomous Driving via Counterfactual Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10688)
*Luca Lusvarghi,Javier Gozalvez,Pablo Urbano Hidalgo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Self-supervised counterfactual relevance model for autonomous driving, trained on synthetic causal data, enabling real-time relevance estimation and heatmaps; open-source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce computation, latency, and perception-noise by focusing on objects that matter for driving decisions.

Method: Counterfactual analysis-based self-supervised learning; trained on a synthetic urban scenario causal dataset; outputs per-object relevance scores and relevance heatmaps for real-time use.

Result: Achieves millisecond-level latency for relevance estimation; robust in high-density scenes; relevance heatmaps provide insights into driving policy; relevance model and causal dataset released.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that explicit relevance modeling can improve perception/planning efficiency and transparency; a reproducible framework and dataset to study object relevance in autonomous driving.

Abstract: Autonomous driving relies on computationally intensive perception pipelines to continuously detect and track objects in the surrounding environment. While some objects are key to plan safe and effective maneuvers, others may not be relevant and have no impact on the autonomous vehicle's driving decisions. Focusing on relevant objects allows a more efficient usage of available computational resources, reduces processing latencies, and limits the downstream propagation of perception noise. In this work, we propose a novel self-supervised approach based on counterfactual analysis to develop a relevance model - an AI-based tool that quantifies the relevance of objects for an autonomous vehicle. To demonstrate the potential of the proposed approach, we train a relevance model on a synthetic causal dataset generated in a selected urban scenario. Results show that the relevance model is able to accurately estimate the objects' relevance with millisecond-level latency, enabling real-time relevance estimation also in high-density scenarios. We also show that the relevance model can be used to build relevance heatmaps that offer valuable insights into the autonomous vehicle's driving policy and can be used to proactively inform perception and planning tasks. We openly release both the relevance model and the causal dataset.

</details>


### [366] [Vehicle Prediction Model for Enhanced MPC Path Tracking in Formula Student Driverless](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10732)
*Sebastian Baader,Tamara Bergerhoff,Pascal Meißner,Frank Deinzer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A real-time adaptive prediction model for autonomous race cars combining nominal Kinematic Bicycle Model, offline BLR, and online SGPR to enhance MPC-based path tracking; achieves up to 57% accuracy gain and provides uncertainty estimates from run start.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Race cars operate near handling limits; highly nonlinear dynamics complicate path tracking, especially on narrow tracks. An accurate, online-adaptive predictive model with uncertainty is needed to improve MPC performance.

Method: 3-stage model: (i) nominal Kinematic Bicycle Model, (ii) offline Bayesian Linear Regression to capture residual dynamics using past data, (iii) online Sparse Gaussian Process Regression to adapt residuals to current conditions in real time; data fusion allows past and current data to contribute with manageable compute; uncertainty quantified from the outset; integrated into an MPC-based path tracker.

Result: Prediction accuracy improved up to 57% over baselines; validated on a real Formula Student race car within an MPC path-tracking controller.

Conclusion: The proposed hybrid modeling framework provides accurate, data-driven predictions with quantified uncertainty in real time, enhancing MPC performance for autonomous race cars and is practically applicable.

Abstract: Autonomous race cars, such as in Formula Student Driverless, operate close to their physical handling limits. The resulting highly nonlinear vehicle behavior increases the path tracking complexity, especially on narrow tracks. Model Predictive Control (MPC) is commonly used to address this issue, a method whose performance is closely tied to the accuracy of the underlying prediction model. This paper presents a novel, real-time capable prediction model for autonomous race cars that adjusts to changing conditions by combining information from past runs and the current driving situation. Our model is divided into three consecutive submodels: a nominal Kinematic Bicycle Model, an offline Bayesian Linear Regression (BLR) model, and an online Sparse Gaussian Process Regression (SGPR) model. The proposed approach enables efficient integration of all available data without significantly increasing computational cost, ensuring high prediction accuracy and a quantitative uncertainty assessment right from the start of the run. Compared to existing approaches, an improvement in prediction accuracy of up to 57% was achieved. Further, we successfully demonstrated the practical applicability of the model within an MPC-based path tracking controller on a real Formula Student race car.

</details>


### [367] [Pushing the Performance Limits in Autonomous Racing: Continuous Stability-Aware Adaptive Velocity Planning in Formula Student Driverless](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10733)
*Tamara Bergerhoff,Sebastian Baader,Pascal Meißner,Frank Deinzer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptive velocity planning for autonomous racing using stability-derived friction scaling to build a friction map for optimal speed, yielding substantial lap-time gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time velocity planning under changing track, environmental, and mechanical tolerances; direct estimation of tire-road friction (TRFC) is difficult and error-prone, motivating a robust adaptive approach.

Method: Infer a continuous friction scaling factor indirectly from vehicle stability rather than estimating TRFC; use this factor to generate a continuous friction map; compute the optimal target speed from vehicle and environmental limits using the friction map.

Result: Real-world evaluation on a Formula Student race car showing 35% lap-time improvement over ten laps and an 8% average improvement against a non-adaptive baseline.

Conclusion: A stability-informed, adaptive friction map provides a robust basis for velocity planning in autonomous racing, improving performance under varying conditions; demonstrates practical viability on a real race car.

Abstract: In autonomous racing, especially in competitions such as Formula Student Driverless, precise planning of the target velocity of a race car is crucial for competitive lap times and stable driving behavior. Especially at high speeds, Velocity Planning (VP) is a significant challenge as it has to be performed in real time, taking into account track layouts, environmental influences, mechanical tolerances, and the resulting control inaccuracies. In this paper, we present a novel approach to VP that dynamically adapts to such changing conditions. Instead of estimating the physical Tire-Road Friction Coefficient (TRFC), a continuous scaling factor is inferred indirectly from vehicle stability. This factor not only reflects the effective tire-road interaction but also captures effects of control inaccuracies. From this, we generate a continuous friction map, which serves as a robust, adaptive basis for computing the optimal target speed, accounting for both vehicle and environmental limits. Our proposed approach was evaluated on a real Formula Student race car, showing a lap time improvement of 35 % over ten laps and an average increase of 8 % compared to a non-adaptive approach.

</details>


### [368] [Hand-centric Human-to-Robot Trajectory Transfer from Video Demonstrations via Open-World Contact Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10743)
*Yitian Shi,Di Wen,Zhengqi Han,Zicheng Guo,Yu Hu,Edgar Welte,Kunyu Peng,Rainer Stiefelhagen,Rania Rayyes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HOWTransfer is a hand-centric framework that converts noisy human grasp demonstrations into diverse, contact-aware robotic trajectories without object-specific descriptors, achieving 86% success and outperforming teleoperation in user studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome challenges of noisy hand-object interactions, unseen objects with partial observation, and cross-embodiment discrepancies in learning from human demonstrations.

Method: A hand-centric pipeline that (1) reconstructs temporally consistent 3D hand motion and localizes contact intervals from interaction cues; (2) retargets grasp intent into multi-modal parallel-jaw grasp hypotheses along the recovered wrist trajectory; (3) generates executable robot motions and (4) employs a trajectory editing stage to refine contact alignment and produce diverse variants from a single demo.

Result: Demonstrates accurate contact localization and high-quality robot motion retargeting with 86% success; users prefer HOWTransfer trajectories over teleoperation in blinded studies.

Conclusion: HOWTransfer enables robust cross-domain transfer from human to robot grasp demonstrations without object-centric representations, yielding multiple executable variants and improved success rates across tasks.

Abstract: Learning from human video demonstrations remains challenging due to noisy hand-object interactions, unseen objects with partial observation, and cross-embodiment discrepancy. To address these challenges, we present \textit{HOWTransfer} (\emph{H}and-\emph{O}bject \emph{O}pen-\emph{W}orld Transfer), a hand-centric framework that distills human demonstrations into contact-aware, taxonomy-informed, and diverse robotic trajectories. Instead of relying on object-specific descriptions, vision-language queries, or explicit object-state tracking, \emph{HOWTransfer} recovers temporally consistent 3D hand motion and localizes temporal contact intervals by reasoning over observed hand-object interaction cues. The localized contact onsets are then used to retarget human grasp intent into multi-modal parallel-jaw grasp hypotheses, which are propagated along the recovered wrist trajectory to generate robot-executable motions. Finally, a trajectory editing stage refines contact alignment and produces diverse executable variants from a single demonstration. Experiments across diverse manipulation tasks show that \emph{HOWTransfer} enables accurate contact localization and high-quality robot motion retargeting with $86\%$ success, which is preferred over teleoperated trajectories in a blinded preference study.

</details>


### [369] [ros2probe: Non-intrusive, Kernel-selective Observability for Robot Operating System 2 Middleware](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10746)
*Jisang Yu,Sanghoon Lee,Yeonwoo Choi,Kyung-Joon Park*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Ros2probe is a non-intrusive ROS 2 observation framework that reconstructs the DDS discovery state from discovery packets and uses an in-kernel filter to observe only user-specified topics, avoiding the probe effect and matching standard ROS 2 interfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Observing ROS 2 DDS inter-node communication perturbatively (by joining the domain and deserializing data) changes system behavior and yields biased measurements; a passive, accurate observer is needed.

Method: Reconstructs the full ROS 2 communication state from DDS discovery packets without adding bandwidth cost; implements an in-kernel filter limited to selected topics to lift only those packets; preserves message semantics and interfaces with standard ROS 2 tooling; evaluated across hardware (laptop, Jetson, Raspberry Pi), two DDS implementations, and seven workloads.

Result: Discovery graph fidelity within 0.5% of an unobserved system; domain-joining observers inflate discovery up to 2.6× and drop 38.5% of messages at saturation, while ros2probe drops none; recall of loss reporting is 1.0; observer CPU and memory reduced up to ~7× and ~28×; practical on embedded robots.

Conclusion: Non-intrusive, high-fidelity ROS 2 observation is feasible with ros2probe, delivering accurate visibility with substantially lower perturbation and overhead than traditional domain-joining monitors across diverse hardware and DDS implementations.

Abstract: Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2), the de facto standard middleware framework for robots, runs each robot as a graph of nodes communicating over the Data Distribution Service (DDS), a publish/subscribe substrate. Observing this inter-node communication in real time is essential to robot development, yet it has a price. A tool can receive data only by joining the DDS domain as a subscriber that discovery has matched to the publisher, so observing folds the tool into the system it measures and perturbs it. We define this protocol-inherent perturbation as the observer's probe effect. It inflates the discovery plane, adds deserialization cost on the observer, makes the loss it reports diverge from what the subscriber actually received, and near saturation displaces the subscriber's messages. The only escape, capturing all wire traffic passively, discards ROS 2 message semantics and scales with total traffic, not what is observed. We present ros2probe, a non-intrusive observation framework that removes the probe effect. It reconstructs the full ROS 2 communication state from the domain's discovery packets at no bandwidth cost, then drives an in-kernel filter restricted to the topics the user asks for, lifting only those packets at minimal cost and observing what the real subscriber receives. Its interfaces and recordings match the standard ROS 2 tools. Across three hardware platforms (laptop, Jetson, and Raspberry Pi), two DDS implementations, and seven robot-operation workloads, ros2probe holds the discovery graph within 0.5% of an unobserved system, whereas domain-joining tools inflate discovery up to 2.6$\times$ and drop 38.5% of the subscriber's messages at saturation while ros2probe drops none. It reports loss with a recall of 1.0, cuts observer CPU and memory by up to 7$\times$ and 28$\times$, and stays practical on the embedded robots where existing tools overload the system.

</details>


### [370] [Bridging Semantics and Physical Execution: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Multi-Pair Robotic Assembly](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10808)
*Xinyi Li,Aiguo Song,Linhu Wei,Huijun Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A hierarchical neuro-symbolic framework for robust multi-pair robotic assembly in unstructured environments, combining LLM-derived subgraphs, edge-case discriminators, and divergence-based planning with dynamic behavior trees; demonstrates high offline executability and real-robot success under interference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the gap between cognitive decision-making and physical execution in multi-pair assembly, tackling state-space explosion, knowledge bottlenecks, hallucinations, and topological conflicts in unstructured scenes.

Method: 1) For each pair, generate optimal subgraphs using an LLM guided by barely basic actions to reduce hallucinations. 2) Insert supportive actions for edge cases via a lightweight discriminator. 3) Quantify scene divergence to drive framework extensibility and cross-pair interference resolution. 4) Topologically coordinate augmented subgraphs into global sequences. 5) Use dynamic behavior trees with atomic skills for force-aware execution loop. 6) Evaluate offline on 100 real-world scenes and deploy on UR3 robot.

Result: Offline evaluation on 100 real-world scenes yields 97.00% global executability. Real-robot deployment on a UR3 arm achieves 90% success with 0.5 mm tolerance under strong interference.

Conclusion: The work presents a unified, extensible, and verifiable solution for complex autonomous assembly, combining cognitive planning with robust execution and demonstrating strong practical performance.

Abstract: Multi-pair robotic assembly in unstructured environments faces spatial interference and contact uncertainties. Existing paradigms fail to bridge cognitive decision-making and physical execution, as they either encounter state-space explosion and knowledge bottlenecks or suffer from logical hallucinations and topological conflicts. We propose an end-to-end neuro-symbolic framework that solves the challenge hierarchically: generating optimal subgraphs for each pair, decoupling generality from edge cases, and then resolving cross-pair interferences. Given an eye-on-hand RGB-D assembly scene, the framework extracts semantic instance identity and state while quantifying the scene for divergence calculation. For each pair, optimal subgraph is generated via LLM using barely basic actions to mitigate hallucinations. Supportive actions for edge cases are reasoned and inserted with a lightweight discriminator. Driven by the divergence between the quantified baseline and current scene, it is easily extensible at low cost. Augmented subgraphs are topologically coordinated into global sequences while preserving internal behavioral coherence. Dynamic behavior trees embedding atomic skills close the force-aware execution loop. Offline evaluation on 100 real-world scenes achieves 97.00% global executability, outperforming classical and state-of-the-art planners. Real-robot deployment on a UR3 arm attains 90% success rate with 0.5 mm tolerance under strong interference, demonstrating a unified and verifiable solution for complex autonomous assembly.

</details>


### [371] [IMPACT: Learning Internal-Model Predictive Control for Forceful Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10818)
*Jiawei Gao,Chaoqi Liu,Peilin Wu,Haonan Chen,Yilun Du*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: IMPACT decouples task planning from internal-model predictive control to handle forceful manipulation, yielding better generalization to unseen weights and improved safety/energy efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world manipulation involves forceful interactions and varying object weights; existing imitation-learning approaches with impedance control struggle with generalization or require hardware sensors; a decoupled approach can improve robustness without extra hardware.

Method: Propose IMPACT framework that decouples forceful tasks into task planning and internal-model-based predictive control; utilizes predictive models for contact-rich interactions; likely two modules: a task planner and an internal-model predictive controller.

Result: Simulation and real-world experiments show higher success rates, better generalization to unseen object weights, and improvements in safety and energy efficiency.

Conclusion: Decoupling task planning from internal-model predictive control provides robust, hardware-friendly manipulation across forceful tasks and weights, reducing reliance on specialized sensors.

Abstract: Real-world robotic manipulation tasks often involve forceful interactions with the environment, such as using tools of varying weights, transporting objects with different masses, and performing contact-rich tasks like table wiping. Previous learning-based approaches typically employ imitation learning policies that output target end-effector poses tracked by low-level impedance controllers. In these systems, forceful interactions are either implicitly realized through steady-state tracking errors or explicitly commanded using wrist force/torque or tactile sensors. However, implicit approaches generalize poorly across object weights, while explicit approaches require specialized hardware and increase system complexity. In this work, we propose IMPACT, a framework that decouples these forceful tasks into task-planning and internal-model-based predictive control. Extensive simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves higher success rates and improved generalization to unseen object weights, as well as better safety and energy efficiency.

</details>


### [372] [GUIDE: Goal-Initialized Directional Understanding for End-to-End Visual Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10832)
*Liang Wang,Jin Jin,KanZhong Yao,YiBin Wu,Fangqiang Ding,Jin Wang,Jun Wu,Zhe Sun,Qiuguo Zhu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GUIDE enables end-to-end reinforcement learning for goal-initialized navigation in legged robots, using a spatial anchor predictor to extract egomotion from proprioceptive history and raw depth for local geometry, maintaining long-horizon spatial context without ongoing external goal updates; demonstrated on a quadruped in simulation and real-world clutter and mazes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome reliance on continuous external goal updates and myopic policies under partial observability, enabling fully end-to-end mobile autonomy with internal spatial memory.

Method: Propose GUIDE, an end-to-end RL framework with (1) a spatial anchor predictor that uses multi-frequency proprioceptive history to derive egomotion representations for persistent spatial context, and (2) raw depth streams to capture local geometry. Operates in goal-initialized settings (goal provided only at episode start) without external goal guidance or maps.

Result: Evaluations in both simulation and real-world on a quadruped show GUIDE learns robust egomotion and directional awareness, enabling a fully end-to-end deployed policy to navigate safely through dense clutter and structured mazes without further goal guidance or prior maps.

Conclusion: End-to-end reinforcement learning with internal directional awareness and persistent egomotion memory is viable for goal-initialized navigation, enabling robust autonomous navigation in partially observable and cluttered environments.

Abstract: Learning-based visual navigation for legged robots typically relies on continuous goal updates from hierarchical state estimation to provide a persistent directional reference. This reliance incurs additional sensory and computational overhead and deviates from fully end-to-end mobile autonomy. Furthermore, under partial observability, policies are prone to learn myopic behaviors, easily becoming trapped in dead ends and complex structural layouts. To address these limitations, we investigate a goal-initialized navigation setting, where the target is provided only once at the beginning of an episode, requiring the robot to operate based on intrinsic spatial memory without subsequent goal updates from external modules. In this work, we propose GUIDE, a fully end-to-end reinforcement learning framework designed to cultivate internal directional awareness. Specifically, GUIDE incorporates a spatial anchor predictor that leverages multi-frequency proprioceptive history to extract egomotion representations, thereby maintaining a persistent long-horizon spatial context for navigation. Concurrently, it utilizes raw depth streams to perceive local environmental geometry. We evaluate the proposed framework across both simulation and real-world scenarios on a quadruped robot. Experiments show that GUIDE learns reliable egomotion and directional awareness, enabling a fully end-to-end deployed policy to safely navigate through dense clutter and structured mazes without subsequent goal guidance or prior maps.

</details>


### [373] [Gradient based Bilevel for Inverse Optimal Control, a Riemannian approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10841)
*Ahmed-Manaf Dahmani,Vincent Bonnet,David Daney,François Charpillet*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RIOC reframes inverse optimal control as optimization on the manifold of optimal trajectories, enabling stable projection-based recovery with significant speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical bilevel IOC is computationally expensive; projection-based methods suffer from numerical instability due to constraint qualifications. A geometric view may improve scalability and reliability.

Method: Identify the IOC feasible set under optimality as a manifold; formulate IOC as optimization on this manifold; develop Riemannian Inverse Optimal Control (RIOC) that projects observed trajectories onto the manifold while preserving feasibility by construction.

Result: On real human arm trajectories, RIOC achieves comparable or better reconstruction accuracy than bilevel IOC and reduces computation time by about 4x.

Conclusion: Geometric optimization on manifolds offers scalable and reliable IOC for robotics and human motion analysis.

Abstract: Inverse Optimal Control (IOC) aims to recover the cost function that explains observed trajectories as solutions of an optimal control problem. Classical IOC formulations rely on bilevel optimization, which repeatedly solves a nested optimal control problem and quickly becomes computationally prohibitive for realistic systems. Recent projection-based approaches offer a promising alternative but suffer from numerical instability when solved with gradient-based methods due to violations of standard constraint qualifications.
  In this paper, we show that these difficulties stem from the geometric structure of the IOC feasible set. We demonstrate that the set of trajectories satisfying the optimality conditions naturally forms a manifold and reformulate IOC as an optimization problem on this manifold. Based on this insight, we propose a Riemannian Inverse Optimal Control (RIOC) method that projects observed trajectories onto the manifold of optimal solutions while preserving feasibility by construction. Experiments on real human arm trajectories show that the proposed method achieves comparable or better reconstruction accuracy than classical bilevel IOC while reducing computation time by about a factor of four. These results highlight the potential of geometric optimization methods to improve the scalability and reliability of IOC for robotics and human motion analysis.

</details>


### [374] [An Exposure-Time-Aligned Primary-Path Architecture for Autonomous-Driving ECUs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10856)
*Toru Saito,Yuki Hagura,Tatsuya Konishi,Satoru Mizusawa,Takumi Yajima*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Architectural framework enabling coexisting modular and end-to-end (E2E) paths on production-grade SoCs through Primary-Path, Exposure-Time-Aligned, and Co-Path Coexistence principles, achieving closed-loop latency of 296 ms within a 350 ms budget and enabling staged migration of E2E across production AD-ECUs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the transitional phase in autonomous driving where production vehicles rely on modular multi-NN pipelines while E2E approaches mature. Existing approaches like egalitarian late fusion are compute-inefficient and lack a natural unit for staged E2E substitution, impeding safe, progressive migration.

Method: Introduce three design principles: (i) Primary-Path to prioritize a primary perception chain within a single SoC pair, (ii) Exposure-Time-Aligned to tag data with the primary sensor's exposure time and trigger fusion based on matched exposure rather than fixed cycles, and (iii) Co-Path Coexistence to allow an E2E output path to co-run with the modular pipeline within the same exposure cycle. Implemented on a Dual-SoC production AD-ECU to demonstrate latency performance; E2E runs as a shadow path and can be expanded over time.

Result: Latency from camera shutter to planner output averaged 296 ms, within the 350 ms design budget. Under Co-Path Coexistence, the modular pipeline is primary at production launch while the E2E path runs in shadow and is incrementally expanded as evidence accumulates.

Conclusion: The proposed design principles enable safe, staged migration by allowing parallel E2E and modular pipelines with synchronized timing, meeting latency constraints and facilitating progressive E2E scope expansion in real vehicles.

Abstract: While end-to-end (E2E) autonomous driving has become the dominant research direction, production vehicles continue to rely on modular multi-NN pipelines for a non-trivial transitional period. The subject of this paper is the design of an architecture that, during this phase, supports a modular pipeline and an E2E path side by side and embeds a path for staged migration. Transplanted to a production SoC, egalitarian late fusion is compute-inefficient and offers no natural unit for staged E2E substitution. As an alternative, we propose three design principles: (i) Primary-Path, which explicitly selects a primary perception chain and prioritizes its enclosure within a single SoC pair over the non-critical paths (ii) Exposure-Time-Aligned, which propagates the primary sensor's exposure time $τ_{\rm exp}$ as a tag along the chain and event-drives the fusion node on matched $τ_{\rm exp}$ rather than a fixed cycle and (iii) Co-Path Coexistence, which, building on (i) and (ii), lets an E2E output path co-run with the modular pipeline within the same $τ_{\rm exp}$ cycle. On a Dual-SoC production AD-ECU, the implementation closes camera-shutter to planner-output latency at a mean of 296 ms within the 350 ms design budget. Under (iii), the modular pipeline is primary at production launch and the E2E path runs as shadow on real vehicles, and the E2E scope is expanded as evaluation evidence accumulates.

</details>


### [375] [Embodiment-conditioned Generalist Control for Multirotor Aerial Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10857)
*Orestis Konstantaropoulos,Welf Rehberg,Mihir Kulkarni,Kostas Alexis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A generalist, morphology-agnostic multirotor controller using a single network conditioned on an embodiment descriptor; trained via PPO in fast simulation and capable of zero-shot transfer across diverse hexarotor configurations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable a single control policy to govern heterogeneous multirotor morphologies without retraining, reducing engineering effort and enabling rapid deployment on different platforms.

Method: Train a compact network with Proximal Policy Optimization, conditioning on a physics-grounded embodiment descriptor: a mass- and inertia-normalized control allocation matrix that maps motor thrusts to body-frame accelerations. The policy is trained over a broad distribution of configurations (planar and non-planar, symmetric and asymmetric) using a Warp-based dynamics simulator; training time ~5 minutes on an RTX 3090.

Result: Embodiment conditioning enables robust generalist control across arbitrary morphologies. Demonstrated zero-shot real-world transfer to three hexarotor systems: a planar robot, a partially symmetric non-planar system, and a random asymmetric non-planar configuration.

Conclusion: A single, embodiment-conditioned policy can generalize across a wide range of multirotor morphologies, enabling fast training and direct transfer to diverse hardware without per-configuration retraining.

Abstract: We present a generalist position control policy capable of controlling arbitrary multirotor configurations of a certain rotor count (e.g., hexarotors or quadrotors) with a single set of network weights. The policy is conditioned on a physics-grounded embodiment descriptor: a mass and inertia-normalized control allocation matrix that captures how mass-normalized motor thrusts generate linear and angular accelerations in the body-frame. To train the policy, we sample from a broad distribution of arbitrary multirotor configurations, including non-planar and asymmetric systems, and optimize a single, compact network using Proximal Policy Optimization. Training requires only five minutes on an RTX 3090 GPU using a custom NVIDIA Warp-based dynamics simulator. Through extensive simulation experiments, we show that embodiment conditioning enables robust generalist control across arbitrary morphologies. We demonstrate zero-shot real-world transfer of this generalist policy on three diverse hexarotor systems, including a planar robot, a partially symmetric non-planar system, and a random asymmetric, non-planar configuration.

</details>


### [376] [MV-Actor: Aligning Multi-View Semantics and Spatial Awareness for Bimanual Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10899)
*Yinchen Tian,Huan Li,Muyao Peng,Xi Wang,Yan Wang,You Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MV-Actor introduces a unified multi-view perception framework for bimanual manipulation, leveraging cross-view semantic interaction, semantic-to-spatial grounding via token interaction, and a depth repair module. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on PerAct2 and strong real-world robustness against viewpoint changes and depth noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-view policies either process views independently or fuse shallowly, limiting semantic sharing and spatial awareness. A unified semantic-spatial representation could improve perception fidelity and manipulation reliability in bimanual setups.

Method: 1) Multi-view Semantic Interaction to share high-level semantic features across views. 2) Semantic-Spatial Token Interaction grounds semantic features with spatial-aware tokens derived from a feed-forward reconstruction model, enhancing spatial awareness. 3) Guided Metric Depth Repair refines degraded sensor depth to provide reliable metric anchors under consumer-grade noise. 4) Evaluations on the PerAct2 bimanual benchmark (simulation) and real-world tests with viewpoint variation and depth noise.

Result: In simulation, MV-Actor achieves 87.8% average success rate on PerAct2, surpassing prior methods. In real-world tests, it outperforms RGB and RGB-D baselines under frequent viewpoint changes and depth noise, confirming benefits of shared semantic perception and reliable spatial grounding.

Conclusion: MV-Actor demonstrates that unifying semantic and spatial representations across views improves bimanual manipulation robustness and performance, with depth repair further stabilizing metric grounding under noisy depth sensors.

Abstract: Robotic manipulation has been widely applied in industrial scenarios. Compared with single-arm manipulation, bimanual manipulation is equipped with multiple cameras to capture information from different viewpoints. However, existing multi-view policies encode each view independently or fuse view features shallowly, resulting in limited sharing semantic perception and unreliable spatial awareness. In this paper, we propose \textbf{MV-Actor}, a multi-view perception framework that builds a unified semantic-spatial representation for bimanual manipulation. First, MV-Actor performs Multi-view Semantic Interaction to share semantic perception across views. Then it uses Semantic-Spatial Token Interaction to ground visual semantics with feed-forward reconstruction model features and acquire reliable spatial awareness. Finally, a Guided Metric Depth Repair module refines degraded sensor depth to provide more reliable metric anchors under consumer-grade depth noise. In simulation experiments conducted on the PerAct2 bimanual benchmark, MV-Actor achieves a state-of-the-art average success rate of 87.8\%. In real-world evaluations with more frequent viewpoint changes and unstable consumer-grade depth, MV-Actor outperforms both RGB and RGB-D baselines, further demonstrating the benefit of sharing semantic perception and reliable spatial awareness for bimanual manipulation.

</details>


### [377] [AgniNav: Configuration-Driven Cross-Embodiment Local Planning for Robot Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10903)
*Tianhao Zang,Siwei Cheng,Haidong Huang,Shanze Wang,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AgniNav is a monocular local navigation framework that enables cross-embodiment transfer by standardizing a four-parameter safety envelope (collision-relevant height, front length, rear length, half width) to jointly condition perception (image-to-scan) and planning (dimension-aware local planner), enabling zero-retraining across wheeled, quadruped, and humanoid platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-based navigation policies are typically tightly coupled to a robot's body and footprint, requiring retraining or depth sensors for transfer between platforms. A configuration-driven envelope could decouple perception from platform specifics and enable cross-embodiment deployment.

Method: Train a height-conditioned image-to-scan network that predicts a one-dimensional collision-relevant pseudo-laserscan from a monocular image. Use the other three footprint parameters to configure a dimension-aware local planner for collision checking. Training uses height-conditioned column-minimum scan labels derived from color-depth pairs, allowing the same image to supervise different envelopes without robot-specific data.

Result: Real-robot experiments across three platforms (Turtlebot2, Unitree Go2, Accelerated Evolution K1) achieved high success rates (39/40, 18/20, 18/20) with low collisions (0/40, 1/20, 2/20) at 30 Hz on Jetson Orin.

Conclusion: AgniNav, to the best of the authors' knowledge, is the first monocular local-navigation framework to jointly condition perception and planning on a shared collision-envelope configuration, enabling zero-retraining deployment across wheeled, quadruped, and humanoid platforms.

Abstract: Monocular local navigation is attractive for lightweight robots, but existing vision-based policies often couple perception to a specific body, camera height, and footprint, making transfer from wheeled bases to legged platforms dependent on retraining or active depth hardware. This paper introduces AgniNav, a configuration-driven local navigation framework that standardizes cross-embodiment transfer at the collision-envelope level. Each robot is specified by a measurable four-parameter safety envelope: collision-relevant height, front length, rear length, and half width. The height parameter conditions an image-to-scan network to predict a one-dimensional, collision-relevant pseudo-laserscan from a monocular color image, while the remaining footprint parameters configure a dimension-aware local planner for collision checking. Training uses height-conditioned column-minimum scan labels generated from paired color-depth data, allowing the same image to supervise different safety envelopes without collecting robot-specific data. To the best of our knowledge, AgniNav is the first monocular local-navigation framework that jointly conditions perception and planning on a shared collision-envelope configuration for zero-retraining deployment across wheeled, quadruped, and humanoid platforms. Real-robot experiments on a Turtlebot2, Unitree Go2, and Accelerated Evolution K1 achieve 39/40, 18/20, and 18/20 successes with 0/40, 1/20, and 2/20 collisions, respectively, while running at 30 Hz on Jetson Orin.

</details>


### [378] [Task Robustness via Re-Labelling Vision-Action Robot Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10918)
*Artur Kuramshin,Özgür Aslan,Cyrus Neary,Glen Berseth*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TREAD uses large Vision-Language Models to augment robotics data by re-labelling and decomposing demonstrations into grounded language-action pairs, improving planning and language-conditioned policy generalization without extra data collection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotics datasets lack sufficient linguistic and action sequence diversity, limiting instruction following and generalization in scalable manipulation policies.

Method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) generate semantic sub-tasks from original instructions and scenes using a pretrained VLM; (2) segment demonstration videos conditioned on these sub-tasks; (3) produce diverse, object-property–aware instructions to decompose long demonstrations into grounded language-action pairs, plus linguistically diverse text goals for robustness, all without collecting new data.

Result: On LIBERO, policies trained on TREAD-augmented data show improved performance on unseen tasks/goals, with gains in planning generalization via trajectory decomposition and language-conditioned generalization from increased linguistic diversity.

Conclusion: TREAD provides a scalable, data-efficient way to boost robot learning by leveraging VLMs to expand linguistic-action coverage, yielding more robust and generalizable policies without additional data collection.

Abstract: The recent trend in scaling models for robot learning has resulted in impressive policies that can perform various manipulation tasks and generalize to novel scenarios. However, these policies continue to struggle with following instructions, likely due to the limited linguistic and action sequence diversity in existing robotics datasets. This paper introduces Task Robustness via Re-Labelling Vision-Action Robot Data (TREAD), a scalable framework that leverages large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to augment existing robotics datasets without additional data collection, harnessing the transferable knowledge embedded in these models. Our approach leverages a pretrained VLM through three stages: generating semantic sub-tasks from original instruction labels and initial scenes, segmenting demonstration videos conditioned on these sub-tasks, and producing diverse instructions that incorporate object properties, effectively decomposing longer demonstrations into grounded language-action pairs. We further enhance robustness by augmenting the data with linguistically diverse versions of the text goals. Evaluations on LIBERO demonstrate that policies trained on our augmented datasets exhibit improved performance on novel, unseen tasks and goals. Our results show that TREAD enhances both planning generalization through trajectory decomposition and language-conditioned policy generalization through increased linguistic diversity.

</details>


### [379] [AllDayNav: Lifelong Navigation via Real-World Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10927)
*Hang Yin,Yinan Liang,Jiazhao Zhang,Jiahang Liu,Minghan Li,Zhizheng Zhang,He Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AllDayNav proposes lifelong embodied navigation by encoding scene dynamics into billion-parameter models via reinforcement learning, using a self-evolving multimodal memory to store and update visual keyframes, semantic descriptions, and temporal context, which autonomously generates open-vocabulary instructions, image goals, and structured rewards. It achieves near-100% success and outperforms map-based, VLM, and RL baselines, suggesting implicit memory-driven RL as a scalable alternative to explicit mapping for lifelong navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lifelong navigation in dynamic environments requires persistent scene understanding from fragmentary observations without relying on explicit maps or scene graphs, which fail to generalize to unstructured settings.

Method: AllDayNav encodes scene dynamics into the parameters of a large multimodal model via reinforcement learning. It uses a self-evolving multimodal memory that maintains and updates visual keyframes, semantic descriptions, and temporal context, and it autonomously generates open-vocabulary navigation instructions, image goals, and structured rewards to guide learning.

Result: Experiments in both synthetic and real-world environments show high robustness across cross-room, cross-episode, and cross-task scenarios. AllDayNav achieves success rates approaching 100% and consistently surpasses strong map-based, VLM, and RL baselines in path efficiency and robustness.

Conclusion: Implicit, memory-driven reinforcement learning is a scalable alternative to explicit mapping for reliable lifelong navigation, with a memory module enabling persistent scene understanding and generalization across changing environments.

Abstract: Lifelong embodied navigation in dynamic environments requires robots to form persistent scene understanding from fragmentary observations, which remains difficult for existing methods that rely on explicit maps or scene graphs and struggle to generalize beyond structured settings. We propose AllDayNav, a lifelong self-learning navigation framework that implicitly encodes scene dynamics into the billion-scale parameters of a large model via reinforcement learning, powered by a self-evolving multimodal memory that maintains and updates visual keyframes, semantic descriptions, and temporal context while autonomously generating open-vocabulary instructions, image goals, and structured rewards. Experiments in both synthetic and real-world environments across cross-room, cross-episode, and cross-task scenarios show that AllDayNav achieves success rates approaching $100\%$ and consistently surpasses strong map-based, VLM, and RL baselines in path efficiency and robustness, demonstrating implicit, memory-driven reinforcement learning as a scalable alternative to explicit mapping for reliable lifelong navigation.

</details>


### [380] [Resilient Navigation for Autonomous Farm Robots by Leveraging Jerk-Augmented Models with IMU-Only Disturbance Rejection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10971)
*Batu Candan,Mohammed Atallah,Simone Servadio,Saeed Arabi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A jerk-augmented EKF with MTF adaptation improves robustness of autonomous agricultural robot state estimation under sensor outages and vibrations, outperforming baseline EKF in 3D position RMSE on real Salin247 data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address degradation of navigation accuracy due to sensor outages (GNSS/LiDAR/Visual) and high-frequency off-road vibrations. Traditional EKF assumes constant noise and cannot rapidly adapt to disturbances, risking degraded dead-reckoning.

Method: Develop a jerk-augmented Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) combined with a Multiple Tuning Factor (MTF) adaptation that dynamically adjusts the measurement covariance in real time, enabling rapid handling of disturbances and outliers; evaluate on real-world data from a Salin247 autonomous robot.

Result: The proposed approach significantly reduces 3D position RMSE compared with baseline EKF models, indicating improved dead-reckoning performance under challenging sensing conditions.

Conclusion: Integrating jerk-augmentation with MTF-adaptive noise modeling yields a robust navigation algorithm for agricultural robotics, particularly beneficial under sensor outages and high-vibration environments; further validation and potential extension to other sensors could enhance robustness.

Abstract: Precise state estimation for navigation of autonomous agricultural robots is often compromised by sensor outages (GNSS/LiDAR/Visual) and high-frequency vibrations inherent in off-road environments. This paper proposes a robust navigation algorithm based on a jerk-augmented Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) integrated with a Multiple Tuning Factor (MTF) adaptation method. Unlike standard EKF approaches that assume constant measurement noise, our method dynamically adjusts the measurement covariance matrix in real-time, allowing the system to cope with sudden disturbances and sensor outliers. We evaluate the algorithm using real-world data from a Salin247 autonomous robot. Results demonstrate that jerk-augmentation combined with MTF adaptation significantly reduces 3D position Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) compared to baseline EKF models, providing superior dead-reckoning capabilities.

</details>


### [381] [Language-Driven Cost Optimization for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10974)
*Diego Martinez-Baselga,Khaled Mustafa,Javier Alonso-Mora*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LLM-driven, user-adaptive cost design for autonomous driving using a risk-aware MPPI controller, with human-in-the-loop validation and iterative feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackling manual tuning of motion-planning cost parameters to enable end-user preferences and adaptability to varying traffic scenarios.

Method: Leverage a Large Language Model to translate structured scenario descriptions and natural-language queries into MPPI controller parameters for risk-aware motion planning; include a human-in-the-loop validation stage for non-technical description and agreement; support pre/post deployment user feedback for iterative refinement; evaluate via simulation across realistic driving scenarios.

Result: Demonstrates that language-driven parameter generation can induce behavioral changes aligned with user requirements in simulation, bridging intelligent control and end users.

Conclusion: The framework shows feasibility of intuitive, user-driven adaptation of autonomous-vehicle behavior through language-guided cost design with human oversight.

Abstract: The driving behavior of autonomous vehicles is typically governed by the cost function of their motion planner, which encodes objectives such as speed tracking, smoothness, lane keeping, and collision avoidance. However, tuning the parameters that shape this cost function is a challenging task that requires technical expertise, limiting the vehicle's ability to adapt to evolving traffic scenarios or end-user preferences. This work presents a language-driven framework for adaptive cost design in autonomous driving. A Large Language Model (LLM) interprets structured scenario descriptions and natural language user queries to generate the parameters applied to a risk-aware Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) controller. The system incorporates a human-in-the-loop validation stage in which the proposed behavioral changes are described in non-technical language and confirmed prior to deployment. Users may additionally provide feedback either before or after deployment, enabling iterative refinement of the vehicle's motion behavior. The framework is evaluated across multiple queries in realistic driving scenarios to assess its effectiveness. Simulation results demonstrate that the method successfully induces behavioral changes that align with the intended requirements in an intuitive manner, thereby bridging the gap between intelligent vehicle control systems and end users.

</details>


### [382] [Multi-UAV Active Sensing with Information Gain-based Planning and Belief Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10986)
*S. Habibi,L. Marques*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Real-world validation of a multi-UAV active sensing framework for probabilistic binary terrain mapping using IGbIPP, with comparisons to baselines; results show uncertainty-driven planning improves entropy, mapping accuracy, and robustness under different fusion rules and weights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome UAV sensing limitations (limited flight time, sensing uncertainty) and balance spatial coverage with observation accuracy in active sensing, especially for terrain mapping in precision agriculture.

Method: A probabilistic belief map is constructed with spatial dependencies modeled via a factor graph. UAVs use Information Gain based Informative Path Planning (IGbIPP) for decision making. Evaluation against Random Walk and Sweep baselines on synthetic terrains and real UAV-derived agricultural imagery. Analysis includes spatial correlation weights and probabilistic belief-fusion rules (Bayesian, log-odds, Dempster–Shafer) for multi-UAV information sharing.

Result: IGbIPP reduces entropy and mapping error more effectively than baselines. A wider field of view improves real-world coverage and map accuracy. Simple equal or biased spatial weights can be more robust than adaptive weights. Bayesian, log-odds, and Dempster–Shafer fusion achieve the best cooperative mapping performance.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-driven planning, sensing geometry, spatial modeling, and probabilistic fusion are crucial for effective real-world UAV-based active sensing in distributed environments.

Abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are increasingly used for active sensing and information gathering in spatially distributed environments. Their performance, however, is constrained by limited flight time, sensing uncertainty, and the trade-off between spatial coverage and observation accuracy. This paper presents a real-world validation of a multi-UAV active sensing framework for probabilistic binary terrain mapping, with precision agriculture used as the application case. The environment is represented as a probabilistic belief map, where spatial dependencies are modeled through a factor-graph formulation.
