UAV decision making is guided by Information Gain based Informative Path Planning (IGbIPP), and the approach is compared with Random Walk and Sweep coverage path planning baselines using both synthetic terrains and real UAV-derived agricultural imagery. The study also evaluates spatial correlation weights and several probabilistic belief-fusion rules for multi-UAV information sharing. Results show that IGbIPP reduces entropy and mapping error more effectively than the baselines, while a wider field of view improves real-world coverage and map accuracy. The results further show that simple equal or biased spatial weights can be more robust than adaptive weights, and that Bayesian, log-odds, and Dempster--Shafer fusion achieve the best cooperative mapping performance. These findings highlight the importance of uncertainty-driven planning, sensing geometry, spatial modeling, and probabilistic fusion for real-world UAV-based active sensing.

</details>


### [383] [Diffusion Forcing Planner: History-Annealed Planning with Time-Dependent Guidance for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11019)
*Zehan Zhang,Neng Zhang,Yaoyi Li,Jia Cai,Zhiling Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A diffusion-based planner (DFP) stabilizes learning-based motion planning by jointly denoising history and future trajectory segments with segment-specific noise, enabling controllable, continuous plans via classifier-free guidance and history annealing, evaluated with theoretical and ablation analyses on nuPlan.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Temporal inconsistency in learning-based motion planners caused by small frame perturbations; static history conditioning tends to copy past patterns rather than adapt to context; need a method that uses history to guide planning without constraining adaptability.

Method: Decompose full trajectory into history, current, and future segments; assign independent noise levels to each segment; perform a heterogeneous joint diffusion process denoising both history and future; at inference, apply classifier-free guidance to steer future sampling using annealed history for controllable influence.

Result: Closed-loop evaluation and ablations on nuPlan show DFP achieves competitive performance while producing continuous, stable, and controllable motion plans in complex driving scenarios.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based planning with history-guided control can stabilize trajectories and maintain adaptability; CFG with history-annealing provides controllable future sampling; the approach generalizes beyond static conditioning and offers a viable route for robust, comfortable autonomous driving planning.

Abstract: Learning-based motion planners, despite recent progress, often suffer from temporal inconsistency. Small perturbations across frames can accumulate into unstable trajectories, degrading comfort and safety in closed-loop driving. Several methods attempt to inject history as a static conditioning signal to stabilize outputs, only to induce the planner to copy historical patterns instead of adapting to environment contexts. To address this limitation, we propose Diffusion Forcing Planner (DFP), a diffusion-based planning framework driven by history-guided control. Specifically, DFP decomposes the full trajectory into history, current and future segments, and assign independent noise levels to each segment. The model jointly denoises the historical and the future segments, enforcing a heterogeneous joint diffusion process. At inference, classifier-free guidance (CFG) is applied to steer future sampling using annealed history in a controllable manner. Closed-loop evaluation and comprehensive ablations on nuPlan show that DFP achieves competitive performance while producing continuous, stable, and controllable motion plans in complex driving scenarios.

</details>


### [384] [A Spiking Neural Architecture for Coordinating Arm and Locomotor Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11034)
*Lea Steffen,Kathryn Simone,Graeme Damberger,Travis DeWolf,Hudson Ly,Chris Eliasmith*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: First integrated spike-based controller coordinating bipedal locomotion and arm manipulation on a full-scale humanoid in simulation, using NEF/SPA with a basal-ganglia gate, enabling task switching and real-time control, advancing toward neuromorphic deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the gap of jointly controlling locomotion and manipulation in SNN-based humanoids to enable cohesive, energy-efficient, real-time behavior on neuromorphic hardware.

Method: Co-simulation of Nengo (neural control using the Neural Engineering Framework and Semantic Pointer Architecture) with Isaac Sim for physics, incorporating a biologically grounded spiking basal ganglia model for high-level action selection; demonstrations include target reaching, digit drawing, path-following, and switch between walking and arm control.

Result: Demonstrated successful integration with target reaching, continuous digit drawing, path-following locomotion, and switching between walking and arm control via basal-ganglia disinhibition; claims first integrated spike-based controller on a full-scale humanoid in simulation.

Conclusion: Provides the first integrated spike-based controller combining bipedal locomotion and arm control on a full-scale humanoid in simulation, with full spike-based implementation supporting future deployment on low-power neuromorphic hardware.

Abstract: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) coupled with neuromorphic hardware offer energy-efficient solutions for humanoid robot control. However, existing SNN-based motor control systems address bipedal locomotion and arm control in isolation, leaving integrated control of both unaddressed. We present a spiking architecture that coordinates force-based arm control and bipedal locomotion in a simulated humanoid, using the Neural Engineering Framework (NEF) and Semantic Pointer Architecture (SPA). High-level action selection between locomotor and arm control is mediated by a biologically grounded spiking basal ganglia model. We validate the system through co-simulation of Nengo, for the neural control, and Isaac Sim, demonstrating successful target reaching, continuous digit drawing, path-following locomotion, and finally, switching between walking and arm control via basal ganglia disinhibition. To our knowledge, this is the first integrated spiking controller to combine bipedal locomotion and arm control on a full-scale humanoid platform. The full spike-based implementation enables future deployment on low-power neuromorphic hardware.

</details>


### [385] [Generation of Diverse and Functional Robot Designs using Superquadrics Parametrisation and Quality-Diversity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11037)
*Leni Le Goff,Simon Smith,Emma Hart*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SQs (superquadrics) as a compact, interpretable morphology representation paired with MAP-Elites improves quality-diversity in robotic design, outperforming CPPN-based morphologies and standard EAs across two tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prevent premature convergence and promote broad exploration in robot design optimization by maintaining morphological diversity and leveraging an interpretable geometry representation.

Method: Introduce superquadrics (SQs) as the morphology generator. Compare with Compositional Pattern Producing Networks (CPPNs). Run standard evolutionary algorithms (EAs) and MAP-Elites (a quality-diversity method) to evolve bodies, in two test environments. Evaluate diversity and functionality using QD-score and related metrics.

Result: SQs combined with MAP-Elites achieved the highest quality-diversity score across both environments, increasing both the diversity and functional variety of generated robots compared to CPPN-based and EA baselines.

Conclusion: A compact, interpretable geometric representation (SQs) together with an explicit diversity mechanism (MAP-Elites) effectively explores large design spaces, yielding more diverse and functional robot morphologies; this approach enhances design exploration and potentially informs future morphology optimization.

Abstract: Generative design of robots requires navigating a vast search-space, encompassing physical configurations and behavioural parameters. Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs) have shown promising results, but often converge prematurely to a small set of sub-optimal designs. Most EAs fail to maintain sufficient diversity in the population that would allow the discovery of distinct functional robots. To counter premature convergence, we introduce a superquadrics-based representation (SQs) for robot bodies. SQs are interpretable, compact and computationally efficient mathematical representations of 3D geometrical shapes that can be tuned to specific design-spaces. To encourage morphological diversity, we combine this representation with a quality-diversity (QD) algorithm (MAP-Elites). We compare SQs and Compositional Pattern Producing Networks representations as generators of morphologies, combining them with standard EAs and MAP-Elites. In two test environments, we find that using SQs to generate morphology in conjunction with the MAP-Elites algorithm reaches the highest QD-score across both environments, maximising diversity of design and functionality of generated robots. The findings highlight the benefits of using a compact and interpretable geometric representation for exploring a complex design-space and suggest that combining SQs with an explicit diversity mechanism increases the quality and number of designs generated.

</details>


### [386] [A Distributed Multi-UGV Exploration Framework With Loop-Aware Planning and Descriptor-Aided Localization in Resource-Limited Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11088)
*Zhiwei Li,Haiou Liu,Xijun Zhao,Ji Li,Yingze Wang,Boyang Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Distributed loop-closure aided exploration for multi-UGVs in GPS-denied, unknown environments using descriptor-based cross-robot place recognition, uncertainty-aware loop selection, and loop-aware planning to maintain global consistency and improve efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate localization drift and map inconsistency in cooperative UGV exploration without prior maps, biased by limited bandwidth and lack of GPS.

Method: Lightweight LiDAR global descriptor with range-image prealignment for cross-UGV place recognition; verified loop closures to keep global trajectories and a sparse topological map; uncertainty-aware loop-closure scoring to choose planning anchors; fully distributed optimization for global consistency and local route refinement; hierarchical planning combining global task allocation with local planning.

Result: Loop-closure module achieves AR@1/AR@1% of 89.9%/95.5%, distributed optimization reduces absolute trajectory error and two-way communication volume, and overall exploration time and travel distance decrease by 15% and 14% respectively, compared with an mTSP baseline; validated in simulations and real-UGV experiments.

Conclusion: Coupling descriptor-based inter-UGV loop closures with loop-aware hierarchical planning and distributed optimization yields robust, efficient cooperative exploration in GPS-denied, bandwidth-limited environments, with improved localization consistency and reduced exploration time and distance.

Abstract: Robust and efficient cooperative exploration with multiple unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs) in unknown, GPSdenied, and bandwidth-limited environments without prior maps remains challenging, as localization drift degrades map consistency and induces redundant coverage. This paper presents a fully distributed exploration framework that couples descriptoraided inter-UGV loop closure with loop-aware hierarchical planning while enabling autonomous localization and exploration. We develop a lightweight LiDAR global descriptor with range-image prealignment to enable robust cross-UGV place recognition under large yaw and lateral variations, and use verified loop closures to maintain globally consistent trajectories and a sparse topological representation. We further introduce an uncertainty-aware crossUGV loop-closure selection module that scores candidate loop closures under pose uncertainty and retains high-utility loop closures as planning anchors for global task allocation and local route refinement. Simulations and real-UGV experiments show that the loop-closure module achieves AR@1/AR@1% of 89.9%/95.5%, distributed optimization reduces absolute trajectory error, the system substantially reduces two-way communication volume, and the overall framework reduces exploration time and travel distance by 15% and 14%, respectively, compared with an mTSP baseline.

</details>


### [387] [RoboNaldo: Accurate, Stable and Powerful Humanoid Soccer Shooting via Motion-Guided Curriculum Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11092)
*Yichao Zhong,Yidan Lu,Yuhang Lu,Tianyang Tang,Haoguang Mai,Yixuan Pan,Tianyu Li,Li Chen,Jingbo Wang,Zhongyu Li,Peng Lu,Hongyang Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoboNaldo is a three-stage curriculum RL framework that uses a single human kick as a scaffold to progressively learn high-impulse humanoid shooting, achieving substantial improvements in free-kick accuracy and shot speed in both simulation and real-world tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of fixed-reference, motion-tracking RL and the exploration challenges of reward-driven RL for high-impulse, whole-body kicking. The goal is adaptable, stable kicking across varied ball positions and timings, with promising sim-to-real transfer.

Method: A three-stage curriculum RL: (1) learn a stable whole-body kicking prior; (2) adapt to stationary free-kicks with random ball positions; (3) extend to moving-ball shooting via a locomotion-command and kick-trigger interface. A high-level heuristic planner guides this interface during training, while alternative high-level controllers can drive the same low-level policy at inference. A single human-kick reference serves as a scaffold to bias learning toward shooting performance.

Result: Simulation: free-kick shot error reduced by 48.6%; shoot velocity increased to 2.96x baselines. Real-world (Unitree G1) with onboard perception: average target shooting error 0.73 m (free-kick) and 0.86 m (moving-ball) from 3 m; post-contact ball velocity 13.10 m/s, about 59–71% of professional open-play shot speed.

Conclusion: The three-stage, human-scaffolded curriculum RL enables stable, high-impulse humanoid shooting with improved accuracy and velocity, demonstrating promising sim-to-real transfer and flexible inference steering via high-level controllers.

Abstract: Elite humanoid soccer shooting requires whole-body stability, high-impulse whole-body interactions, and accuracy to targets. Motion tracking-driven reinforcement learning (RL) provides stability in whole-body movement coordination, but a fixed reference makes it hard to adapt to varied ball positions and strike timings; in contrast, task reward-driven RL struggles to explore and discover valid kicks from scratch. We therefore introduce RoboNaldo, a three-stage motion-guided curriculum RL framework for high-impulse humanoid interaction. A single human-kick reference is used as a scaffold and progressively shifts optimization towards shooting performance. The curriculum first learns a stable whole-body kicking prior, then adapts the kick to free-kick settings where the ball is stationary at random positions, and finally extends it to moving-ball shooting through a locomotion-command and kick-trigger interface. A high-level heuristic planner controls this interface during training, while alternative high-level controllers can drive the same low-level policy at inference. In simulation, RoboNaldo demonstrates free-kick shot error 48.6% lower and shoot velocity 2.96x than prior work baselines. In real world on a Unitree G1 with onboard perception, RoboNaldo attains 0.73 m and 0.86 m average target shooting error from 3 m away in free-kick and moving-ball cases, accordingly. And the post-contact ball velocity reaches 13.10 m/s, which is 59-71% of reported professional open-play shot speed. Project page: $\href{https://opendrivelab.com/RoboNaldo}{\text{opendrivelab.com/RoboNaldo}}$.

</details>


### [388] [EM-Fall: Embodied mmWave Sensing for Day-and-Night Fall Detection on Humanoid Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11109)
*Yanshuo Lu,Yuxuan Hu,Shenghai Yuan,Xinyu Zhou,Kuangji Zuo,Bofan Lyu,XiChen Yuan,Jianfei Yang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Embodied mmWave fall detection on a mobile humanoid robot (EM-Fall) uses active viewpoint adjustment and lightweight temporal modeling to robustly detect falls in-home, supported by a new in-home mmWave fall dataset collected across eight environments with four participants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wearable or fixed sensors for fall detection face compliance, limited coverage, and occlusion issues; mobility enables persistent observability and active sensing; mmWave offers privacy and robustness to lighting.

Method: Integrates mmWave sensing with robotic mobility to actively reposition sensing viewpoint; employs a human-centered perception pipeline with lightweight temporal modeling to capture motion before, during, and after falls; validates on eight real indoor environments with four participants and builds an in-home mmWave fall detection dataset.

Result: Embodied mobile sensing improves monitoring continuity and maintains robust fall detection across diverse environmental conditions.

Conclusion: Embodied mobile sensing with mmWave is a practical and effective approach for robot-assisted home safety monitoring, addressing occlusion and coverage challenges in real-world settings.

Abstract: Falls are one of the leading causes of injury and hospitalization among elderly individuals, making reliable fall awareness an essential capability for safety monitoring in residential environments. However, existing fall detection systems often rely on wearable devices or fixed sensing installations, which may suffer from low user compliance, limited spatial coverage, or degraded performance under occlusion and poor lighting conditions. In this work, we propose \textbf{EM-Fall}, an embodied fall detection framework deployed on a mobile humanoid robot. The system integrates millimeter-wave (mmWave) sensing with robotic mobility, allowing the robot to actively adjust its sensing viewpoint and maintain target observability across rooms and under occlusion. To address interference in complex residential environments, including pet motion and multipath artifacts, we design a human-centered perception pipeline combined with lightweight temporal modeling to capture motion evolution before, during, and after fall events. We evaluate the proposed system across eight real indoor environments with four participants and construct an in-home mmWave fall detection dataset. Experimental results show that the embodied mobile sensing paradigm improves monitoring continuity and maintains robust fall detection performance under diverse environmental conditions. The proposed framework provides a practical solution for robot-assisted safety monitoring in home environments.

</details>


### [389] [JOIN: Anchor-Grasp-Conditioned Joining via Opposition, Inference, and Navigation for Bimanual Assistive Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11151)
*Drake Moore,Matt Cheng,Xiang Zhi Tan,Taşkın Padır*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Presents JOIN, a heterogeneous, on-demand bimanual system that pairs a wheelchair-mounted anchor arm with a summoned mobile manipulator to perform bimanual ADLs. Guided by a vision-language model and geometric tools, it achieves higher task success with less user correction than state-of-the-art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bimanual ADLs are essential for many tasks, yet single-arm wheelchair systems cannot accomplish them. Adding a second arm is impractical due to power, cost, and space constraints; there is a need for an on-demand, complement-arm solution.

Method: Three-phase decomposition for bimanual joining: plan, drive, grasp. Integrates a vision-language model with standard geometric tools for task-level knowledge. Introduces wheelchair-referenced opposition score and task-conditioned directional manipulability. Hardware includes Kinova Gen3 anchor and Hello Robot Stretch 3 complement; evaluated on same-object and different-object tasks.

Result: JOIN achieved 19/20 task attempts, surpassing state-of-the-art 14/20, and required markedly less operator correction.

Conclusion: An on-demand bimanual joining approach can robustly enable bimanual ADLs for wheelchair users. The combination of VLM-guided planning with geometric reasoning and the proposed metrics demonstrates improved reliability and reduced operator intervention.

Abstract: Assistive mobility and manipulation platforms have received increasing attention as a means of restoring independence to individuals with disabilities. While effective for many basic activities of daily living (ADLs), a significant percentage of everyday tasks such as opening a jar, pouring a liquid, lifting a tray, or basic meal preparation, is fundamentally bimanual and remains out of reach for any single-arm system. Adding a second arm to a wheelchair is impractical, due to the additional power draw, cost, and the loss of space required for transfers and mobility. We instead propose a heterogeneous, on-demand bimanual system, in which a wheelchair-mounted anchor arm is joined when needed by a summoned mobile manipulator that serves as a complement arm. The central technical problem, which we call bimanual joining, is conditional: the anchor has already committed to a grasp, and the complement arm must choose where to stand and what to grasp to complete the task. We formulate bimanual joining as a three-phase decomposition (plan, drive, grasp) and show that a vision-language model (VLM), coupled with standard geometric tools, provides task-level knowledge sufficient to solve a representative class of bimanual ADLs. Our system JOIN, contributes (i) a wheelchair-referenced opposition score, and (ii) task-conditioned directional manipulability. We evaluate JOIN on a Kinova Gen3 anchor and a Hello Robot Stretch~3 complement on representative same-object and different-object tasks. JOIN accomplished more attempts (19/20) than state-of-the-art methods (14/20) and required markedly less correction by the operator.

</details>


### [390] [TacForeSight: Force-Guided Tactile World Model for Contact-Rich Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11184)
*Yujie Zang,Yuhang Zheng,Xian Nie,Yupeng Zheng,Shuai Tian,Songen Gu,Chen Gao,Zining Wang,Shuicheng Yan,Wenchao Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TacForeSight introduces a lightweight, force-conditioned tactile foresight framework that predicts short-horizon tactile dynamics in latent space to enable real-time, proactive contact reasoning during manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the asymmetric spatiotemporal roles of global forces and local tactile sensing in contact-rich manipulation, enabling robust control under dynamic contact transitions and complex geometries.

Method: TacForeSight comprises TacForceWM, a tactile world model predicting short-horizon tactile latent dynamics from dual-finger tactile observations conditioned on high-frequency wrist wrench signals; and a Predictive Tactile-Conditioned Policy that uses predicted latents as anticipatory priors, models future tactile evolution via cross-attention, and fuses visuo-tactile features with a tactile-guided gating module; all operates in a compact latent space for real-time inference.

Result: Real-robot experiments across five representative tasks and three in-process perturbation settings show TacForeSight consistently outperforms baselines, with notable robustness to dynamic contact disturbances.

Conclusion: The approach enables proactive, real-time contact reasoning and inference in a compact latent space; datasets and models will be publicly released on the project website.

Abstract: Contact-rich manipulation requires robots to continuously perceive and regulate evolving physical interactions under dynamic contact transitions or complex surface geometries. Recent imitation learning methods improve contact-aware control by incorporating tactile or force feedback, but they rarely model the asymmetric spatiotemporal roles of global force and local tactile sensing. To address this, we propose TacForeSight, a lightweight force-conditioned tactile foresight framework for real-time manipulation. The core component is TacForceWM, a tactile world model that predicts short-horizon tactile latent dynamics from dual-finger tactile observations conditioned on high-frequency wrist force and torque signals. Another key component, the Predictive Tactile-Conditioned Policy, leverages the predicted latents as anticipatory contact priors, models the current-to-future tactile evolution via cross-attention, and adaptively fuses visuo-tactile features through a tactile-guided gating module. By forecasting purely within a compact latent space, TacForeSight enables proactive contact reasoning with efficient real-time inference suitable for high-frequency manipulation control. Real-robot experiments on five representative tasks and three in-process perturbation settings show that TacForeSight consistently outperforms existing baselines, particularly under dynamic contact disturbances. All models and datasets will be made publicly available on the project website at https://tacforesight.github.io/ProjectPage.

</details>
